19.25: vaani yadati goa'li chai ve'acharon al afar yaqum: 'For I know that my Redeemer [go'el] lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth' (or 'upon the dust'). This is one of the OT's most disputed verses — the text is difficult (the next verse, 19:26, is even more so: 'after my skin has thus been destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see God'). The term go'el (kinsman-redeemer) in the context of Job's suffering suggests: the one who will vindicate Job after death, who will see to it that justice is done. Whether Job envisions a bodily resurrection (as 19:26 suggests in the MT) or a post-mortem vindication, the theological content is the same: a personal Redeemer who is alive, who will act at the last, who will secure Job's vindication. The NT identifies this Redeemer as Christ (1 Cor 15:20: Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep).
19.1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ wayyōʾmar: 'Then Job answered and said.' The standard dialogue-opening formula appears 28 times in the book; its repetition creates the rhythm of a formal disputation. Job's response here opens his most extended and passionate speech, moving from accusation of the friends to the transcendent confession of vv.23-27.
19.2: ʿaḏ-ʾānāh tᵉḏaʾᵃḇûn nap̄šî ûṯᵉdakkᵉʾûn ḇiḏḇārîm: 'How long will you torment my soul and crush me with words?' Dāʾaḵ (crush, be dimmed/faint) is used of a lamp going out (Isa 42:3; 43:17) and of the spirits sinking. Dākāʾ is more violent — to shatter, reduce to powder (Ps 90:3; 94:5). The pairing: they torment his living inner self (nepeš) and simultaneously destroy him with verbal force. The cruelty the friends inflict with language is presented as physical-grade violence against Job's person.
19.3: zeh ʿeśer pᵉʿāmîm takᵉlîmûnî: 'Ten times now you have reproached me.' Ten times is likely idiomatic for 'repeatedly' (cf. Gen 31:7; Num 14:22) rather than a precise count of speech rounds. Kālaṃ (to humiliate, put to shame) describes public dishonor — the social violence of stripping someone of their standing before witnesses. Lōʾ-ṯēḇōšû takkᵉrû-lî: 'you are not ashamed to wrong me' — the lack of shame compounds the offense; they have no moral brake on their verbal attacks.
19.4: wᵉʾap̄ ʾim-šāgîṯî ʾittî tālîn mešûḡāṯî: 'Even if I have truly erred, my error remains with me.' Šāgāh = to go astray, err inadvertently (Lev 4:13; Num 15:28; Prov 19:27). The condition is hypothetical ('even if'); Job does not concede the point. The verb's semantic range emphasizes unintentional sin rather than deliberate transgression — even at its most modest, his admission would be only of an error, not of the guilt Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar are asserting. Ittî tālîn: 'it lodges with me' — the error, if any, remains in his own sphere; it does not constitute grounds for the friends' attack.
19.5: ʾim-ʾāmnām ʿālāy taḡdîlû wᵉṯôkîḥû ʿālay ḥerpātî: 'If you truly exalt yourselves over me and press my disgrace against me as evidence.' Gādal ʿālay (exalt yourselves over me) is the posture of domination and condescension. Tôkîaḥ (from yākaḥ = to argue/reprove/adjudicate) — their proof-from-disgrace reasoning: taking Job's suffering as evidence of guilt is a category error that the entire book exists to correct. The friends' logic is the proto-retribution theology Job is dismantling.
19.6: dᵉʿû ʾēp̄ô kî-ʾĕlôah ʿiwwᵉṯanî ûmṣûḏô ʿālay hiqqîp̄: 'Know then that God has wronged me (ʿiwwᵉṯanî) and thrown his net around me.' ʿĀwat (to bend, pervert, twist) is a legal term for injustice and distortion of judgment (Lam 3:36; Amos 8:5). Job dares to say that what God has done to him is, by the normal standard of justice, perversion. Mᵉṣûḏāh (net/siege-works) from ṣuḏ (to hunt); the hunting-net image opens the extended military metaphor that runs through vv.6-12. God as hunter and besieger rather than protector.
19.7: hēn ʾeṣʿaq ḥāmās wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿāneh: 'I shout “Violence!” (ḥāmās) but no one answers.' Ḥāmās is one of the weightiest Hebrew words for wrongful harm — the word used of the violence that provoked the flood (Gen 6:11; 6:13), the blood of Abel (Hab 2:8), and prophetic indictments throughout (Amos 3:10; Ezek 7:11). By crying ḥāmās, Job invokes the full legal-moral weight of injustice; it is the victim's cry when the offense rises to God's attention. The silence from the heavens — no answer, no verdict — is the theological crisis at the core of the book.
19.8: ʾorḥî gāḏar wᵉlōʾ ʾeʿĕbōr wᵉʿal nᵉṯîḇôṯay ḥōšeḵ yāśîm: 'He has blocked my way so I cannot pass; he has put darkness over my paths.' The imagery of blocked paths and enforced darkness frames Job's situation as imprisonment by divine agency. Gāḏar (to wall up, block) is used in Lam 3:7 ('he has walled me in so I cannot escape') and Hos 2:6 ('I will hedge up her way with thorns'). The blocked path and darkened way together invert the protective guidance language of Ps 23:3 ('he leads me in right paths') and Ps 119:105 ('your word is a lamp to my feet').
19.9: kᵉḇôḏî mēʿālay hip̄šîṭ wᵉʿăṭeret rōʾšî lāqāḥ: 'He has stripped me of my honor (kāḇôḏ) and taken the crown (ʿăṭāret) from my head.' Kāḇôḏ (honor/glory/weight) is the fundamental social-moral currency in the OT; its stripping is public humiliation at the most profound level. ʿĂṭāret rōʾš (the crown of the head) appears as a symbol of status and dignity (Prov 4:9; 12:4; 17:6). The two images together — robes stripped and crown removed — are the language of dethronement and disgrace: Job's former eminence (ch. 29) has been publicly annulled by God's action.
19.10: yittᵉṣēnî missābîḇ wāʾēlek wayyasēaʿ kāʾēṣ tiqwāṯî: 'He has broken me down on every side and I am gone; he has pulled up my hope like a tree.' Nāsaʿ is the verb used for uprooting tent pegs and breaking camp (Exod 12:37; 17:1; Num 9:17) — the nomadic image of a total displacement. Tiqwāh (hope, expectation — literally 'that toward which one looks forward') is a key wisdom term. Job's hope is not merely diminished but uprooted, its roots torn out — the image makes clear this is not discouragement but ontological devastation.
19.11: wayyaḥar ʿālay ʾappô wayyaḥšᵉḇēnî lô kᵉṣārāyw: 'He has kindled his wrath against me and counted me among his enemies.' Ḥārāh ʾap̄ (his anger burns) is the standard expression for divine wrath in action (Exod 4:14; Num 11:10; 32:13). To be counted among God's ṣārîm (adversaries, enemies, those who press against) is the most extreme possible statement about Job's condition — he is not merely abandoned but positionally assigned to the category of God's enemies. The theological shock is that Job knows himself innocent of the conduct that would deserve enemy-status.
19.12: yaḥad yāḇōʾû gᵉḏûḏāyw wayyiśpᵉlû ʿālay darkām wayyaḥănû sāḇîḇ lᵉʾohᵃlî: 'His troops advance together; they heap up siege ramps against their way against me and encamp around my tent.' Gᵉḏûḏ (marauding troop, raiding party) — a military force. Sālaḥ derek (heap up siege ramp) is the technical vocabulary of ancient siege warfare (cf. 2 Sam 20:15; Isa 37:33). The military metaphor is extended: God is a general conducting a systematic siege of a single individual. Job's tent (the last remnant of his domestic life) is surrounded. The image is grotesque in proportion — infinite divine power mobilized against one helpless man.
19.13: ʾaḥay mēʿālay hirḥîq wayyōdᵉʿay ʾak-zārû mimmennî: 'He has put my brothers far from me; those who knew me have become wholly estranged.' The catalogue of abandoned relationships in vv.13-19 is a carefully structured social inventory working inward: brothers → acquaintances → relatives → familiar friends → household guests → servants → wife → children's family → companions. Each relationship, which in ANE culture carried obligation of loyalty, has been severed. Zûr (estranged, foreign, a stranger) — those who knew him now experience him as an alien, not as a person they recognize.
19.14: ḥāḏᵉlû qᵉrôbāy ûmᵉyuddāʿay šᵉḵēḥûnî: 'My relatives have failed me, and my familiar friends have forgotten me.' Ḥāḏal (to cease, desist, fail) — they have simply stopped; the relational obligations they once performed have been discontinued. Šāḵaḥ (to forget) — the forgetting that in Israel's theology was the deepest relational failure (God 'forgetting' Israel in Isa 49:14; Job 'forgetting' God in 8:13). The friends have done what the faithless do: treated covenant relationships as optional when they become costly.
19.15: gārê ḇêṯî wᵉʾamᵉhōṯay lᵉzār taḥšᵉḇûnî nāḵrî hāyîṯî bᵉʿênêhem: 'Those in my house and my female servants count me as a stranger; I have become a foreigner in their eyes.' Zār (stranger, alien, outsider) and nāḵrî (foreign, from another nation) are the strongest words for social exclusion — Job is now 'a foreigner' in his own household. This inverts the social world of ch. 29-31: the master of a household whose servants honored him is now unrecognized in his own house. The reversal is complete when even those who share physical space treat him as an intruder.
19.16: lᵉʿaḇdî qārāʾtî wᵉlōʾ yaʿăneh bᵉp̄î ʾeṯḥannen-lô: 'I called my servant, but he gave me no answer; I had to beg him with my own mouth.' The collapse of the master-servant hierarchy: the servant who would previously have come at a word now does not answer, and the master must beg — literally supplicate (ḥānan = to implore, entreat favor). Ḥānan is the word used for prayer before God; here it is degraded to a plea to an ignoring slave. The reversal demonstrates that Job's former social power has been entirely stripped.
19.17: rûḥî zārāh lᵉʾišṯî wᵉḥannōṯî liḇnê ḇiṭnî: 'My breath is repulsive to my wife; I am loathsome to the children of my own body.' Rûaḥ (breath/spirit) — the intimacy that breath represents makes the repulsion more visceral: the most intimate physical marker of presence (breath) is the exact thing his wife finds repellent. Ḥānan liḇnê ḇiṭnî: 'imploring the sons of my womb' — beṭen usually refers to the mother's womb, but here applied to Job's own children with an unusual intimacy. These are the children born from his own body and they too require supplication.
19.18: gam-ʿᵉwîlîm māʾăsû bî ʾāqûmāh waydabbᵉrû-bî: 'Even young children despise me; when I stand up they speak against me.' ʿĀwîl (young child, child — sometimes child of low social standing) — even those who command no social authority openly mock Job. The phrase ʾāqûmāh waydabbᵉrû-bî ('when I rise, they speak against me') may imply that merely making a move — trying to assert some presence or dignity — provokes verbal attack from the lowest social tier. The inversion of Job's ch. 29 honor is complete: previously elders fell silent when he rose (29:9); now children jeer.
19.19: tāʿᵃḇûnî kāl-mᵉṯê sōḏî waʾᵃhāḇtî hāp̄ᵉḵû-bî: 'All my intimate companions abhor me; those I loved have turned against me.' Sôḏ (intimate circle, secret counsel, close associates) — the word for the innermost ring of trusted friendship (Jer 23:18; Ps 25:14; Prov 3:32; 11:13). The abhorrence (tāʿaḇ = to abhor, detest) of those in the innermost circle is the final and most devastating betrayal. ʾĀhăḇtî (those I loved) — the emotional vocabulary here is striking: Job describes these relationships with the language of love, making the turning-against an act of betrayal at the most intimate level.
19.20: bᵉʿôrî ûḇiḇśārî dāḇᵉqāh ʿaṣmî wāʾitmallēṭāh bᵉʿôr šinnāy: 'My bones cling to my skin and my flesh; I have escaped with the skin of my teeth.' Bᵉʿôr šinnāy is a unique hapax expression — literally 'in the skin of my teeth' — meaning escaped with absolutely nothing, by the barest margin. The teeth have no skin, so the phrase describes zero margin — survival of less than the minimum. Dāḇaq (to cling, cleave) — normally used of covenant loyalty (Gen 2:24; Ruth 1:14) is here used for bones clinging to skin because the flesh has wasted away; the word of marital union is applied to the grotesque adhesion of disease.
19.21: ḥonnûnî ḥonnûnî ʾattem rēʿāy kî yaḏ-ʾĕlôah nāḡᵉʿāh bî: 'Have pity on me, have pity on me, O my friends, for the hand of God has struck me.' The doubled ḥonnûnî (be gracious to me, have mercy on me) is an intensification — the same verb used in prayer to God (Ps 6:2; 30:10; 31:9; 56:1) is now directed at humans. Job applies to his friends the vocabulary of petitionary prayer, underscoring both his desperation and the irony: those from whom he begs mercy are the ones most persistently adding to his suffering. Yaḏ ʾĕlôah nāḡᵉʿāh bî: 'the hand of God has touched/struck me' — nāgaʿ (to touch, smite, reach) is the verb from 1:11 and 2:5 where the Adversary predicted Job would 'curse God to his face' if God's hand touched him. Job acknowledges the touch but does not curse.
19.22: lāmmāh tirḏᵉp̄ûnî kēʾl ûmibᵉśārî lōʾ ṯiśbāʿû: 'Why do you pursue me like God, and are not satisfied with my flesh?' Rāḏap̄ (pursue, chase) — the word of relentless hunting. By pursuing Job 'like God' (kēʾl), Job accuses the friends of acting with divine-scale relentlessness against a human being. They are not merely critical advisers but persecutors. Mibbᵉśārî lōʾ ṯiśbāʿû: 'you are not satisfied with my flesh' — the appetite-for-flesh imagery suggests predatory consumption; the friends have devoured his honor and reputation and still want more.
19.23: mî-yittēn ʾēp̄ô wᵉyikkāṯᵉḇû miллāy: 'O that my words were written down!' The longing for written testimony is the legal act of creating a permanent record that cannot be denied. Yikkāṯᵉḇû (niphal = 'were written') — the passive formulation leaves open who would inscribe them. Millîm (words, discourse) — specifically legal speech, not merely conversation. Job wants an irrefutable deposition that will outlast the moment and the witnesses. The desire for permanent testimony anticipates the divine answer: God will vindicate Job (42:7-9), and the book itself becomes the permanent record Job longed for.
19.24: bᵉʿēṭ bārzel wᵉʿōp̄eret laʿaḏ bᵉṣûr yᵉḥōṣᵉḇûn: 'That with an iron pen and lead they were cut into the rock forever!' ʿĒṭ bārzel (iron stylus) — a sharp iron tool for carving; ʿōp̄eret (lead) poured into the cuts to make them permanent and visible. The technology of rock inscription with metal and lead filling was practiced in the ancient world to create indelible monuments. Laʿaḏ (forever, perpetually) — Job wants testimony that outlasts any human witness. The irony: Job desired his words cut permanently in rock; instead they have been preserved in the most-read book of world literature.
19.26: wᵉʾaḥar ʿôrî niqqᵉp̄û-zōʾt ûmibbᵉśārî ʾeḥĕzeh ʾĕlôah: 'And after my skin is thus destroyed, yet from within my flesh I shall see God.' This verse (with v.27) is one of the most textually disputed passages in the OT. The key question is whether mibbᵉśārî means 'from my flesh' (i.e., still in the body = bodily resurrection) or 'from after/away from my flesh' (i.e., as a disembodied spirit). The Hebrew preposition min is ambiguous. The verb ḥāzāh (to see, behold — especially used of prophetic vision and direct divine encounter, Exod 24:11; Ps 17:15; 63:2) adds force to the statement: not mere knowledge of God but direct visual encounter.
19.27: ʾăšer ʾănî ʾeḥĕzeh-lî wᵉʿênay rāʾû wᵉlōʾ-zār kālû ḵilᵉyōṯay bᵉḥēqî: 'Whom I myself shall see, and my own eyes shall behold — not a stranger's eyes. My heart fails within me with longing.' The emphatic personal pronouns: ʾănî (I myself), ʿênay (my own eyes), wᵉlōʾ-zār (not a stranger's, not another's) — Job insists on personal, individual, direct encounter, not a third-party report. Kālû ḵilᵉyōṯay bᵉḥēqî: 'my kidneys (kilyôt) fail within my bosom with longing' — the kidneys were understood as the seat of the deepest emotions and will (Ps 7:9; 26:2; Jer 11:20); their 'failing' (consuming away) describes an intensity of longing that is physically felt.
19.28: kî tōʾmᵉrû maddûaʿ nirḏāp̄-lô wᵉšōreš dāḇār nimṣāʾ-bî: 'If you say, “Why are we still pursuing him?” when the root of the trouble lies within him.' Job addresses a hypothetical future conversation the friends might have. Šōreš dāḇār (root of the matter/word) — the friends assume the root of Job's problem is internal sin; Job knows the root of the matter is the divine action described in vv.6-12. The word šōreš (root) is taken up by God himself in 42:7-8, reversing the friends' diagnosis: the root of the problem was their incorrect speech about God, not Job's concealed sin.
19.29: gûrû lāḵem mippᵉnê-ḥereb kî-ḥēmāh ʿăwōnôt ḥāreb lᵉmaʿan tēḏᵉʿûn šaddāy: 'Fear the sword, for wrath brings the sword's punishment, so that you may know there is indeed a judgment.' Job's warning is a reversal of the retribution principle the friends have been applying to him: the sword does not threaten Job but the friends, whose wrath-driven pursuit constitutes the actual sin requiring judgment. Šaddāy — ʾēl šaddāy (God Almighty) is the divine name used almost exclusively in the book of Job (31 of the 48 total OT occurrences), emphasizing the overwhelming sovereign power of God before whom all judgment ultimately stands.

38.4: The divine speech from the whirlwind (Job 38-41) is the OT's most extended meditation on the incomprehensibility and majesty of God. YHWH's questions ('Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding') do not answer Job's question about suffering — they reframe it by confronting Job with the Creator's perspective. The response to theodicy is not a logical explanation but a theophanic encounter: when Job sees YHWH, his questions are transformed, not answered (42:5: 'my eye sees you'; he is satisfied). This pattern — suffering resolved not by explanation but by encounter with God — points to the incarnation: the answer to the problem of suffering is not a theodicy argument but the Son of God entering the suffering and going through it.
38.1: wayyaʿan YHWH ʾet-ʾîyôḇ min-haśśᵉʿārāh wayyōʾmar: 'Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind and said.' The divine name YHWH appears here for the first time in the entire dialogue (chs. 3-37), where only ʾĕlôah (God) and šaddāy (Almighty) have been used. The shift to the covenant name is significant: Job is addressed not by the remote transcendent deity but by YHWH, the God of the covenant and of personal relationship. Śᵉʿārāh (whirlwind, storm-wind — a theophanic term): the whirlwind is YHWH's vehicle of revelation (cf. Ezek 1:4; Zech 9:14; Nah 1:3), consistent with the storm theophany tradition of Sinai (Exod 19:16-19) and the Psalms (Ps 18:10-15).
38.2: mî zeh maḥšîḵ ʿēṣāh bimillîm bᵉlî-ḏāʿat: 'Who is this who darkens counsel with words without knowledge?' The opening charge is forensic and rhetorical: maḥšîḵ ʿēṣāh (darkening counsel) — clouding what is clear, making wisdom unclear. Job's speeches, for all their emotional authenticity, have treated the divine governance as something that can be adjudicated by human speech (millîm) that lacks knowledge (bᵉlî ḏāʿat). The charge is not that Job lied but that he spoke beyond his competence. The same charge could be laid at the friends; YHWH addresses it to Job because Job is the one demanding an answer.
38.3: ḥăḡōr-nā kᵉḡeḇer ḥălāṣêḵā wᵉʾešʾālᵉḵā wᵉhôḏîʿēnî: 'Brace yourself like a man; I will question you and you must answer me.' ḥăḡōr kᵉḡeḇer (gird your loins like a man, a warrior) — the command to prepare for combat, addressed to warriors before battle (1 Kgs 20:11; Jer 1:17). God does not destroy Job with his power but invites him into a contest of questions. The role reversal is complete: Job has been demanding a hearing before God; now God gives it — but as questioner, not defendant. Wᵉhôḏîʿēnî (and you must instruct me/make me know) — the ultimate ironic impossible demand: the creature instructing the Creator.
38.5: mî-śām mᵉmaddêhā kî ṯēḏāʿ ʾô mî-nāṭāh ʿālêhā qāw: 'Who determined its measurements — do you know? Or who stretched the measuring line across it?' The question form (mî = who?) dominates the divine speech: 77 questions in chs. 38-41. Each question is a demonstration of divine knowledge that Job lacks and cannot claim. Qāw (measuring line, plumb line) — the architectural surveying tool used for the earth's dimensions. The earth was laid out with divine precision; Job was not present for the measurements and cannot claim knowledge of them.
38.6: ʿal-mah ʾadānêhā huṭbāʿû ʾô mî-yārāh ʾeḇen pinnātāh: 'On what were its foundations (ʾadānîm) set? Or who laid its cornerstone?' ʾAḏen (base, socket, foundation — also used for the tabernacle's silver bases, Exod 26:19) — the foundations of the earth are its divine-architected supports. ʾEḇen pinnāh (cornerstone) — the capstone that locks an arch or the first stone that determines alignment. The building-metaphor for creation (foundations, measuring line, cornerstone) will be used in the NT for Christ as the cornerstone (Ps 118:22; Matt 21:42; Eph 2:20) — the divine builder of creation becomes the cornerstone of the new creation.
38.7: bᵉron yaḥad koḵᵉḇê bōqer wayyārîʿû kol-bᵉnê ʾĕlōhîm: 'When the morning stars rang out together and all the divine beings shouted for joy?' Koḵᵉḇê bōqer (morning stars) and bᵉnê ʾĕlōhîm (sons of God, divine beings — the same term as 1:6; 2:1) — the angelic assembly that witnessed and celebrated creation. The cosmic joy at creation's completion (rûaʿ = shout with a ringing cry, used of battle-cries and worship, Ps 47:1; 98:4) is the inverse of the lament that pervades chs. 3-37: before there was suffering, there was rejoicing at the world's making. The question implies: were you there for that joy, Job?
38.8: wayyāsōḵ baḏᵉlāṯayim yām bᵉgîḥô mēreḥem yēṣēʾ: 'Who shut in the sea with doors when it burst out from the womb?' The sea's origin: gîḥô mēreḥem yēṣēʾ (when it burst out from the womb) — the sea is born like a creature, emerging from a womb. But God immediately confines it with doors (dalatāyim = double doors). The contrast between the sea's violent emergence (it bursts) and God's calm architectural response (doors and bars, v.10) is the creation theology of Gen 1:9-10 dramatized: chaos is bounded by divine decree. The sea as embodiment of chaos (cf. Ps 93:3-4; Isa 27:1; Rev 21:1) is controlled from birth.
38.9: bᵉśûmî ʿānān lᵉḇūšô waʿărāp̄el ḥᵉtullāṯô: 'When I made clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling cloth.' The extended birth metaphor continues: the newborn sea is clothed (lᵉḇûš = garment) in clouds and wrapped (ḥᵉtullāh = swaddling bands, the cloth used for newborns, Ezek 16:4; Luke 2:7) in ʿărāp̄el (thick darkness, the darkness of a storm or the Sinai theophany, Exod 20:21; Deut 4:11). God tends to the newborn sea as a parent, providing it coverings — but those coverings are also restraints. Divine care and divine boundary-setting are the same act.
38.10: wāʾešbōr ʿālāyw ḥuqqî wāʾāśîm bᵉrîaḥ ûḏᵉlāṯāyim: 'When I fixed its boundaries and set its bars and doors.' Ḥōq (decree, boundary, prescribed limit) — the same word used for the Torah's commandments; the sea has its own divinely given ḥōq: a decree about how far it may go (cf. Prov 8:29: 'he prescribed a limit for the sea'). Bᵉrîaḥ ûḏᵉlāṯāyim (bar and double doors) — the cosmic lockwork that holds the sea in its place. YHWH is the locksmith of the ocean; the forces of chaos are not suppressed by their own weakness but contained by divine architecture.
38.11: wāʾōmar ʿaḏ-pōh tāḇôʾ wᵉlōʾ tôsîp̄ wᵉp̄ō-yāśît bᵉgaʾôn gallêḵā: 'When I said, “This far you may come and no farther; here your proud waves will stop.”' The divine boundary-decree is given in direct speech — the most direct form of authority. ʿAḏ-pō tāḇôʾ wᵉlōʾ tôsîp̄: 'until here you will come and not add/continue' — the sea's advance is terminated by divine word. Bᵉgaʾôn gallêḵā: 'here the pride of your waves will be set/stopped' — the gaʾôn (majesty, pride) of the waves is the sea's power claiming space; God stops that claim with a word. The same divine word that stopped the primordial sea speaks to Job from the whirlwind.
38.12: hᵉmiyyāmêḵā ṣiwwîṯā bōqer yiddaʿᵉtāh šaḥar mᵉqōmô: 'Have you ever commanded the morning since your life began, or told the dawn where to take its place?' The dawn is a standing appointment (mᵉqôm = place, station) that it has kept since creation. Ṣiwwāh bōqer (commanded the morning) — the morning receives a daily divine command to rise. Šaḥar (dawn, early light) — the dawn knows its place and goes there; Job does not command it. The daily recurrence of dawn is a standing demonstration of divine faithfulness and sovereignty: creation's order is not automatic but God's ongoing governance.
38.13: lᵉʾĕḥōz biḵanpôt hāʾāreṣ wᵉyinnāʿᵃrû rᵉšāʿîm mimmennāh: 'So that it would seize the earth's edges and shake the wicked out of it.' The dawn's function: it grabs the edges of the earth (ʾāḥaz biḵanpôt hāʾāreṣ = grip the wings/corners of the earth) and shakes it like a garment being shaken to dislodge dirt (nāʿar = to shake out). The wicked (rᵉšāʿîm) who operate under cover of darkness are exposed and dislodged by the dawn — the daily sunrise is a daily moral event, a repeated act of divine justice that removes the dark's shelter from those who use it for evil (John 3:19-20; Eph 5:13).
38.14: titᵉhappēḵ kaḥōmer ḥôṯām wayyiṯyaṣᵉḇû kᵉmô lᵉḇûš: 'The earth changes like clay under a seal; its features stand out like a garment.' Kᵉḥōmer ḥôṯām (like clay under a seal) — a signet ring pressed into clay wax to make an impression; the dawn's light impresses features on the earth's surface just as a seal impresses a pattern in clay. Wayyiṯyaṣᵉḇû kᵉmô lᵉḇûš: 'they (the features) stand out like a garment' — the earth's topography is revealed by light, as features of fabric are revealed when light hits it. The dawn literally creates visible reality by revealing what darkness concealed.
38.15: wᵉyimmānaʿ mērᵉšāʿîm ʾôrām ûzᵉrôaʿ rāmāh tiššāḇēr: 'From the wicked their light is taken away, and the arm raised in violence shall be broken.' The dawn denies the wicked their darkness: yimmānaʿ mērᵉšāʿîm ʾôrām — their light is denied (paradoxically — the wicked's 'light' is the darkness in which they operate; the true light of day is their enemy). Zᵉrôaʿ rāmāh tiššāḇēr: 'the raised arm will be broken' — the arm of violent power, raised in the darkness, is broken by the morning. God's daily act of dawn is simultaneously the daily defeat of those who rely on darkness for their power.
38.16: hăḇāʾtā ʿal-niḇkê-yām ûḇᵉḥēqer tᵉhôm hiṯhallāḵtā: 'Have you gone down to the springs of the sea, or walked through the depths of the abyss?' Niḇkê yām (springs of the sea — where fresh springs emerge from the sea floor; known phenomena in the ancient world) and tᵉhôm (the deep, the abyss — the primordial deep of Gen 1:2). Job has not descended to the sea floor or walked through the primordial deep. The geography of the question is vertical: YHWH's governance extends to depths Job has never visited and cannot even conceive of fully.
38.17: hăniḡlû lᵉḵā šaʿărê māwet wᵉšaʿărê ṣalmāwet tirʾeh: 'Have the gates of death been revealed to you? Have you seen the doors of deepest darkness?' Šaʿărê māwet (gates of death) — the entrance to Sheol, the underworld (Ps 9:13; 107:18; Isa 38:10). Ṣalmāwet (deep shadow, death-shadow — possibly from ṣēl + māwet = shadow of death, though some parse it differently) — the darkness beyond death. Job has not passed through death's gates to verify their existence or structure. The question horizontally extends the previous vertical survey: above (storm, stars) and below (sea depths, death) — the full axis of created space is beyond Job's experience.
38.18: hiṯbônantā ʿaḏ-rōḥᵃḇê ʾāreṣ haggēḏ ʾim-yāḏaʿtā ḵullāh: 'Have you comprehended the earth's full breadth? Tell me, if you know all of it.' Rōḥab ʾāreṣ (breadth of the earth) — the horizontal extent. The survey is now complete: depth (v.16 sea, v.17 death), breadth (v.18 earth). YHWH's questions have mapped the created world's full spatial extent — and Job has visited none of its edges. The epistemological point: you cannot speak confidently about the governance of a creation whose dimensions you cannot grasp.
38.19: ʾê-zeh haddereḵ yiškon-ʾôr waḥōšeḵ ʾê-zeh mᵉqômô: 'Where is the way to where light lives? And where does darkness make its home?' Yiškon-ʾôr (where light dwells) — light is personified as a being with a dwelling place (šāḵan = to dwell, the word for the tabernacle's divine indwelling). The same for darkness: it has a mᵉqôm (place, habitation). The light and darkness that Genesis 1 describes as separated by God are here further personified as entities with cosmic addresses. Their addresses are known to God and unknown to Job.
38.20: kî tiqqāḥennû ʾel-gᵉḇûlô wᵉkî-ṯāḇîn nᵉṯîḇôṯ bêṯô: 'Can you take it to its territory and find the paths to its dwelling?' Light and darkness have their own territories (gᵉḇûl = border, boundary) and dwelling-paths. The ability to escort light or darkness to their proper domains would require knowledge of their cosmic geography — knowledge that only the original creator of the light-darkness division (Gen 1:3-5) possesses. The question is another form of 'were you there?' — present at the original ordering of light and darkness.
38.21: yāḏaʿtā kî-ʾāz tiwwālēḏ ûmispar yāmêḵā rabbîm: 'You know, of course — for you were born then, and the number of your days is very great!' The divine irony is devastating: kî-ʾāz tiwwālēḏ (for you were born back then!) — the sarcasm of impossible antiquity. Job was not born at creation; his days are not great enough to have witnessed the earth's founding. The irony inverts Job's complaint that his days are too short and his suffering too fresh: they are far too short to qualify him as a cosmological expert. The rhetorical irony here is the most artistically pointed moment in the divine speech.
38.22: hăḇāʾtā ʾel-ʾōṣᵉrôṯ šāleg wᵉʾōṣᵉrôṯ bārāḏ tirʾeh: 'Have you gone into the storehouses of the snow, or seen the storehouses of the hail?' ʾŌṣār (storehouse, treasury) — the weather phenomena are stored in divine treasuries, like a king's armory. Šāleg (snow) and bārāḏ (hail) are kept in reserve (ṣāpan = to store up, hide in reserve, v.23). The storehouse image implies intentionality: God is not the passive backdrop of weather systems but maintains a stockpile from which he deploys snow and hail for specific purposes.
38.23: ʾăšer-ḥāśaḵtî lᵉʿet-ṣār lᵉyôm qᵉrāb ûmilḥāmāh: 'Which I have kept in reserve for the time of trouble, for the day of battle and war.' The divine arsenal: snow and hail are not merely weather but weapons held in reserve for strategic moments. ʿEt ṣār (time of trouble/distress) and yôm qᵉrāb ûmilḥāmāh (day of battle and war) — God has cosmic weapons stockpiled for use in history's turning points. This was experienced literally in Israel's battles (Josh 10:11: hailstones against the Amorites; 1 Sam 7:10: thunder against the Philistines) and in eschatological judgment (Rev 8:7; 16:21).
38.24: ʾê-zeh haddereḵ yēḥāleq ʾôr yāp̄ēṣ qāḏîm ʿălê-ʾāreṣ: 'By what path does the light divide, or the east wind scatter itself across the earth?' The east wind (qāḏîm) — in the Levant the east wind (rûaḥ qādîm) was the hot, desiccating sirocco from the desert, capable of devastating crops and intensifying heat (Exod 14:21; Jonah 4:8; Hos 13:15). Its path is unknown to meteorologists and unknown to Job; only the one who sends it knows where it will go.
38.25: mî-p̄illag laššeṭep̄ tᵉʿālāh ûdereḵ ḥăzîz qōlôṯ: 'Who has cut a channel for the flood of waters, or a path for the thunderbolt?' Pālag (to divide, split, cut a channel) — irrigation canals are dug by human engineers; but who dug the channels that direct rainstorms? Ḥăzîz qōlôṯ (thunderbolt of the thunder-voice) — the lightning bolt also follows a path cut for it. The hydrological and electrical systems have their prescribed paths; God is the engineer who laid them out.
38.26: lᵉhaMṭîr ʿal-ʾereṣ lōʾ-ʾîš miḏḇār lōʾ-ʾāḏām bô: 'To bring rain on a land where no person lives, on the desert with no human in it.' God waters the wilderness where no human benefits from the rain. This single verse undoes utilitarian theodicy: not all divine action is organized around human benefit or human observation. Creation is not anthropocentric; God waters deserts for reasons that have nothing to do with human prosperity or suffering. The implication for Job's case: God's governance of the universe does not have human happiness as its organizing principle.
38.27: lᵉhaśbîaʿ šōʾāh ûmᵉšōʾāh ûlᵉhaṣmîaḥ môṣāʾ ḏeše: 'To satisfy the desolate wasteland and make tender grass spring up.' Šōʾāh ûmᵉšōʾāh (desolate wasteland) — the most barren landscape, where human life is impossible. God waters it to satisfy it (śābaʿ = to satisfy, fill to fullness) and make grass grow for no human observer. The satisfaction of the desert is an end in itself in the divine economy. Creation has intrinsic value before God independent of human use — a counter to the purely anthropocentric universe the friends assumed.
38.28: hăyēš-lāmāṭār ʾāḇ ʾô mî-hôlîḏ ʾeglê-ṭāl: 'Does the rain have a father? Who has fathered the drops of dew?' Rain and dew are personified as requiring a father (ʾāḇ = father, one who generates). Eglê ṭāl (drops of dew) — literally 'dewdrops.' The question of paternity: who generates rain and dew? Not human action; they arise from the divine-natural interaction that YHWH alone comprehends. The extended birth/parent metaphor (sea was born, v.8-9; now rain and dew need a father) presents the whole meteorological system as a family of phenomena fathered and mothered by God.
38.29: mibbeṭen mî yāṣāʾ haqqāraḥ ûkp̄ōr šāmayim mî yᵉlāḏô: 'From whose womb did the ice come? Who gave birth to the frost of heaven?' Qāraḥ (ice) and kp̄ôr (frost) are also personified as having a birth-womb. The birth-vocabulary for weather phenomena is theologically loaded: reḥem (womb) is the same word used for God's compassion (raḥămîm = compassion, from womb-roots). God is simultaneously the father and mother of the natural world's phenomena — the gendered metaphors are inclusive and complementary.
38.30: kāʾeḇen māyim yiṯḥabbeʾû ûp̄ᵉnê ṯᵉhôm yiṯlakkāḏû: 'The waters harden like stone, and the surface of the deep is locked.' The freezing of water: māyim... kāʾeḇen (waters like stone) — water solidifying to stone-hardness in winter. Ûp̄ᵉnê ṯᵉhôm yiṯlakkāḏû: 'the face of the deep is caught/locked' — the primordial deep (tᵉhôm of Gen 1:2) is locked in ice. The same chaos-waters that had to be bounded in creation (v.8-11) are seasonally locked by cold — a recurring divine act of ordering. Who commands this? Not Job.
38.31: hᵉṯaqqšēr maʿădannôṯ kîmāh ʾô-mošᵉkôṯ kᵉsîl tᵉpattēaḥ: 'Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades, or can you release the belt of Orion?' Kîmāh (Pleiades — the cluster of seven stars, visible spring through autumn) and Kᵉsîl (Orion — the winter constellation whose belt is three stars; also used metaphorically for the fool, kᵉsîl = fool). The chains (maʿădannôṯ = bonds, fetters) of the Pleiades and the belt (mošᵉkôṯ = bands, cords) of Orion are used as astronomical metaphors for constellation behavior. Can Job alter the seasonal positions of these stars? The question implies governance of the stellar calendar.
38.32: hătôṣîʾ mazzārôṯ bᵉʿittô wᵉʿayiš ʿal-bānêhā tanḥēm: 'Can you bring out Mazzaroth in its season? Can you guide the Bear with its cubs?' Mazzārôṯ is uncertain — possibly the zodiac, possibly the twelve constellations of the year, or possibly Venus as the morning star. ʿAyiš (the Bear — Ursa Major/the Big Dipper) and its cubs (bānêhā) — the circumpolar constellation that never sets in the northern sky. The question asks about seasonal guidance of these constellations. The astronomical vocabulary confirms the ancient world's detailed observation of stellar positions; YHWH is the guide of the celestial calendar.
38.33: hāyāḏaʿtā ḥuqqôṯ šāmayim ʾim-tāśîm mišṭārô bāʾāreṣ: 'Do you know the laws of heaven? Can you establish its rule over the earth?' Ḥuqqôṯ šāmayim (laws/statutes of heaven) — the fixed ordinances by which the heavens operate, the physical laws governing celestial bodies. Mišṭārô (its rule, dominion — from šāṭar = to write, rule) — can Job extend heaven's governance down to earth? The question is about the communication between celestial and terrestrial order. Job cannot write the laws of the stars, let alone enforce them on earth. The universe is a coherent rule-governed system whose constitution is divine.
38.34: hᵉṯārîm lāʿāḇ qôleḵā wᵉšip̄ʿat-māyim tᵉḵassêḵā: 'Can you raise your voice to the clouds and make a flood of water come down?' The question of meteorological command: human voice cannot address clouds with any effect. Tᵉḵassêḵā šip̄ʿat māyim (a flood of water covers you) — the answer to the divine cloud-command is the downpour that covers the earth. Job can call to the clouds; they will not answer. Only YHWH's voice commands the precipitation systems of the earth.
38.35: hᵉtᵉšallaḥ bᵉrāqîm wᵉyēlēḵû wᵉyōʾmᵉrû lᵉḵā hinnēnû: 'Can you send out lightning bolts on commission, and do they report back to you: “Here we are”?' The lightning as divine messengers: yᵉšallaḥ (to send, as a šālîaḥ = commissioned agent) — the lightning is dispatched like an ambassador and reports back (hinnēnû = here we are, the readiness formula used by Abraham at God's call, Gen 22:1, and Isaiah before God, Isa 6:8). The lightning obeys YHWH like a servant waiting to be sent; it does not obey Job. The image of lightning saying “here we are” is one of the divine speech's most vivid personifications.
38.36: mî-śāt baṭṭûḥôṯ ḥoḵmāh ʾô mî-nāṯan laśśeḵwî bînāh: 'Who has placed wisdom in the hidden self (ṭûḥôṯ), or given understanding to the mind (śeḵwî)?' Both ṭûḥôṯ and śeḵwî are hapaxes — their meanings are disputed. Ṭûḥôṯ: possibly 'inward parts, hidden self' (from Ugaritic cognate); śeḵwî: possibly 'cock' (rooster), 'mind,' or 'seeing-organ.' If referring to the rooster, the question is: who gave the rooster the instinct to announce the dawn? If referring to the human mind, the question is: who endowed humans with wisdom at all? Either reading points to the divine origin of natural intelligence and discernment.
38.37: mî-yᵉsappēr šᵉḥāqîm bᵉḥoḵmāh ûnᵉḇlê šāmayim mî yašᵉḵîḇ: 'Who has the wisdom to count the clouds? Who can tilt the water jars of heaven?' Sāpar šᵉḥāqîm (count the clouds) — a task no human can accomplish, either because of sheer number or because of their constant change. Nᵉḇlê šāmayim (water jars of heaven) — the clouds as jars (nēḇel = skin-jar, jug) full of water that God tilts to pour. The tilting metaphor is deliberately domestic and intimate: the master of the universe manages weather with the same intentionality as a servant serving water from a jar. Divine sovereignty operates at all scales simultaneously.
38.38: bᵉṣeqet ʿāp̄ār lammuṣṣāq ûrᵉḡāḇîm yiḏḇāqû: 'When the dust hardens into a mass and the clods of dirt cling together?' The condition that follows the tilting of the water jars (v.37): when rain falls, dust hardens to mud and clods cling. The transformation of loose dry soil into cohesive wet earth is another ordinary observation presented as a divine act: God manages the water cycle down to its soil-level effects. The same God who counts clouds also knows what happens to the dirt when rain hits it.
38.39: hᵉṯaṣṣûḏ laʾaryēh ṭārep̄ wᵉḥayyat kᵉp̄îrîm tᵉmallēʾ: 'Do you hunt prey for the lion and fill the hunger of its cubs?' The divine speech shifts from cosmic (stars, seas, weather) to biological (animals). God is the hunter-provider for lions — not a distant deity but an active sustainer of the carnivores. Ṭārep̄ (prey, torn food) — God hunts prey for animals. This is the theodicy answer in miniature: God provides for the predators too, not just the meek. The universe's food chain is sustained by divine provision at every level.
38.40: kî yišḥû bammᵉʿônôṯ yēšᵉḇû bassukāh lᵉʾāraḇ: 'When they crouch in their dens and wait in ambush in their thickets?' The lion's hunting posture: šāḥāh (to bow down, crouch) in its den (mᵉʿônāh = den, lair, dwelling of wild animals — used metaphorically for God's dwelling in Ps 26:8; 68:6). God knows the posture of each lion as it crouches in ambush. The divine knowledge extends from the stellar (v.31-33) to the predatory animal's daily hunting position. Omniscience of the particular and the cosmic simultaneously.
38.41: mî yāḵîn lāʿōrēḇ ṣêḏô kî-yaldāyw ʾel-ʾēl yᵉšawwēʿû yiṯʿū bᵉlî-ʾōḵel: 'Who provides the raven its food when its chicks cry out to God and wander about hungry?' The raven (ʿōrēḇ) — the bird associated with wilderness and death in the OT (1 Kgs 17:4-6: ravens fed Elijah; Gen 8:7: the raven from the ark). Its chicks cry to God (ʾel-ʾēl yᵉšawwēʿû) when hungry — the raven's young are presented as petitioners before God. God hears and provides for the ravens, whose young cry out. The divine speech ends its first movement with a crying creature whose petition God meets — an implicit question to Job: if God hears the ravens, will he not hear you?

1.1: The four-descriptor portrait of Job — tam (blameless/complete), yashar (upright/straight), yare’ ’elohim (fearing God), and sar mera’ (turning from evil) — forms a merism of moral completeness. Tam is the same root used for the unblemished sacrificial animal (Lev 1:3, tamim); it means without spot or defect, complete. Yashar = morally straight, aligned with the right. The four terms together are the OT’s most comprehensive moral character description applied to a human, echoed only in YHWH’s own repeated endorsement of Job (v8; 2:3).
1.2: The seven sons and three daughters, seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred donkeys — the numbers are schematic for totality (seven = complete, the totals doubly round). The three-seven structure of Job’s possessions and children appears at the book’s end (42:12–13) but doubled, marking the restoration as exceeding the original in every category except the children, who are replaced in equal number (not doubled) — a structural detail the narrator maintains precisely.
1.3: Job is described as the greatest (gadol) among all bene qedem (sons/people of the East). The ‘East’ (qedem) is the region associated with wisdom traditions in the OT (1 Kgs 4:30; Isa 2:6). Job’s identification as an easterner places him in the wisdom tradition, consistent with his dialogues’ high poetic and philosophical register. His non-Israelite identity (Uz is likely in Edom or trans-Jordan) is significant: the book makes its most acute theological argument through a figure outside the covenant community.
1.4: The sons’ feasts (mishteh, drinking-feast) each on his yom (appointed day) — the seven sons in rotation create a cycle of seven feasts. The phrase yom hullado (day of his birth) in 3:1 and 3 uses the same yom vocabulary. The cyclical festivity of the sons will be shattered by the wind that strikes on one of these feast days (v18–19).
1.5: Viqaddishem (he consecrated/sanctified them) — Job’s regular priestly intercession for his children before dawn. The verb qadash in the pi’el = cause to be holy, purify. Job functions as a priestly intercessor: he offers burnt offerings (‘olot) for each child. The concern: perhaps they ber’u ’elohim (cursed/blessed God) bilbavam (in their hearts). Note: berakh here is the first use of the curse-euphemism in the book (cf. v11, 2:5, 2:9) — the same word as “bless,” used to avoid writing the actual curse.
1.6: Ha-Satan — with the definite article ha, making it a title or role rather than a personal name: the adversary/accuser. Satan is from the root satan = to accuse, be hostile to, obstruct. In the legal context of the heavenly court (bene ha-’elohim presenting before YHWH), the adversary functions as a prosecuting attorney who challenges YHWH’s assessment of Job. The bene ha-’elohim (sons of God) is the divine council, not a reference to angelic beings in the later Christian sense but to the heavenly court of YHWH’s royal household (cf. Ps 82; 1 Kgs 22:19-22).
1.7: Missut ba-’arets umitthallek bah (from roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it) — the adversary’s self-description. The sut (roam, wander) and halak (walk) describe ceaseless investigative movement. The same vocabulary appears in Zech 1:10–11 and 6:7 for the angelic patrol of the earth — the divine intelligence network. The adversary has been on patrol and has evidently been observing Job.
1.8: YHWH initiates: hasamta libbeka ’al ’abdi ’iyyov (have you set your heart on/taken notice of my servant Job?) — the verb sam leb = to fix attention on, observe carefully. YHWH initiates the Job discussion, not the adversary. This is a detail of the Hebrew structure that the prose preserves: the test originates in YHWH’s own move to draw the adversary’s attention to Job. The fourfold moral description of v1 is repeated verbatim, framing YHWH’s own endorsement as narrative proof of Job’s character.
1.9: Hinam — ‘for nothing,’ ‘freely,’ ‘without cause’ — is the adversary’s thesis word. The one Hebrew word sets the book’s central question: Does Job fear God for nothing (hinam)? The same word appears in 2:3 (to ruin him hinam) and Ps 35:19 / 69:4 (hinam enemies). The adversary’s claim is that piety is always instrumental: no one loves God for God’s own sake; everyone has a price.
1.10: Vayyasek ba’adhu (you have put a protective fence around him) — suk = to hedge in, protect. The adversary sarcastically identifies YHWH’s protection as the reason for Job’s piety. Ironically, in 3:23 Job will use the same concept of being ‘fenced in’ (yasek be’ado) as a complaint against God — the same enclosure becomes oppression when viewed from within suffering.
1.11: Velukh — stretch out. The imperative-like ulam shelah-na (but reach out now) followed by the oath ’im-lo’ ’al-panekha yevarekheka (he will surely curse you to your face) — the ’im-lo’ oath formula = “truly / if not.” The yevarakhekha = will bless/curse you. The Hebrew euphemism: the scribe (or narrator) writes “bless” (barakh) where the adversary means “curse.” The phrase ’al-panekha (to your face) heightens the audacity: Job will deny God to his face, not privately.
1.12: YHWH’s limit: hinno veyadelkha raq ’elaw ’al-tishlah yadekha (all he has is in your power; only do not lay a hand on the man himself). The divine limit (raq = only/but) is the theological boundary that makes the test meaningful: suffering is permitted up to a defined boundary. The adversary has power only within YHWH’s granted permission — a structural feature of Job’s suffering that Job himself never learns within the book’s narrative.
1.13: The four messenger scenes (vv14–19) overlap in rapid sequence — each begins “while he was still speaking” (’od zu medabber zeh). The four catastrophes bracket the full range of loss: livestock (Sabeans), livestock and servants (fire from heaven), livestock and servants (Chaldeans), and finally children. The overlapping messengers create the Hebrew narrative’s most concentrated portrait of simultaneous catastrophe.
1.14: The messenger (mal’ak) arrives at a run (vayarots). The oxen (baqqar, cattle for plowing) and donkeys grazing beside them — the work-animals at their tasks. The placement of the animals in normal agricultural activity highlights the ordinariness of the moment of catastrophe.
1.15: Vatipol Sheba (the Sabeans fell upon them) — the verb naphal = fell/attacked. The Sabeans (Sheva) are associated with South Arabia or Ethiopia in OT geography. Anokhi levaddi niltatiy (I alone escaped to tell you) — the messenger formula. The phrase will be repeated by all four messengers, creating a liturgical refrain of total destruction.
1.16: ’Esh ’elohim (fire of God/divine fire) fell from the heavens — lightning or what later readers might call divine fire. The destruction of the sheep and their servants by divine fire from heaven is particularly ironic: Job has been offering burnt offerings (’olot, literally “things that go up in smoke”) to God on behalf of his children (v5); now YHWH’s own fire descends and burns the sheep and servants. The sacrificial vocabulary and the destructive fire occupy the same semantic space.
1.17: Three Chaldean (kasdim) raiding parties (reshim shalosha = three heads/divisions) take the camels and kill the servants. The Chaldeans appear in the OT as the Babylonian empire’s ethnic core (Dan 1:4; Jer 5:15). Their appearance alongside the Sabeans (v15) places the story in a world of both south Arabian and Mesopotamian raiders — geographically broad, spanning the known world.
1.18: The fourth and final messenger: Job’s sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine at the oldest brother’s house — the feasting scene from v4 now given as the setting for the final catastrophe. The detail that they were at the bekhor’s (firstborn’s) house may be deliberate: the day of the firstborn, the eldest sibling’s feast, is the day of the wind.
1.19: Ruah gedola (a great wind) from across the desert (midbar) strikes the four corners of the house (’arba’ pinnot habayit). The “four corners” detail implies the wind surrounds and collapses the house entirely, not just strikes one side. Vayyamotu (and they died) — all the young people. The catastrophe is total: all ten children in a single event.
1.20: Job’s five-verb response: wayyaqom (he arose), wayyiqra’ et-me’ilo (he tore his outer garment), wayyigallach et-rosho (he shaved his head), wayyippol ’artsah (he fell to the ground), wayyishtahu (he bowed/worshipped). The first three are acts of mourning (tearing garments and shaving the head are standard mourning rituals, Isa 15:2; Ezra 9:3); the last two are acts of worship. The sequence is crucial: Job moves from mourning to worship in a single continuous gesture — without any reported pause or struggle.
1.21: The vocabulary of Job’s declaration: ’erom (naked) twice; mibeten ’immi (from my mother’s womb) / shamah ’ashub (there I shall return) — the earth as mother womb imagery. Most significantly: YHWH natan v-YHWH laqah (YHWH gave and YHWH has taken) — both the giving and the taking attributed to YHWH directly, not to the adversary. Job never learns about the heavenly court scene; his attribution of both blessings and disasters to YHWH is theologically correct but based on no inside information. The verse ends: yehi shem YHWH mevorakh (blessed/praised be the name of YHWH) — using barakh in its positive sense, the exact opposite of what the adversary predicted he would do with the same root.
1.22: Lo-hata’ (did not sin) and lo natan tifla le-’elohim (did not give/assign unsavoriness/blame to God). Tifla is a rare word (only Job 1:22; 24:12; Jer 23:13) meaning something improper, unsavory, without taste — a word of moral and aesthetic disgust. Job did not charge God with tifla = did not find God’s action shameful or improper. This is the narrator’s own verdict: the first round of the test is complete; the adversary’s thesis is falsified.

2.1: The scene resets: the heavenly council (bene ha-’elohim) presents before YHWH again, and the adversary comes among them to present himself (lehityatsev ’al-YHWH). The verb yatsav in the hithpael = to take one’s stance, present oneself, stand (used of soldiers in formation and of judicial standing). The adversary is again in his position as the accusing officer of the divine court.
2.2: The adversary’s answer is verbatim from 1:7: missut ba-’arets umitthallek bah. The repetition is not careless; the prose prologue uses deliberate word-for-word repetition to highlight the structural parallelism of the two tests. The second scene is a controlled variation of the first: same setting, same question, different stakes.
2.3: YHWH’s response includes a significant new element: od hevel bemummatoh (he still maintains his integrity/wholeness). Tummah is the noun of the tam family, meaning completeness, moral wholeness, integrity. YHWH then acknowledges: watsetitehu bi (you incited/moved me against him) and lehabbel’o hinam (to ruin/swallow him for nothing). Balla’ = to swallow/destroy; hinam = for nothing (reprising 1:9 — the book’s thesis word). YHWH describes the adversary’s incitement and the destruction of Job as genuinely causeless (hinam), yet the test proceeded under YHWH’s permission.
2.4: ’Or be’ad ’or (skin in exchange for skin) — the adversary’s counter-claim is a commercial or proverbial formula whose exact meaning is debated. The most natural reading: one skin (one’s own life) will be given to protect another skin (possessions, children). The formula asserts that human self-interest is bottomless: when the real stakes (one’s own body) are raised, Job will crack. Kol ’asher le-’ish yitten be’ad nafsho (everything a man has he will give for his life) — the adversary’s anthropology: pure self-preservation at the core of every human.
2.5: ’ets ’atsamaw (his flesh and his bones) — the adversary adds the second condition: if YHWH strikes Job’s own body, he will curse God. The progression from 1:11 (strike what he has) to 2:5 (strike his body) tracks the escalation. ’Im-lo’ ’al-panekha yevarekheka — the same oath formula and curse-euphemism from 1:11, verbatim.
2.6: YHWH’s second permission: hinno veyadekha raq ’et-nafsho shomor (he is in your hands; only preserve his life). The limit is more specific now: the adversary may attack Job’s body but not take his life (nefesh). Nefesh = life-breath, person, the animating principle. The adversary has full access to Job’s physical existence but is bounded at the line of death.
2.7: Shehin ra’ (painful/festering sores) — shehin = an inflammatory skin disease; ra’ = severe/evil. The same word (shehin) is used for the Egyptian plague of boils (Exod 9:9–10) and the skin disease in Leviticus 13. Job is afflicted mikapo regel ’ad qoqodoh (from the sole of his foot to the top of his head) — the merism of totality: every point of his body. The comprehensiveness echoes the totality of the first round of disasters.
2.8: Job scrapes himself with a potsherd (heres) while sitting betok ha-’efer (in the midst of the ashes). The ashes are the standard location of mourning and lament (Ezek 27:30; Jer 6:26; Isa 58:5). The broken pottery as a scraper for diseased skin is a detail of physical degradation; Job moves from the position of greatest man of the east (1:3) to sitting among ashes scraping sores.
2.9: Job’s wife: ha-’odhekha mahaziq betummatekha (are you still holding fast in your integrity?) — hazaq = hold fast/be strong; tummah = the wholeness-integrity from vv3 and 1:1. Her advice: barekh ’elohim ve-mut (curse God and die). The same curse-euphemism (barakh) for third time in the prologue. The irony: Job’s wife uses the same euphemistic blessing-word that the adversary used. Whether the wife is acting as an agent of the adversary or as a broken woman in genuine despair is left ambiguous by the narrator.
2.10: Job’s rebuke: kedabber ’ahat hanevelot tedabberi (you are speaking as one of the foolish women speaks). Naval = senseless/foolish in a moral-practical sense — the person who acts as though there is no God, the same root as Nabal (1 Sam 25:25: “his name is Nabal and folly is with him”). Job’s response: ha-tov neqabbel me-’et ha-’elohim ve-’et hara’ lo neqabbel (shall we accept the good from God and not accept the adversity?). The verb qibbel = receive/accept; both good and adversity are received from the same source. The narrative verdict (v10b): lo-hata’ ’iyyov bifsatav — Job did not sin with his lips. Note the precision: not “in his heart” (as in 1:5’s concern about the children) but with his lips.
2.11: The three friends: ’Eliphaz ha-Temani (from Tema/Teman, in Edom, associated with wisdom, Jer 49:7), Bildad ha-Shuhi (from Shuah, possibly near the Euphrates), Tsofar ha-Naamati (from Naamah, uncertain location). All three are outsiders like Job; the wisdom dialogue takes place in a non-Israelite setting. Their purpose: lenud-lo (to express grief/sympathy to him) and lenakhamo (to comfort him) — two verbs of pastoral presence. Nud = to nod/wag the head in sympathy; naham = to comfort/console.
2.12: From a distance they could not recognize him (lo hikkiruhu) — Job’s transformation from the greatest man of the east is so complete that his own friends cannot identify him at a distance. The three gestures: wayyis’u qolam wayyivku (they lifted their voices and wept), wayyiqre’u ’ish me’ilo (each tore his outer garment), and wayyizrequ afar ’al-rosheihem hashamaymah (threw dust toward the sky over their heads). The dust thrown skyward is a grief gesture directed toward heaven — an appeal or expression of communal mourning before God.
2.13: Seven days and seven nights (shiv’at yamim veshiv’at leilot) of silent presence. Shiv’a (seven) is the number of the mourning week; cf. the seven-day mourning for Jacob (Gen 50:10). Velo-dibber ’elav dabar (and no one spoke a single word to him) — the friends’ silence before Job’s suffering is, paradoxically, the most theologically sound thing they do in the entire book. YHWH will later criticize their words (42:7) but the silence is not criticized. The narrator’s reason: ki ra’u ki gadal hake’ev me’od (they saw that the pain was very great). Ke’ev = anguish/pain.

3.1: ’Ahar ken (after this) marks the pivot from prose prologue to poetic dialogue. Patah ’iyyov ’et-pihu (Job opened his mouth) — the formal introduction of direct speech, used for significant utterances (cf. Balaam’s oracles, Num 22:28; 23:5). Vayeqallel ’et-yomo (he cursed his day) — the verb qalal in the pi’el (intensive stem) = curse thoroughly, call down misfortune. This is not barakh (the adversary’s predicted curse-word) but qalal — Job curses the day, not God. The distinction is precise and legally significant within the book’s framing.
3.2: The transition formula vaya’an ’iyyov vayyomar (Job responded/spoke and said) introduces the poetic speech. ’Anah in the qal = to respond/answer, even when addressing no prior speaker. Here Job responds to his own silence, opening what will become the book’s central 35-chapter dialogue. The formal speech introduction distinguishes the poetic speeches from the prose narrative, marking the generic shift.
3.3: Yo’vad yom (let the day perish) — the jussive (yo’vad) marks the curse: let it perish. The jussive forms (yo’vad, v3; yehi hoshekh, v4; yig’alehu, v5; yiqahenu, v6) carry the chapter’s curse-prayer syntax throughout. Gavar zakhar (a male was conceived/was strong) — the root gavar = to be strong, prevail; its nominal form gever = a strong man (as opposed to ’ish = man of standing, ’adam = humanity, ’enosh = mortal). The announcement “a gaver (strong male) has been conceived” celebrates the birth of one expected to have strength; Job curses that announcement.
3.4: Haya hahu hoshekh (let that day be darkness) — Job reverses the creation-light of Gen 1:3-4 (yehi ’or, let there be light; vayar ’elohim ki-tov, God saw it was good). Job’s curse inverts the creation sequence: he is uncreating the day of his birth. The phrase ’al-yidrsheihu ’eloah mima’al (may God above not seek/care for it) — God’s providential attention (darash = to inquire/seek) is the source of light; Job prays God would not seek this particular day.
3.5: Yig’alehu hoshekh vetsalmavet (let darkness and deep shadow reclaim/redeem it) — the verb ga’al = to redeem, to function as next-of-kin redeemer. The bitter irony: ga’al is used throughout the OT for YHWH’s redemptive action (Exod 6:6; Isa 43:1); here Job prays that darkness, not YHWH, would be the go’el (redeemer) of his birthday. Tsalmavet = deep shadow / shadow of death, a compound: tsel (shadow) + mavet (death).
3.6: Hallaylah hahu yiqqahehu ’ofel (let deep darkness seize that night) — ’ofel = gloom, thick darkness. The night of his conception is to be excluded from the annual count (bal-yihad bimei-shanah = let it not be counted among the days of the year). The complete erasure from the calendar: the day/night of Job’s conception and birth is to be excised from time.
3.7: Galmud (barren, sterile) — used for the infertile woman in Isa 49:21 and Job 15:34. The night is to be barren: lo tavo renana vo (let no shout of joy enter it). Renana = a shout of joy, ringing cry. Job wishes the night of his conception had been infertile and joyless.
3.8: ’orere yom (those who curse days) — a reference to professional cursers, specialists in cursing or incantation, associated with astrological practices. Ha-’atidim ’orer livyatan (those who are skilled at rousing Leviathan) — Leviathan is the chaos monster of ancient Near Eastern cosmology (appearing in Job 3:8; 40:25[41:1]; Ps 74:14; 104:26; Isa 27:1). Job calls on the professional cursers to activate the chaos monster against his birthday, placing his lament in a mythological register that goes beyond conventional Hebrew prayer.
3.9: The wish that the stars of the predawn twilight (kokevei nishpoh) would go dark — nishpoh probably = its twilight/its eyelashes (the stars as the flickering lashes of the dawn). Yequwweh la-’or ve-’ayin (let it wait for light but there be none) — the night of his birth should experience what Job is currently experiencing: waiting for relief that does not come. Ve-’al-yir’eh be’afappei-shahar (and let it not see the dawning of the morning) — ’afappei shahar = the eyelids of the dawn (a striking poetic phrase for the dawn’s first light).
3.10: The reason for the curse: ki lo sakar dalte bitni (because it did not close the doors of my mother’s womb). The womb-door image: if only the womb-doors had remained closed, the birth prevented. Vataster ’amal me’einai (and did not hide trouble from my eyes) — ’amal = toil, trouble, mischief (the same word used in 4:8). If birth had been prevented, Job would not have seen the trouble (’amal) he now experiences.
3.11: Lamma’ (why) introduces the first of Job’s three lamma’ questions in this chapter (vv11, 20, 23). Lamma’ in lament poetry is the interrogative of protest rather than inquiry — not seeking information but expressing the unbearability of the situation. Lo ’emut meraham (why did I not die from the womb) — Job regrets surviving birth.
3.12: Madu’a qiddemonti berakhayim (why were there knees to receive me?) — the knees of the midwife or father receiving the newborn, a gesture of acceptance into the family (cf. Gen 50:23, Jacob’s grandsons placed on Joseph’s knees). The alternative is direct death without acceptance. Shaddayim ki yana’qti (breasts that I nursed) — the second element: why was there milk to sustain the life that Job now wishes had ended at birth?
3.13: The description of Sheol as a place of rest: az ashkav vashaqot (then I would have lain down and been at rest) — the verbs shakav (lie down) and shaqat (be still/quiet). Yashanti az yanuah li (I would have slept and then rested to myself). The fourfold vocabulary of rest in Sheol (lie down, be still, sleep, rest) forms a picture of peaceful death that Job contrasts with his present suffering.
3.14: Job imagines being at rest with melakhim ve-yo’atse ’arets (kings and counselors of the earth) who habaneh horavot lamoh (built ruins for themselves) — a puzzling phrase that likely means “who built tombs/monuments for themselves” or “whose great works are now ruins.” The point is egalitarian: in death, Job would rest alongside those who had power and built monuments, all now reduced to silence.
3.15: ’Osarot levamo millu (who filled their mansions with silver) — the kings and princes who hoarded wealth in life are reduced to the same silence as the poorest. Wealth and power are no armor against Sheol. The kasar (princes/officials) of v15 are rulers; their treasuries are irrelevant in death.
3.16: Ke-nefel tamun (like a hidden stillborn) — nefel = that which falls out, a stillbirth (Ps 58:8; Eccl 6:3). Job imagines a preferable alternative existence as a stillborn child: never having emerged, never having seen the light. The stillborn is “hidden” (tamun) — buried without ceremony, its brief existence concealed from the world.
3.17: Sham rasha’im hadlu rogez (there the wicked cease from rogez) — rogez = turmoil, agitation, disturbance. In Sheol, the wicked no longer trouble their victims; the yegi’e koah (those exhausted in strength) find rest. Job’s picture of Sheol in ch3 is notably positive — a place of rest, equality, and peace — contrasting with later passages where Sheol is a place of forgetfulness and darkness.
3.18: Yishhadu la-’otsar (the prisoners rest together) — all prisoners (’asir) find rest there, no longer hearing the overseer’s voice (qol nogesh). Nogesh = taskmaster, driver (same word as the Egyptian taskmasters of Exod 5:6). The oppressive social structures of the living world cannot reach into Sheol.
3.19: Qaton vegadol sham hu (the lowly and the great are alike there) — Sheol levels all social distinctions. Ve-’eved hofshi me-’adonav (and the slave is free from his master) — the ultimate social leveling: the slave, who in life is owned and directed, is freed (hofshi = free, freed slave) from the master’s authority by death. This is one of the OT’s most radical statements about death as the equalizer of social hierarchy.
3.20: Lamah yitten la-’amel ’or (why does he give light to the suffering one?) — the second lamah (why). ’Amel = the one who labors under burden/toils/suffers. The gift of life (light) to those in bitter anguish (mare nefesh = bitter of soul) seems cruel: existence itself is the torture when the sufferer longs for Sheol’s peace.
3.21: Hameyahhalim lemmavet ve-’einenu (those who long for death that does not come) — yahhal in the pi’el = to wait/long intensely. Death refuses to come to those who seek it most desperately. Vayhapseruhu mimmattamonot (they dig for it more eagerly than for buried treasure) — the metaphor of mining/digging for treasure from 28:1–11 is anticipated here: death is searched for with the intensity that treasure hunters search for gold.
3.22: Ha-semehim ’ale-gil yasisu ki yimshe’u qaver (those who are overjoyed and exult when they finally reach the grave) — the grave is the longed-for destination; finding it produces joy. This is grief pressed to its extremity: the only good news Job can imagine is death. Qaver = the grave/tomb (as distinct from she’ol, the realm of the dead).
3.23: The third lamah: le-gever asher darko nistarah (to a man whose way is hidden) — gever = a strong man; his way (derek) is hidden (nistar, from sathar = hide). Vayasek ’eloah ba’ado (and whom God has fenced in on every side) — the verb sakak = to hedge/fence, the same root the adversary used in 1:10 (vayyasek) to describe YHWH’s protective hedge around Job. The adversary saw the hedge as protection; Job now experiences the same enclosure as entrapment. Same word, opposite evaluation.
3.24: Ki lifnei lhmi ’anhati tavo (for my sighing comes before my food) — ’anhah = sighing/groaning (the involuntary sound of grief, Ps 31:10; 102:5). Job’s grief is prior to and greater than his hunger; the body’s need is subordinated to the soul’s anguish. Vayyittpeku kamayim she’agotai (and my cries pour out like rushing water) — she’agah = a roar/cry of pain; nathakh = to pour out, flow freely.
3.25: Ki pahad pahad wayyi’teni (what I dreaded most has come upon me) — pahad twice: the noun and the verb from the same root, an emphatic figura etymologica. Job’s fear (pahad) anticipated the catastrophe; the catastrophe arrived. The iterative: the fear itself, not merely the event, is named twice. This verse is sometimes read as a theological problem: Job feared disaster, suggesting his fear was a failure of faith. But the context is lamentation, not confession.
3.26: Lo shalavti ve-lo shaqatti ve-lo-nahatti vayavo rogez (I had no ease, no rest, no peace, yet trouble came) — three negated nouns of rest: shalav (ease), shaqat (quiet), nuah (rest) — all denied. Then: vayavo rogez (yet rogez came) — rogez is the same word used in v17 for what ceases in Sheol. Job has the opposite of Sheol’s peace: constant agitation (rogez) where peace should be. The chiasm closes ch3: the rogez that will cease in Sheol (v17) is the same rogez that has overtaken Job’s waking life (v26).

4.1: Vaya’an ’Eliphaz ha-Temani (then Eliphaz the Temanite replied) — the standard dialogue introduction. Eliphaz is the first and most theologically sophisticated of the three friends. His speeches (chs 4–5, 15, 22) represent the most developed defense of the retributive justice principle. Teman is associated with wisdom in Jer 49:7 (“Is wisdom no more in Teman?”), making Eliphaz a representative of the best available wisdom tradition outside Israel.
4.2: Hannissa davar ’elekha tila’eh (if one ventures a word with you, will it trouble you?) — nasa’ = to lift up/carry/attempt; tila’eh = to be weary/troubled. Eliphaz begins with a rhetorical apology for speaking, acknowledging the sensitivity of the moment. Va’atsor be-millim mi yukal (who can hold back from speaking?) — implying the inner compulsion to offer wisdom overrides the caution to remain silent.
4.3: Hinneh yissarta rabbim (look, you have instructed many people) — yasar in the pi’el = to discipline, instruct, train. Eliphaz acknowledges Job’s prior role as a teacher and strengthener of others. Veyadayim rafot tezaqzeq (and you have strengthened weak hands) — rafot = drooping/hanging loose; hazaq = to strengthen/make firm. Eliphaz is not contemptuous: he concedes Job’s past ministry.
4.4: Mikhshol yakim millatekha (your words have upheld the one who was stumbling) — mikhshol = the one who stumbles, literally “a stumbling one.” Birkhayim kor’ot te’ammets (you have steadied feeble knees) — kor’ot = bending/buckling knees. The very knees that Job’s wisdom steadied in others are the knees that buckle in his own crisis (v4 and v5 form the before-and-after contrast).
4.5: Ki ’atah tavo ’elekha vatilla’ (but now it has come upon you, and you are exhausted) — the pivot: ’atah (now). Vatilla’ = you are weary/impatient. Tigga’ ’adekha vattibbahel (it has touched you, and you are terrified) — tibbahel = to be dismayed/panicked (pi’el passive). Eliphaz’s implicit accusation: Job counseled others admirably but cannot take his own medicine.
4.6: Halo yir’atekha kislat’kha (is not your fear of God your confidence?) — yir’atekha = your fear (of God) — the same root as Job’s description in 1:1 (yere’ ’elohim). Eliphaz directly invokes Job’s own acknowledged piety: if Job truly fears God, that should be his source of confidence (kislah = confidence/trust). Tiqvatekha ve-tom derakhekha (your hope and the integrity of your ways) — tom is the same root as tam (1:1, blameless) and tummah (2:3, 9, integrity). Eliphaz is using Job’s own moral vocabulary against him.
4.7: Zekhor-na mi hu naqqi ’abad (think: who, being innocent, has perished?) — the rhetorical question is the friends’ foundational axiom, stated most starkly here. Naqqi = innocent, clean, without guilt. Eliphaz’s implied answer: no innocent person has ever perished. This is the retributive justice principle (lex talionis applied to providence): suffering = guilt; innocence = protection. The book’s entire argument is designed to refute this principle, since Job is innocent and suffering.
4.8: Ka’asher ra’iti horesh ’aven (as I have observed, those who plow trouble/iniquity) — harash ’aven (to plow iniquity) and zore’e ’amal (to sow trouble/mischief). The agricultural metaphor: those who plant wickedness harvest wickedness. ’Amal = trouble/mischief (the same root as the “trouble” Job’s mother’s womb failed to hide, 3:10).
4.9: Minishmat ’eloah yo’vedu (by the breath of God they perish) — nishmah = breath/spirit, the animating breath (Gen 2:7). Umeruah ’appo yikhlah (and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed) — ’ap = nose/nostril/anger. The dual meaning of ’ap (nose and anger) allows: both the literal blast of YHWH’s nose/breath and his wrath destroy the wicked.
4.10: She’agat ’aryeh ve-qol shahats (the roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion) — the lion imagery of vv10–11 is a traditional wisdom metaphor for the powerful/arrogant who are eventually brought low. Shinnei keppirim nittu’u (the teeth of the young lions are broken/shattered) — the powerful who rule by force will have their instruments of power destroyed.
4.11: Layish ’oved me-bli-teref (the mature lion perishes for lack of prey) — layish = a mature/aged lion (only four occurrences in OT; the rare word for the most experienced, dangerous lion). Even the most powerful predator perishes when cut off from its food supply. Ugnei leviyah yitparedu (the cubs of the lioness scatter) — without the dominant lion, the family structure dissolves. The point: the wicked will fall, however strong.
4.12: Ve-’elay davar yegunnav (a word was stolen/came stealthily to me) — gannav from ganav = to steal. The revelation arrived by stealth, like a thief. Vattiqqah ’ozni shemets mimmennu (and my ear received a whisper of it) — shemets = a whisper, a faint rustling sound (only here in the OT). The divine communication arrived as the faintest possible sound, barely a whisper.
4.13: Bise’ipim meh ezyonot laylah (in disturbing thoughts from visions of the night) — se’ipim = disturbing/troubling thoughts, branching thoughts (from sa’ap = to divide, branch). The hefal tardema (when deep sleep falls on people) — tardemah = deep sleep, the same word used for Adam’s sleep in Gen 2:21 and Abraham’s in Gen 15:12, both precursors of divine revelation.
4.14: Pahad qera’ani vare’adah (fear came upon me and trembling) — pahad = terror/fear (the same word Job used for his own terror in 3:25). Vere’adah = trembling. Ve-rov ’atsmotay hiyhid (making all my bones shake) — hiyhid = to make tremble (hiphil). The theophanic experience is described through its physical effects on the recipient.
4.15: Veruah ’al-panay yahhalof (a spirit passed before my face) — ruah = breath/spirit/wind; the theophanic presence passing before the recipient (cf. Elijah’s cave experience, 1 Kgs 19:11–13, where ‘passing before’ is the theophanic pattern). Yisammer se’ar besari (the hair of my flesh bristled) — a precise physical description of the goosebump response to numinous terror.
4.16: Ya’amod ve-lo ’akir mareihu (it stood still, but I could not discern its form) — the spirit is present but invisible; it stops, but without form (temunah). Temunah = shape/form/likeness (same word as Num 12:8: Moses saw the temunah of YHWH; Deut 4:12: no form seen at Sinai; Ps 17:15: seeing God’s temunah in the afterlife). The revelation to Eliphaz withholds what Moses received: no form, only a voice.
4.17: Ha-ye’enosh me-’eloah yitsdak (can a mortal be more righteous than God?) — ’enosh = the frail human, the mortal (as opposed to ’ish, gever, or ’adam). The choice of ’enosh emphasizes human fragility and mortality. Yitsdak = be righteous, be vindicated (in the forensic sense). The rhetorical question expects a negative answer: of course not. But the book’s subsequent argument will complicate this: YHWH himself says Job ‘spoke what is right’ (42:7).
4.18: Hen ba’avadav lo ya’amin (God places no trust even in his servants) — ’eved = servant; the divine servants (angels) are not trusted by God without question. Uvemal’akhav yasim teholah (he charges his angels with error) — toholah = folly, error (only here and Jer 23:13, where it is used for the prophets’ error). Eliphaz’s argument: if God finds error even in his angelic servants, how much more in mortals made of clay?
4.19: Af shokhnei batei-homer (how much more those who dwell in houses of clay) — batei-homer = houses of clay. Homer = clay, the stuff from which humans are made (Gen 2:7, afar min-ha’adamah = dust from the ground; but homer also = clay, used in pottery). ’Asher-be’afar yesadam (whose foundation is in the dust) — the foundation of the clay house is the very dust it’s made of. Yedakke’um lifnei-’ash (who are crushed more easily than a moth) — the comparison: humans are more fragile than a moth (which dies at a touch).
4.20: Miboqer la’erev yikkattu (between morning and evening they are destroyed) — the full length of a day is more than enough time for human life to end. Mibbeli measim ye’avdu lanetsah (without anyone noticing, they vanish forever) — netsah = forever/permanently. Human death goes largely unnoticed in the cosmic scheme: the world continues without marking individual human exits.
4.21: Halo nissa’ yiteram bam (is not their tent-peg pulled up from within them?) — a difficult phrase. Yeter = cord/tent-peg/stake. The tent-peg (yeter) that holds the structure upright, removed from within — the human body as a tent whose internal structure collapses at death (2 Cor 5:1 uses the same metaphor: “if the earthly tent [house] we live in is destroyed”). Yamuthu velo behokhma (they die, and that without gaining wisdom) — behokhma = in/with wisdom. The final word: human life ends before wisdom is acquired. This is the final argument of Eliphaz’s first stanza, and it sets up what ch28’s great wisdom hymn will address: wisdom is inaccessible to mortal searching.

15.1: The speech-introduction formula wayyaʿan + name + speech-type marker is the standard poetic frame for all dialogue rounds (cf. 4:1; 8:1). Temanite (tēmānî) marks Eliphaz as a sage of the south Arabian wisdom tradition.
15.2: Rûaḥ qāḏîm (east wind, lit. 'wind of the east') denotes the sirocco, the hot dry desert wind that scorches crops. Eliphaz accuses Job of filling himself with dāʿat rûaḥ — 'empty wind-knowledge': fine-sounding but hot and destructive. Ḥākām (wise man) sets up the irony: is this how a sage speaks?
15.3: Millîm lōʾ yôʿîl: 'words that accomplish nothing' — the term yôʿîl (Hiphil of yāʿal) means 'to profit/benefit'; wisdom speech must produce tangible good. The wisdom tradition prizes utility: speech that edifies vs. speech that merely fills time.
15.4: Yirʾat ʾēl (fear of God): the foundational wisdom category (cf. 1:1, 8; Prov 1:7). Eliphaz charges Job with undermining this fear — tāpar (to loosen, weaken) — and with impeding śîḥāh (meditation/prayer/reflection) before God. The accusation is serious: Job's speech is not merely imprudent but anti-pious.
15.5: ʿĂwōnĕkā (your iniquity/guilt) from ʿāwōn, the standard OT word for moral deviation and its consequences. Lĕšôn ʿărûmîm (tongue of the cunning/crafty): Eliphaz links Job's speech to the serpent's wisdom (ʿārûm, Gen 3:1), a damning insinuation.
15.6: Pîkā (your mouth) and śĕpātêkā (your lips) are the instruments of self-incrimination. The legal language — mouth/lips as witnesses (ʿānāh bĕkā, 'testify against you') — frames Job's speech as courtroom evidence. Eliphaz claims not to be the accuser; Job's own words are.
15.7: Hārîšôn āḏām tiwwālēḏ: 'Were you the first man born?' Rîšôn (first) may allude to a primal wisdom figure — the primordial man of Ezek 28:12-14 or the figure behind Prov 8:22-31 — someone present at creation. Eliphaz mocks Job's implicit claim to unique insight.
15.8: Sôḏ ʾĕlôah: the 'council/secret of God' — the heavenly assembly where divine deliberations occur (cf. 1:6; Amos 3:7; Jer 23:18). Tišmaʿ (did you hear/listen?) implies participation: Eliphaz denies that Job has been admitted to the divine council that might explain his suffering.
15.9: The rhetorical questions mah yādaʿtā (what do you know?) / mah tābîn (what do you understand?) challenge Job's epistemic standing. Bîn (discern/understand) is the deep wisdom term alongside ḥāḵam; Eliphaz claims parity or superiority in both.
15.10: Śêbāh (gray-haired) and yāšîš (very aged): the elders who anchor wisdom in generational tradition. Both terms emphasize extreme age. Kabbîr from kbr (great in years) intensifies: these men make even Job's father seem young. Tradition as epistemology.
15.11: Tanḥûmôt ʾēl: 'consolations of God' — the term tanḥûmāh (comfort, consolation) from nḥm (comfort, console). Eliphaz claims Job has refused divine comfort. Dābār laʾaṭ (gentle/soft word) emphasizes that God has spoken mildly to Job; Job should have received it gratefully.
