15.12: Mah-yiqqāḥăkā libbĕkā: 'What carries your heart away?' — lēb (heart) as the seat of will and judgment. Mah-yirzmûn ʿênêkā: 'Why do your eyes flash/glare?' — rāzam (to wink, flash provocatively, HALOT) appears only here; the gesture implies arrogance or aggressive dismissal.
15.13: Rûḥăkā ʾel-ʾēl: 'you turn your spirit against God' — the charge of hostility toward God (cf. 7:11). Hôṣēʾtā (you bring out) — Job's words are portrayed as weapons brought out of the mouth rather than wisdom offered from the heart.
15.14: Mah-ʾĕnôš kî-yizkeh: 'What is a mortal that he could be pure?' — ʾĕnôš (mortal, frail humanity) and zāḵāh (to be clean, morally pure). Yĕlûd ʾiššāh (born of woman) is a creaturely limitation phrase (cf. 25:4; Sir 10:18; Matt 11:11) — birth from a woman signals creaturely constitution. Eliphaz uses this anthropological premise to ground universal moral limitation.
15.15: Qĕdōšāyw (his holy ones): the heavenly beings/angels (cf. 4:18; 5:1). The claim that God trusts neither angels nor the heavens themselves extends moral impurity to the entire created order. Yēʾāmēn (put trust/confidence in): Niphal of ʾāman — even the celestial realm is not sufficiently reliable in God's sight.
15.16: Nĕtʿāb (abominable) and neʾĕlāḥ (corrupt, vile — hapax): the escalating scale of human depravity from creaturely limitation (v14) to active wickedness. Šōteh kĕmayim ʿawlāh: 'drinks wickedness like water' — the image of wicked behavior as an involuntary drink, absorbed naturally and constantly.
15.17: Ḥăwîtĕkā (I will show you) from ḥwh: the wisdom declaration-formula introducing Eliphaz's extended portrait of the wicked man's fate. Ḥāzîtî (I have seen): appeals to personal experience as well as tradition (v18-19).
15.18: Lōʾ kāḥādû: 'without concealment' — the ancestral wisdom was openly transmitted, not hidden. The wisdom tradition claims public, verifiable transmission in contrast to esoteric knowledge. Mēʾăbôtāyw (from their fathers): multigenerational authority.
15.19: Lāhem lĕḇaddām: 'to them alone' — the tradition belonging to those whose land was exclusively theirs before foreign contamination. The phrase implies ethnic-intellectual purity: the wisdom Eliphaz recites is pre-syncretistic, untouched by foreign influence. A claim to authentic transmission.
15.20: Mityaqqēl (twists in anguish): Hithpoel of ḥwl (to writhe in pain — used for labor pain and anguish). Rāšāʿ (wicked man): the standard OT legal-moral term for the guilty/condemned. Mispār šānîm (number of years): an appointed span, fixed for the tyrant (ʿārîṣ, lit. 'violent/ruthless one').
15.21: Qôl pĕḥādîm bĕʾoznāyw: 'a sound of terrors in his ears' — paḥad (terror, dread, sometimes of theophany) is what the wicked man carries. Šôḏēḏ (destroyer): the divine agent or personified disaster that strikes even in šālôm (peace/prosperity).
15.22: Lōʾ yāʾmîn: 'he has no hope' — from ʾāman (to trust/believe): the wicked man cannot trust that he will escape darkness. Ṣāpûn (reserved/marked) for the sword: the certainty of destruction already appointed against him.
15.23: Nōdēḏ (wanders): from nwḏ (to wander aimlessly, flee) — the exile-motif. The wicked man wanders like a fugitive. Yôm-ḥōšeḵ (day of darkness): the day of judgment/death already nākôn (established, ready) at his hand.
15.24: Ṣar ûmĕṣûqāh: 'distress and anguish' — the paired terms for extreme pressure. Tikpĕhû (overpower him): from kph (to subdue, overwhelm) — like a king advancing to war. The wicked man's anticipated judgment already assails him psychologically.
15.25: Nāṭāh yāḏô ʾel-ʾēl: 'he stretched out his hand against God' — the gesture of aggressive defiance. Yitgabbār (he defies/acts powerfully against): Hithpael of gbr, reflexive intensification — he makes himself strong against the Almighty.
15.26: Yārûṣ ʾēlāyw bĕṣawwāʾr: 'charging neck-forward' — a warrior's attack posture. Gabbê māginnîm (thick boss of shields): the convex central boss, the strongest defensive point. The wicked man attacks God's strongest point — an image of suicidal futility.
15.27: Kissāh pānāyw bĕḥelĕḇô: 'his face covered with fat' — wealth-fattened, obese prosperity. Pimaḥ-ʿal-kāsel (padding on his loins): the physical description of the prosperous wicked man. Eliphaz identifies his portrait with the very condition Job once had.
15.28: Yiškôn ʿārîm nĕḥārābôt: 'he inhabits ruined cities' — an ironic reversal. The man who built settlements will himself end up in desolate ruins. Bāttîm lōʾ-yēšĕḇû (houses no one inhabits): the ḥērem-formula of total desolation applied to his dwelling.
15.29: Lōʾ-yeʿĕšar (not remain wealthy): the wealth is temporary. Miqlāh (holdings/property): hapax; the sense of productive land. The three negatives in rapid succession (lōʾ × 3) drive home the total reversal.
15.30: Lōʾ-yāsûr miḥōšeḵ: inescapable darkness. Rûaḥ pîw (breath of his [God's] mouth): divine speech as destructive wind — the same instrument that creates (Gen 2:7) also destroys the wicked man's flourishing.
15.31: ʾal-yaʾmēn baššāw: 'Let him not trust in emptiness' — šāwʾ (vanity, emptiness, falsehood) is the term Ecclesiastes uses for the futility of earthly gain. The warning is that the wicked man's self-trust is misplaced in something that will prove worthless.
15.32: Lōʾ bĕyômô: 'before its time' — premature completion/fulfillment of doom. Kappātô lōʾ-tarʿănennāh: 'his branch will not stay green' — the kappāh (frond/branch) as the symbol of flourishing life cut off before maturity.
15.33: Yĕḥabbēl kaggefen: 'he sheds like the vine' — ḥbl (Piel: to act corruptly, or here: to shake off) — the vine drops its unripe grapes. Kazzayit (like the olive) drops its blossoms prematurely — the destruction of potential, not just actuality.
15.34: ʿĂḏat ḥānēp: 'assembly of the godless' — ḥānēp (godless, profane, polluted) is a strong term for religious apostasy. ʾēš (fire) as divine judgment on ʾohŏlê šōḥaḏ (tents of bribery/corruption): the institutional wickedness brought to ruin.
15.35: Hārāh ʿāmāl: 'they conceive trouble' — the pregnancy-metaphor for wickedness: ʿāmāl (toil, trouble, oppression) conceived and birthed as ʾāwen (iniquity, wickedness). Biṭnām tākîn mirmāh: 'their inmost being produces deceit' — the inner life as the womb of evil.

16.1: Job's counter-speech opens without the conventional wisdom-claims Eliphaz deployed. The stark speech-introduction marks the tonal shift: Job does not argue epistemically but existentially.
16.2: Mĕnaḥămê ʿāmāl: 'miserable comforters' — ʿāmāl (trouble/toil) qualifies the noun: the comforters produce the very trouble they claim to address. A devastating reversal of the comforter role. Kullĕkem (all of you): Job indicts the entire group.
16.3: Dibrê rûaḥ: 'words of wind/spirit' — empty talk (cf. 15:2). Mah-yammîṣĕkā: 'what emboldens you?' — from ʾmṣ (to be strong, courageous): what gives you the courage to keep answering? Job questions the source of their confidence.
16.4: Gam-ʾānōkî kĕṣbkem ʾădabbērāh: 'I too could speak as you do' — Job's hypothetical: if the roles were reversed, he could pile up millîm (words) against them. ʾānîʿāh ʿălêkem bĕrōʾšî: 'shake my head at you' — the gesture of contempt (cf. Ps 22:8; Lam 2:15).
16.5: ʾĂḥazbēq bĕpî: 'I would strengthen you with my mouth' — the conditional opposite of what the friends have done. Job claims he would have offered genuine nîḥûm (comfort) rather than accusation, had the situation been reversed. The contrast exposes the friends' failure of pastoral care.
16.6: ʾim-ʾădabbĕrāh lōʾ-yēḥāśēk kĕʾēbî: 'if I speak, the pain is not held back' — kĕʾēb (pain, anguish) persists regardless of whether Job speaks or stays silent. The dilemma of the sufferer: neither speech nor silence provides relief. ʾeḥĕšāh (I am silent) — used of God's silence in Ps 50:3, here of the sufferer's exhaustion.
16.7: ʿAttāh hĕlaʾānî: 'But now he has utterly exhausted me' — the shift to second person addressing God directly. Lʾh (to be weary, exhausted) in Hiphil: God has worn Job out. Hăšimmôtāh (made desolate): the congregation/circle of Job's community is now made empty.
16.8: Wattiqmĕṭēnî: 'you have wasted/shriveled me' — from qmṭ (to wrinkle, shrivel — hapax in this sense). Kāḥaš (emaciation, failure) is the unusual term for what Job's physical condition has become: his shrunken flesh is itself a witness (ʿēḏ) against him in the court of public perception.
16.9: ʾappô ṭārap: 'his wrath has torn me' — the verb ṭārap (to tear prey) is the predator's act. Yiḥărōq-ʿālay bĕšinnāyw: 'gnashes his teeth at me' — divine hostility figured as predatory animal. Ṣārî (my enemy/adversary): Job attributes the attacks to God-as-enemy.
16.10: Pāṣû ʿālay bĕpêhem: 'they open wide their mouths at me' — the communal mockery gesture (cf. Ps 22:14; Lam 2:16). Yakkûnî bĕleḥî: 'they strike my cheek' — judicial humiliation, a physical act of contempt. Yitḥabbrû ʿālay: they gather as a band against me.
16.11: Yasgîrēnî ʾēl: 'God has surrendered me' — the term sāgar (to shut up, deliver over) is the treaty/covenant term for handing someone over to an enemy (cf. Obad 14; Ps 31:9). Job charges God with delivering him over to ʾăwîl (the evildoer) and rĕšāʿîm (the wicked).
16.12: Šālēw hāyîtî: 'I was living in ease' — from šlw (to be at ease, prosperous) — the condition before the disruption. Yĕparpĕrēnî (shattered me): intensive Pilpel form for repeated shattering. The sequence of violent verbs (grabbed, broke, set up as target) creates a courtroom of divine violence.
16.13: Rabbāyw (his archers): God's multiple marksmen. Yipĕlaḥ kilĕyôtāy: 'he slashes into my kidneys' — kilāyāh (kidneys) as the seat of emotional life, the innermost self. Yišpôk lāʾārṣ mĕrērātî: 'spills my gall on the ground' — mĕrērāh (gall/bile) = bitterness, the substance of suffering poured out.
16.14: Yipĕrĕṣēnî pereṣ ʿal-pĕnê-pāreṣ: 'breach after breach' — the cognate construction pereṣ/pāraṣ (breach/to breach) intensifies the destruction. Gibbôr (mighty warrior): God depicted as a great warrior charging the breach he himself opened. The warrior-God motif is used here paradoxically against the covenant people's representative.
16.15: Śaq tāpartî ʿălê giltî: 'I have sewn sackcloth over my skin' — sackcloth as mourning garment, here literally sewn onto the body. ʿôlaltî lĕʿāpār qarnî: 'I have pressed my dignity down into the dust' — qeren (horn) = dignity, honor, power; pressed into the dust = total humiliation.
16.16: Pānay ḥāmarmĕrāh mibbekî: 'my face is flushed from weeping' — ḥāmar (to boil, foam red) describes the inflamed skin. ʿal-ʿapʿappay ṣalmāwet: 'on my eyelids is the shadow of death' — ṣalmāwet (deep shadow/shadow of death): either a compound of ṣēl + māwet or an intensive form for 'deep darkness'; the death-shadow rests on his very eyelids.
16.17: ʿal lōʾ-ḥāmās bĕkappāyw: 'though no violence has been done by his hands' — ḥāmās (violence, injustice) is the comprehensive term for covenant-breaking violence (Gen 6:11; Hab 1:2). Job asserts his innocence using the highest-stakes vocabulary. Tĕhillātî (my prayer) is pure — liturgical and moral integrity maintained.
16.18: ʾErṣ ʾal-tĕkassî dāmî: 'O earth, do not cover my blood' — the appeal to the earth to preserve the blood-witness, drawing on the Abel-precedent (Gen 4:10: qôl dĕmê ʾāḥîkā ṣōʿăqîm). Zĕʿāqātî (my cry) is the ṣĕʿāqāh — the oppressed person's cry that reaches God. Job demands his cry remain audible, refusing burial.
16.19: ʿĒḏî baššāmayim: 'my witness is in heaven' — the ʿēḏ (witness/testifier) is a heavenly figure parallel to śāhĕḏî (my advocate). The latter term śāhēḏ is an Aramaic loanword (cf. Aram. sāheḏ) appearing only here; it means 'one who testifies in defense.' These two parallel terms (witness + advocate) describe a heavenly being who holds Job's case. This is the same figure anticipated in 9:33 (mediator) and named in 19:25 (Redeemer).
16.20: Mĕlîṣay rēʿāy: 'my friends are my scorners' — lîṣ (to mock, scorn) — the friends who should comfort now mock. ʾEl-ʾĕlôah: yet Job's eye (ʿênî) pours out tears to God. The contrast between human rejection and divine address is the structural center of Job's faith.
16.21: Wĕyôkaḥ lĕgeber ʿim-ʾĕlôah: 'if only a mortal man could argue/plead with God' — yākaḥ (to reason together, argue, adjudicate): the legal-wisdom term for formal disputation. Geber (mortal man) vs. ʾĕlôah (God): the ontological gap that makes the lawsuit impossible without a mediator. Bēn ʾîš lĕrēʿēhû: 'as between a man and his neighbor' — Job longs for human-level accessibility to divine adjudication.
16.22: Šĕnôt mispar: 'the few numbered years' — the appointed span, small. ʾOrāḥ lōʾ-ʾāšûb: 'a road from which there is no return' — the irreversibility of death that makes Job's urgency understandable. The lawsuit must be settled before the one-way road is taken.

17.1: Rûḥî ḥubbĕlāh: 'my spirit is ruined/spent' — ḥābal (to bind, destroy, corrupt) in Pual: the spirit passively destroyed by forces beyond Job's control. Yāmay niʿăkû: 'my days are put out' — kāḥāh (to be extinguished, dimmed — of a lamp): life as a lamp going dark. Qĕbārôt lî: 'the grave is ready for me' — the plural of qeḇer as an intensive or honorific plural for the burial place.
17.2: ʾim-lōʾ hătûlîm ʿimmādî: 'surely there are mockers all around me' — hātûl (mocker, scoffer) from htl (to mock, deride) — a rare form. Bĕtamdĕrôtām (their provocation/their contentions): another rare term; the continuous hostile pressure of opponents.
17.3: Śîmāh-nāʾ ʿorbēnî ʿimmāk: 'Give me a pledge yourself' — the legal term ʿēreḇ (surety, guarantor): someone who stands in as financial or legal guarantee for another. Job asks God himself to be the ʿārēḇ (guarantor) for him — to post the bond that would allow Job's case to proceed. Mî hûʾ lĕyāḏî yitqāʿ: 'who else will strike hands with me?' — the handshake-pledge gesture of legal guarantee. No human will take the risk; only God can stand surety for Job before God.
17.4: Libbām ṣāpartā mibbiynāh: 'you have closed off their hearts from understanding' — the irony: God has prevented the very friends who claim wisdom from understanding Job's situation. Lōʾ tĕrōmmēm: 'you will not exalt them' — they have presumed to speak for God but God will not honor their presumption.
17.5: Lĕḥēleq yaggîḏ rēʿîm: 'he who informs against friends for personal gain' — ḥēleq (portion/share): the one who betrays associates for material advantage. ʿênê bānāyw tikhlenāh: 'his children's eyes will fail' — the punishment falls on the next generation; the betrayer's progeny will experience the blindness of deprivation.
17.6: Hissîgānî lĕmāšāl ʿammîm: 'he has turned me into a proverb among the nations' — māšāl (proverb, byword, taunt-saying): Job has become a negative example, a warning story. Topet-pānîm ʾehyeh: 'I am one before whose face men spit' — the extreme contempt gesture, used in laws of public shaming (Deut 25:9; Num 12:14).
17.7: Wattikhah mikkāʿas ʿênî: 'my eye has grown dim from sorrow' — kāʿas (vexation, grief) has physiologically dimmed his sight. ʿĂṣûmay kĕṣēl kullām: 'all my limbs are like shadows' — the wasting-away of the body; ṣēl (shadow) suggests insubstantiality, almost translucence.
17.8: Yāšōmmû yĕšārîm ʿal-zōʾt: 'upright men are appalled at this' — šmm (to be appalled, desolate) — the same root used for the desolation of Jerusalem. The yāšār (upright, straight) person is horrified not by Job's sin but by the sight of righteous suffering. Nāqî ʿal-ḥānēp yitʿōrār: 'the innocent rises up against the godless' — the moral reflex of the innocent community.
17.9: Wayyōʾḥez ṣaddîq darkô: 'the righteous holds to his way' — ṣaddîq (righteous/just) persisting in his path. Ûṭĕhar-yādayim: 'the one with clean hands' — the purity-term from Ps 24:4: clean-handed integrity as the qualification for the divine presence. Yaʾăṣōr kōaḥ: 'grows stronger' — the paradox of suffering that increases rather than depletes moral strength.
17.10: Wĕʾûlām kullām tāšubû ûbōʾû: 'but come, all of you, return and come' — Job's challenge to debate again. Lōʾ-ʾemṣāʾ bākem ḥākām: 'I cannot find a wise man among you' — the devastating reversal: the friends who came to impart wisdom are themselves found lacking it.
17.11: Yāmay ʿābārû: 'my days are over' — the perfect of completion: they are already done. Zimmōtay nittĕqû: 'my plans are torn apart' — zimmāh (plan, purpose, purpose) ripped away. Môrāšê lĕḇābî: 'the longings of my heart' — the deep possessions of the inner life, now lost.
17.12: Laylāh lĕyôm yāśîmû: 'they make night into day' — the perverse wisdom of those who reverse categories. Qārôb ʾôr mippĕnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'light is near, even in the darkness' — the friends' false optimism: offering easy resolution while darkness surrounds. A critique of cheap comfort.
17.13: ʾIm-ʾăqawweh šeʾôl bêtî: 'if I hope, Sheol is my home' — Sheol as the only dwelling Job can look forward to. Baḥōšeḵ rāpaʿdtî yĕṣûʿî: 'in the darkness I have spread my bed' — the language of habitation: Job is already at home in the realm of death. The qawwāh (hoping/waiting) that normally implies divine expectation is here redirected toward Sheol.
17.14: Laššaḥat qārāʾtî ʾābî ʾāttāh: 'I call corruption my father' — šaḥat (pit, grave, corruption) — the normal object of divine rescue (cf. Ps 16:10; Jon 2:7) is here addressed as family. Rimmāh ʾimmî waʾăḥōtî: 'the worm is my mother and my sister' — the darkest inversion of family language, establishing the intimacy of death.
17.15: ʾAyyēh ʾēpôh tiqwātî: 'Where then is my hope?' — tiqwāh (hope, cord — from qwh to wait/hope) is the key anthropological term for orientation toward the future. The double interrogative (where + where) drives home Job's complete loss of future-orientation. Wĕtiqwātî mî yĕšûrennāh: 'who can see my hope?' — no one can perceive it because it no longer exists visibly.
17.16: Baḏdê šeʾôl tērednāh: 'they will descend to the bars of Sheol' — baḏ (bar, plank — the structural bars of Sheol as a locked underworld). Hope and the one who held it descend together. Yaḥad ʿal-ʿāpār nāḥat: 'together we go down into the dust' — hope dies with the person. Or perhaps: hope is what will die first, and Job will follow. The ambiguity intensifies the desolation.

18.1: Bildad's second speech opens with the standard formula. His speech is more compact than his first (ch8) and focuses entirely on the fate of the wicked, implicitly applying the portrait to Job.
18.2: ʿad-ʾānāh tāśîmûn qinṣê lĕmillîn: 'How long will you set snares/ends to words?' — qēṣ (end, snare) of words: either 'put an end to words' or 'set traps with words.' Tābîn wĕʾaḥar nĕdabbēr: 'get insight, and then we will speak' — Bildad demands that Job achieve understanding before speaking further.
18.3: Maddûaʿ neḥšabnû kabbĕhēmāh: 'Why do you count us as animals?' — bĕhēmāh (beast, animal) — the friends feel demoted by Job's critique of their wisdom. Niṭmînu bĕʿênêkem: 'we are stupid in your eyes' — ṭāmāʾ in Niphal (to be made unclean, or here, worthless) — an unusual use suggesting their perceived contemptible status.
18.4: Ṭōrēp napšô bĕʾappô: 'you who tear yourself apart in your anger' — the self-destructive character of Job's rage in Bildad's view. Hătaʿăzab-lĕmaʿankā ʾāreṣ: 'will the earth be forsaken for your sake?' — a cosmological challenge: does Job think his individual case will cause the moral order to collapse?
18.5: Gam-ʾôr rĕšāʿîm yiḏʿāk: 'the light of the wicked is extinguished' — the lamp-metaphor for life. Rĕšāʿîm (wicked ones): Bildad's term for the condemned; he is building a portrait that fits Job's situation. Šabebet ʾiššô: 'the flame of his fire' — the doubled light-image emphasizes the totality of extinction.
18.6: Nēr ʿālāyw yiḏʿāk: 'the lamp over him is extinguished' — nēr (lamp) as the symbol of life and divine blessing (cf. Prov 20:27: nēr YHWH nišmat ʾāḏām). When the lamp goes dark, the divine connection is severed. Dāk (extinguished) completes the double extinction begun in v5.
18.7: Yēṣrû ṣaʿădê ʾônô: 'his powerful strides are shortened' — ʾôn (strength, vigor) — the once-powerful man is diminished. Taššīlĕḵēhû ʿăṣātô: 'his own schemes throw him down' — self-brought ruin: the wicked man is not merely punished externally but destroyed by the internal logic of his own plans.
18.8: Bĕšālah bĕreget ʾĕlāyw: 'he is thrown into a net by his own feet' — rešet (net), śĕbākāh (lattice/mesh): the hunting-trap imagery. The wicked man walks into his own trap. The passive (yušlaḵ) suggests the inevitability of the outcome.
18.9: Yōʾḥez bĕʿāqēb pāḥ: 'a trap catches him by the heel' — pāḥ (bird-snare, trap) and ṣammîm (snare, noose) continue the hunting imagery. The heel-trap recalls Jacob/Esau's heel-motif (ʿāqab), but here there is no escape from the snare.
18.10: Ṭāmûn bāʾāreṣ ḥeblô: 'a rope is buried in the ground for him' — the hidden snare, pre-placed. Mallĕkōdtô ʿălê nātîb: 'his trap waits on his path' — the path the wicked man takes is itself the trap. The series of trap-images (vv8-10) accumulates to produce inevitability of capture.
18.11: Ballāhôt yĕbaʿătuhû sābîb: 'terrors frighten him on every side' — ballāhāh (sudden terror, dismay): the psychological dimension of the wicked man's fate. Fear surrounds completely. Hēpîṣuhû lĕraglāyw: 'they push him forward at every step' — terror drives him as a herd animal is driven.
18.12: Yĕhî rāʿēb ʾônô: 'his strength is starved' — an unusual idiom: rāʿēb (hungry, famished) modifying ʾôn (strength) — the vigor itself is starving, depleted. ʾĔd nākôn lĕṣalʿô: 'disaster stands ready at his side' — ʾēd (calamity, disaster) as a waiting companion.
18.13: Yōʾkal baḏdê ʿôrô: 'it devours the limbs of his skin' — baḏ (parts, limbs): eaten piece by piece. Bĕkôr māwet: 'firstborn of death' — a unique personification: death has a firstborn, a powerful first agent. The bĕkôr (firstborn) holds the place of greatest power and authority. Death's most powerful instrument is the disease that consumes the wicked man from within.
18.14: Yinnātaq mibĕṭaḥô: 'his confidence is ripped away from his home' — bāṭāḥ (confidence, security) torn loose. Melek ballāhôt: 'king of terrors' — ballāhāh (terror, horror) personified as royalty. Death enthroned as king, to whom the wicked man is brought as a prisoner. The title melek (king) applied to death anticipates NT language about the last enemy's kingship (1 Cor 15:26; Rev 20:14).
18.15: Tiškôn bĕʾohŏlô mibbelî lô: 'something alien inhabits his tent' — mibbelî lô (not his own): a stranger or alien force takes over the home. Gāprît tizzāreh ʿal-nāwēhû: 'brimstone is scattered over his home' — the Sodom-judgment vocabulary (Gen 19:24), the smell of divine wrath.
18.16: Miṭṭāḥat šārāšāyw yiḇāšû: 'his roots dry up below' — the tree metaphor for the righteous/wicked person (cf. Ps 1:3; Jer 17:8) inverted: here the wicked man's roots and branches both die. Ûmimmaʿal yimmāl qĕṣîrô: 'his branches wither above' — total death from roots to crown.
18.17: Ziḵrô ʾāḇad min-ʾāreṣ: 'his memory perishes from the land' — zēḵer (memory, name) as the posthumous existence: to be remembered is to continue existing in the community. The wicked man loses both. Wĕlōʾ-šēm lô ʿal-pĕnê-ḥûṣ: 'no name in the open places' — public space, where names are proclaimed (gates, marketplaces), holds no record of him.
18.18: Yihdĕpūhû mēʾôr el-ḥōšeḵ: 'he is driven from light into darkness' — the active expulsion: no passive fading but violent thrust from the realm of the living into the realm of the dead. Ûmitēbēl yĕniddĕhû: 'he is banished from the world' — tēbēl (the inhabited world, the civilized order) expels him. Social death precedes physical death.
18.19: Lōʾ nîn lô wĕlōʾ-neḵed: 'no offspring and no descendant' — nîn (offspring, son) and neḵeḏ (posterity): the total extinction of the family line. The double negative drives home the complete annihilation of legacy. No survivor (śārîd) anywhere.
18.20: ʿal-yômô nāšammû ʾaḥărōnîm: 'those who come after are horrified' — the wicked man becomes a horror for all future generations. Qadmōnîm ʾāḥăzû śāʿar: 'those who went before are seized with dread' — the temporal frame extends in both directions: both predecessors and successors are appalled. The portrait is universally witnessed.
18.21: ʾAk-ʾēlleh miškĕnôt ʿawwāl: 'surely these are the dwellings of the wicked' — the summary application. Wĕzeh mĕqôm lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾēl: 'and this is the place of the one who does not know God' — the definitional formula: the wicked man is lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾēl, one who does not know God. Yāḏaʿ (to know) in the covenantal sense: not mere intellectual awareness but covenantal relationship. The speech ends by locating the problem not in behavior but in relational status before God.

12.1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ — the standard narrative formula (vayya'an + name) that opens each of Job's replies; the book's speeches alternate between three cycles of the friends and Job. Job's reply here comes after Zophar's first speech (ch. 11), where Zophar claimed to know the depths of God and accused Job of hidden sin.
12.2: ʾattēm ʿām — 'you are the people' — a cutting irony: ʿām normally means 'a people' or 'the people' as a collective, but used singularly of these three men it implies they fancy themselves the entire repository of wisdom. wĕʿimmaḵem tāmûṯ ḥāḵmāh — 'and wisdom will die with you' — sarcasm; if wisdom dies when they die, it is as fragile and contingent as they are. The verse targets the friends' assumed epistemic privilege.
12.3: lēḇāḇ — 'mind/heart' in the full biblical sense of the seat of understanding and will, not just emotion. nōpēl ʾānōḵî mimmkkem — 'I do not fall below you' — the verb nāpal (to fall) used figuratively for status inferiority; Job denies any intellectual subordination. The rhetorical question 'who does not know such things?' dismantles the friends' claim to special insight: the doctrine they proclaim is the most common possible orthodoxy, not hidden wisdom.
12.4: śĕḥôq lĕrēʿēhû — 'a laughingstock to his neighbor' — śĕḥôq is laughter in the mocking sense (cf. Gen 21:9, where Ishmael 'mocks'). The following phrase is the scandal: the one who becomes a laughingstock is qārāʾ ʾel-ʾĕlôhîm wayyaʿănēhû — 'who called to God and he answered him.' Answered prayer is supposed to mark the righteous; Job's answered-prayer history makes his current mockery a theological scandal. ṣaddîq tāmîm — 'just and blameless' — the precise epithets from 1:1 and 1:8 (God's own verdict); Job now deploys them of himself, underscoring the contradiction.
12.5: lappîḏ — torch/lamp; in the thought of the prosperous man, the lamp of the afflicted (one who is stumbling, nākôn lĕmiʿad rāgel = 'ready to slip with the foot') is an object of contempt rather than compassion. The verse indicts the comfort-bias of prosperity theology: the comfortable naturally interpret the stumbling of others as moral failure rather than undeserved calamity.
12.6: šālēwû — 'undisturbed/at ease' (from šālāh) — the friends' retributive framework is undermined by the observable fact that the tents of violent men are šālēwû while the righteous Job suffers. ʾăšer hēḇîʾ ʾĕlôhîm bĕyādô — 'into whose hand God has brought [it]' — an ironic acknowledgment that God himself facilitates the security of those who provoke him, which is precisely what the friends' theology cannot accommodate.
12.7: Job shifts to a wisdom-hymn structure using the 'ask the creation' motif — šĕʾal-nāʾ bĕhēmôṯ (ask the animals), ʿôp̄ haššāmayim (birds of the air). The appeal to non-human creation as teacher is a striking rhetorical move: the very creatures who cannot speak or reason are more reliable instructors than the friends. The four creation-witnesses (animals, birds, earth, fish) form an ascending cosmic series.
12.8: śîaḥ lāʾāreṣ — 'speak to the earth' — śîaḥ normally means to reflect or meditate (the word for meditation in Ps 1:2 is also from this root); here it is the activity of conversing with the earth itself. ḏāgê hayyām yĕsappĕr-lāḵ — 'the fish of the sea will declare it to you' — the fish use sāpar (the verb for counting and narrating); the same verb governs Ps 19:1-2 (the heavens sāpar the glory of God). Creation does theology that the friends have missed.
12.9: mî lō-yāḏaʿ bĕḵol-ʾēlleh — 'who does not know in all these?' — rhetorical question capping the creation-witness section. The grammatically significant feature is what follows: kî yaḏ-YHWH ʿāśṯāh zōʾṯ — 'that the hand of YHWH has done this.' This is the only occurrence of the divine name YHWH in the poetic core of Job (chs. 3–42:6); throughout the poetry the terms are ʾĒl, Ĕlôah, Šaddai. Most scholars regard v9 as preserving a traditional idiom or cited proverb, since the narrator and the divine speeches freely use YHWH but the poetry avoids it.
12.10: ʾăšer bĕyāḏô nep̄eš kol-ḥay — 'in whose hand is the life (nepeš) of every living creature' — nepeš here is not 'soul' in the platonic sense but the animating life-force; the full phrase 'the breath of all human flesh' (rûaḥ kol-bĕśar-ʾîš) pairs the immaterial animating breath with its bodily vehicle. Divine sovereignty over all life is the theological premise underlying the creation-teacher argument of vv7-9.
12.11: hăʾōzen millîn tiḇḥān — 'does not the ear test words?' — the sense-metaphor of tasting: the root of tiḇḥān is bḥn (to test/assay); the parallel ḥēk is the palate/roof of the mouth that discerns taste. The verse rebukes the friends by analogy: as the palate can taste good food from bad, a discerning mind should be able to hear the difference between genuine wisdom and false comfort. The ear-as-taster metaphor recurs in 34:3.
12.12: bĕyĕšîšîm ḥāḵmāh — 'with the aged is wisdom' — a conventional wisdom maxim the friends would affirm. ûbōrek yāmîm tĕḇûnāh — 'and with length of days, understanding.' Job states the truism ironically, as a setup for v13: these conventional attributes are not the possession of aged humans but of God himself. The verse performs a rhetorical reversal: yes, wisdom belongs to the ancient — the most ancient of all.
12.13: ʿimmô ḥāḵmāh ûgĕḇûrāh — 'with him are wisdom and strength' — the verse transfers the attributes of v12 (aged human wisdom) to God exclusively; the poetic shift is marked by the pronoun ʿimmô ('with him' = God) displacing the human subject. ʿēṣāh ûṯĕḇûnāh — 'counsel and understanding' — this exact tetrad (ḥāḵmāh, gĕḇûrāh, ʿēṣāh, tĕḇûnāh) reappears in Isa 11:2 as attributes of the messianic king on whom the Spirit rests; Job recites God's essential nature.
12.14: yihrōs — 'he tears down' (from hāras, to demolish); wĕlō yibbāneh — 'and it cannot be built again' — the absolute finality of divine demolition contrasted with human rebuilding. yisgōr ʿal-ʾîš — 'he shuts up against a man' — the preposition ʿal gives the sense of imprisoning; the parallel is God locking a man in and there being no release (wĕlō yippāṯēaḥ). The verse begins a hymn of divine sovereignty that continues through v25.
12.15: yiṣrōr — 'he holds back' (from ṣārar, to bind/restrict); applied to waters, the holding-back/releasing pattern demonstrates God's absolute control of natural forces. wĕyišlaḥēm wĕyahpĕḵû ʾāreṣ — 'he releases them and they overturn the earth' — the two antithetical divine acts (withhold/release) frame the absolute range of divine authority over hydrology.
12.16: ʿōz ûṯûšîyyāh lô — 'strength and effective wisdom are his' — tûšîyyāh is a rare and distinctively Joban term (also in 6:13; 11:6; 26:3; and Proverbs 2:7; 3:21; 8:14), often rendered 'sound wisdom' or 'effective power'; it has the sense of resourcefulness that achieves results. The phrase haššōgēg ûhamašgeh — 'the one who errs and the one who causes to err' (i.e., the deceived and the deceiver) — is striking: both belong to God, which means divine sovereignty comprehends even error and deception, not merely righteousness.
12.17: môlîḵ yôʿăṣîm šōlāl — 'he leads counselors away stripped' — šōlāl means naked/stripped of possessions, used of prisoners of war being led away stripped; applied to 'counselors' (yôʿăṣîm, plural of yāʿaṣ), it is deeply ironic. wĕšōp̄ĕṭîm yĕhôlēl — 'and turns judges into fools' — hōlēl (from hll used in the pi'el for 'to make foolish') applied to those who administer justice. The reversal-of-dignity hymn begins here and runs through v25.
12.18: mûsar mĕlāḵîm pittēaḥ — 'he loosens the belt/authority of kings' — mûsar here is not 'discipline' (its common sense) but the bound garment or sash/authority-girdle worn by kings; loosening it symbolizes stripping of royal authority. wayyeʾsōr ʾēzôr bĕmoṯnêhem — 'and girds a servant's cloth around their loins' — the reversal is complete: the royal girdle removed, a servant's cloth applied. The royal humiliation imagery will be echoed in Isa 20 and the suffering-servant passages.
12.19: hakkōhănîm — priests — Job includes priests in the list of those God strips, alongside counselors, judges, kings (v18), elders (v20), and princes (v21). The comprehensiveness of the list (military, legal, cultic, political leadership) suggests that no human institution is insulated from divine reversal. wĕʾêṯānîm yĕsallēp̄ — 'and overturns those who were established/mighty' — ʾêṯānîm is 'enduring streams' / 'perennial' (cf. Amos 5:24), applied to those who seemed permanently established.
12.20: sēp̄er sāp̄ at neʾĕmānîm — 'he removes the speech of the trusted' — sāpar in the sense of removing/taking away (cf. Gen 8:13, the surface of the earth 'was dry'); neʾĕmānîm are those who are trustworthy/reliable. ûṭaʿam zĕqēnîm yiqqāḥ — 'and takes away the discernment of the aged' — ṭaʿam is taste/discernment (the same root as v11's palate metaphor), applied here to the deliberative wisdom of elders. The verse directly undermines the friends' appeal to age and reliability as credentials.
12.21: šôpēḵ bûz ʿal-nĕḏîḇîm — 'he pours contempt on nobles' — the verb šāpak ('to pour out') is used of pouring water or blood; 'contempt/dishonor' poured like water onto those of high status. wĕḥezqaṯ ʾappîqîm rippāh — 'and loosens the strength of the mighty' — ʾappîqîm are 'channels' or 'strong places' (the root means to be strong, to restrain); loosening their strength is the undoing of every earthly power structure.
12.22: mĕgalleh ʿămûqôṯ miṇ-ḥōšeḵ — 'he brings to light deep things from darkness' — ʿămûqôṯ (deep things, from ʿāmōq) also appears in Dan 2:22 (God 'reveals deep and hidden things') and 1 Cor 2:10 (the Spirit searches 'the depths of God'). ṣalmāwet — 'shadow of death' — one of Job's signature terms (occurring 10 times in Job out of 18 total OT uses); a compound of ṣēl (shadow) + māwet (death), a word for extreme darkness that Ps 23:4 deploys for the valley of mortal danger. God brings even what is hidden in death-shadow into the open.
12.23: maśgîʾ laggôyim wayyaʾbĕḏēm — 'he makes nations great, then destroys them' — the juxtaposition of national enlargement and destruction within a single verse shows the rapidity and totality of divine sovereignty over history. šōṭēaḥ laggôyim wayyanḥēm — 'he expands them and then leads them away' — nāḥāh can mean to guide/lead (like a shepherd), ironic here since the leading results in exile or dispersal. The verse anticipates the Deuteronomic theology of nations rising and falling by divine decree.
12.24: mēsîr lēḇ rāʾšê ʿam-hāʾāreṣ — 'he strips the heart from the leaders of the peoples of the earth' — lēḇ (heart = understanding/mind) removed from the ruling class; without lēḇ they cannot govern. wayyatʿēm bĕtōhû lō-ḏāreḵ — 'and makes them wander in tōhû without a path' — tōhû is the primordial formlessness of Gen 1:2 ('without form and void'); the cosmos-undoing word applied to the condition of leaderless nations stumbling through pathless wilderness. The cosmic resonance is deliberate.
12.25: yĕmašaššû-ḥōšeḵ wĕlō-ʾôr — 'they grope in darkness without light' — māšaš is tactile groping, the same verb used of the blind Philistines in Judg 16:26 and of the Egyptians in the darkness plague (Exod 10:21). wayyatʿēm kĕšikkôr — 'and he makes them stagger like a drunkard' — the drunkenness motif for divine disorientation recurs in Isa 29:9; 63:6; Jer 25:27. The chapter ends with the powerful image of once-glorious national leaders reduced to blind, lurching figures in the dark.

13.1: hēn-kōl rāʾăṯāh ʿênî — 'lo, all of this my eye has seen' — the direct sensory claim; Job opens his reply asserting first-person ocular witness. šāmĕʿāh ʾāznî wattāḇen lāh — 'my ear has heard and understood it' — the use of both eye and ear establishes comprehensive apprehension; Job has perceived both the observable facts and their meaning. He is not speaking from ignorance but from full-sensory witness that matches or exceeds the friends'.
13.2: kĕdaʿtḵem yāḏaʿtî gam-ʾānî — 'what you know, I know too' — the emphatic gam-ʾānî ('even I') and the repetition of the root yāḏaʿ insist on equal epistemic standing. lō-nāp̄ēl ʾānōḵî mimmkem — 'I do not fall below you' — the same negated-nāpal construction as 12:3; the repetition of the claim across these two chapters suggests it is a rhetorical refrain challenging the friends' implicit intellectual superiority claim.
13.3: ʾûlām ʾănî ʾel-Šaddai ʾădabbēr — 'surely I want to speak to El Shaddai' — ûlām is an adversative particle marking a sharp contrast with what precedes: 'yes, I know what you know — but what I actually want is to argue before God directly.' wĕhôḵēaḥ ʾel-ʾĒl ʾeḥpōṣ — 'and to argue my case before El' — hôḵēaḥ is from yāḵaḥ (to rebuke, argue, adjudicate); Job is invoking the legal connotation explicitly. He wants not religious submission but forensic vindication.
13.4: tōp̄ĕlê šāqer — 'plasterers of falsehood' — tāp̄al is to plaster or coat over, used in Ezek 13:10-15 of prophets 'plastering' a flimsy wall with whitewash; the friends are applying a deceptive surface coating of doctrine to Job's situation without engaging its actual substance. rōp̄ĕʾê ʾĕlîl — 'physicians of worthlessness' — ʾĕlîl means 'nothingness/worthlessness' (the same word used for false gods in Lev 19:4; Isa 2:8); the friends' remedies are made of the same substance as idols — nothing.
13.5: mî-yittēn haḥărêš taḥărîšûn — 'O that you would indeed keep completely silent' — the infinitive absolute haḥărêš intensifies the verb taḥărîšûn: 'truly keep silent.' wĕtihî lāḵem lĕḥāḵmāh — 'and it would be wisdom for you' — ironic wisdom-maxim: in your case, the wiser path is to stop speaking. Prov 17:28 makes the same point explicitly ('even a fool is counted wise when he holds his tongue'); Job anticipates and inverts the proverbial form by applying it to ostensible sages.
13.6: šimĕʿû-nāʾ tôḵaḥtî — 'hear now my argument/reproof' — tôḵaḥat is the legal-wisdom term for rebuke, argument, or reproof (from yāḵaḥ, same root as v3); it appears in Prov 1:23; 29:15 for disciplinary correction. wĕriḇôṯ śĕp̄āṯay haqšîḇû — 'and give attention to the pleadings of my lips' — rîḇ is the formal legal dispute or lawsuit; Job is asking the friends to hear his actual legal argument rather than precluding it with theological frameworks.
13.7: hălĕʾêl tĕdabbĕrû ʿawlāh — 'will you speak injustice on God's behalf?' — ʿawlāh is ethical injustice/wrong, not merely falsehood; Job is charging the friends with advocacy that is morally corrupt, not just factually mistaken. ûlô tĕdabbĕrû rĕmiyyāh — 'and for him speak deceit' — rĕmiyyāh is slackness/deception (cf. Ps 52:2 'tongue like a sharp razor, working deceit'). The accusation is that the friends are willing to lie for God — a form of false witness.
13.8: hăp̄ānāyw tiśśĕʾûn — 'will you lift his face?' — nāśāʾ pānîm is the idiom for showing partiality/favoritism (lit. 'lifting the face' of the favored party); its prohibition runs through Lev 19:15; Deut 10:17; and the prophets. Job is accusing the friends of judicial bias — accepting God's side in the dispute without examining the evidence — which is precisely what law forbids. lāʾĒl tĕrîḇûn — 'will you contend for El?' — the same rîḇ vocabulary; they have appointed themselves God's lawyers in a case where God is a party.
13.9: hăṭôḇ kî-yĕḥqōr ʾetḵem — 'is it good that he should search you out?' — if God examines the friends as he might examine any disputant, their advocacy will be exposed as partial and corrupt. ʾim-kĕhāṯēl ʾĕnôš tĕhāṯĕlû-bô — 'or will you deceive him as one deceives a mortal?' — hāṯal means to mock/deceive by playing tricks; the question exposes the absurdity of attempting to deceive God the way one might trick a credulous human adversary.
13.10: hôḵēaḥ yôḵîaḥ ʾetḵem — 'he will certainly reprove you' — the doubled infinitive absolute (hôḵēaḥ yôḵîaḥ) emphasizes certainty of divine judgment against the friends; the same yāḵaḥ root that Job used in v3 (wanting to argue his own case before God) is now turned on the friends: the very divine court Job is invoking will find against them. ʾim-bĕsēṯer pānîm tiśśĕʾûn — 'if in secret you show favoritism' — the 'secret' aspect; what the friends do in their private reasoning, God exposes.
13.11: hălōʾ śĕʾēṯô tĕḇahēl ʾetḵem — 'will not his majesty terrify you?' — śĕʾēṯô is 'his rising/elevation' (from nāśāʾ), used as a term for divine majesty or the awesome upwelling of divine presence. wĕp̄aḥdô yippōl ʿălêḵem — 'and his dread fall upon you' — paḥad (dread/terror) falling as a weight on a person is an OT theophanic language pattern; the same idea governs the theophanic visions of Isaiah (Isa 2:10,19; 6:5) and Job's own encounter (23:15-16).
13.12: zikrōnêḵem mišlê ʾēp̄er — 'your wise maxims are proverbs of ash' — mišlê ʾēp̄er is a devastating compound: māšāl (proverb, wisdom saying) reduced to ʾēp̄er (ash/dust), the residue of burning. The friends' celebrated wisdom is literally the ash left after the fire of genuine insight has been extinguished. ligbê-ḥōmer gabbêḵem — 'your defenses/arguments are defenses of clay' — gaḇ (a raised defense or raised back?) modeled in clay (ḥōmer) — perishable, earthy, fragile.
13.13: haḥărîšû mimmennî wăʾăḏabbĕrāh ʾānî — 'be silent, leave me alone, and let me speak' — Job asks for silence from the friends so he can present his case directly to God. wĕyaʿăḇor ʿālay mah — 'and let whatever comes, come upon me' — the fatalistic openness is deliberate; Job is willing to face whatever divine response his direct confrontation brings. The clause functions as an act of existential courage: consequences accepted in advance.
13.14: maddûaʿ ʾeśśāʾ bĕśārî bĕšinnay wĕnap̄šî ʾāśîm bĕkappî — 'why do I take my flesh in my teeth and put my life in my hands?' — two idioms for extreme recklessness and mortal risk. Biting one's own flesh is a sign of desperation; 'putting one's life in one's hands' (nepeš + kap = palm) is a fixed idiom for risking death, used in Judg 12:3 and 1 Sam 19:5; 28:21. Job is asking rhetorically why he persists in a confrontation that could destroy him — yet proceeds anyway.
13.15: hēn yiqtĕlēnî lō ʾăyaḥēl — the most disputed verse in the book. The Masoretic text has a qere/ketib variant: the written text (ketib) has lō (negative particle) = 'though he kills me, I have no hope'; the marginal reading (qere) has lô (to him) = 'though he kills me, yet I will hope in him.' The KJV ('yet will I trust in him') follows the qere. In context the latter reading coheres better: v16 ('this will be my salvation') and the whole trajectory of chs. 13–14 point toward a defiant hope rather than nihilism. The phrase ʾaḵ-ḏĕrāḵay ʾel-pānāyw ʾôḵîaḥ — 'but I will defend my ways before his face' — is unambiguous: Job intends to argue his case regardless of the outcome, which presupposes hope rather than its absence.
13.16: gam-hûʾ lî lîšûʿāh — 'this itself will become my salvation' — gam-hûʾ ('even this' or 'he himself also') marks the paradox: the very confrontation with the God who might kill him (v15) will be the source of his vindication/salvation. kî-lō-lĕp̄ānāyw ḥānēp̄ yāḇôʾ — 'for the godless cannot come before his presence' — ḥānēp̄ is the impious/godless person (used 13x in Job); Job's argument is that his willingness to present himself before God is itself evidence he is not godless, since hypocrites cannot endure divine scrutiny.
13.17: šimĕʿû šāmôaʿ millātî — 'hear carefully my declaration' — šāmôaʿ is the infinitive absolute intensifying šimĕʿû: truly/attentively hear. ûʾaḥwāṯî bĕʾoznêḵem — 'and my declaration into your ears' — ʾaḥwāh is to declare/show (from the root ḥwh, to show); Job is making a formal declaration that requires a formal hearing. The verse functions as a legal preamble to his ordered legal case in v18.
13.18: hinnēh-nāʾ ʿāraḵtî mišpāṭ — 'look, I have ordered my case' — ʿāraḵ mišpāṭ is technical legal vocabulary: to marshal/arrange one's legal brief (ʿāraḵ = to set in order, as in battle array or the Tabernacle showbread; mišpāṭ = judgment/case). Job has prepared his legal case with the same deliberateness as setting up for a legal proceeding. yāḏaʿtî kî-ʾāniʾ ʾeṣdāq — 'I know that I will be justified/vindicated' — the verb ṣādaq in the nip'al means to be proved righteous or vindicated; Job's certainty of ultimate vindication is the basis of his courage.
13.19: mî-hûʾ yārîḇ ʿimmî — 'who is there who will bring a charge against me?' — another legal formula; rîḇ + ʿim = to bring a dispute/charge against. The question is both a challenge (who can oppose me?) and an expression of Job's longing: he wants an adversary to appear so the case can be tried and won. kî-ʿattāh ʾaḥărîš wĕʾegwaʿ — 'for then I would be silent and die' — the conditional of despair: if no one can bring a charge and clear the air, Job would rather die in silence. The verse frames his speech as the only alternative to death by unresolved suffering.
13.20: ʾaḵ šĕtayim ʾal-taʿaś ʿimmî — 'only do not do these two things to me' — Job makes a precise and limited request of God. ʾāz mippāneyḵā lō ʾessātēr — 'and then I will not hide myself from your presence' — the condition for Job's direct engagement with God is the removal of two impositions (named in v21): the divine hand and the divine dread. The hiddenness/presence language echoes 13:24 (why does God hide his face?) — Job's offer to stop hiding is contingent on God removing what makes his presence unbearable.
13.21: kappĕḵā mēʿālay harḥēq — 'your hand — remove it far from me' — the divine hand (kap, palm/hand) as the instrument of affliction; removing it is the first condition. wĕʾêmāṯĕḵā ʾal-tĕḇahălannî — 'and do not let your terror overwhelm me' — ʾêmāh is numinous dread/terror (cf. 9:34; 33:7); bāhal in the piel means to terrify or throw into panic. Job cannot mount a rational legal case if God's terrifying presence overwrites his ability to think and speak coherently.
13.22: ûqrāʾ wĕʾānōḵî ʾeʿĕneh — 'or let me speak first and you answer me' — the two alternatives for structuring the divine-human dialogue. Job is willing either to respond to God's call or to initiate and let God respond. The symmetry is striking: Job as respondent OR Job as initiator, both equally acceptable — the crucial thing is that the encounter happens. This is the most direct assertion of dialogic reciprocity between Job and God in the entire book.
13.23: kammāh lî ʿăwōnôṯ waḥaṭṭāʾôṯ — 'how many are my iniquities and sins?' — ʿāwōn (moral iniquity, guilt) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (missing the mark, sin) are the two most common OT sin vocabulary words; Job's question is not rhetorical denial but a genuine desire to know the specific charges against him. pišʿî wĕḥaṭṭāʾṯî hôḏîʿēnî — 'make my transgression and my sin known to me' — pešaʿ is rebellion/willful transgression (the most morally serious of the three terms); Job uses all three sin-words together, covering the full spectrum, as if to say: whatever I have done, just show me what it is.
13.24: lāmmāh-p̄āneyḵā tastîr — 'why do you hide your face?' — the divine face-hiding (hester pānîm) is a major OT theological motif (Ps 13:1; 27:9; Isa 8:17; 45:15); it denotes divine withdrawal that leaves the sufferer in darkness and abandonment. Job is calling God to account for a hidden-face experience that he has done nothing to merit. wĕtiḥšĕḇēnî lĕʾôyēḇ lāḵ — 'and regard me as your enemy?' — ʾôyēḇ (enemy); Job protests that God's behavior toward him is indistinguishable from how one treats a hostile enemy.
13.25: hăʿaleh nidāp̄ tĕʿārôṣ — 'will you pursue a wind-driven leaf?' — nādap̄ means blown/driven (as in Isa 19:7 of reeds by a river); the image is of a completely powerless, insubstantial object that poses no threat. wĕʾet-qǎš yābēš tirḏōp̄ — 'and chase after dry stubble?' — qāš is the chaff/stubble; rādap̄ is to pursue with hostile intent (the verb for persecution). The incongruity of the Almighty chasing a dried leaf and stubble makes Job's suffering appear cosmically disproportionate to any supposed offense.
13.26: kî-ṯiḵtōḇ ʿālay merōrôṯ — 'for you write bitter things against me' — the image of written charges is a legal motif; God is cast as a plaintiff filing a formal written indictment (mĕrōrôṯ = bitter things/bitter charges). wĕṯôrîšēnî ʿăwōnôṯ nĕʿûrāy — 'and saddle me with the sins of my youth' — the imputed sins of youth (nōʿar = youth) are the charge-content; Job protests that God is charging him with offenses from an earlier life, a form of injustice since the normal moral logic assumes such debts have been settled or left behind.
13.27: wĕtāśem bassad raglāy — 'you put my feet in stocks' — sāḏ is the stock or wooden restraint for prisoners; applied to a righteous man it is a judicial travesty. ûtiśmōr kol-ʾorḥōṯāy — 'and keep watch over all my paths' — šāmar in the sense of surveillance rather than protection (the same verb used for guarding/keeping in Gen 3:24); God as warden rather than shepherd. ʿal-šorĕšê raglay tĕṯḥaqqeh — 'you mark the prints of my feet' — ḥāqaq means to inscribe/engrave; God etching the footprint-marks as permanent evidence, as one documents the movements of a prisoner.
13.28: wĕhûʾ kĕrāqāḇ yibleh — 'and he wastes away like a rotten thing' — rāqāḇ is decay/rottenness (wood-rot or general decomposition); the shift to third-person 'he' from the preceding 'I' signals that v28 either summarizes Job's condition from outside or is a scribal connection. kĕbegeḏ ʾăḵālô ʿāš — 'like a garment eaten by moths' — the moth-eating-garment metaphor for gradual, invisible deterioration; the same image appears in Job 4:19; Isa 50:9; 51:8; and Matt 6:19. The chapter closes on an image of quiet dissolution rather than dramatic collapse.

14.1: ʾāḏām yĕlûḏ-ʾiššāh — 'man born of woman' — the phrase is a formal identification of human creatureliness: to be born is to be limited, mortal, and embedded in the generational chain of finitude. This exact phrase recurs in Job 15:14 and 25:4 (where Bildad uses it to deny human purity before God) and enters the NT in Gal 4:4 ('born of woman, born under the law'). qĕṣar-yāmîm ûśĕḇaʿ-rōgez — 'short of days and full of agitation' — rōgez is turmoil, agitation, trembling (cf. Isa 14:3 where rest from rōgez is promised to the oppressed); the two phrases together give the double diagnosis of human existence: brief and troubled.
14.2: kĕṣîṣ yāṣāʾ wayyimmāl — 'like a flower he comes up and is cut off' — nāmal (to be cut off/wither) is used here as in Ps 90:6 for the brief flourishing of human life; the flower metaphor for human transience appears also in Isa 40:6-8 ('all flesh is like grass... the flower of the field') and 1 Pet 1:24. wĕlō yāʿăḇōr — 'and he does not endure/pass over' — ʿāḇar normally means to cross/pass over; here the sense is 'he does not persist' or 'he does not abide,' reinforcing the shadow-image of v2b.
14.3: ʾap̄-ʿal-zeh p̄āqaḥtā ʿêneyḵā — 'yet on such a one do you open your eyes?' — p̄āqaḥ ʿênayim is to open the eyes with scrutinizing attention; the question is almost incredulous: this frail, ephemeral creature is the object of divine forensic attention? wĕʾōṯî ṯāḇîʾ bĕmišpāṭ ʿimmāḵ — 'and bring me into court with you?' — mišpāṭ + bôʾ ʿim = judicial proceeding; the disproportion between the creature's fragility (v1-2) and the solemnity of the divine court is Job's complaint.
14.4: mî-yittēn ṭāhôr miṭṭāmēʾ lō ʾeḥāḏ — 'who can bring something pure from something impure? Not one' — this is one of the most theologically dense one-line statements in Job. ṭāhôr/ṭāmēʾ (pure/impure) are the cultic purity categories; the rhetorical question with the answer 'not one' (lō ʾeḥāḏ) asserts the universal impossibility of purity arising from impurity. Paul in Rom 3:10-12 strings together similar ʾeḥāḏ / 'not one' statements from the Psalms; the logic of universal human uncleanness that undergirds the need for an external righteousness begins to take shape here.
14.5: ʾim ḥărûṣîm yāmāyw — 'since his days are determined/decided' — ḥāraṣ means to cut/decide; the passive participle ḥărûṣîm = 'decreed/fixed' (cf. Dan 9:24-26 where the same root governs divinely determined periods of history). mispār ḥŏḏāšāyw ʾittĕḵā — 'the number of his months is with you' — the months of human life are God's to count, not the human's to determine. ḥuqqāyw ʿāśîṯā wĕlō yaʿăḇōr — 'you have set his limit that he cannot cross' — ḥōq (a fixed statutory limit) functions here as Job's argument for why God should ease up: if the limits are already fixed, there is no point maintaining adversarial pressure.
14.6: šĕʿēh mēʿālāyw wĕyiḥdāl — 'look away from him so he can rest' — šāʿāh (to look away, avert gaze) used with mēʿālāyw (from upon him) — the request is simply for divine inattention, the relief of not being watched. ʿaḏ-yirṣeh kĕśākîr yômô — 'until he completes, like a hired worker, his day' — the hired worker (śākîr) simile: a day-laborer's term of service is finite and acknowledged; at its end he has rest and his wages. Job is asking for a similar finitude of divine attention.
14.7: yēš tiqwāh lāʿēṣ — 'there is hope for a tree' — the word tiqwāh (hope, from qāwāh = to wait/hope) is used here for the first time in the chapter in a positive sense, though its application is to a tree rather than a human. The tree-hope becomes a foil for human hopelessness in vv10-12. wĕḥôṭer miggizʿô lō yĕḥdāl — 'and its new shoot will not stop' — ḥōṭer is a young shoot or sucker growing from a cut stump; gizʿāh is the stump; the tree regenerates from below ground even after being severed. The image of hope-through-remnant anticipates the messianic stump imagery of Isa 11:1 ('a shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse').
14.8: ʾim-yazzîn bāʾāreṣ šorĕšô — 'though its root grows old in the earth' — zāqan (to be old, to age) applied to a root hidden in soil; even if the visible part of the tree is gone, the aging root persists underground. wĕbĕʿāpār yāmûṯ gizʿô — 'and its stump dies in the dust' — gizʿāh (stump); māwet (death); ʿāpār (dust/earth). The imagery of death-in-dust will be reversed in v9 by the water-scent miracle, setting up the contrast with humanity in v10.
14.9: mērêaḥ mayim yapprîaḥ — 'at the scent of water it blossoms' — the tree's response to water is spontaneous and irrepressible: pāraḥ (to blossom/sprout) triggered by mere rêaḥ (smell/scent) of water. The sensory detail (smell, not taste or touch) emphasizes the tree's deep responsiveness to life-conditions. wĕʿāśāh qaṣîr kĕnāṭaʿ — 'and makes branches like a new plant' — the dead stump recovering to produce fresh growth ('like a new plant') is the image of resurrection without using the word; the trajectory from vv7-9 models renewal-from-death precisely to set up the contrast with human mortality in vv10-12.
14.10: wĕgāḇer yāmûṯ wayyeḥĕlaš — 'but man dies and falls helpless' — wayyeḥĕlaš from ḥālaš = to be weak/prostrate; the contrast with the tree's vigorous resprouting is stark. wĕgāwaʿ ʾāḏām wĕʾayyô — 'and man breathes his last — and where is he?' — gāwaʿ is the verb of expiration (breathing out the last breath); ʾayyô is the haunting interrogative 'where is he?', which carries the same existential weight as Cain's 'am I my brother's keeper?' (Gen 4:9) and the angels' 'man, where are you?' (Gen 3:9). The 'where?' question will echo in the Resurrection proclamation.
14.11: ʾāzĕlû mayim miyyām — 'as waters vanish from the sea' — āzal (to go away, to be gone; used very rarely, possibly related to a root meaning 'to exhaust'). The simile of water draining from a lake (yām can be any body of water) introduces the first of three water-failure images (v11: lake; v12a: river) that parallel human death. wĕnāhār yeḥĕraḇ wĕyāḇēš — 'and a river wastes and dries up' — ḥāraḇ (to be dry/desolate, the root of ḥōreḇ = desert) and yāḇēš (to dry up) together describe total desiccation with no prospect of return.
14.12: wĕʾîš šāḵaḇ wĕlō-yāqûm — 'man lies down and does not rise' — the water-failure simile completed: as water drains irreversibly, so man lies down irreversibly. The key phrase is the temporal clause: ʿaḏ-biltî šāmayim lō yêqîṣû — 'until the heavens are no more, they will not wake.' biltî is a negative particle (without/unless); the clause literally 'until the failure/absence of heavens, they will not awake.' This does not close the door on resurrection — it opens it: when the heavens finally end (as Isa 34:4; 51:6; Rev 21:1 affirm they will), the sleepers will wake. The clause is eschatologically charged.
14.13: mî-yittēn bišʾôl taps̈pĕnēnî — 'O that you would hide me in Sheol!' — the optative mî-yittēn ('who will give?' = O that!) expressing a wish; Job's longing is for Sheol not as final destination but as temporary shelter from divine wrath. The phrase taps̈pĕnēnî (from ṣāp̄an = to hide/store) uses the same verb as Ps 31:19 ('how great is your goodness which you have stored up [ṣāp̄antā] for those who fear you') — hiding in Sheol paradoxically connects to the divine storage of the righteous. ṯāšît lî ḥōq wĕtizkĕrēnî — 'set a fixed time for me and remember me!' — ḥōq (appointed/fixed limit) and zāḵar (remember); Job asks for a divine appointment in Sheol from which God will eventually summon him back, using the covenant-recall language of divine remembrance (cf. Gen 8:1, 'God remembered Noah').
14.14: ʾim-yāmûṯ gāḇer hăyiḥyeh — 'if a man dies, will he live again?' — the resurrection question, the hinge of the chapter and one of the most theologically significant single-verse questions in the OT. ḥāyāh (to live) after māwet is either the question of resuscitation (will he simply revive?) or resurrection (will he be raised to new life?). The NT uses this exact logic-of-impossibility-now-made-possible structure in John 11:25 ('I am the resurrection and the life'). kol-yĕmê ṣĕḇāʾî ʾăyaḥēl ʿaḏ-bôʾ ḥălîp̄āṯî — 'all the days of my hard service I will wait until my release comes' — ṣāḇāʾ is military service/hard labor (used also in Isa 40:2 for Jerusalem's completed 'hard service'); ḥălîp̄āh is exchange/relief (from ḥālap̄ = to pass through, to change; used of a military rotation). Job envisions waiting in a post-mortem state of service until a divinely decreed moment of exchange/relief arrives — the closest OT parallel to a resurrection hope.
14.15: tiqrāʾ wĕʾānōḵî ʾeʿĕneh — 'you will call and I will answer' — the restoration of the divine-human dialogue broken by death; in Job's vision of the post-mortem state, God calls and Job responds. This is the inverse of the divine silence Job experiences in the present. tiḵsōp̄ lĕmaʿăśēh yāḏeyḵā — 'you will long for the work of your hands' — kāsap̄ means to long for/desire with yearning (used in Gen 31:30 of Laban longing for his household gods; Ps 84:2 of the soul longing for God's courts). God longing for his own handiwork (Job) is a remarkable attribution: the Creator is portrayed as yearning for the creature he made. This verse is the theological counterweight to all of Job's complaint — the ultimate frame is divine yearning, not divine indifference.
14.16: kî-ʿattāh ṣĕʿādî tispôr — 'for now you count my every step' — sāpar (to count/number) applied to steps; the divine surveillance experienced not as care but as obsessive prosecution. The contrast with v15 is sharp: there, God yearns; here (in present reality), God prosecutes. lō-ṯišmôr ʿal-ḥaṭṭāʾṯî — 'you do not overlook my sin' — in the hoped-for future relationship (v15), the implication is that God will choose not to prosecute every infraction; in the present, none escapes. The verse captures the present experience of grace-less divine scrutiny that makes existence unbearable.
14.17: ḥātûm bĕṣerôr pišʿî — 'my transgression sealed in a bag' — ṣārôr is a knotted pouch or bundle; pešaʿ (willful transgression) sealed inside it as if God is storing the evidence. wattipĕl ʿal-ʿăwōnî — 'and you stitch up my iniquity' — ṭāpal (to plaster/fasten over); the image is of God sealing and stitching shut the bag of Job's sins so nothing escapes the eventual reckoning. The forensic archive metaphor: God as keeper of perfectly preserved prosecutorial records.
14.18: wĕʾûlām hār-nôp̄ēl yibbôl — 'and surely a falling mountain crumbles' — the erosion section (vv18-19) uses geological imagery for the human condition under divine attrition. nāpal (to fall) + nāḇal (to decay/crumble); a collapsing mountain erodes over time rather than in a single dramatic event, mirroring the slow dissolution of Job's hope. wĕṣûr yeʿĕtaq mimmĕqōmô — 'and the rock is displaced from its place' — ʿātaq (to move on/pass over, used in Gen 12:8 of Abram moving on); rocks considered immovable are yet dislodged by geological processes — another impossibility-become-possible that grounds Job's eschatological hope.
14.19: ʾăḇānîm šāḥăqû mayim — 'waters wear away stones' — šāḥaq (to wear down/pulverize, from which šāḥaq = fine dust/sky) used of water's slow grinding effect on rock; the same inexorable attrition applies to human hope. tišṭōp̄ sĕpîḥeyāh ʿăp̄ar-ʾāreṣ — 'its floods wash away the dust of the earth' — šāṭap̄ (to overflow/wash away) of floodwaters sweeping the topsoil away. wĕtiqwaṯ ʾĕnôš ʾibbadtāh — 'and the hope of man you destroy' — tiqwāh (hope, the same root used for the tree's hope in v7) now applied to man and declared destroyed; the geological erosion is the metaphor for the erosion of hope. Job returns to tiqwāh to close the circle from v7.
14.20: tĕqāp̄ēhû lāneṣaḥ wayyēlek — 'you overpower him once and he is gone forever' — lāneṣaḥ (forever, perpetually); the divine coup de grâce is delivered once (not repeatedly) but is permanent. mĕšanneh p̄ānāyw waṯĕšallĕḥēhû — 'you alter his face and send him away' — šānāh (to change); the alteration of the face at death — the features becoming unrecognizable, strange. The sending-away (šālaḥ) is a dismissal; life is a temporary summons and death is the dismissal after the audience.
14.21: yikbĕḏû bānāyw wĕlō-yēḏāʿ — 'his sons rise to honor and he does not know it' — yāḏaʿ (to know) is the verb of epistemological isolation; the father in Sheol cannot perceive the progress or regress of his children. wĕyiṣʿărû wĕlō-yāḇîn lāmô — 'they are brought low and he does not understand' — the double ignorance (honor and dishonor) is equally unknown; post-mortem existence as total epistemic isolation from earthly life. The verse is one of the OT's clearest statements of the disembodied state's disconnection from history.
14.22: ʾaḵ-bĕśārô ʿālāyw yikʾāḇ — 'but his flesh feels pain within him' — kāʾab (to feel pain/be grieved); the final verse describes the dying/dead man's last experience: exclusively inward, bodily pain. wĕnap̄šô ʿālāyw tiʾēḇāl — 'and his soul mourns over himself' — ʾāḇal (to mourn, used for mourning the dead); the soul's grief is reflexive — mourning itself rather than others. The chapter that began with man's brief, troubled existence (v1) ends with pain and self-mourning: the most existentially isolated possible image of death, which makes Job's expressed hope in vv13-15 all the more remarkable.

8.1: Bildad the Shuhite is identified by region: Shuah was a son of Abraham by Keturah (Gen 25:2), placing him in the Aramean/North Arabian belt. The three Friends represent the wisdom traditions of the eastern peoples — Eliphaz (Teman/Edomite), Bildad (Shuhite/Aramean), Zophar (Naamathite/unknown Arabian) — all respectable carriers of international wisdom tradition. Their credentials are part of the narrative setup: these are not ignorant accusers but representatives of the best human wisdom available.
8.2: Kammāh timallēl-rûaḥ — “how long will you speak wind?” The Hebrew rûaḥ (wind/spirit/breath) functions as a derogatory term for empty speech; Bildad is essentially calling Job’s lament hot air. The irony is that YHWH will speak from a storm/whirlwind (sᵉʿārāh) in chapter 38, and will condemn the Friends’ words, not Job’s, as theologically incorrect (42:7).
8.3: Hăyᵉʿawwēl ʾēl mišpāṭ — “does God pervert justice?” The piel of ʿāwat (pervert/distort) uses the same root as ʿawlāh (wickedness, injustice). Bildad’s theology is correct in the abstract: YHWH does not pervert justice. The MKT renders “distort” to capture the spatial metaphor — justice bent, curved away from the straight. The error is not in Bildad’s theology but in his application: he assumes the present situation is a straightforward case of justice in operation.
8.4: ʾim-ḥāṭᵉʾû ḇānêḵā lô wayyᵉšallᵉḥēm biḏê pišʿām — “if your children sinned against him, he gave them over to the consequences of their own sin.” Pišʿām (their transgression) uses the same root as the legally weightiest word for deliberate rebellion; šillaḥ (gave them over) is the same word used for divine abandonment to consequences. Bildad is saying Job’s dead children got what their sins deserved — a brutal pastoral move against a grieving father, and one the narrative frame of chapters 1-2 has already refuted.
8.5: ʾim-ʾattāh tᵉšaḥēr ʾel-ʾēl — “if you will seek God earnestly.” Šāḥar means “to seek/look for early in the morning” — the dawn-seeking intensity of diligent prayer; the same root appears in Psalm 63:1 (“early I will seek you”). Ḥānan (plead for grace) is the grace-vocabulary of the Psalter; Bildad instructs Job to do exactly what Job has been doing — pleading before God — but frames it as the condition Job has not yet met.
8.6: ʾim-zak wᵉyāšār ʾāttāh kî-ʿattāh yāʿîr ʿāleḵā — “if you are pure and upright.” Zak (pure) and yāšār (upright) are the exact terms the narrator uses to describe Job in 1:1 (“blameless and upright” — tām wᵉyāšār); Bildad’s conditional uses Job’s own credentials as if they were yet to be established, while the reader knows they were established before the opening. The promise “he will restore your righteous home” will be fulfilled at the book’s end, but not because of Bildad’s advice.
8.7: wᵉhāyāh rēʾšîṯᵉḵā miṣʿār ûʾaḥărîṯᵉḵā yiśgeh mᵉʾōḏ — “though your beginnings were humble, your latter years will be far greater.” The saying echoes the conventional wisdom of restoration after humble beginnings; the identical promise is quoted in Job 42:12 when God actually does restore Job. Bildad gets the outcome right while completely misreading the path.
8.8: šāʾal-nāʾ lᵉḏôr rišôn — “ask the former generations.” The appeal to ancestral wisdom (ḏôr rišôn = first/former generation) is Bildad’s epistemological method: traditional wisdom accumulated across generations is the most reliable guide. This is contrasted with Job’s appeal to his own experience. The tension between inherited tradition and lived experience is one of the book’s structural concerns.
8.9: kî-ṯmôl ʾᵃnaḥnû wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ — “for we are but of yesterday and know nothing.” The phrase kî-ṯmôl (for yesterday/as of yesterday) is an idiom for temporal insignificance; compare the same expression in 1 Chr 29:15 (“our days on earth are like a shadow”) and Ps 90:4 (a thousand years are like a watch in the night). Bildad’s epistemological humility about the present generation ironically applies more to himself than he realizes.
8.10: hălōʾ-hēmmāh yôrûḵā wᵉyōmrû lāḵ — “will they not teach you, tell you, and speak?” The three verbs of instruction (yārah = teach/instruct; ʾāmar = tell; ṣāʾ = bring forth words) describe the full transmission process of traditional wisdom. Yārah is the same root as tôrāh (instruction/Torah), placing Bildad’s appeal to tradition in the same category as divine instruction.
8.11: hăyiśgeh-gōmeʾ bilōʾ biṣṣāh — “can papyrus grow where there is no marsh?” Gōmeʾ (papyrus/bulrush) is an Egyptian loanword — the same plant Moses’ basket was made of (Exod 2:3). The botanical analogy is precise: marsh plants require their specific habitat. The argument is analogical — just as these plants require their cause, suffering requires a cause (sin). The analogy is logically valid; the application to Job is not.
8.12: ʿōḏennû ḇᵉʾibbô lōʾ yiqqāṭēp wᵉlipnê ḵol-ḥāṣîr yiḇāš — “though it is still green and not yet cut, it withers before any other herb.” The sudden collapse of the plant that seemed well-watered is the analogy for the apparent prosperity of the wicked; yibbāš (wither) is the same verb used in Ps 102:4 and Isa 40:7-8 (the grass withers). The reversal from green-and-lush to withered-before-anything-else captures the instability of prosperity without God.
8.13: kēn ʾorḥôṯ kol-šōkᵉḥê ʾēl wᵉtiqwaṯ ḥānēp tōʾḇēḏ — “such is the end of all who forget God; the hope of the godless will come to nothing.” Tiqwāh (hope) is a key Job-term, appearing also in 14:19 (you wash away the hope of man) and 19:10 (my hope he has uprooted like a tree). Bildad claims confident knowledge of whose hope will fail; the book’s irony is that it is Bildad’s own theological hope (Job is guilty and will be exposed) that fails.
8.14: ʾᵃšer-yāqôṭ kiślô wᵉbêṯ ʿakkāḇîš miṭḥô — “what he trusts in will be cut off, and his confidence is like a spider’s web.” ʿAkkāḇîš (spider) is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the OT; the image of spider-web fragility is striking precisely because the web appears intricate and strong while being wholly unable to bear weight. Kiślô (his confidence) shares a root with the constellation Kesil/Orion — both carry the sense of foolish overconfidence in something that will not hold.
8.15: yiššāʿēn ʿal-bêṯô wᵉlōʾ yaʿᵃmōḏ — “he leans against his house, but it does not stand.” The image of leaning on something that gives way is the standard OT figure for misplaced trust (cf. Ezek 29:7 — leaning on Egypt as a broken reed). The house (bêṯ) that cannot support the one who clings to it is both literal and metaphorical: the worldly establishment that prosperity-religion builds will not hold when tested.
8.16: rāṭōb hûʾ lipnê-šāmeš ûʿal gannāṯô yanṭᵉriḥa yônaqtô — “he is well-watered in the sunshine, his shoots spreading over the garden.” The image shifts from the spider’s web to a lush garden plant — the wicked man’s temporary prosperity is like rapid growth that looks impressive but is not rooted. The garden imagery inverts the Eden pattern: apparently flourishing, actually headed for destruction.
8.17: ʿal-gal šārāšāyw yᵉsûḇāḵû bêṯ ʾᵃḇānîm yehĕzeh — “his roots twine around the pile of stones; he forces his way into a stony place.” The aggressive root-growth into inhospitable stone looks like resilience but is actually rootlessness in disguise: there is no genuine nourishment in stone. The image of invasive roots finding purchase in rock only to be suddenly uprooted (v.18) captures the sudden reversibility of ungodly prosperity.
8.18: ʾim-yᵉḇallᵉʿennû mimᵉqômô wᵉkiḥeš bô lōʾ rᵉʾîṯîḵā — “but if he is destroyed from his place, it says to him: ‘I have never seen you.’” The impersonal “it says” refers to the place itself — the land the wicked man inhabited denies knowing him. This personification of place as witness echoes Abel’s blood crying from the ground (Gen 4:10); the earth itself testifies against the wicked.
8.19: hēn-hûʾ mᵉśôś darkô ûmeʿāpār ʾaḥēr yiṣmāḥû — “behold, such is the joy of his path; and from the earth others will spring up in his place.” The sarcastic “such is the joy” (mᵉśôś darkô) announces the replacement of the wicked by others — the earth continues indifferently. Bildad’s point is that the pattern recycles: the godless flourish and fall, and others take their place; only the righteous escape this cycle.
8.20: hēn ʾēl lōʾ yimʾas tām — “God does not reject the blameless.” Tām (blameless/complete) is the same word used of Job in 1:1 and 1:8; YHWH himself calls Job tām. Bildad’s assertion is true — God does not reject the blameless — and will be demonstrated by the book’s end. But Bildad’s implication (therefore Job is not blameless, since he is apparently rejected) is the error the narrative refutes.
8.21: ʿaḏ-yᵉmallēʾ śᵉḥôq pîḵā ûśpāṯeḵā tᵉrûʿāh — “he will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with joyful shouts.” Śᵉḥôq (laughter) is the word used for Sarah’s laughter (Gen 21:6) and for the laughter of restored Zion (Ps 126:2). Bildad’s promise of restored laughter is ironically accurate: in 42:10-17, Job’s latter days are greater; but the path there is through the whirlwind, not through Bildad’s prescribed repentance.
8.22: śōnᵉʾêḵā yilbᵉšû-ḇōšeṯ wᵉʾōhel rᵉšāʿîm ʾênennû — “your enemies will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will vanish.” The reversal-of-garments motif — enemies wearing shame while the righteous wear joy (cf. Ps 109:29) — is standard covenant-theology language. The “tent of the wicked” that vanishes echoes 8:15’s house that cannot stand. Bildad ends with standard encouragement, theologically correct, pastorally catastrophic in its implicit accusation.

9.1: Chapter 9 is Job’s response to Bildad and marks the escalation from theological discourse to legal metaphor: Job begins to frame his situation as a lawsuit (rîḇ) against God. The courtroom vocabulary that accumulates in ch 9 — justify, answer, argue, umpire, summon — is the most sustained legal imagery in the book outside the divine speeches.
9.2: ûmah-yiṣdaq ʾĕnôš ʿim-ʾēl — “how can a man be justified before God?” Yiṣdaq is the niphal of ṣādaq (to be righteous/declared righteous) — a legal/forensic term, not merely a moral description. Job takes Bildad’s implicit question (8:3 — does God pervert justice?) and applies it existentially: even granting that God’s justice is real, how can any creature establish righteousness in a divine court? The question is not rhetorical despair but genuine legal inquiry.
9.3: ʾim-yāḥpoṣ lārîḇ ʿimmô lōʾ-yaʿᵃnennû ʾaḥaṯ mittî-ʾelep — “one could not answer him once in a thousand attempts.” The legal odds — 1 in 1000 — are not pessimism but legal realism about the asymmetry between human and divine standards of proof. The same impossibility is used positively in Eccl 7:28 and Job 33:23 (if there is an angel to interpret, one among a thousand).
9.4: ḥᵃḵam lēḇāḇ wᵉʾammîṣ kōaḥ — “God is wise in heart and mighty in strength.” The pairing of wisdom and power is the same pairing Job celebrates in 12:13 (“with God are wisdom and strength”) and that Paul applies to Christ (1 Cor 1:24). Job’s acknowledgment of divine wisdom-and-power is not defiance but context-setting: if even Rahab’s allies are subdued by this power (v.13), what chance does Job have in court?
9.5: hāmmַʿtîq hārîm wᵉlōʾ yāḏᵉʿû — “he removes mountains before they know it.” The suddenness of divine action — mountains overturned in anger before they are aware — echoes the cosmological power descriptions that recur through the book. The image of mountains uprooted anticipates the whirlwind speech’s questions about cosmological foundations (38:4-6).
9.6: hammargîz ʾereṣ mimmᵉqômāh wᵉʿammûḏeḥā yiṯpallāṣûn — “who shakes the earth from its place so that its pillars quake.” The ʿammûḏîm (pillars) of the earth are cosmological elements; the image of earth-pillars quaking connects to Ps 75:3 (I have set the pillars firm), Isa 24:18-20, and the theophany descriptions of divine judgment. Seismic disruption as divine action is a consistent OT theophanic motif.
9.7: hāʾōmēr laḥereš wᵉlōʾ yizrāḥ wᵉḇaʿᵃḏ kôḵāḇîm yaḥtōm — “who commands the sun not to rise, and seals up the stars.” The command-form (hāʾōmēr — the one who says to) emphasizes divine speech-as-command over celestial mechanics; the sun’s non-rising and the stars’ sealing (like a letter sealed with wax) represent the suspension of natural order. This is the counterpart to Gen 1:3 (“and God said... let there be light”) — the same God who issued the creative command can revoke it.
9.8: nōṭeh šāmayim lᵉḇaddô wᵉdōrēḵ ʿal-bāmŏṯê yām — “who alone stretched out the heavens and walks on the crests of the sea.” Dōrēḵ ʿal-bāmŏṯê yām (walks/treads on the heights/back of the sea) is the earliest attested OT form of the divine sea-walking motif; cf. Hab 3:15; Ps 77:19. This is the conceptual precursor to Jesus walking on water (Matt 14:25-26; John 6:19), where the disciples’ recognition — “truly you are the Son of God” — reflects the Joban theology that only God treads the sea.
9.9: ʿōśeh ʿāš kᵉsîl wᵉḵîmāh wᵉḥaḏrê ṯêmān — “who made the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades.” ʿAš (the Bear/Arcturus), kᵉsîl (Orion — from the root for foolishness; the fool-constellation), and kîmāh (Pleiades) are three of the book’s five astronomical references (also in 38:31-32). The Creator’s authorship of the constellations is the ground of YHWH’s own challenge in 38:31: “Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades?” — the same constellations Job cites here as evidence of divine power.
9.10: ʿōśeh gᵉḏōlôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên ḥēqer wᵉniplāʾôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispār — “who does great things beyond comprehension and marvelous things without number.” This verse is nearly identical to 5:9 (Eliphaz’s doxology); Job is quoting Eliphaz’s own theological description of God and applying it as evidence that Job cannot win a divine lawsuit. The shared vocabulary (“unsearchable,” “without number”) shows Job mastering the Friends’ own theological categories while reaching opposite practical conclusions.
9.11: hēn yaʿᵃḇōr ʿālay wᵉlōʾ ʾerʾeh wᵉyaḥᵃlōp wᵉlōʾ-ʾāḇîn lô — “when he passes by me, I cannot see him; when he moves on, I cannot perceive him.” The invisibility of God in action — passing but unseen — is not atheism but a description of the divine incognito; cf. the divine presence passing before Moses (Exod 33:22) and Elijah (1 Kgs 19:11). The inability to perceive God passing is one of the existential crises of Job’s situation: God is active but not legible.
9.12: hēn yaḥtōp mî yᵉšîḇennû mî-yōmar ʾēlāyw māh-taaśeh — “if he snatches something away, who can stop him? Who will dare say to him, ‘What are you doing?’” The question “what are you doing?” (māh-taʿᵃśeh) echoes the question that Job wishes he could ask; it also anticipates the ironic reversal in the whirlwind when God asks Job essentially the same question — “Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?” (38:4).
9.13: ʾĕlôhah lōʾ-yāšîḇ ʾappô taḥtāyw šāḥᵃḥû ʿōzᵉrê Rahaḇ — “God does not restrain his anger; even the allies of Rahab crouched beneath him.” Rahaḇ here is the chaos-dragon of cosmogonic conflict (distinct from Rahab of Jericho), paralleling the Babylonian Tiamat and related to tannîn (dragon) in 3:8 and 26:12. The “allies of Rahab” — forces of primordial chaos — are subdued; if even cosmic chaos forces submit to God, Job has no legal recourse through power.
9.14: ʾap kî-ʾānōḵî ʾeʿᵃnennû ʾeḇḥᵃrāh ḏᵉḇāray ʿimmô — “how then can I answer him, or select my words to argue before him?” Eḇḥᵃrāh ḏᵉḇāray (I will choose/select my words) describes the careful rhetorical preparation of a legal advocate; Job recognizes that even if he prepares his best case, the asymmetry of the courtroom makes victory impossible on his own terms. The careful word-selection of chapters 9-10 is itself the enacted form of this preparation.
9.15: ʾᵃšer ʾim-ṣāḏaqtî lōʾ ʾeʿᵃneh lᵉmišpāṭî ʾetḥannān — “even if I were righteous, I could not answer him; I would have to plead for mercy to my own judge.” The legal paradox: even a righteous defendant must appeal to mercy rather than justice before this judge. ʾetḥannān (I would plead for grace/mercy) uses the same ḥānan root as Bildad’s advice in 8:5. Job discovers that the only possible stance before God is grace-seeking — but even this seems inadequate given God’s apparent disregard for Job’s cries.
