9.16: ʾim-qārāʾṯî wayyaʿᵃnēnî lōʾ-ʾaʾᵃmîn kî-yaʾᵃzîn qôlî — “if I summoned him and he responded, I still could not believe he was truly listening to me.” The crisis of divine responsiveness: even a divine answer would not resolve Job’s epistemic problem, because he cannot verify that God is genuinely attending to his case. This anticipates the whirlwind response — God does answer Job, and Job’s response (40:4-5; 42:1-6) is the unexpected form of “believing God is listening.”
9.17: ʾᵃšer bisᵉʿārāh yᵉšûpᵉnî wᵉhirᵉbāh pᵉṣāʿay ḥinnām — “for he would crush me with a storm and multiply my wounds for no reason.” Bisᵉʿārāh (with a storm/whirlwind) uses the exact same root as the sᵉʿārāh from which YHWH actually speaks in 38:1 — “out of the storm the LORD answered.” Job anticipates the divine answer will be crushing force; YHWH’s actual whirlwind-speech is confrontational but not obliterating — it is the answer that restores Job (42:5-6).
9.18: lōʾ-yitᵉnnênî hāšēḇ rûḥî kî yaśbîʿanî mamrōrîm — “he does not let me catch my breath, but fills me with bitter suffering.” Hāšēḇ rûḥî (restore my breath/spirit) is the language of near-death recovery; Job is at the edge of being overwhelmed. Mamrōrîm (bitter things) shares a root with Naomi’s name-change request (Mārā = bitter; Ruth 1:20) — the vocabulary of suffering that has turned life to bitterness.
9.19: ʾim-lᵉkōaḥ ʾammiṣ hinnēh wᵉʾim-lᵉmišpāṭ mî yôʿîḏēnî — “if it is a contest of strength, he is the stronger; if it is a matter of justice, who can summon him to court?” The double impossibility: strength argument fails (God has more); justice argument fails (God cannot be summoned). The only remaining resource is the mediator/umpire Job will request in v.33.
9.20: ʾim-ʾeṣdaq pî yaršîʿēnî tām-ʾānî wayᵉʿaqqᵉšēnî — “if I justified myself, my own mouth would condemn me; if I declared myself blameless, he would prove me crooked.” The legal trap: self-justification generates self-condemnation because the standard being applied exceeds what any creature meets. The same logic drives Paul in Rom 3:19-20 (the law silences every mouth and makes all the world accountable to God).
9.21: tām ʾānî lōʾ-ʾēḏaʿ napšî ʾemʾas ḥayyāy — “though I am blameless, I do not trust my own self-knowledge; I despise my own life.” Tām ʾānî (I am blameless) reaffirms Job’s integrity even as he disclaims confident self-knowledge. The epistemological humility — not knowing his own soul fully — is one of Job’s most honest moments; he is not asserting perfect self-transparency but genuine integrity based on available evidence. This anticipates Paul’s similar statement in 1 Cor 4:3-4.
9.22: ʾaḥaṯ hîʾ ʿal-kēn ʾāmartî tām wᵉrāšāʿ hûʾ mᵉkalleh — “it is all the same to me; this is why I say: he destroys the blameless and the wicked alike.” Job’s most radical claim — that divine retributive distinction is empirically absent. This is not atheism but crisis-theodicy: Job is not denying God’s existence but questioning the operational reliability of the retributive principle. The book will eventually validate Job’s integrity while not vindicating this particular claim.
9.23: ʾim-šôṭ yāmîṯ pitʾōm limsaṯ nᵉqiyyîm yilʿāḡ — “when sudden disaster brings death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.” Yilʿāḡ (mocks/laughs) applied to God is Job’s most provocative statement — yet cf. Ps 2:4 (God laughs at human schemes) and Prov 1:26 (Wisdom laughs at the calamity of those who rejected her). Job’s accusation is extreme but uses vocabulary the tradition applied to God in other contexts.
9.24: ʾereṣ nitᵉnāh bᵉyaḏ-rāšāʿ pᵉnê-šōpᵉṭeḥā yᵉkasseh ʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôʾ mî-hûʾ — “the earth is given into the power of the wicked; he blindfolds its judges. If not he, then who?” The rhetorical question “if not he, then who?” (ʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôʾ mî-hûʾ) is Job’s form of theodicy-demand: if God is sovereign, God is responsible for the observable injustice; if God is not responsible, then who? The Friends have no answer to this question; the whirlwind does not give a direct answer either — it reframes the question.
9.25: wᵉyāmay qallû minnî-rāṣ — “my days are swifter than a runner.” The speed-of-life metaphors multiply: runner, reed skiffs, diving eagle — each faster than the last. The accumulation of velocity images captures the experience of time accelerating in suffering; there is no time to establish the righteousness Job claims before his life ends.
9.26: ḥālᵉpû ʿim-ʾonîyyôṯ ʾēḇeh kᵉneser yāṭûš ʿᵃlê-ʾōkel — “they glide away like skiffs of reed, like an eagle diving on its prey.” ʾOnîyyôṯ ʾēḇeh (boats/skiffs of papyrus/reed) — light Egyptian papyrus vessels known for speed on the Nile; the eagle diving (yāṭûš) on prey is the same eagle of 39:29-30; Job’s life is as fleeting as the fastest things he knows.
9.27: ʾim-ʾāmartî ʾeškaḥāh śîḥî ʾeʿezbāh pānay wᵉʾabliḡāh — “if I say, ‘I will forget my complaint, I will put on a cheerful face and be bright.’” Job considers the option of forced optimism — the social performance of well-being — and immediately rejects it (v.28): he knows it is impossible because the underlying dread remains. The authentic lament tradition refuses cosmetic cheerfulness as a response to genuine suffering.
9.28: yāgōrtî kol-ʿōṣᵉḇōṯay yāḏaʿtî kî-lōʾ tᵉnaqqēnî — “I dread all my suffering, for I know you will not acquit me.” Tᵉnaqqēnî (acquit/declare innocent) is the legal term from nāqāh (to be clean/innocent); Job’s certainty of divine non-acquittal is a crisis of faith, not an absence of faith. The certainty that God will not declare him innocent despite his being innocent is the book’s central paradox until the whirlwind resolves it by declaring Job’s words “right” (42:7).
9.29: ʾānōḵî ʾerāšaʿ lāmmāh-zeh heḇel ʾiyygāʿ — “if I am condemned already, why do I exhaust myself for nothing?” Heḇel (vapor/breath/futility) — the same word that dominates Ecclesiastes — here applies to Job’s legal effort: if the outcome is predetermined, the effort is heḇel. The word’s Ecclesiastes usage asks the same theodicy question from a different angle.
9.30: ʾim-hiṯrāḥaṣtî ḇᵉmê-šāleg — “even if I washed myself with snow water.” Snow water (mê šāleg) was regarded as the purest available water; the conditional sets up the maximal purity scenario. The futility of even maximum purity before God parallels Isa 64:6 (“all our righteous acts are like filthy rags”) — though Job’s lament is about innocence-while-condemned, not about the insufficiency of righteousness per se.
9.31: ʾāz baššaḥaṯ tiṭbᵉlēnî wᵉtiʿᵃḇûnî śalmôṯay — “you would still plunge me into a slime pit so that my own clothes would recoil from me.” Šaḥaṯ (pit/grave/corruption) — the same word used in Ps 16:10 (“you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead, nor will you let your faithful one see decay”) and Jon 2:6. The clothes recoiling from the defiled wearer — even Job’s garments reject him — captures extreme defilement imagery.
9.32: kî lōʾ-ʾîš kāmônî ʾeʿᵃnennû nāḇôʾ yaḥḏāw bammišpāṭ — “for he is not a man as I am, that I could answer him or that we could appear in court together.” Lōʾ-ʾîš kāmônî (not a man like me) is one of Job’s most theologically loaded statements. The asymmetry Job laments — God is not human, therefore the legal process is inaccessible — is the precise condition that the incarnation addresses: “the Word became flesh” (John 1:14) makes the divine party to the case human and therefore subject to the legal encounter Job cannot achieve.
9.33: lōʾ-yēš bênênû môkîaḥ yāšêṯ yāḏô ʿal-šᵉnênû — “there is no umpire between us who could place his hand upon us both.” Môkîaḥ (umpire/arbiter/one who argues/decides) is from the root yākaḥ (to argue, reprove, decide a case); the same root produces the prophetic hôkîaḥ (to rebuke/decide). The umpire who can lay hands on both God and human is the central longing of the book — and its NT answer is explicit: “For there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus” (1 Tim 2:5). Job’s umpire-longing is one of the OT’s clearest anticipations of the incarnate mediator.
9.34: yāsēr mēʿālay šiḇṭô wᵉʾêmāṯô ʾal-tᵉḇaʿᵃtanî — “let him remove his rod from me, and let his terror not overwhelm me.” The šēḇeṭ (rod/staff) here is the instrument of divine discipline/punishment, not of comfort as in Ps 23:4. Job is asking for the removal of both the punishment and the terror — what he wants is a hearing without the judicial pressure that makes honest speech impossible. He will eventually get this at the whirlwind: God speaks, but from 38:3 onward it is God who does the cross-examining.
9.35: ʾᵃḏabbēr wᵉlōʾ ʾîrāʾennû kî lōʾ-ḵēn ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḏî — “then I would speak without fear, for I am not so in myself.” Job’s condition for honest speech is removal of the terror that suppresses his words; the whirlwind encounter actually fulfills this when God commands Job: “brace yourself like a warrior; I will question you, and you will answer me” (38:3; 40:7). The invitation to answer without annihilation is the version of Job’s request that YHWH grants.

10.1: nāqᵉṭāh napšî bᵉḥayyāy ʾeʿezbāh ʿālāyw śîḥî ʾᵃḏabbēr bᵉmar napšî — “my soul is sick of my life; I will give free rein to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.” Nāqᵉṭāh napšî (my soul loathes/is sick) — the verb qûṭ carries the sense of being cut off or disgusted; cf. Gen 27:46 (Rebekah’s loathing of life over Esau’s wives). ʾeʿezbāh ʿālāyw śîḥî — “I will release/let go my complaint upon him” — the idiom of dropping one’s restraint; Job’s address to God becomes direct from this verse forward.
10.2: ʾomar ʾel-ʾĕlôhah ʾal-taršîʿēnî hôḏîʿēnî ʿal-māh tᵉrîḇēnî — “Do not condemn me; let me know why you bring charges against me.” Taršîʿēnî (condemn/declare wicked) is the legal opposite of ṣādaq (declare righteous); Job requests both an acquittal and an explanation. The request for an explanation — “let me know why” — is the central question of the book, and the whirlwind speech conspicuously does not answer it directly.
10.3: hăṭôḇ lᵉḵā kî-taʿᵃšōq kî-timʾas yᵉgîaʿ kappêḵā — “does it please you to oppress, to reject the work of your own hands?” Yᵉgîaʿ kappêḵā (the work of your hands/the labor of your palms) appeals to the creator’s investment in the creature; the same phrase appears in Ps 128:2 (you shall eat the fruit of the labor of your hands) and 138:8 (do not abandon the work of your hands). The theological force: God has a stake in what God made; destruction of the creature undermines divine creative purpose.
10.4: hăʿênê ḇāśār lāḵ ʾim-kirʾôṯ ʾĕnôš tirʾeh — “do you have eyes of flesh? Do you see as a human being sees?” The question is anthropological: divine perception versus human perception. The ʿênê ḇāśār (eyes of flesh) are eyes that are limited, temporal, fallible; YHWH’s eyes are not — yet Job is asking whether God is operating as if he has these limitations, judging by surface appearances like a human being. This is the problem that Job’s friends have: they see exactly as human beings see, drawing human inferences.
10.5: hǎkîmê ʾĕnôš yāmeḵā ʾim-šᵉnôṯeḵā kimê gāḇer — “are your days like those of a human being, or your years like a man’s years?” The time-limitation question: if God’s days were finite, the urgency to condemn Job before evidence emerges would make sense. But God is eternal — there is no rush; no expiry date on investigation. The argument is that divine eternity should enable divine patience, which the present situation appears to contradict.
10.6: kî-ṯᵉḇaqqēš laʿᵃwōnî ûlᵉḥaṭṭāʾṯî ṯiḏrôš — “that you search out my wrongdoing and inquire into my sin.” Ṯᵉḇaqqēš (search for/seek) and ṯiḏrôš (inquire into/seek out) together describe intensive forensic investigation. The irony Job notes: God is applying diligent prosecutorial resources to find Job’s guilt, but God knows (v.7) there is no guilt to find.
10.7: ʿal-daʿtᵉḵā kî-lōʾ ʾerāšaʿ wᵉʾên miyyāḏᵉḵā maṣṣîl — “though you know I am not guilty, and no one can rescue me from your power.” The theological crux: God’s omniscience includes knowledge of Job’s innocence, yet the treatment continues. The omniscience-and-suffering tension is not resolved by Job but is implicitly resolved in 42:7-8 when God validates Job’s case retroactively.
10.8: yāḏeḵā ʿiṣᵉḇûnî wayyaʿᵃśûnî yaḥaḏ sāḇîḇ wattᵉḇallᵉʿēnî — “your hands shaped and formed me — the whole of me — and now you turn and destroy me.” Two verbs of craftsmanship: ʿāṣaḇ (fashion/form/shape — used of potters, sculptors, image-makers) and ʿāśāh (make/do — the general creation verb of Gen 1); together they emphasize deliberate, skilled construction. Yaḥaḏ sāḇîḇ (altogether/all around) — God shaped every aspect of Job. The contrast with “swallow/destroy” (bālaʿ) is stark: the same hands that made now apparently consume.
10.9: zᵉḵār-nāʾ kî-kaḥōmer ʿāśîṯānî wᵉʾel-ʿāpār tᵉšîḇēnî — “remember that you formed me like clay; will you now bring me back to dust?” Kaḥōmer (like clay) — the potter-clay metaphor of Gen 2:7 (formed from the dust of the ground) and Isa 64:8 (we are the clay, you are the potter). The appeal to clay-formation is simultaneously an argument for divine responsibility and for divine tenderness: clay is fragile; the potter should remember what the clay is made of.
10.10: hălōʾ kaḥālāḇ tattiḵēnî waggᵉḇînāh taqpîʾēnî — “did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?” The embryological analogy — liquid becoming solid, shapeless becoming shaped — is the OT’s most specific description of fetal development; cf. Ps 139:13-15 (woven in the womb). Ancient physiology understood conception as a process of coagulation of vital fluids; the poet uses this to describe the deliberateness and intimacy of divine creative involvement in human formation.
10.11: ʿôr wāḇāśār talḇîšēnî ûḇaʿᵃṣāmôṯ wᵉḡîḏîm tᵉśōḵᵉḵēnî — “you clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.” Tᵉśōḵᵉḵēnî (knit/weave together) — the weaving metaphor for skeletal formation connects to Ps 139:13 (tᵉsukkanî — you wove/knitted me). The body described as a garment put on (talḇîšēnî — clothed me) positions the physical form as given by God, not inherent to the self — a garment that can be removed but was given by the same hands now apparently destroying it.
10.12: ḥayyîm wāḥeseḏ ʿāśîṯā ʿimmāḏî ûpᵉquddāṯᵉḵā šāmᵉrāh rûḥî — “you gave me life and steadfast love (ḥeseḏ), and your watchful care preserved my spirit.” The attribution of ḥeseḏ (covenant steadfast love/loyalty) to God’s past action is theologically central: Job is not denying that God has shown covenant faithfulness — he is in crisis because the present experience contradicts the past. The tension between remembered ḥeseḏ and present silence is the spiritual anatomy of the book’s lament.
10.13: wᵉʾēlleh ṣāpantā ḇilḇāḇeḵā yāḏaʿtî kî-zōʾṯ ʿimmāḵ — “yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your purpose all along.” Ṣāpantā ḇilḇāḇeḵā (you hid in your heart) — Job suggests that the suffering was a predetermined divine intention, not an improvised punishment. The “purpose all along” reading gives Job’s theology a hidden-plan quality — divine concealment of intent that the reader knows was the testing of chapters 1-2.
10.14: ʾim-ḥāṭāʾṯî wᵉšāmartanî wᵉmeʿᵃwônî lōʾ ṯᵉnaqqēnî — “if I sin, you take note of it and will not let my guilt go.” The relentless divine observation of sin (šāmar — keep watch) is experienced by Job as prosecutorial surveillance rather than protective care; the same word is used for protective keeping in Ps 121:4 (“he who keeps Israel will neither slumber nor sleep”). Divine watchfulness that ought to be comforting has become threatening in Job’s experience.
10.15: ʾim-rāšaʿtî ʾallay lî wᵉṣāḏaqtî lōʾ-ʾeśśāʾ rōʾšî — “if I am wicked, woe to me! And if I am righteous, I still cannot hold my head up.” The double bind: the wicked are condemned; but the righteous cannot raise their heads either, because of the present humiliation. ʾAllay lî (woe to me!) is a self-directed curse expressing utter misery. The logical structure — wicked: condemned; righteous: still condemned — is the impossible situation Job is in.
10.16: wᵉyiśgeh kaššaḥal tᵉṣûḏēnî — “but if I do hold my head up, you hunt me like a fierce lion.” Šaḥal (fierce lion) is used in Ps 91:13 for the lion the protected person treads upon; here it describes God as hunter. The inversion of the divine hunter-and-helper imagery is part of Job’s theodicy vocabulary — God as adversary rather than ally, using the strongest possible animal metaphors for power.
10.17: tᵉḥaddeš ʿēḏeḵā nᵉḡḏî — “you bring fresh witnesses against me.” ʿēḏeḵā (your witnesses) — God as the one who produces witnesses for the prosecution rather than defending the accused. The Hebrew legal system required multiple witnesses for conviction (Deut 17:6); the image of God as the one continuously producing fresh witness-evidence against Job is the legal metaphor for the continuous suffering Job experiences.
10.18: wᵉlāmmāh mērāḥem hôṣēʾṯānî mibbeṭen — “why then did you bring me out of the womb?” The birth-curse and wish-never-to-have-been pattern echoes Job’s opening speech in chapter 3 and connects to Jeremiah’s lament (Jer 20:14-18). The question “why bring me out of the womb?” is not suicidal but theodical — if this is what divine care looks like, the initial act of bringing to life appears to have been cruelty rather than kindness.
10.19: kaʾᵃšer lōʾ-hāyîṯî ʾihyeh mibbeṭen laqqeḇer ʾûḇāl — “I should never have existed; I should have been carried straight from the womb to the grave.” The theological coherence of womb-to-grave without intermediate life — bypassing the suffering — is the impossible wish that echoes Ecclesiastes’s stillborn as better off than the living (Eccl 6:3-5). The OT allows lament to reach these extremes; Job’s hyperbole is authentic suffering speech, not theological confession.
10.20: hălōʾ-miʿaṭ yāmay yāḥᵃḏāl wᵉšîṯ mimmennî wᵉʾaḇliḡāh mᵉʿāṭ — “are my days not few? Cease — leave me alone, that I may have a little relief before I go.” ʾAḇliḡāh mᵉʿāṭ (a little brightness/relief) — the verb bālag means to shine/gleam; the request for a brief brightening before death is the minimum request, not a demand for restoration. The mᵉʿāṭ (a little) acknowledges the terminal expectation.
10.21: bᵉṭerem ʾēlēḵ wᵉlōʾ ʾāšûḇ ʾel-ʾereṣ ḥōšeḵ wᵉṣalmāweṯ — “to the land of darkness and the shadow of death, from which there is no return.” Ṣalmāweṯ (shadow of death) — a compound of ṣēl (shadow) and māweṯ (death); used in Ps 23:4 (“though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death”), Ps 107:10, and Isa 9:2 (“the people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death a light has dawned”). The darkness that Job anticipates entering is the darkness from which Isaiah promises a great light emerges.
10.22: ʾereṣ ʿêpāṯāh kaʾᵃpēlāh ṣalmāweṯ wᵉlōʾ sᵉḏārîm wattōpiaʿ kaʾōpel — “a land of utter gloom like thick darkness, of the shadow of death and disorder, where even the light is like darkness.” The description of Sheol as a place of disorder (lōʾ sᵉḏārîm — without order/arrangements) and inverted light (light there is like darkness) echoes the pre-creation chaos of Gen 1:2 (formless and void, darkness over the deep). Job’s Sheol is a return to uncreation — the undoing of the cosmic order God established.

11.1: Zophar the Naamathite is the least developed of the three Friends biographically; Naamah may be in northwestern Arabia (cf. Josh 15:41) or is unidentified. His is the shortest first speech (20 verses) and the shortest second speech (20:29); he alone has no third speech in the Masoretic text. His style is the most blunt and accusatory of the three.
11.2: hărōḇ dᵉḇārîm lōʾ yēʿāneh wᵉʾîm-śᵉpāṯayim yiṣdaq — “should this flood of words go unanswered? Should a man of endless talk be counted righteous?” Rōḇ dᵉḇārîm (multitude of words) — the critique of verbosity as a moral indicator; cf. Eccl 5:2-3 (let your words be few; many words come with dreams and folly). The sarcasm is that quantity of speech is being conflated with quality of argument: Zophar dismisses Job’s words by volume, not content.
11.3: baddêḵā mᵉtîm yaḥᵃrîšû wattilaʿ wᵉʾên maklîm — “shall your boasting silence everyone? When you scoff, will no one rebuke you?” Baddêḵā (your boastings/empty claims) — the plural of baḏ (linen/empty boast) — the Friends are silenced not because they lack arguments but because Job’s lament has overpowered their formulaic responses. Zophar frames this as a deficit: someone should be speaking back against Job’s scoffing.
11.4: kî tōmar zak leqqāḥî wᵉḇar hāyîṯî bᵉʿêneḵā — “for you have said, ‘My doctrine is right and I am clean in God’s sight.’” Leqqāḥî (my doctrine/reception) — from lāqaḥ (to take/receive); the word implies received teaching, traditional doctrine accepted and carried. Zophar puts in Job’s mouth a doctrinal claim Job has not made in these terms; Job has claimed innocence of behavior, not doctrinal infallibility. This misrepresentation is part of the Friends’ pattern.
11.5: wᵉʾûlam mî-yittēn ʾĕlôhah dabbēr wᵉyipṯaḥ śᵉpāṯāyw ʿimmāḵ — “if only God would speak and open his lips to answer you.” The irony is enormous: Zophar wishes God would speak, apparently confident that God would vindicate his (Zophar’s) position; God will speak (chapters 38-41), but in vindication of Job, not of Zophar. The Friends’ call for divine intervention backfires.
11.6: wᵉyaggēḏ lᵉḵā taʿᵃlumôṯ ḥokmāh kî-kiplayim lᵉṯûšiyyāh — “and reveal to you the secrets of wisdom — for wisdom has more facets than you know.” Kiplayim lᵉṯûšiyyāh (double in sound wisdom) — kiplāyim (twofold/double) and tûšiyyāh (sound wisdom/abiding success/efficacy). Tûšiyyāh is a wisdom-technical term (Prov 2:7; 3:21; 8:14; Job 5:12; 12:16; 26:3) suggesting practical effectiveness that accomplishes its purpose. Zophar is saying: God has twice as much wisdom as you can see — implying Job’s suffering reveals a double portion of hidden divine wisdom-in-judgment that Job cannot access.
11.7: hăḥēqer ʾĕlôhah timṣāʾ ʾim ʾad-takkîṯ šadday timṣāʾ — “can you search out the deep things of God? Can you find the limit of the Almighty?” Ḥēqer (searching, limit) — “unsearchable” in the NT (anexeraunētos, Rom 11:33) — Zophar uses the vocabulary of divine incomprehensibility correctly but with the wrong pastoral application: the unsearchability of God should produce humility in the sufferer, not accusations of the sufferer’s guilt.
11.8: gāḇhê šāmayim māh-tipʿāl ʿᵃmuqqāh mišᵉʾôl māh-tēḏāʿ — “it is higher than the heavens — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?” The fourfold spatial description — higher than heaven, deeper than Sheol, longer than earth, broader than sea — is a cosmological mapping of divine incomprehensibility using all four dimensions: vertical up/down and horizontal. The quadrant imagery echoes Isa 40:12 (measuring the waters in the hollow of the hand).
11.9: ʾᵃrukāh mēʾereṣ midāḥ ûrᵉḥāḇāh min-yām — “its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.” The horizontal dimensions complete the cosmic mapping. The accumulation of spatial impossibilities — God’s wisdom exceeds every human framework — is the same argument the whirlwind speech makes, but where Zophar uses it as accusation (you can’t know, therefore submit to punishment), YHWH uses it as invitation to awe and trust.
11.10: ʾim-yᵉḥᵃlōp wᵉyaśgîr wᵉyaqhîl mî yᵉšîḇennû — “if he sweeps through, imprisons, and convenes a court — who can stop him?” Three swift verbs of divine judicial action: yᵉḥᵃlōp (sweep through/pass by — same root as “pass by” in 9:11), yaśgîr (shut in/imprison), yaqhîl (convene an assembly/court). The swift judicial process allows no appeal; the rhetorical force parallels what Job said in 9:12 (“if he snatches away, who can stop him?”). Zophar and Job are using the same divine power-descriptions, but with different theological conclusions.
11.11: kî-hûʾ yēḏaʿ mᵉṯê-šāwᵉ — “for he knows worthless people.” Mᵉṯê-šāwᵉ (men of vanity/worthlessness) — šāwʾ carries the sense of deception, emptiness, falsehood; used in the third commandment (“do not take the name of the LORD in vain” — laššāwʾ). Zophar is effectively classifying Job in this category without saying it directly — the whole speech is an extended accusation with the final term withheld.
11.12: wᵉʾîš nāḇôḇ yillāḇēḇ wᵉʿayir pereʾ ʾāḏām yiwwālēḏ — “a hollow-headed man will become wise when a wild donkey gives birth to a human being.” Nāḇôḇ (hollow/empty) — the word for an empty vessel or reed; the hollow man becoming wise when a wild donkey gives birth to a human is an extreme adynaton (impossibility idiom) — the structural equivalent of “when pigs fly.” Zophar is saying Job’s becoming wise is as likely as this natural impossibility.
11.13: ʾim-ʾattāh hᵉḵînôṯā libbᵉḵā ûpāraśtā ʾēlāyw kappêḵā — “if you would prepare your heart and stretch out your hands toward him.” Hᵉḵînôṯā libbᵉḵā (prepare/establish your heart) — kûn in the hiphil means to make firm, establish, fix rightly; cf. Ps 78:8 (their hearts not established/firm with God) and 1 Sam 7:3 (prepare your hearts for the LORD). The stretching of hands (pāraś kappayim) is the gesture of prayer; Zophar’s conditional instruction is genuinely pious — but applied to an innocent man.
11.14: ʾim-ʾāwen bᵉyāḏᵉḵā harḥîqēhû wᵉʾal-tašken bᵉʾohāleḵā ʿawlāh — “if there is sin in your hand, remove it far away, and let wickedness have no place in your home.” Harḥîqēhû (remove it far) — the same idiom appears in Ps 103:12 (as far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions). Zophar’s advice is theologically correct but morally catastrophic: Job’s home has already been destroyed, his children dead; commanding him to remove wickedness from his household is a second wound.
11.15: kî-ʾāz tissāʾ pāneḵā mimmûm wᵉhāyîṯā môṣāq wᵉlōʾ ṯîrāʾ — “then surely you will lift up your face without shame; you will stand firm and have no fear.” The promise of restored dignity (lifting the face without shame) echoes the promise of 22:26 (Eliphaz) and the actual restoration in 42:8-10. The conditional “if you repent” will be fulfilled — but not because of Zophar’s diagnosis; it will be fulfilled because YHWH restores Job after the whirlwind, not after Zophar’s prescribed repentance.
11.16: kî-ʾattāh ʿāmāl tiškaḥ kᵉmayim ʿāḇrû tizkōr — “for you will forget your suffering; you will remember it only as water that has long since flowed on.” The water-memory image — suffering as rivers that pass and leave no trace — is a comforting image of selective forgetting; cf. Isa 65:16 (the former troubles will be forgotten). The promise of future forgetfulness will be partially fulfilled: 42:10-17 shows restoration, though the deaths of Job’s first children are never unfelt.
11.17: ûmissohᵃrayim yāqûm ḥeled ṯᵉʿupāh kaḇōqer yihyeh — “your life will shine brighter than noon; even your darkest time will be like dawn.” Light/darkness reversal as the metaphor for restoration from suffering; the darkest time becoming dawn inverts the Amos 5:20 judgment image (the day of the LORD: darkness not light, gloom not dawn). The restoration-as-light vocabulary will be fulfilled but through a very different path than Zophar envisions.
11.18: wᵉḇāṭaḥtā kî-yēš tiqwāh wᵉḥāpâartā lāḇeṭaḥ tiškaḇ — “you will have confidence, because there is hope; you will look around you and rest in security.” Yēš tiqwāh (there is hope) — the word tiqwāh appears again; Zophar is offering hope to the one whom he has accused of hopelessness. The pastoral form is correct; the theological premise (you are being punished for sin) is not.
11.19: wᵉrāḇaṣtā wᵉʾên maḥᵃrîḏ ûḥillû pāneḵā rabbîm — “you will lie down, and no one will make you afraid; many will come seeking your goodwill.” The reversal from isolation (Job has been abandoned by kinsmen and friends, 19:13-19) to being sought out is the restoration image. Ḥillû pāneḵā (seek your favor/caress your face) — a phrase for supplication; the restored Job will be the one to whom others come for favor and intercession, which is exactly what happens in 42:8-9 when the Friends must ask Job to pray for them.
11.20: wᵉʿênê rᵉšāʿîm tiklenāh wᵉmānôs ʾāḇaḏ minnêhem wᵉtiqwāṯām mappāḥ-nāpeš — “but the eyes of the wicked will fail; all means of escape will be closed to them, and their only hope is to breathe their last.” The climactic contrast — Job’s restored hope versus the wicked’s hopeless tiqwāh. Mappāḥ-nāpeš (the breathing out of the soul/the gasp of the dying) — the most bitter possible content of hope: the wicked’s only hope is to exhale their last breath. The grim wordplay on tiqwāh as both legitimate hope (vv.18) and the mere hope of death frames the chapter’s final irony.

20.1: wayyaʿan ṣôp̄ar hannaʿᵃmāṯî wayyōʾmar: 'Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said.' Zophar's second and final speech (ch.20) is his response to Job's great Redeemer declaration (19:25-27). His reply does not engage Job's vision of vindication; instead he reaches past it to reassert the classical retribution doctrine with escalating rhetorical ferocity. The deliberate non-engagement with Job's confession is revealing: the friends are unable to process a claim that exceeds their theological framework.
20.2: lāḵēn śēʿîpāy yᵉšîḇûnî ûḇaʿăḇûr ḥûšî bî: 'Therefore my thoughts push me to answer; I feel urgency within me.' Śāʿap̄ (thoughts that agitate, troubled reflections — from śāʿap̄ = to split, divide) — Zophar's inner turmoil drives the speech. His urgency is presented as intellectual compulsion rather than compassionate response to Job's suffering. The contrast with Job's anguished speech (ch.19) is sharp: where Job's emotion was grief and longing, Zophar's is irritated argument.
20.3: mûsar kelimmaṯî ʾešmaʿ wᵉrûaḥ mibbinatî yaʿănênî: 'I hear a reproof that dishonors me, and a spirit from beyond my understanding compels me.' Kelimmāh (dishonor, shame, humiliation) — Zophar feels personally shamed by Job's arguments. Rûaḥ mibbinatî: 'a spirit from beyond/out of my understanding' — Zophar claims to be moved by something beyond his normal rational process, adding prophetic authority to his speech. Yet the content of his speech is entirely conventional; the claim of spiritual compulsion underscores how readily such claims are made in the defense of traditional theology.
20.4: hă-zōʾt yāḏaʿtā minnî-ʿad minnî śîm ʾādām ʿălê-ʾāreṣ: 'Do you not know this from of old, since man was placed upon the earth?' Zophar opens his argument by appealing to tradition — the appeal to antiquity (minnî-ʿad = from of old, from then on) is the standard wisdom-school move. The appeal to what 'everyone has always known' insulates the argument from critical examination. Job has already challenged this move (12:3, 9-10) by pointing to the observable world contradicting the traditional teaching.
20.5: kî rinnāt rᵉšāʿîm miqārôḇ wᵉśimḥat ḥānēp̄ ʿăḏê-rāgaʿ: 'That the triumph of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless lasts but a moment.' Rinnāh (triumphal shout, ringing cry of joy) — the emotional peak of the wicked's prosperity. ʿĂḏê-rāgaʿ: 'until a moment' — the brevity is emphatic. Zophar's assertion about the wicked's brief prosperity is the central claim of his speech; Job will systematically refute it in ch.21 by pointing to the observable fact that the wicked often live long and prosper to old age.
20.6: ʾim-yaʿăleh laššāmayim śiʾô wᵉrōʾšô lāʿāḇ yaggiʿa: 'Though his greatness mounts to the heavens and his head reaches the clouds.' The imagery of sky-high ambition echoes the tower of Babel (Gen 11:4) and prophetic mockery of Babylon (Isa 14:13-14). The wicked man's vertical ambition — his claim to heaven-touching greatness — is the setup for the fall in v.7. The contrast between v.6 (sky-height) and v.7 (dung) is deliberately grotesque: the height of human self-assertion collapses into the most degraded imagery.
20.7: kᵉgāllô lānetsaḥ yōʾḇēḏ rōʾāyw yōʾmᵉrû ʾayyô: 'He will perish forever like his own dung; those who saw him will say, “Where is he?”' Gāllāh (his dung, his own excrement) — one of the Bible's most blunt uses of scatological language to illustrate the worthlessness of the wicked's legacy. The fall from cloud-height (v.6) to dung (v.7) is the sharpest possible contrast. The rhetorical question ʾayyô (where is he?) echoes the divine question to Cain (Gen 4:9) and becomes a funeral formula: what once seemed great has vanished without a trace.
20.8: kᵉḥălôm yāʿûp̄ wᵉlōʾ yimṣāʾûhû wayyûḏad kᵉḥezyôn lāylāh: 'He flies away like a dream and cannot be found; he is chased away like a vision of the night.' The dream/night-vision double comparison emphasizes insubstantiality: both are experiences of reality that dissolve upon waking. Ḥalôm (dream) and ḥezyôn lāylāh (vision of the night) are related but distinct terms. The wicked man's greatness is phenomenological — subjectively experienced as real but ontologically empty, gone when the dawn of judgment comes.
20.9: ʿayin šᵉzāp̄athu wᵉlōʾ-tôsîp̄ wᵉlōʾ-ʿôḏ tᵉšûrennû māqômô: 'The eye that saw him will see him no more; his own place will no longer recognize him.' The personification of the wicked man's place (māqôm) as having memory — the place that knew him will no longer recognize him — adds cosmic weight. The language echoes the prophetic passages where the land mourns the absence of the departed wicked (Isa 14:16-17) and anticipates Revelation's erasure of the wicked from the book of life.
20.10: bānāyw yᵉraṣṣû ḏallîm wᵉyāḏāyw tāšêṣnāh ʾônô: 'His children will seek favor from the poor, and his own hands will give back their wealth.' The reversal extends to the next generation: the children of the formerly prosperous wicked are reduced to begging from the poor — the exact people their father oppressed. Tāšêṣnāh ʾônô: 'his hands will restore/give back his wealth' — the restitution principle: ill-gotten gain is returned, whether by compulsion or divine judgment. The children of the wicked inherit not wealth but obligation.
20.11: ʿăṣāmōṯāyw mālaʾû ʿălûmôṯāyw wᵉʿimmô ʿal-ʿāp̄ār tiškāḇ: 'His bones are full of the sins of his youth; they will lie down with him in the dust.' ʿĂlûmôt (youthful vigor, the vitality of youth) — his bones are saturated with the stored-up sin-energy of his youth; when he dies, this accumulated sin lies down with him. The image presents sin as a physical substance that inhabits the body — an almost material conception of moral corruption. The dust-bed destination (ʿāp̄ār) connects to the creation-and-death framework of Gen 3:19.
20.12: ʾim-tāʾēraḇ bᵉp̄îw rāʿāh yaḵᵉḥîḏennāh taḥat lᵉšônô: 'Though evil is sweet in his mouth and he hides it under his tongue.' The sweetness-of-evil imagery has wide resonance: Prov 9:17 ('stolen water is sweet'), Ezek 3:3 (the scroll sweet in the prophet's mouth). The hiding under the tongue is a culinary metaphor — savoring, like wine held under the tongue before swallowing. The pleasure precedes the poison. Tāʾab (to be sweet) connects to the enticement of sin that Proverbs consistently addresses: evil presents itself as desirable before revealing its destructive nature.
20.13: yaḥmōl ʿāleyhā wᵉlōʾ yaʿazᵉḇennāh waymannaʿennāh bᵉṯôḵ ḥikkô: 'Though he spares it and will not let it go, but keeps it lodged in his palate.' The extended oral-retention metaphor (v.12-14) presents the wicked man as unable to release what he knows will destroy him — gripping pleasurable evil in his mouth even as it turns. Ḥēḵ (palate, roof of the mouth) — the most sensory part of tasting. The man is so habituated to the sweetness of evil that he cannot spit it out even when its venom is revealed. The structural parallel to addiction is striking.
20.14: laḥmô bᵉmēʿāyw nehpāḵ mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm bᵉqirbô: 'Yet his food turns in his stomach; it becomes cobra venom (rōʾš, mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm) inside him.' Rōʾš (gall, bitter herb, venom) and mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm (venom of cobras) — cobra poison was one of the most feared in the ANE. The reversal of sweetness to venom inside the body is Zophar's central biological metaphor for retribution: what was consumed as pleasure becomes the poison that destroys from within. This is moral physiology — the nature of sinful pleasure is self-destructive at a fundamental level.
20.15: ḥayil bālaʿ wayyᵉqîʾennû mibbiṭnô yôrišennû ʾēl: 'He swallowed wealth down and will vomit it up again; God will expel it from his belly.' Bālaʿ (swallow, engulf) — a violent consuming. Qîʾ (vomit up) is rare and deliberately crude. God is the active agent of the expulsion: what was consumed as wealth is ejected by divine force. The image is the inverse of receiving a divine gift: God gives by provision, but what is seized unjustly God takes back by extraction. The stomach as the seat of greed that cannot retain its ill-gotten contents.
20.16: rōʾš-pᵉtānîm yinqāq waharg-lô leššôn ʾep̄ʿeh: 'He will suck the venom of cobras; the tongue of the viper will kill him.' The cobra imagery continues: what he swallowed as food turns out to be venom; what he sucks seeking nourishment is poison. The peten (asp/cobra) and ʾep̄ʿeh (viper) are two distinct venomous snakes. The tongue of the viper — the instrument of both deception and injection — is the agent of his death. The serpent imagery connects to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:1-5) where serpentine deception first presented evil as sweet and desirable.
20.17: ʾal-yirʾeh bipelaggôt nᵉhārôt naḥălê devāš waḥemʾāh: 'He will not look upon the rivers, the flowing channels, the streams of honey and butter.' The positive imagery of abundance — honey, butter, flowing rivers — is denied to the wicked. This is a promised-land refusal: the wicked man is excluded from the eretz zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûḏᵉḇaš (land flowing with milk and honey, Exod 3:8) that represents YHWH's provision. What the righteous inherit, the wicked are barred from — not by geography but by the moral logic of their own choices.
20.18: mᵉšîḇ yāgāʿ wᵉlōʾ yiḇlāʿ ḵᵉḥêl tᵉmûrāṯô wᵉlōʾ yaʿălōs: 'He must restore the fruits of his labor without consuming them; his restitution will be according to his wealth, and he shall not enjoy it.' The principle of šāḇ (restoring/returning) — what he earned by exploitation he must give back. The commercial vocabulary (yāgāʿ = labor, toil; tᵉmûrāh = exchange value, barter price) makes this a legal financial restitution. The double negative pleasure-denial: he returns what he earned and cannot even enjoy the process of exchange.
20.19: kî-rāṣaṣ ʿāzaḇ dallîm bêt gāzal wᵉlōʾ yiḇnēhû: 'For he oppressed and abandoned the poor; he seized a house he did not build.' The ethical charge that grounds the punishment: rāṣaṣ (to crush, oppress, break) the poor — the word used in Isa 58:6 ('untie every yoke') and Luke 4:18 ('to set the oppressed free' — rᵉṣûṣîm). He seized (gāzal = take by force, rob) a house he did not build — the Naboth's vineyard dynamic (1 Kgs 21) where the powerful seize what belongs to the vulnerable. The punishment (vv.20-28) is proportioned to the offense.
20.20: kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ šāleū bᵉbiṭnô bᵉḥămûḏô lōʾ yimmalēṭ: 'He will not feel peace (šālem) within; he will retain nothing of what he desired.' Šālem (peace, completeness, wholeness — shalom root) within his belly: the man who seized prosperity by violence cannot experience the inner wholeness that is the organic result of righteous living. Ḥāmaḏ (desire, covet) — the verb of the tenth commandment (Exod 20:17; Deut 5:21). Coveting generates restlessness, not satisfaction: the very desire-structure that drove the acquisition undermines the enjoyment of it.
20.21: ʾên-śārîḏ lᵉʾoḵlô ʿal-kēn lōʾ-yāḥîl ṭûḇô: 'Nothing is left of his food; therefore no one will inherit his prosperity.' Śārîḏ (survivor, remnant) — the word used for the remnant of Israel (Isa 1:9; 10:20; Joel 2:32). When nothing survives of what he consumed, there is no legacy. The theological irony: the man who consumed and hoarded has no remainder — no remnant of food, no remnant of children to inherit. The family-legacy termination is Zophar's most pointed threat, striking at the ANE's deepest investment: perpetuation through heirs.
20.22: bimᵉlōʾ śipqô yēṣar lô kol-yāḏ ʿāmēl tāḇôʾ-ʿālāyw: 'In the fullness of his plenty he will be in distress; every hand of the suffering will come against him.' The paradox: his maximum prosperity coincides with maximum distress. Śipeq (plenty, sufficiency, fullness) — he achieves abundance; at that exact moment (yēṣar lô = he will be pressed, in straits) the distress arrives. The phrase reverses the normal expectation: fullness brings comfort, not anguish. The arriving hands of the ʿāmēl (those who toil/suffer) are the oppressed he once crushed, now rising against him.
20.23: yᵉhî lᵉmalēʾ biṭnô yᵉšallaḥ-bô ḥărôn ʾappô wayyamṭēr ʿālāyw bilᵉḥûmô: 'When he is set to fill his belly, God will hurl his fierce anger upon him and rain it down on him as he eats.' Šālaḥ ḥărôn ʾap̄ (send/hurl fierce anger) — divine wrath as a thrown weapon. Māṭar (to rain) — used of the Sodom judgment (Gen 19:24: 'YHWH rained sulfur and fire') and the plagues (Exod 9:18). The eating imagery is deliberate: the man who seized others' food will find that while eating, divine judgment falls on him.
20.24: yibrāḥ minnešeq bārzel tiḥᵉlᵉp̄ennû qešeṯ nᵉḥûšāh: 'He flees from the iron weapon, and the bronze bow strikes him through.' The double military imagery of flight and pursuit: even fleeing the iron weapon (neše) the man is struck by the bronze bow (qešeṯ nᵉḥûšāh). Inescapability of divine judgment is the theme — there is no direction to flee that avoids the sentence. The combination of two weapon-types (iron = short-range; bronze bow = long-range) suggests omnidirectional pursuit with no gap.
20.25: šālaph wayyēṣēʾ miggēwāh ûḇārāq mimmᵉrārāṯô yahalōk ʿālāyw ʾêmîm: 'The arrow is drawn; it comes out through his body; the gleaming blade exits through his gall; terrors (ʾêmîm) come upon him.' Bārāq (lightning bolt, gleaming metal) — the sword blade gleaming as it exits is a vivid martial image. Mᵉrōrāh (gall, bile) — the arrow or sword passes through the organ of bitterness; as the blade exits it draws out the gall that symbolizes his bitter accumulated evil. ʾÊmîm (terrors) — the same word used in 9:34 for Job's fear of God; here divine terrors accompany the physical death.
20.26: kōl-ḥōšeḵ ṣāpûn liṣᵉpûnāyw tᵉʾaḵlēhû ʾēš lōʾ-nuppāḥ yērāʿ śārîḏ bᵉʾohᵃlô: 'All darkness is stored up for his treasures; a fire not fanned by wind will consume him; disaster will destroy what remains in his tent.' Ḥōšeḵ ṣāpûn liṣᵉpûnāyw: 'darkness stored up for his stored-up things' — the wordplay (ṣāpûn twice) equates his treasure-hoarding with the darkness stored for his destruction: he stored, and darkness is stored for him. ʾĒš lōʾ-nuppāḥ (fire not blown upon) — spontaneous, self-sustaining divine fire, not human-kindled; the same quality as the fire of Sodom (Gen 19:24).
20.27: yᵉgallû šāmayim ʿăwônô wᵉʾereṣ miṯqōmāmāh lô: 'The heavens will expose his guilt, and the earth will rise up against him.' The cosmic witness against the wicked: heaven above and earth below both testify to his iniquity. Gālāh (to expose, uncover, reveal) is the verb of the prophetic disclosure of hidden things (Amos 3:7: 'he reveals his secrets to his servants'). The earth 'rising' against him (qûm lô) personifies creation as judge — a motif seen in Isa 1:2 (heaven and earth as YHWH's witnesses) and Rev 20:11 (the earth and heaven fleeing from the judgment throne).
20.28: yiggāl yᵉḇûl bêṯô niggārôṯ bᵉyôm ʾappô: 'The produce of his house will be carried away; his possessions will pour away in the day of his anger.' Nāgar (to flow, pour out) — liquid imagery for total dispersal of wealth: like water, it cannot be held. Yôm ʾappô (the day of his anger) — ambiguous pronoun: could be God's anger or the wicked man's anger. Most likely God's; 'the day of wrath' is standard eschatological-judgment language (Zeph 1:15; Prov 11:4). The house that symbolized his wealth and dynasty is emptied out.
20.29: zeh ḥēleq-ʾādām rāšāʿ mēʾĕlōhîm wᵉnaḥălat ʾimrô mēʾēl: 'This is the portion (ḥēleq) of the wicked man from God — the heritage (naḥălāh) decreed for him by God.' The summary verdict. Ḥēleq (portion, lot, assigned share) and naḥălāh (inheritance, heritage) are both used for Israel's covenantal inheritance of the land; here applied to the wicked man's 'inheritance' of judgment. The formulaic ending (mēʾēl = from God) makes the retribution explicitly theological: this is not mere natural consequence but divine assignment. Job will contest this portrait in ch. 21 by pointing to observable reality.

21.1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ wayyōʾmar: 'But Job answered and said.' Job's reply in ch. 21 is his most sustained and direct confrontation with the friends' retribution theology. It is also his most intellectually rigorous speech: he does not appeal to his own innocence (that was ch. 16-17, 19) but to observable reality — the empirical observation that the wicked frequently prosper and die peacefully. The chapter is a systematic dismantling of Zophar's thesis in ch. 20.
21.2: šimᵉʿû šāmôaʿ millatî wᵉhî tᵉhî tanḥûmōṯêḵem: 'Listen carefully to what I say; let this be your consolation.' Šāmôaʿ šāmaʿ (infinitive absolute + finite verb = listen carefully, truly listen) — the imperative doubled for emphasis. The sardonic offer: 'let my listening be your consolation' reverses the normal consolation formula. The friends came to console Job (2:11); he asks instead that they accept consolation by listening to his refutation of their theology. The consolation they owe him they will receive by being corrected.
21.3: śᵉʾûnî wᵉʾānōḵî ʾăḏabbēr wᵉʾaḥar ḏabbᵉrî talʿîg: 'Bear with me so I may speak; and after I have spoken, mock away.' Śāʾ (to lift, bear, endure) — 'endure me, carry me through this speech.' Job asks for the minimal courtesy of an uninterrupted hearing before the mockery begins. The offer to let them mock afterward has a brittle dignity: he does not expect understanding, only silence long enough to make the argument. The Hebrew word for 'mock' (lāʿag) is the same used of enemies mocking in the Psalms (Ps 22:7; 44:13).
21.4: hăʾānōḵî lᵉʾādām śîḥî wᵉʾim-maddûaʿ lōʾ-tiṣr rûḥî: 'As for me, is my complaint directed to man? Why then should I not be impatient?' Śîaḥ (complaint, meditation, musing) — Job distinguishes the addressee of his complaint: it is directed to God (ʾĕlôah), not to humans. If God is his interlocutor and God is silent, his impatience (qāṣar rûaḥ = short of breath/spirit, frustrated, impatient) is understandable. The distinction matters: his complaint is a theological dispute, not a social one, and therefore does not require the friends' approval or resolution.
21.5: pᵉnû ʾēlay wᵉhiššāmmû wᵉśîmû yaḏ ʿal-peh: 'Look at me and be appalled; lay your hand over your mouth.' Šāmam (to be appalled, devastated, deserted) — the same root used of the desolated temple (Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11) and waste land. Job's condition is so extreme that visual encounter with it should produce the physical gesture of stunned silence: śîm yaḏ ʿal-peh (lay the hand over the mouth) is the gesture of awed, speechless respect (Job 29:9; 40:4; Mic 7:16). Job is asking the friends to consider that what they are seeing should silence their comfortable theodicy.
21.6: wᵉʾim-zāḵarᵉtî wᵉniḇhāltî ûʾāḥaz bᵉśārî palāṣûṯ: 'Even when I think of it I am troubled; shuddering seizes my flesh.' Nāḇah (niphal = to be terrified, overwhelmed) and pālaṣ (shuddering, shivering with horror — a hapax). The physical response (shuddering flesh) to intellectual reflection on his question anticipates the divine speech from the whirlwind (chs. 38-41) where the appropriate human response to God's question-fronted reality is precisely this awe. Job is epistemically honest: the question he is about to ask is one that genuinely disturbs him, not a rhetorical device.
21.7: maddûaʿ rᵉšāʿîm yiḥyû ʿāṯᵉqû gam-gāḇᵉrû ḥāyil: 'Why do the wicked go on living, grow old, and even grow mighty in power?' The central theodicy question that runs through the Psalms of Asaph (Ps 73:3-12), Jeremiah's complaints (Jer 12:1-2), Habakkuk (Hab 1:2-4), and Ecclesiastes (Eccl 7:15; 8:14). Yiḥyû (they live, they go on living), ʿāṯaq (to grow old — the opposite of dying young), gāḇar ḥāyil (grow mighty in strength) — the triple accumulation describes not just survival but increasing prosperity. This directly contradicts Zophar's claim in 20:5 that 'the triumph of the wicked is short.'
21.8: zarʿām nāḵôn lip̄nêhem ʿimmām ûṣeʾĕṣāʾêhem lᵉʿênêhem: 'Their children are established around them, and their offspring before their eyes.' In the ANE theological framework, prosperous descendants were the primary sign of divine favor. The wicked man sees his children and grandchildren growing up in front of him — the most concrete embodiment of blessing and continuity. This too directly contradicts the standard retribution formula which should see the wicked man's family cut off (cf. Ps 37:28; Prov 14:11).
21.9: bāttêhem šālôm mippāḥad wᵉlōʾ šêḇeṭ ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem: 'Their houses are safe from fear; the rod of God is not upon them.' Šālôm mippāḥad (peace from fear) — the kind of security promised to the righteous (Prov 1:33; Ps 91:5) is actually enjoyed by the wicked. Šēḇeṭ ʾĕlôah (the rod/staff of God) — the instrument of discipline and judgment is conspicuously absent from the wicked man's household. The observation is theologically scandalous: the God who should be punishing them is apparently protecting them instead.
21.10: šôrô ʿibbār wᵉlōʾ yiḡʿāl pārāṯô pālᵉṭāh wᵉlōʾ tᵉšakkēl: 'Their bull breeds without fail; their cow calves without miscarriage.' Agricultural fertility and successful livestock reproduction were direct signs of covenant blessing (Deut 28:4; 28:11). The wicked man's animals breed reliably — the same blessing promised to obedient Israel is enjoyed by those who refuse God (v.14-15). The biological evidence of divine favor is distributed without apparent moral sorting.
21.11: yᵉšallᵉḥû kᵉṣōʾn ʿᵉwîlêhem wᵉyaldêhem yᵉraqᵉḏûn: 'Their little ones run out like a flock, and their children dance.' The festive domestic image of children and dancing is the biblical picture of a community at peace and flourishing (Jer 31:13; Zech 8:5; Ps 30:11). The wicked man's household is characterized by exactly the visible joy and abundance that his theology should have denied him. The dancing children are a pointed counter-image to the suffering Job's household (ch. 1-2) where real piety produced disaster.
21.12: yiśśᵉʾû ṯōp̄ wᵉḵinnôr wayyiśmᵉḥû lᵉqôl ʿûḡāḇ: 'They take up timbrel and harp and rejoice at the sound of the flute.' Three instruments: tōp̄ (timbrel/tambourine), kinnôr (lyre/harp), ʿûḡāḇ (a wind instrument, possibly pipes or flute). The same instruments associated with worship and celebration (Ps 150:3-5; 1 Sam 10:5) are here in the hands of those who reject God (v.14). The irony is deliberate: the wicked celebrate with the same music Israel uses for divine praise, and their celebration is not interrupted by divine judgment.
21.13: yᵉḵallû ḇaṭṭôḇ yᵉmêhem ûḇeregaʿ šᵉʾôl yēḥāṯû: 'They spend their days in prosperity and go down to Sheol peacefully (bᵉrageaʿ).' Bālāh (to wear out, finish off, exhaust) is used of clothes wearing out (Deut 8:4; 29:5; Josh 9:13) and of time being consumed; here their days are 'worn out' in prosperity — fully spent in good. Bᵉrageaʿ šᵉʾôl yēḥāṯû: 'in a moment they go down to Sheol' — a peaceful, sudden death in old age at the peak of prosperity, not the prolonged dying of the punished wicked that Zophar described. The contrast with Job's prolonged suffering is implicit and pointed.
21.14: wayyōʾmᵉrû lāʾēl sûr mimmennû wᵉḏaʿat dᵉrāḵeḵā lōʾ ḥāp̄āṣnû: 'Yet they say to God, “Leave us alone; we do not want to know your ways.”' Job quotes the wicked man's own words — their explicit rejection of God. Sûr mimmennû (depart from us) echoes Israel's wilderness rebellion and the prophetic accusation that the people have turned away from YHWH (Isa 30:11; Jer 2:31). The explicit rejection of divine instruction (ḏaʿat dᵉrāḵeḵā) while enjoying full material prosperity is the datum that destroys simple retribution theology: prosperity is not correlated with piety in observed experience.
21.15: mah-šaddāy kî naʿăḇᵉḏennû ûmah-nôʿîl kî nip̄gᵉʿ-bô: 'Who is the Almighty that we should serve him? What do we gain by praying to him?' The wicked man's explicit calculus: God is worth serving only if the service produces material benefit. Yāʿal (to profit, benefit, avail) — what is the return on investment of prayer and obedience? This is the same transactional theological framework the Adversary implied in 1:9-10 ('Does Job fear God for nothing?'). The wicked man's explicit utilitarian theology is the dark mirror of the Adversary's accusation against Job's piety.
21.16: hēn lōʾ ḇᵉyādām ṭûḇām ʿăṣat rᵉšāʿîm rāḥᵉqāh mimmennî: 'Indeed, their prosperity is not in their own hand — the counsel of the wicked is far from me.' Job makes an important qualification: the wicked man's prosperity is not self-generated but is within God's domain. This prevents a deistic conclusion (the wicked prosper because God is absent). Job is insisting that divine sovereignty extends to the wicked's prosperity — God allows it — while simultaneously distancing himself from the wicked man's theology (rāḥᵉqāh mimmennî = I keep far from me).
21.17: kammāh nēr-rᵉšāʿîm yiḏʿāḵ wᵉyāḇōʾ ʿălêhem ʾêḏām ḥᵉḇālîm bᵉʾappô yᵉḥallēq: 'How often is the lamp of the wicked actually extinguished? How often does disaster come upon them? God distributes pain in his anger.' Nēr rᵉšāʿîm (lamp of the wicked) — the friends promised this lamp would be put out (18:5-6), but Job asks: how often does this actually happen? The rhetorical question (kammāh = how many times?) implies: rarely, or less often than the friends claim. The empirical challenge: observable experience contradicts the confident assertion of retribution theology.
21.18: yihyû kᵉteḇen lip̄nê-rûaḥ ûkᵉmōṣ gānāḇathu sûp̄āh: 'They are as straw before the wind, and chaff swept away by the tempest.' Job does not deny that the wicked sometimes face judgment; his challenge is the frequency and consistency that the friends assert. The images of straw/wind and chaff/storm are the standard biblical metaphors for the wicked's fate (Ps 1:4; Isa 17:13; Hos 13:3). Job acknowledges these happen — but how often? The point is the gap between theological assertion and empirical frequency.
21.19: ʾĕlôah yiṣp̄ōn laḇānāyw ʾônô yᵉšallem ʾēlāyw wᵉyēḏāʿ: 'You say, “God stores up the father’s punishment for his children.” Let him repay the man himself and he will know it.' Job attacks a specific theodicy escape route: when the wicked man himself does not suffer, appeal is made to his children bearing the punishment (Exod 20:5; Deut 5:9). Job demands direct personal punishment: if God is just, the transgressor himself should know it, not merely his descendants. Yēḏāʿ (he will know/experience it) — the experiential knowledge that comes from direct encounter with consequences.
21.20: yirʾû ʿênāyw kîḏô wᵉmēḥămaṯ šaddāy yišteh: 'Let his own eyes see his destruction; let him drink of the wrath of the Almighty.' The demand for first-person moral experience: the transgressor's own eyes (ʿênāyw) must witness his judgment, not a surrogate's. Šāṯāh ḥămaṯ šaddāy (drink the wrath of the Almighty) — the cup of divine wrath is one of the OT's most vivid judgment images (Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15-17; Rev 14:10; 16:19). Job insists that moral accountability requires direct personal experience, not generational displacement.
21.21: kî mah-ḥep̄ṣô bᵉḇêṯô ʾaḥărāyw ûmispar ḥodāšāyw ḥuṣṣāṣû: 'For what pleasure does he take in his house after him, when the number of his months is severed?' The forward-looking question: once the man himself is dead, what does he care about his house and posterity? The logic undermines the delayed-generational-punishment argument: if the wicked man is dead, he has no experiential stake in the fate of his children. Justice requires contemporaneous consequences, not posthumous ones. Ḥāṣaṣ (to cut, divide, sever) — his months are 'cut short' in death.
21.22: hăʾēl yᵉlammēḏ-dāʿat wᵉhûʾ rāmîm yišpôṭ: 'Will anyone teach God knowledge? He is the one who judges those on high.' A rhetorical boundary-setting: the question in v.7 (why do the wicked prosper?) does not license human instruction of God about how to run his moral universe. Rāmîm (the high ones, the exalted) — possibly angels or heavenly beings; God judges those above Job's level. The acknowledgment of God's sovereignty does not resolve Job's theodicy question but prevents the question from becoming an attempt to rewrite God's governance. The tension between epistemic humility and honest complaint is sustained rather than resolved.
21.23: zeh yāmûṯ bᵉʿeṣem tummô kullô šalʾănān wᵉšāleō: 'One person dies in full vigor (bᵉʿeṣem tummô), completely at ease and secure.' ʿOṣem (bone, strength, essence) + tōm (completeness, integrity, wholeness) — 'in the very bone of his completeness'; at the apex of his strength and life-fullness. Šalʾănān wᵉšāleō (at rest and at ease) — the state of prosperous security that Ps 73:12 describes as the wicked man's condition. The observation in vv.23-26 is deliberately universal: all kinds of people die, with no apparent moral correlation to their quality of death.
21.24: ʿăṭînāyw mālᵉʾû ḥālāḇ ûmaḥ ʿaṣᵉmôṯāyw yᵉšuqqeh: 'His sides full of milk and his bones saturated with marrow.' The physical description of peak-health death: ḥālāḇ (milk/fat — used figuratively for abundant nourishment) and maḥ (marrow — the innermost richness of the bones). Both are images of abundant interior nourishment — this man dies with every biological indicator of flourishing. The grotesque health at death makes the contrast with Job (whose bones burn with fever, 30:17; whose skin clings to his bones, 19:20) all the more pointed.
21.25: wᵉzeh yāmûṯ bᵉnep̄eš mārāh wᵉlōʾ-ʾāḵal baṭṭôḇāh: 'Another person dies in bitterness of soul, having never tasted what is good.' The exact opposite life-trajectory to v.23-24: nep̄eš mārāh (bitter of soul) — the phrase used of Hannah (1 Sam 1:10) and Naomi (Ruth 1:20: 'call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt bitterly with me'). This person has suffered throughout life and has 'not eaten of the good' — never experienced the prosperity that should, according to retribution theology, reward the righteous. The pairing of vv.23-26 presents four deaths that cut across the moral categories: prosperous death, bitter death — then...
21.26: yaḥḏāw ʿal-ʿāp̄ār yiškaḇû ûrimāh tᵉḵassēh ʿălêhem: 'They lie down together in the dust, and worms cover them both.' Yaḥḏāw (together) — the most theologically loaded word in the verse. The prosperous man of v.23-24 and the bitter man of v.25 lie together, undifferentiated in death. Rimmāh (worm, maggot) covers them equally — the same image of final corruption for all flesh (Isa 14:11; 66:24; Job 17:14). The leveling of death cuts through all moral sorting: the grave receives the righteous and wicked alike without apparent distinction, which is exactly the empirical observation that makes retribution theology inadequate as a complete account of divine justice.
21.27: hēn yāḏaʿtî maḥšᵉḇôṯêḵem ûmᵉzimmôṯ ʿālay taḥmōsû: 'I know your thoughts and the schemes you wrongfully contrive against me.' Job names the interpretive move the friends are about to make: maḥăšāḇôt (plans, schemes, inner thoughts) and mᵉzimmôt (devious plans, malicious intentions — from zāmam = to plan evil). The friends will argue that Job is the wicked man of their description; Job preempts this by naming it as a wrong (tāḥmōs = to do violence to, wrong) and demonstrating that he knows their rhetorical play before they execute it.
21.28: kî tōʾmᵉrû ʾayēh bêṯ-nāḏîḇ wᵉʾayēh ʾōhel miškᵉnôṯ rᵉšāʿîm: 'For you say, “Where is the house of the noble man? Where are the dwelling places of the wicked?”' The friends' implied argument: the prosperity of the wicked is always destroyed — look, their houses are gone, their tents ruined. The rhetorical question (ʾayēh = where is it?) implies the answer is 'destroyed,' validating retribution theology. Job will rebut this with a traveler's report (v.29-31): the evidence from the roads tells a different story.
21.29: hălōʾ šᵉʾaltum ʿôḇᵉrê ḏāreḵ wᵉʾōṯōṯām lōʾ tᵉnakkērû: 'Have you not asked those who travel the roads? Do you not recognize their evidence?' Job appeals to empirical testimony — travelers who have observed the world broadly, not just within the friends' theological tradition. ʿôḇᵉrê ḏāreḵ (those who pass by on the road) — witnesses who have seen many places and many lives. ʾōṯōṯām (their signs, testimonies) — the observable evidence they can report. Job's empirical method here is striking: he appeals to experience and observation against the friends' appeal to tradition (cf. Zophar's 'Do you not know from of old...?' 20:4).
21.30: kî leḥeḏ rāʿ yēḥāśēḵ bᵉyôm ʿeḇrôṯ yûḇālû: 'That the wicked is spared on the day of disaster and delivered to the day of wrath.' The traveler's report contradicts the friends: the wicked are often spared on the day of disaster — not judged, but protected. Yôm ʿeḇrôṯ (day of wrath) — possibly a future eschatological event rather than the personal death Zophar described. Job may be acknowledging a final judgment while insisting it does not reliably operate in present experience. The gap between present observation and final accountability is the unresolved theological space the book of Job inhabits.
21.31: mî-yaggîḏ ʿal-pānāyw darkô ûmah-pāʿal mî yᵉšallem-lô: 'Who will tell him to his face what he has done? Who will repay him for what he has done?' The double question highlights the gap between sin and observable consequence: no one confronts the powerful wicked man with his deeds while he lives, and no retributive agent appears to repay him during his lifetime. The silence of accusation and the absence of repayment during life is the empirical observation that Job offers against the friends' tidy theology of immediate retribution.
21.32: wᵉhûʾ liqᵉḇārôt yûḇāl wᵉʿal-gādîš yišqôḏ: 'Yet he is carried to the grave, and a vigil is kept over his tomb.' The wicked man receives the full burial honors due to the respected: šāqaḏ (to watch, keep vigil) over his gādîš (tomb-mound, burial heap). This is high-status burial — a watched and honored grave. The contrast between the wicked man's death without consequences and Job's own suffering is complete: the man who rejected God (v.14-15) receives public honor in death while Job, who maintains his integrity, is abandoned and suffers.
21.33: māṯqû-lô riḇᵉḏê nāḥal wᵉʾaḥărāyw kāl-ʾāḏām yimšôḵ wᵉlip̄ānāyw ʾên mispar: 'The clods of the valley are sweet to him, and every person follows after him, as countless as those who went before him.' Māṯaq (sweet, pleasant) — even the earth's embrace of the wicked is gentle. The numberless procession of humanity — those who came before and those who follow after — makes the wicked man's death part of the universal flow of mortality rather than a punitive end. He dies with the company of all humanity; his death is not a special judgment but a common destiny.
21.34: wᵉʾêḵ tᵉnaḥămûnî hāḇel ûtᵉšûḇôṯêḵem nišʾar māʿal: 'How then do you comfort me with empty words? Your answers are nothing but falsehood!' Heḇel (vanity, breath, emptiness) — the key Ecclesiastes word: the friends' consolation is vapor, insubstantial. Māʿal (treachery, faithlessness) — a strong term used of covenant betrayal (Lev 5:15; Ezra 9:4; 1 Chr 10:13). The friends' false answers constitute not mere error but a kind of faithlessness — a betrayal of their duty as truth-speakers and consolers. Job ends the speech with the same verdict YHWH will pronounce in 42:7: 'you have not spoken of me what is right.'

22.1: Eliphaz's third speech opens with the standard formula. This is his most direct speech: he abandons general argument and launches specific accusations against Job.
22.2: Hăyiskōn lĕʾēl gāber: 'Can a human be of use to God?' — sākan (to be useful/profitable, to have advantage): the commercial term applied to the human-divine relationship. Eliphaz's argument: since humans cannot profit God, God's judgment of Job cannot be motivated by self-interest — it must be pure justice responding to Job's sin. The logic will be refuted in 40:1-5.
22.3: Ḥēpeṣ lĕšadday: 'Does the Almighty take pleasure?' — ḥāpeṣ (desire, delight, pleasure) is the will-term. Biṣdāqĕtĕkā (in your being righteous) and tĕtammîm dĕrākêkā (in your walking blameless paths): these are the Job-identity terms from 1:1. Eliphaz implicitly denies that God would care about such righteousness — a position God directly refutes in the prologue and epilogue.
22.4: Hayirʾātĕkā: 'Is it because of your fear/reverence?' — the sarcastic pivot: Eliphaz claims Job's trouble cannot be divine response to his piety. Yāḇôʾ ʿimmĕkā bammišpāṭ: 'he enters into judgment with you' — the legal term mišpāṭ (judgment, legal proceeding). Eliphaz's claim: God would not bother with a lawsuit just because Job is pious.
22.5: Rabbāh rāʿātĕkā: 'Your wickedness is immense' — the direct accusation. Rabbāh (great, immense) and ʾên-qēṣ (without end): the unprecedented scope of the charges Eliphaz is about to fabricate. The hyperbole reveals the poverty of his evidence.
22.6: Taḥbol ʾaḥêkā ḥinnām: 'You seized collateral from your kinsmen without reason' — ḥābal (to take a pledge/collateral): the legal-economic act of debt-collateral, regulated in Deut 24:10-13 to protect the poor. Ḥinnām (for nothing, without cause): the same word used of the adversary's challenge in 1:9 — Job suffered ḥinnām; now Eliphaz accuses Job of sinning ḥinnām. Tĕpassēṭ lĕḇûšîm: 'you stripped people of clothes' — the specific violation of Deut 24:12-13.
22.7: Mayim ʿāyēp lōʾ hišqîtāh: 'You have not given water to the weary' — the specific breach of desert hospitality, a capital moral failure in the ANE. Lāʾeh (weary traveler) and rāʿēb (starving/hungry): the two most vulnerable conditions. These charges have no basis in the narrative — Eliphaz invents them to fit his retributive scheme.
22.8: ʾîš zĕrôaʿ lô hāʾāreṣ: 'The man of strong arm possessed the land' — zĕrôaʿ (arm/strength) as the means of land acquisition by force. Nĕśûʾ pānîm (man of high standing/lifted face): social status that enables land-seizure. Eliphaz implies Job was such a man — benefiting from structural injustice.
22.9: ʾAlmānôt šillaḥtā rêqām: 'You have sent away widows empty-handed' — ʾalmānāh (widow) is one of the covenant-care triad (orphan, widow, stranger) whose protection defines covenant faithfulness (Deut 10:18; 14:29). Zĕrōʿôt yĕtōmîm yĕdukkāʾ: 'the strength of orphans is crushed' — dāḵāʾ (to crush) — the most vulnerable social categories systematically oppressed. These are the very categories Job claims to have protected (29:12-16).
22.10: Pāḥîm sĕḇîḇôtêkā: 'That is why traps are all around you' — the causal connector: Eliphaz's retributive logic applied. Paḥaḏ piṯʾōm yĕḇahălekā: 'sudden terror terrifies you' — paḥad piṯʾōm (sudden terror/dread): the paired wisdom term for divine surprise-judgment (Prov 3:25; Isa 47:11).
22.11: ʾôr lōʾ-tirʾeh: 'darkness so dense you cannot see' — light is the metaphor for divine presence and moral clarity. Šeṭep mayim yĕkassekā: 'a flood of waters sweeps over you' — the flood-judgment image (cf. Ps 69:1-2). Eliphaz applies the full weight of covenant-curse vocabulary to Job's condition.
22.12: ʾĔlôah gōbah šāmayim: 'Is not God in the heights of heaven?' — the rhetorical question sets up Eliphaz's critique of Job's implied theology. Rōʾš kôkābîm: 'the top of the stars' — the highest point of the created order. Eliphaz is preparing to accuse Job of thinking God is too remote to see human affairs.
22.13: Wĕʾāmartā mah-yāḏaʿ ʾēl: 'And you say, What does God know?' — Eliphaz puts these words in Job's mouth; Job never said them. ʾărāpel yišpōṭ: 'Can he judge through the dense cloud?' — ʿărāpel (deep darkness, storm-cloud): the same word used for the theophanic cloud at Sinai (Exod 20:21). Eliphaz inverts the theophanic vocabulary into an accusation of Job's supposed theology.
22.14: ʿābîm sēter-lô: 'clouds screen him' — Eliphaz attributes to Job the belief that clouds prevent divine vision. Ḥûg šāmayim yithallāk: 'he walks only along the circuit of the sky' — ḥûg (circle, vault): the word used in 26:10 and Isa 40:22 for the circle of creation. Eliphaz reduces the transcendent God to a distant walker on the sky-vault.
22.15: Hăhăšmōr ʾōraḥ ʿôlām: 'Will you keep to the old path?' — ʾōraḥ ʿôlām (ancient path/road of ages): the road of the wicked from of old. The implied accusation: Job is repeating the path of the pre-flood wicked.
22.16: Lōʾ-ʿēt quṣṣāpû: 'cut down before their time' — qāṣap (to cut off prematurely): the violent early death of the wicked as confirmation of guilt. Yûṣaq nāhār yĕsōḏām: 'a river poured out their foundation' — flood-judgment as historical paradigm (the flood generation, Gen 6-7).
22.17: ʾĂšer ʾāmĕrû lāʾēl: 'those who said to God, Leave us alone' — the refusal of divine claim, echoing the wicked generation's rejection. Mah-yišʿad šadday lāhem: 'What can the Almighty do to us?' — divine impotence asserted as basis for autonomous living. Eliphaz links Job's protests to this ancient rebellion.
22.18: Wĕhûʾ millēʾ ḇāttêhem ṭôb: 'Yet he had filled their houses with abundance' — the paradox of the wicked prospered by God. ʿAṣat rĕšāʿîm rāḥăqāh mimmennî: 'the counsel of the wicked is far from me' — Eliphaz distances himself from the implications of his own observation; the statement begins to undermine his retributive case.
22.19: Yirʾû ṣaddîqîm wĕyiśmāḥû: 'the righteous see their destruction and celebrate' — the divine reversal: the community of the righteous witnesses the fall of the wicked. Nāqî yilaʿag lāmô: 'the innocent man mocks them' — lāʿag (to mock, scoff): the derisive laughter of vindication.
22.20: ʾIm-lōʾ nikkĕdū qîmānû: 'Surely our opponents are destroyed' — the confession of the righteous community. ʾEšām ʾāḵlāh ʾēš: 'what remained of them the fire has devoured' — the totality of destruction. Eliphaz is sketching a picture of where Job's path leads.
22.21: Haskēn-nāʾ ʿimmô: 'Make peace with him now' — sākan returns: the same word from v2 (to be of use/profit) now means 'to be at peace/reconciled with.' The commercial term captures Eliphaz's transactional theology: reconciliation is profitable. Wĕḇāhem tāḇôʾ tôḇātĕkā: 'in that way good will come to you' — moralistic promise of conditional prosperity.
22.22: Qĕḥal-nāʾ mippiyw tôrāh: 'Accept his instruction from his own mouth' — qāḥal (to receive, take) + tôrāh (instruction, teaching, law): the wisdom-reception formula. Śîm ʾămārāyw bĕlibbĕkā: 'store his words in your heart' — the Torah-internalization command (Deut 6:6; Ps 119:11).
22.23: ʾIm-tāšûḇ ʿad-šadday tibbāneh: 'If you return to the Almighty, you will be restored' — šûb (to return, repent) + bānāh in Niphal (to be built up, restored): the restoration promise is conditional on repentance. Tarḥîq ʿawlāh: 'banish iniquity' — the practical repentance Eliphaz demands.
22.24: Šît-ʿal-ʿāpār beṣer: 'treat your gold like ordinary dust' — bāṣer (gold/ore/precious metal) treated as ʿāpār (dust/earth/ground): the value-inversion that characterizes genuine repentance. ʾôpîr (Ophir): the famous gold-producing region of uncertain location (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11), treated like stream-bed pebbles. The point is radical revaluation: when God becomes your wealth, earthly wealth loses its grip.
22.25: Wĕhāyāh šadday bĕṣārekā: 'The Almighty himself will be your gold' — the great inversion: šadday (Almighty) as bĕṣer (gold/precious metal). Wĕkesep tôʿăpôt lāk: 'abundance of silver to you' — Eliphaz's invitation, however misapplied to Job, contains genuine theological insight: God himself as the supreme treasure replaces material wealth.
22.26: Kî-ʾāz ʿal-šadday titʿannāg: 'Then you will take delight in the Almighty' — ʿānag (to delight, take pleasure in) with God as the object: cf. Ps 37:4 ('delight yourself in the Lord'). Tiśśāʾ ʾel-ʾĕlôah pānêkā: 'lift your face toward God' — the gesture of confidence before God, the opposite of shame (cf. Ezra 9:6).
22.27: Teʿtar ʾēlāyw weyišmāʿekā: 'You will pray and he will hear you' — ʿātar (to pray fervently, implore) — the same root as the intercessory prayer of Job for his friends in 42:8-10. Wĕnĕḏārêkā tĕšallēm: 'you will fulfill your vows' — the completion of worship promised upon restoration. Eliphaz's promise accidentally describes the final chapter's reality (42:7-10).
22.28: Wĕtiqmōṣ mimmennî mišpāṭ ʿanî: 'Whatever you determine will be settled' — qāmaṣ is difficult here; the MKT renders it as the righteous person's decisions taking effect. ʿal-dĕrākêkā nōgah ʾôr: 'light will shine on your paths' — the path-imagery of Proverbs wisdom, here promised upon repentance.
22.29: Kî-hišpîlû wattōʾmer gēwāh: 'When others are brought low, you will say, There is hope' — gēwāh (pride/height, here: lifting-up, elevation — i.e., hope): when others experience humiliation, the restored person speaks words of hope. Wĕyôšaʿ šĕpaḥ ʿênāyim: 'he will save the one who bows his eyes' — the reversal pattern: the lowly exalted.
22.30: Yĕmallēṭ ʾî-nāqî: 'He will rescue even the one who is not innocent' — a remarkable statement for Eliphaz: even the not-innocent can be rescued through the purity of another. Wĕnimlat bĕbōr kappêkā: 'he will be delivered through the cleanness/purity of your hands' — the intercessory dimension: one person's clean hands can secure another's deliverance. Eliphaz speaks truer than he knows: Job's prayer for his friends will effect their deliverance (42:8-9).

23.1: Job's ninth speech begins without counter-accusation. He is no longer trying to refute Eliphaz; he is searching for God.
23.2: Gam-hayyôm mārî mĕrî: 'Even today my complaint is bitter/rebellious' — mārî from mārar (to be bitter) or mārāh (to rebel): the ambiguity is intentional. Job's speech is simultaneously lament-bitterness and a kind of rebellion against his situation. Yādî kāḇĕḏāh ʿal-ʾanḥātî: 'the burden laid on me is heavier than my groaning can express' — kāḇēḏ (heavy, serious): the weight of his affliction exceeds his capacity to articulate it.
23.3: Mî-yittēn yāḏaʿtî: 'If only I knew!' — the wish-formula expressing impossible longing (cf. 6:8; 11:5). ʾEmṣāʾĕhû (find him): the dominant verb of this chapter — the hidden God who cannot be located. ʿAd-tĕḵûnātô: 'to the place where he dwells/his establishment' — tĕḵûnāh (dwelling-place, throne-setting): the divine throne-room Job longs to reach.
23.4: ʾEʿĕrĕḵāh lĕpānāyw mišpāṭ: 'I would lay my case before him in full order' — ʿāraḵ (to arrange, set in order): the legal preparation of a case, the same term as the altar-arrangement of sacrifice. Job would present a formally ordered lawsuit. Ûpî ʾĕmallēʾ tôḵāḥôt: 'fill my mouth with the strongest arguments' — tôḵāḥat (argument, proof, reproof): the formal counter-evidence.
23.5: ʾēḏĕʿāh millîm yaʿănēnî: 'I would know what he would say in reply' — Job's primary desire is not vindication but comprehension: to understand God's response. ʾāḇîn mah-yōmar lî: 'and understand every word' — the listening dimension: Job wants to hear and understand, not merely prevail.
23.6: Hĕḇĕrōb-kōaḥ yārîḇ ʿimmî: 'Would he use his vast power to overwhelm me in argument?' — rîḇ (to contend, argue at law): the legal dispute term. Job answers his own question: lōʾ ʾak-hûʾ yāśîm bî — 'No, he would give me a hearing.' The conviction that God would hear him in person is the bedrock of Job's faith.
23.7: Šām yāšār nôkāḥ ʿimmô: 'There an upright man could argue his case before him' — yāšār (upright, straight, honest): Job's self-designation. ʾăpallĕṭāh lāneṣaḥ miššōpĕṭî: 'I would receive final acquittal from my judge' — pālaṭ (to escape, be delivered) + lāneṣaḥ (forever/finally): the permanent acquittal that Job is certain the direct encounter would produce.
23.8: Hēn qeḏem ʾehĕlōk wĕʾênennû: 'I go east and he is not there' — the four-direction search for God. Qeḏem/ʾāḥôr (east/west) + śĕmôʾl/yāmîn (left/right — i.e., north/south): the exhaustive compass search. God's absolute hiddenness in the face of Job's suffering is the experiential problem the book wrestles with.
23.9: Śemôʾl bĕʿośĕhû wĕlōʾ-ʾāḥāz: 'he works on the left and I cannot seize him' — ʾāḥaz (to grasp, take hold of): Job cannot grab hold of the God who is acting (note: God is working — bĕʿăśōtô — even when hidden). Yaʿaṭōp yāmîn wĕlōʾ ʾerʾeh: 'he conceals himself on the right and I cannot see him' — the active divine hiddenness: ʿāṭāp (to cover oneself, conceal) — God wrapping himself in hiddenness.
23.10: Kî-yāḏaʿ derek ʿimmādî: 'But he knows the path I walk' — the pivot: Job cannot find God, but God knows Job. The asymmetry is the ground of faith. Bĕḥānānî kazzāhāḇ ʾēṣēʾ: 'when he has tried/tested me I will come out as gold' — bāḥan (to test, examine metal): the metallurgical refinement metaphor. Suffering as the smelting process that purifies rather than destroys.
23.11: Bĕʾăšurô ʾāḥăzāh raglî: 'My foot has stayed on his track' — the path-metaphor for covenant faithfulness. Darkô šāmartî wĕlōʾ ʾāṭ: 'I have kept his way without turning off' — nāṭāh (to turn aside): the standard warning against apostasy. Job claims unbroken path-fidelity.
23.12: Miṣwat śĕpātāyw wĕlōʾ ʾāmîš: 'I have not departed from his commands' — mûš (to depart, move away): the standing-order term (cf. Josh 1:8: 'let not this Book of Torah depart from your mouth'). ʾImrê pîw ṣāpantî mēḥuqqî: 'I have valued the words of his mouth more than my daily bread' — ḥuqqî (my statute/allotment/daily portion): Job prioritizes divine speech over physical sustenance, prefiguring Matt 4:4.
23.13: Wĕhûʾ bĕʾeḥāḏ ûmî yĕšîḇennû: 'But he is resolute/one in purpose, and who can turn him?' — bĕʾeḥāḏ (in one thing, resolute): God's absolute singularity of purpose, which cannot be reversed. Napšô ʾiwwĕtāh wayyaʿaś: 'what his soul desires, that he does' — the divine will as both unconstrained and unfathomable. This is the God Job cannot argue around.
23.14: Yĕšallēm ḥuqqî wĕkāhēnnāh: 'He will carry out what he has ordained for me' — ḥōq (statute, prescribed portion/decree): the divine allocation for Job is fixed and will be fulfilled. Wĕrabbôt kāhēnnāh ʿimmô: 'and there is much more still with him' — the divine purpose for Job exceeds what has been revealed; more is coming.
23.15: ʿal-kēn mippānāyw ʾebbāhēl: 'That is why I am shaken before his face' — bāhal (to be dismayed, terrified, agitated): the theophanic dread. Job's faith and fear coexist: he wants to find God and yet dreads the encounter. ʾĂḇîn wĕʾepḥad mimmennû: 'when I understand, I am afraid of him' — comprehension increases rather than decreases the dread.
23.16: Wĕʾēl hērak libbî: 'God has made my heart soft/weak' — rāḵaḵ (to soften, melt): the heart losing its firmness. Wĕšadday hĕḇahălānî: 'the Almighty has shaken me' — bāhal (to terrify, agitate) in Piel: intense, repeated terrifying. The very God Job seeks is the one who dismantles his composure.
23.17: Kî lōʾ-niṣmattî mippĕnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'Yet I am not silenced/cut off by the darkness' — ṣāmat (to cut off, destroy): Job refuses to be silenced by the surrounding darkness. The verse is difficult; the MKT renders it as Job's defiant persistence: despite everything, he does not stop. Ûmippānay kissāh-ʾōpel: 'deep darkness covers my face' — the conditions are as bad as possible, yet Job continues to speak and seek.

24.1: Maddûaʿ miššadday lōʾ-niṣpĕnû ʿittîm: 'Why does the Almighty not set appointed times?' — ʿēt (time, appointed moment): the theodicy question framed temporally. The God of history (who by definition controls ʿittîm) appears not to have scheduled judgment for visible wickedness. Yōḏĕʿāyw lōʾ-ḥāzû yāmāyw: 'those who know him cannot see his days of reckoning' — the hiddenness of divine judgment as the existential problem.
