24.2: Gĕbûlôt yassîgû: 'They move boundary stones' — gĕḇûl (boundary, territorial marker): the stones that demarcated tribal allotments. Moving them is the paradigmatic covenant violation (Deut 19:14; 27:17): it dissolves the land-distribution that expressed covenant justice. ʿēḏer gāzĕlû wayirʿû: 'they steal flocks and pasture them' — from gāzal (to seize by force): organized agricultural theft.
24.3: Ḥămôr yĕtōmîm yinḥāgû: 'They drive away the orphan's donkey' — the orphan's donkey is his means of livelihood; taking it is destroying his subsistence. Yāḥăbĕlû šôr ʾalmānāh: 'they seize the widow's ox as collateral' — ḥābal (to take as pledge): the covenant-protection violation of ch22:6 now listed as systemic practice.
24.4: Yaṭṭû ʾeḇyônîm midderek: 'They shove the needy off the road' — ʾeḇyôn (needy, poor): the standard covenant-protection category. ʿAniyyê-ʾāreṣ nistarrû yaḥad: 'the poor of the land go into hiding together' — the victims' response to the predatory society. Job is cataloguing the injustice that the friends say God always punishes immediately.
24.5: Hēn pĕrāʾîm bammiḏbār: 'Like wild donkeys in the desert' — pereʾ (wild donkey): the animal of the wilderness, untamed and desperately foraging. The poor are reduced to animal-level survival. Yāṣĕʾû pāʿălām lĕšaḥēr ṭāreP: 'they go out to work, seeking food' — the predator-prey language applied to the economic system.
24.6: Baśśāḏeh bĕlîlô yiqṣōrû: 'They harvest crops from fields that are not theirs' — the gleaning-rights of the poor violated by the powerful who harvest first. Kerem rāšāʿ yĕlaqqēšû: 'they glean in the wicked man's vineyard' — lāqaš (to gather late harvest): the destitute survive only by gleaning in fields controlled by those who oppress them.
24.7: ʿĀrôm yālînû mibbelî lĕḇûš: 'They spend the night naked, without garment' — the nakedness violation is both literal and covenantal: the law specifically protects the garment-collateral of the poor (Exod 22:26-27; Deut 24:12-13). Ûʾên kĕsût bassimrāh: 'no covering against the cold' — qōr (cold): exposed in winter weather.
24.8: Mizzereṃ hārîm yirṭābû: 'They are soaked by the mountain rains' — the weather-exposure of the homeless poor. Ûmibbelî māḥaseh ḥibbĕqû-ṣûr: 'they press against the rock for lack of shelter' — māḥaseh (shelter, refuge): the standard term for divine protection (Ps 46:2; 61:4) denied them by human wickedness.
24.9: Yigrĕzû miššaḏ yātôm: 'They snatch the infant from its mother's breast' — the ultimate violation: tearing nursing infants from their mothers, presumably for debt-slavery. Wĕʿal-ʿānî yaḥbōlû: 'they take collateral from the destitute' — the systematic ḥābal-extraction targeting the most vulnerable.
24.10: ʿĂrôm hālĕkû bĕlî lĕḇûš: 'They go about without clothing' — the workers who produce the harvest cannot afford to clothe themselves from its proceeds. Wĕrāʿēb nāśĕʾû ʿāmîr: 'starving, they carry the harvest sheaves' — the bitter irony: those who produce food go hungry.
24.11: Bên-šûrōtām yaṣhîrû: 'They press olives among the terrace walls' — the agricultural labor vocabulary. Yĕqāḇîm dārĕkû wayyiṣmāʾû: 'they tread the winepresses, yet go thirsty' — the vintage-labor paradox: processing wine for others while thirsting themselves.
24.12: Mēʿîr mĕtîm yinʾāqû: 'From the city the dying groan' — nāʾaq (to groan in death-agony): the pre-mortem cry. Wĕnepeš ḥălālîm tĕšawwēaʿ: 'the mortally wounded cry out' — šāwaʿ (to cry for help): the urgent distress call. Wĕʾĕlôah lōʾ-yāśîm tiplāh: 'yet God lays no charge of wrongdoing' — the theodicy punch: God sees, yet issues no indictment. This is Job's unresolved problem throughout the chapter.
24.13: Hēmmāh hāyû bĕmōrĕḏê-ʾôr: 'These are among those who rebel against the light' — mārad (to rebel): the rebellion-vocabulary of covenant violation applied to those who love darkness (cf. John 3:19-20). Lōʾ yāḏĕʾû dĕrākāyw wĕlōʾ yāšăḇû: 'they do not know its ways and will not walk its paths' — yāḏaʿ as covenantal knowledge refused.
24.14: Lāʾôr yāqûm haṣṣôrēaḥ: 'The murderer rises before dawn' — using the dark for cover, the criminal inverts the natural order: active when others sleep, hiding when others work. Ballaylāh yihyeh kagannāb: 'in the night he becomes the thief' — the convergence of murder and theft as darkness-crimes.
24.15: Wĕʿên nōpep yāšîm sēter pānāyw: 'No one will spot me; he disguises his face' — the adulterer's calculation of privacy. Šemar nešep ʿên tĕšûrēnî: 'watches for the twilight' — nešep (twilight, dusk): the ambiguous light that enables concealment.
24.16: Baḥōšeḵ yaḥtĕrû ḇāttîm: 'In the darkness they break through walls' — ḥātar (to dig through, break in): the thief's technique (cf. Matt 24:43). Yômām ḥittĕmû lāmô: 'by day they stay shut away' — their lives inverted: hidden by day, active by night.
24.17: Kî yaḥdāw bōqer lāhem ṣalmāwet: 'The shadow of death is their morning' — the criminals' temporal experience inverted: morning (when others wake to life) is their deep darkness. Yakkîr ballĕhôt ṣalmāwet: 'they are familiar with the horrors of death's darkness' — they are habituated to what others flee from.
24.18: Qal-hûʾ ʿal-pĕnê-mayim: 'He skims the surface of water like foam' — the transience of the wicked. Tĕqullal ḥelqātô bāʾāreṣ: 'his portion in the land is under a curse' — qālal (to be cursed/treated lightly): the curse that will ultimately overtake the wicked. Lōʾ-yipen derek kĕrāmîm: 'he does not turn toward vineyards' — perhaps prosperity escapes him.
24.19: Ṣiyāh wĕḥum yiśgĕlû mê-šeleg: 'drought and heat evaporate the snow-waters' — the comparison: just as the desert heat absorbs snow-melt instantly and completely, Šĕʾôl ḥāṭĕʾû: 'Sheol takes those who have sinned' — the same inevitable, total absorption. The movement from life to death is as natural and irresistible as evaporation.
24.20: Yiškĕḥēhû reḥem: 'The womb that bore him forgets him' — the ultimate erasure: the mother who bore him forgets him. Rimmāh timetqā-lô: 'the worm devours him eagerly' — rimmāh (worm, maggot): the post-mortem destruction. Lōʾ-yizzāḵēr ʿôḏ: 'he is no longer remembered' — the double erasure: forgotten by the living, consumed by worms.
24.21: Yirʿeh ʿăqārāh lōʾ-tēlēḏ: 'He preys on the childless woman who cannot conceive' — targeting those without social protection or descendants to advocate for them. Wĕʾalmānāh lōʾ-yēṭîb: 'he shows no kindness to the widow' — the widow and barren woman: the most socially vulnerable, without male protectors.
24.22: Weyimsōḵ ʾabbîrîm bĕḵōḥô: 'By his power he brings even the strong man down' — the tyrant who topples even those with resources. Yāqûm wĕlōʾ-yaʾămîn bĕḥayyāyw: 'he rises up, and no one's life is secure' — a difficult verse, possibly describing the tyrant's insecurity rather than his power.
24.23: Yittĕn-lô lābeṭaḥ wĕyiššāʿēn: 'God allows him to rest in security' — the theological crux: divine provision of security to the wicked. Wĕʿênāyw ʿal-darkêhem: 'yet God's eyes are on their every path' — the tension: security granted while divine surveillance continues. Job observes the complexity of divine providential action without forcing it into simple categories.
24.24: Rāmû mĕʿaṭ wĕʾênennû: 'They are lifted up for a moment and then they are gone' — the temporary exaltation of the wicked, followed by sudden removal. Kĕrōʾš šibbōlet yimmālû: 'they sink down like the top of a grain-stalk' — the grain-stalk bends and is cut: the image of swift, total harvesting.
24.25: Wĕʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôh mî yaḵzîḇēnî: 'And if this is not true, who will prove me wrong?' — Job's challenge to the friends: his observation of the world's moral complexity cannot be refuted. Wĕyāśēm lĕ-ʾepes millātî: 'who will show that my words are worthless?' — ʾepes (nothingness): Job's confidence that his account of reality is true.

25.1: Bildad's third speech is the shortest in the cycle (6 verses), suggesting the friends are running out of arguments.
25.2: Mišrāh wĕpāḥaḏ ʿimmô: 'Dominion and dread belong to him' — mišrāh (dominion, government): the governing authority of God. Paḥaḏ (terror, dread) as the accompanying attribute. ʿōśeh šālôm bimrômāyw: 'he makes peace in his high places' — the divine peacemaking in the heavenly council, which Bildad invokes as contrast to Job's turbulence.
25.3: Hiš ḵĕsāpāyw yimmāneh: 'Can his armies be numbered?' — gĕdûḏ (troop, army): the heavenly host. The unanswerable rhetorical question establishes divine immensity. ʿal-mî lōʾ-yāqûm ʾôrô: 'upon whom does his light not rise?' — universal divine illumination: no one and nowhere outside divine light.
25.4: Ûmah-yiṣdaq ʾĕnôš ʾim-ʾēl: 'How can any mortal be declared righteous before God?' — ṣāḏaq (to be righteous, be declared righteous, be in the right) used legally: the formal verdict of acquittal. This is the question of 9:2 raised again by Bildad. Yĕlûd ʾiššāh (born of woman): the same creatureliness-phrase as 15:14 — birth conditions moral limitation.
25.5: Hēn ʿad-yārēaḥ wĕlōʾ yaʾăhîl: 'Even the moon gives no real light before him' — ʾāhal (to shine brightly): the moon's light is dim before divine glory. Kôḵāḇîm lōʾ-zakkû bĕʿênāyw: 'the very stars are not pure in his sight' — zāḵaḵ (to be pure, clear): celestial impurity before divine holiness. The scale of the comparison makes human righteousness seem impossible.
25.6: ʾAp kî-ʾĕnôš rimmāh: 'How much less a mortal, who is a maggot' — rimmāh (worm, maggot): the decomposition-creature. Ûḇen-ʾāḏām tôlēʿāh: 'and a human being, who is a worm' — tôlēʿāh (worm, crimson-worm): the same word used in Ps 22:7 ('I am a worm and not a man') — the creature beneath notice. Bildad intends to silence Job with creaturely insignificance; Ps 22 and the incarnation reveal that this is exactly the condition God enters in Christ.

26.1: Job's tenth speech opens with devastating sarcasm, meeting Bildad's crushing speech with ironic counter-praise.
26.2: Eziartāh lĕlōʾ-kōaḥ: 'What help you have given to the powerless!' — the sarcasm is biting: ʿāzar (to help) turned mock-praise. Hôšaʿtā zerôaʿ lōʾ-ʿōz: 'what strength you have brought to the feeble arm!' — Bildad has done absolutely nothing for Job. The speech that claims to describe God's might has brought no practical help to the suffering man before him.
26.3: Mah-yāʿaṣtā lĕlōʾ ḥāḵmāh: 'What counsel you have given to one with no wisdom!' — the irony continues: yāʿaṣ (to advise, counsel) applied to Bildad's useless speech. Wĕtûšiyāh lārōḇ hôḏaʿtā: 'how abundantly you have offered sound understanding!' — tûšiyāh (sound wisdom, practical effectiveness): the rarest, most prized wisdom term used in mock-praise.
26.4: ʾet-mî higgadtā millîm: 'Who are you addressing with these words?' — the challenge to Bildad's intended audience. Ûmî-nĕšāmāh yāṣĕʾāh mimmekā: 'whose spirit spoke through you?' — nĕšāmāh (breath, spirit): Bildad claimed divine inspiration (cf. 32:8); Job challenges the source.
26.5: Hārĕpāʾîm yĕḥôlālû mittaḥat mayim: 'The shades of the dead tremble under the waters' — rĕpāʾîm (the Rephaim/shades): the dead in Sheol (cf. Isa 14:9; Prov 2:18). The trembling of the underworld dead before God establishes that divine authority penetrates even the cosmic underworld. Wĕšōkĕnêhem (and those who dwell beneath them): the layered depths of the cosmological underworld.
26.6: ʿĀrôm šeʾôl negdô: 'Sheol is naked/exposed before him' — ʿārôm (naked, exposed): nothing in Sheol is hidden from God. ʾAḇaddôn ʾên kĕsûth: 'Abaddon has no covering' — ʾaḇaddôn (Abaddon, destruction — the place of final destruction, cf. Rev 9:11): no concealment possible in the deepest pit. Both Sheol and Abaddon appear together as the twin death-realms (cf. Prov 15:11).
26.7: Nōṭeh ṣāpôn ʿal-tōhû: 'He stretches out the north over the void' — tōhû (formlessness, void): the same word as Gen 1:2 (tōhû wābōhû). Job uses the creation-vocabulary to describe the divine cosmological act. Tōleh ʾereṣ ʿal-bĕlî māh: 'he hangs the earth on nothing' — the remarkable cosmological statement that the earth is suspended on nothing (bĕlî māh): no visible support. The OT's most explicit statement of the earth's suspension in space.
26.8: ṣōrer mayim beʿābāyw: 'He wraps the waters in his thick clouds' — the cloud-water storage system of the hydrological cycle. Wĕlōʾ-nibqaʿ ʿānān taḥtām: 'the cloud holds without bursting' — the marvel that clouds hold their water: an implicit acknowledgment of the divine engineering of weather.
26.9: Mĕʾaḥēz pĕnê-kissēʾ: 'He screens the face of his throne' — kissēʾ (throne): the divine royal seat. The cloud-screen conceals the divine throne (cf. the Sinai cloud, Exod 24:16). Piśtēhû ʿālāyw ʿănānô: 'he spreads his cloud over it' — the veil of divine transcendence around the throne.
26.10: Ḥôg ḥāḡag ʿal-pĕnê-mayim: 'He has drawn a circle on the face of the waters' — ḥûg (circle, vault): the horizon-circle marking the boundary between waters (cf. Prov 8:27; Isa 40:22). ʿad-taktît ʾôr ʿim-ḥōšeḵ: 'the boundary between light and dark' — the creation-separation of Gen 1 expressed as a drawn circle.
26.11: ʿAmmûḏê šāmayim yĕrōpĕpû: 'The pillars of heaven tremble' — the cosmological pillars supporting the sky vault. Wĕyitmahhû miggĕʿārātô: 'they are stunned at his rebuke' — gĕʿārāh (rebuke, thunderous command): God's authoritative speech that shakes cosmic architecture. The same term used for the divine rebuke of chaos-waters (Ps 104:7; Isa 50:2).
26.12: Bĕkōḥô rāgaʿ hayyām: 'By his power he calmed the sea' — rāgaʿ (to stir up or calm — the term is ambiguous; both meanings appear in the OT). ûḇiṯḇûnātô māḥaṣ Rāhaḇ: 'by his understanding he crushed Rahab' — Rāhaḇ: the sea-dragon/chaos monster (cf. Ps 89:10; Isa 51:9), the mythological figure for the primordial waters of chaos. God's creation is the defeat of chaos.
26.13: Rûḥô šāmayim šiprāh: 'His Spirit made the heavens beautiful' — šāprāh (to be beautiful, clear): the Spirit's aesthetic dimension of creation. Ḥōlĕlāh yāḏô nāḥāš bārîaḥ: 'his hand transfixed the twisting serpent' — nāḥāš bārîaḥ (the twisting/fleeing serpent): the Leviathan/sea-serpent figure (cf. Isa 27:1; Rev 12:9). Creation as divine defeat of chaos-serpent.
26.14: Hēn-ʾēlleh qĕṣôt dĕrākāyw: 'These things are but the edges of his ways' — qāṣeh (edge, extremity): what Job has described — cosmic creation, defeat of chaos, pillars of heaven — are merely the outermost fringes of divine activity. Wĕremes dābār nišmaʿ-bô: 'how faint a whisper we catch of him' — remes (a whisper, a murmur): the great theophanic acts are a barely audible whisper of the divine fullness. Rāʿam gĕḇûrātô mî yiṯbônān: 'the thunder of his power — who can understand?' — the doxological close. Job ends his survey of God's cosmic power with epistemological humility, not despair.

27.1: Wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo (Job again lifted up his mashal and said) — nasa’ = to lift/carry; mashal = a wisdom saying, discourse, parable, extended metaphor. The mashal is not merely a speech but a heightened wisdom form — an authoritative utterance with didactic weight. The same formula appears at 29:1 (wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo), framing chs 27–31 as one extended mashal block. The repetition signals that Job is still in the role of wisdom teacher, not just a sufferer complaining.
27.2: Hai-’el ’asher hesar mishpati (as God lives, who has denied me justice) — the hai-X oath formula (“as X lives”) is the most solemn Hebrew oath form, typically invoking YHWH or the king. Here Job swears by God even as he accuses God of the very injustice he is protesting. The verb sur = to turn aside/deviate — YHWH has turned aside from giving Job justice. Veshaddai ’asher-hemer nafshi (and the Almighty who has embittered my soul) — marar = to be/make bitter (same root as Naomi’s name Mara, Ruth 1:20, and the bitter herbs of Passover, Exod 12:8). Job is bitter-souled but still invoking God.
27.3: Ki-khol ’od nishmat-’eloah be’appi (as long as the breath of God is in my nostrils) — nishmat ’eloah = the breath of God, directly echoing Gen 2:7 (nishmat hayyim, breath of life) where YHWH breathes into Adam’s nostrils. Job’s oath has a double irony: he swears by the same God who sustains his life by that very breath, even while accusing God of injustice. Veruah ’eloah be’appi = “and the spirit/breath of God in my nostrils” — the parallel phrase reinforces the divine-origin-of-breath theology underlying Job’s oath.
27.4: Im-tedabberan sefatay ’avlah (if my lips should speak wickedness) — ’avlah = iniquity/injustice/wrongness, the quality of something morally skewed. Im-yehgeh leshoni remiyyah = “if my tongue should utter deceit/treachery” — remiyyah = deceit, slackness, treachery (from the root of ramah = to deceive). Job’s lips and tongue form the merism of speech: neither will deviate from truthful integrity.
27.5: Hallilah li im-’atsdiqet ’etkhlem (God forbid that I should justify/agree you are right) — hallilah is a strong interjection: “far be it from me!” / “God forbid!” (used 21 times in OT for strong moral rejection). ’atsdiq = declare right/justify (hiphil of tsadaq); Job refuses to justify his friends’ position against him. Im-’agva tummati mimmenni = “I will not surrender my wholeness from me” — tummah = integrity/wholeness (noun of the tam family, same as 1:1 and 2:3,9). The word “surrender” (sur) = turn aside, depart — he will not let his integrity be moved even by death.
27.6: Betsdaqati hehezaqti velo-’arpennah (I hold fast to my righteousness and will not release it) — tsedaqah = righteousness (noun form, distinct from the adjective tsaddiq). hazaq = to hold fast, grip firmly (the same verb as Job’s wife’s rhetorical question at 2:9, hahaziq betummatekha). Lo-yihrap levavi miyamay = “my heart does not reproach me for any of my days” — harap = to reproach, taunt (pi’el). Job’s conscience (lev = heart, the seat of inner knowledge) is clear for every day of his life.
27.7: Yehiy ke-rasha’ ’oyevi (let my enemy be as/like the wicked) — jussive form, an imprecatory wish. Job invokes the fate he is about to describe (the wicked man’s doom, vv8–23) on those who oppose him. The friends argue that Job’s suffering proves his wickedness; Job turns the argument: let those who falsely accuse him receive the lot they attribute to the wicked. The imprecation is not vengeance but the inversion of the friends’ logic.
27.8: Ki mah-tiqwat hanef ki-yivtsa’ (what hope does the godless one have when God cuts him off?) — hanef = the godless/profane person (from hanap = to be polluted, pollute; applied to land, people, speech). ki-yivtsa’ ’eloah nafsho = “when God extracts/cuts off his soul” — batsa’ = to cut off, extract (as cutting a root or stopping a flow); the image of God cutting the thread of the godless person’s life. The godless man’s hope is zero at the moment of divine cutting-off.
27.9: Hatsaqato yishma’ ’el (will God hear his cry?) — tsaqah = to cry out (the cry of the oppressed that reaches God, Exod 3:7; Neh 5:1; Job 34:28). The godless man’s lack of covenant relationship means his cry does not have the standing to reach God. Ki tavo ’alav tsarah = “when trouble comes upon him” — the godless can only expect that his trouble-cry falls into deaf cosmic ears, because he has not cultivated the relationship that gives the cry its standing.
27.10: Im-’al-shaddai ya’ang (does he delight in the Almighty?) — ’anag = to be soft/delicate, to take delight in (only a few occurrences; cf. Ps 37:4, hit’anag ’al-YHWH = delight yourself in YHWH). yiqra’ ’eloah bekhol-’et = “does he call on God at all times?” — the two marks of genuine piety that the godless lack: delight in the Almighty and constant prayer. Both are marks Job himself displayed (1:1,5), which is why his prayers and cries have standing where the godless person’s do not.
27.11: ’Orekh bi yad-’el (I will instruct you about the hand of God) — the verb is yarah (hiphil) = to throw/shoot/direct — the same root as Torah (teaching/direction). Job positions himself as a teacher of divine wisdom; the “hand of God” (yad-’el) is the metaphor for God’s active power and governance in history. ’asher im-shaddai lo-’akhahed = “what is with the Almighty I will not hide” — the verb khahad = to hide/conceal (pi’el). Job claims a kind of prophetic boldness: he will not conceal what he has seen of God’s ways.
27.12: Hen-’attem kullekem hazitem (you yourselves have all seen/beheld it) — hazah = to see, behold (often of prophetic/visionary seeing, more intensive than the ordinary ra’ah). Job charges the friends with willful selective blindness: they have seen the same evidence as Job but draw convenient conclusions. Vema-zeh hevel tehbalu = “why then have you become so thoroughly vain/empty?” — hevel = breath/vapor/vanity (the key word of Ecclesiastes, used 38 times there). The friends’ wisdom has devolved to hevel — empty, meaningless, like a puff of breath.
27.13: Zeh-heleq-’adam ra’sha’ me-’el (this is the lot of the wicked from God) — heleq = portion, lot (the share assigned to someone). The description of the wicked’s fate in vv13–23 is linguistically and theologically close to what the friends have argued throughout. Many commentators consider this section to have been accidentally relocated from a friend’s speech, or to represent Job presenting his opponents’ argument before refuting it. venahalt ’aritsim yiqqahu mishaddai = “and the inheritance of oppressors they receive from the Almighty” — ’aritsim = tyrants/oppressors (from ’arats = to terrify, inspire dread).
27.14: Im-yirbu vanav le-harev (if his children multiply, it is for the sword) — the multiplication of children (normally a sign of blessing, Gen 22:17; Ps 128:3) becomes a multiplication of victims. Vetse’etsa’av lo-yisbe’u-lahem = “his descendants will not be satisfied with bread” — tsotse’et = offspring (a rare form, also in Isa 22:24). The wicked’s family suffers the consequences of his deeds.
27.15: Seridav bammavet yiqberu (his survivors will be buried by death/plague) — serid = survivor, remnant. Mavet = death, or possibly a disease-demon (the Ugaritic Mot, death personified). Ve’almanotav lo-tivkeyyna = “and his widows will not weep for them” — the absence of mourning is a sign of ultimate disgrace in the ancient Near East (cf. Jer 22:18 for the fate of those denied mourning).
27.16: ’Im-yitspor ke’afar kesep (though he pile up silver like dust) — tsaphar = to pile up, heap. The comparison of silver (kesep) to dust (afar) emphasizes the quantitative abundance; so much silver it becomes as cheap and plentiful as dust. Vekikhomer yakhim levush = “and lay up clothing as plentiful as clay” — khomer = clay, mud (the building material as cheap as dirt); clothing stored in such abundance it is like a pile of clay.
27.17: Yakhim vetsaddiq yilbash (he may prepare it but the righteous will wear it) — the wicked man is the inadvertent benefactor of the righteous. Vekhesep naqqi yehaleq = “and the innocent will share out the silver” — the accumulated wealth of the wicked will be redistributed to the innocent by divine reversal. This is the Deuteronomic principle of possessing the property of the defeated wicked (Deut 6:10–11).
27.18: Banah kea’ash beito (he builds his house like a moth builds) — the ash (moth) builds its cocoon, which is destroyed at a touch. Ukhesuka ’asah note = “and like the shelter (sukkah) a watchman puts up” — sukkah = a temporary booth/shelter (the same word as the Feast of Booths); the watchman’s field shelter is deliberately temporary, built to be abandoned. The wicked man’s house, however grand, is actually a moth-cocoon or a harvest-booth: structurally temporary, destined for dissolution.
27.19: ’Ashir yishkav velo-ye’asef (the rich man lies down but is not gathered) — ne’esaf ’el-’ammav (gathered to his people) is the standard Hebrew expression for a peaceful, honored death and burial among family (Gen 25:8; 35:29; 49:33). The negation: the wicked rich man lies down in death but is not gathered — denied the dignified death of the righteous. Yifqah ’enav ve’einennu = “he opens his eyes and he is gone” — one blink of waking and the man is erased.
27.20: Tassigehu kemayim ballahot (terrors overtake him like a flood) — nasag (hiphil) = to overtake, reach; ballahot = terrors (from balal = to terrify). Laylah genavathu sufah = “in the night a storm steals him away” — ganav = to steal (the same root as the vision stealing in 4:12, where Eliphaz said the revelation “stole” to him). The wicked man is stolen in the night, as suddenly as a thief enters.
27.21: Yis’ahu qadim vayelekh (the east wind sweeps him away and he goes) — qadim = the east wind, the hot, destroying wind of the Levant (sirocco), associated with YHWH’s judgment (Exod 14:21; Ezek 17:10). Visarhu mimmiqomo = “and it hurls him from his home” — sara’ = to blow away, toss, scatter. The east wind that gave Job his initial disaster (1:19, “a great wind from the wilderness”) reappears here as the wicked man’s destroyer.
27.22: Veyashlekh ’alav velo-yahemol (for God hurls his power at him without mercy) — shalakh = to throw, hurl (God as the one who hurls against the wicked). Lo-yahemol = without pity (the verb hamal = to spare, have compassion; negated here). Baroah yivrah miyaddo = “he flees desperately from his hand” — the wicked man’s futile flight from the divine assault.
27.23: Yispoq ’alav kappayim (men clap their hands at him) — the hand-clapping here is not applause but the gesture of derision or astonishment (cf. Lam 2:15; Ezek 25:6 where clapping of hands = contempt). Veyisroq ’alav mimqomo = “and hisses him from his home” — saraq = to whistle/hiss (a gesture of contempt, Jer 19:8; Zeph 2:15). The wicked man’s departure from his home is marked by public contempt: clapping and hissing, the sounds of dismissal.

28.1: Ki yesh lakesef motsa’ (surely silver has its mine/source) — motsa’ = outgoing, source, point of exit — the vein of ore from which silver emerges. Vemaqom lazahav yazeqqu = “and a place for gold where they refine it” — zaqaq = to refine by filtering/straining (the pi’el intensive). The chapter opens with the premise that human technology can locate and extract every precious mineral from the earth — a premise that will be systematically applied and then overturned for the one substance (wisdom) that defeats human mining.
28.2: Barzel me’afar yuqqah (iron is taken from the earth/dust) — afar = dust/earth (the same word as Adam’s origin, Gen 2:7). Ve’even yatsaq nehushah = “and from the ore-stone copper is smelted” — yatsaq = to pour/cast/smelt; nehushah = copper/bronze. The smelting of copper from ore requires the same principle: the precious metal is hidden in and extractable from the matrix of common stone.
28.3: Qets sam lahoshekh (he sets a limit/end to the darkness) — qets = end, limit, terminus; lahoshekh = the darkness of the mine shaft. The miner’s lamp penetrates what was dark, setting a boundary on darkness by pushing it back. Uvekhol-takhlit hu hhoqer = “and to every extremity he searches” — takhlit = the uttermost limit. ’Even ’ofel vetsalmavet = “the stone of gloom and deep shadow” — the deepest, darkest rock that no natural light reaches.
28.4: Parats nahal me’im-gar (far from where people live a shaft is opened/broken) — parats = to burst/break through; nahal = a wadi/stream-channel or, here, a mine shaft cut through the rock. Nishkahim min-ragel = “forgotten by the foot” — nishkah = forgotten (niphal of shakah). The mine shafts run directly beneath surfaces where people walk, completely unknown to those feet above. Dallum me’enosh nau’u = “far from mortals they dangle and sway” — the miners hanging in shafts far below, remote from human society.
28.5: ’Erets mimmenah yetse’-lahem (the earth — from it bread comes out) — the contrast: the surface produces agricultural food (lahem = bread/food); vetahteyha nehepakh ke’esh = “but underneath it is turned up as though by fire” — the violent transformation of rock by mining (or possibly volcanic imagery). The same earth that produces nourishing bread on its surface is being burned and turned upside down underneath.
28.6: Meqom-sappirim ’avaneha (its stones are the source of sapphires) — sappirim = sapphires/lapis lazuli (the dark blue stone associated with YHWH’s throne, Exod 24:10; Ezek 1:26). Ve’afrot zahav lo = “and it contains flakes of gold” — afrot = dust/fine flakes; gold dust embedded in the rock. The earth contains all these treasures but keeps them hidden — requiring the labor of breaking in to find them.
28.7: Netiv lo-yeda’o ’ayit (a path no bird of prey knows) — ’ayit = a bird of prey (hawk, vulture; the term for a bird with the sharpest possible vision, used to emphasize the imperceptibility of the mine paths). Velo shezafattu ’ein ha-dayya = “nor has the eye of the falcon observed it” — dayya = a falcon/kite. Even the keenest vision in the natural world cannot discern the path to the mine.
28.8: Lo-hidrukhuhu benei-shahhats (the proud/fierce beasts have not walked it) — benei-shahhats = literally “sons of pride” — the powerful wild animals (lions). Velo ’avhar ’alav shahal = “nor has the fierce lion passed over it” — shahal = a young roaring lion. Even the most powerful predators do not know the path to the mining depths. The chapter constructs an argument by escalation: the birds with sharpest vision and the beasts with greatest power are equally ignorant of this path.
28.9: Baseles shalah yado (he puts his hand to the flint/hard rock) — seles = flint, the hardest rock (used for cutting implements in ancient technology). Hapakh mishores harim = “he overturns mountains from their roots/foundations” — hapakh = to overturn, turn upside down (a strong verb of radical inversion; used of Sodom’s overthrow, Gen 19:25). Human technology is powerful enough to uproot mountains in the search for ore.
28.10: Bayurot ’avot yeqaq (he cuts channels through the rocks) — yeqaq = cuts/hews; channels (yurot) cut through solid rock to allow access. Vekhol-yaqar ra’atah ’eyno = “and everything precious his eye sees” — the miner’s eye systematically takes in every precious thing (yaqar = precious, rare, honored) that the opened rock reveals.
28.11: Mibeki neharot hibesh (he holds back the springs/streams from flowing) — beki = weeping/trickling (used for tears; here for trickling water in underground streams). Vatatsafun yotsi’ or = “and brings hidden things out to the light” — tsafun = what is hidden/buried (from tsafan = to hide/treasure). The miner controls water flow and brings what was hidden to light. This verse is the peak of human technological achievement in the hymn — the turning point before the “but where shall wisdom be found?” of v12.
28.12: Veha-hokhmah me’ayin timmatse’ (but wisdom — from where shall it be found?) — the rhetorical pivot of the entire chapter. me’ayin = from where? — literally “from what nothingness?” (’ayin = nothingness, the absence). Wisdom is being sought like a mine location, but no map or technology can locate it. Ve’ey zeh maqom binah = “and what is the place of understanding?” — maqom = place, site; binah = discernment/understanding (from bin = to distinguish, see between). If the chapter just proved that every precious mineral has a maqom (v1–11), wisdom has no maqom that human exploration can find.
28.13: Lo-yada’ ’enosh ’erkah (no mortal knows its worth/way to it) — ’enosh = frail mortal human (chosen over ’ish or adam to emphasize human limitation). ’erkah = its ordering/worth/path (from ’arak = to arrange, evaluate). The double meaning: no mortal knows wisdom’s value (cannot price it) and no mortal knows its arrangement (cannot find its location). Velo timmatse’ be’erets hahayyim = “and it is not found in the land of the living” — the land of the living (’erets hahayyim) is the domain where human searching operates; wisdom is not there.
28.14: Tehom ’amar lo-vi hi’ (the Deep says “it is not in me”) — tehom = the primordial cosmic deep (Gen 1:2; Ps 42:7; 104:6), personified as a cosmic voice in this passage. Veyam ’amar ’ein imadi = “and the Sea says ‘it is not with me’” — both the Deep and the Sea (yam) speak, searching their own depths and reporting wisdom’s absence. The cosmic realms that dwarf human experience are themselves ignorant of wisdom’s location.
28.15: Lo-yutan segor tahteyha (pure gold cannot be given/paid for it) — segor = closed/refined gold, a gold bar (from sagar = to close/shut). Velo-yishaqel kesep mehirah = “nor can silver be weighed out as its price” — shaqal = to weigh (same root as shekel, the standard weight-unit of silver). The chapter shifts from geography (where?) to commerce (what price?): wisdom cannot be located geographically or purchased commercially.
28.16: Lo-tesulleh bektem-’ofir (it cannot be matched/valued against the gold of Ophir) — sulleh (pu’al of salah) = to be weighed/valued. Ktem-’ofir = the gold of Ophir, the finest gold known in the ancient world (1 Kgs 9:28; Ps 45:9; Isa 13:12). Beshoham yaqar veesappir = “against the precious onyx and sapphire” — the most precious gems of the ancient Near East are insufficient as equivalent value for wisdom.
28.17: Lo-ya’arkennah zahav veukhukhit (gold and glass/crystal cannot equal its value) — ’arakh = to arrange, compare, be equal to (same root as ’erkah in v13). ukhukhit = glass/crystal (probably fine transparent glass, which was extremely valuable in the ancient world). Utemurat ah yeraqah fine gold = “its exchange cannot be in items of fine gold” — the fine gold jewelry is insufficient for the trade.
28.18: Ram vegalit lo yizkker (coral and crystal are not worth mentioning) — ram = coral (from the sea floor, expensive and exotic); galit = crystal or pearl (uncertain). Vemeshekh hokhmah mippeninim = “the acquisition of wisdom is above rubies” — meshekh = drawing/pulling out (the effort of obtaining something valuable); peninim = red coral, rubies, or pearls (cf. Prov 3:15; 31:10 where a capable wife is worth more than peninim). The comparison to Prov 3:15 and 31:10 places this wisdom hymn within the Solomonic-wisdom tradition.
28.19: Lo-yesawennah pitdat-kush (the topaz of Cush cannot compare with it) — pitdah = topaz (Exod 28:17, in the high priest’s breastplate). Cush = Ethiopia/Nubia, the source of the finest topaz. Bektem tahor lo te’arakh = “against pure gold it cannot be priced” — the culmination of the commercial inventory. Every precious material that human commerce knows — gold (various grades), silver, sapphire, onyx, glass, coral, ruby, topaz — is explicitly declared insufficient to purchase wisdom.
28.20: Veha-hokhmah me’ayin tavo’ (where then does wisdom come from?) — the rhetorical question is repeated from v12 (nearly verbatim: timmatse’ = be found → tavo’ = come). The second question is slightly different: not “where is it found?” but “where does it come from?” The change of verb (ba’ = to come/arrive) shifts the focus from location to source. Ve’ey zeh maqom binah = the exact repetition of v12b, bracketing the commercial inventory as a literary bracket.
28.21: Venilmah me’einei kol-hay (it is hidden from the eyes of every living creature) — nilmah = it is concealed (niphal of ’alam = to hide, conceal; cf. Job 6:10 where Job says he has not concealed the Holy One’s words). Umippof hashsayim nisteirah = “and hidden from the bird of the heavens” — the birds of the sky (who had the keenest vision in v7) are equally in the dark. The living world, from earth-dwelling animals to the flying birds, is universally ignorant of wisdom’s location.
28.22: ’Abaddon vamavet ’ameru (Abaddon and Death say) — ’Abaddon = the place of destruction, the realm of the dead (from ’abad = to perish; appears only in Job, Proverbs, and Psalms in the OT; later used in Rev 9:11 as the name of the angel of the abyss). The personification of both the cosmic deep (v14) and the realm of the dead speaks: even Sheol’s rumor-network (“we have heard a whisper”) only catches rumors of wisdom, not its location. The universal ignorance spans from the living world to the dead.
28.23: ’Elohim hevin darkah (God understands the way to it) — the resolution of the chapter’s search: only God (’elohim) has wisdom’s derek (way, path). Vehu yada’ et-meqomah = “and he knows its location” — maqom (place) finally finds its answer: God knows it. The mining metaphor is resolved: God is the only mining engineer who knows the vein location. He has been seeking wisdom in the wrong direction — it cannot be extracted by human technology; it can only be given by the one who knows its location.
28.24: Ki-hu liknot ha’arets yabbit (for he looks to the ends of the earth) — God’s cosmic vision spans the entire created order. Tahat kol-hashamayim yir’eh = “and sees everything under all of heaven” — the scope is total: every point under the sky is within God’s field of vision. Only such cosmic omnivision could know where wisdom is hidden when even the Deep, the Sea, and Abaddon report ignorance.
28.25: La’asot laruah mishqal (when he assigned a weight to the wind) — mishqal = weight (same root as shaqal = to weigh, and shekel). YHWH weighs the wind — a remarkable image of divine precision over the seemingly uncontrollable. Vemayim tikken bemiddah = “and measured the waters by measure” — tikken = he established/measured (from taqan = to set right, measure). God’s precision over wind and water contrasts with the ungovernable appearance of weather.
28.26: Beasoto lematar hoq (when he made a decree for the rain) — hoq = statute, decree, appointed limit (the word for laws inscribed in stone). YHWH gave the rain (matar) a decree that governs it, like a legal statute. Vedderekh lahaziz qolot = “and a path for the lightning and thunder” — the storms are not chaotic but follow derekh (a path, a way) that God has established. The same vocabulary used for wisdom (“where is wisdom’s derek?” v23) appears here: the storms have a derek because God gave it; wisdom has its derek for the same reason.
28.27: ’Az ra’ah vayesapperah (then he saw it and made it known/declared it) — saphar = to count/number/declare (root of sefer = book, and mispar = number). God sees wisdom and “tells” it — numbers it, declares it, records it. Hekhinah vegam-haqarah = “he established it and fully searched it out” — kun = to establish, make firm; haqar = to search/probe thoroughly (same word used of the friends who “searched” Job in 29:16). God has already done the full investigation of wisdom; it does not remain to be found by human searching because it has already been found, established, and declared by God.
28.28: Vayyomer le’adam (and he said to mankind) — this is the chapter’s resolution. After establishing that only God knows wisdom’s location, God communicates to ’adam (humanity, generic) the accessible form of wisdom. Hen yir’at ’adonay hi hokhmah = “look — the fear of the Lord is wisdom” — yir’ah = fear/reverence. The same four-descriptor of Job in 1:1 ended with yare’ ’elohim (fearing God); the wisdom hymn concludes that this very fear is the wisdom that is inaccessible to human mining but accessible through relationship with God. Vesur mera’ binah = “and to turn from evil is understanding” — the same phrase that described Job at the book’s opening (sar mera’, 1:1). The conclusion of the greatest wisdom poem in the OT is: Job already has wisdom; he is defined by the qualities the hymn names as wisdom.

29.1: Wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo (Job again continued his discourse) — the same formula as 27:1, confirming that chs 27–31 form a single extended mashal unit. Chapter 29 begins Job’s nostalgic lament for the days before his suffering. The speech covers three movements: the former blessing (ch29), the present suffering (ch30), and the oath of innocence (ch31). Chapter 29 is the OT’s most extended first-person portrait of righteous social leadership.
29.2: Mi-yiteneni kiyarhe-qedem (oh that I were as in the months of old) — mi-yiteneni = “who will give to me” = the formula of longing/wish (“oh that...!”). Yarhe-qedem = months of qedem (the past, antiquity, the east direction associated with origins). Kimei ’elohah yishmereni = “in the days when God watched over me” — shamar = to keep, guard, watch over (the verb of the Aaronic blessing: YHWH “keep you,” Num 6:24). Job remembers divine watchfulness as blessing; now he experiences it (in his suffering) as curse.
29.3: Behillo nero ’alei ro’shi (when his lamp shone over my head) — nerot = lamp; hilo = its shining (infinitive of halal = to shine/praise). The lamp over the head is the divine light of favor and guidance (Ps 18:28: “you light my lamp”; Ps 119:105: “your word is a lamp to my feet”). Ve’or-lo elek-hoshekh = “and by his light I walked through darkness” — the darkness (hoshekh) was navigated by divine light. This verse is the inversion of Job’s current experience: now he walks in darkness with no lamp.
29.4: Ka’asher hayiti bimei horpi (as I was in the days of my autumn/prime) — horef = autumn/harvest season (the most productive season of the agricultural year). Job’s prime of life is described as his “autumn” — the season of maximum fruit, maturity, and harvest. Besod ’eloah ’ali ’ahali = “when the friendship/counsel of God was over my tent” — sod = intimate circle, secret counsel, confidential gathering (Ps 25:14: “the sod of YHWH is for those who fear him”; Jer 23:18: “who has stood in the sod of YHWH?”). Job remembers being in YHWH’s inner circle of counsel — the most intimate form of divine relationship.
29.5: Besod ’adonay yesaret shaddai ’imadi (when the Almighty was still with me) — ’imadi = with me (the divine presence with the person). Vesevivotay ne’aray = “when my children were around me” — the children who were killed in ch1 are remembered as surrounding Job in his former prosperity. The contrast is acute: the children who circled Job in happiness now lie dead.
29.6: Birhots halikkay behemath (when I bathed my steps in cream/curds) — rahas = to wash/bathe; hemath = curds/cream/butter (the product of cows, here used for extreme agricultural abundance). Vetsur yatsaq ’imadi palgei-shamen = “and the rock poured out for me streams of olive oil” — palgei shamen = streams of oil. The image of oil flowing from solid rock echoes Deut 32:13 (yinqehu shemen mehalhammish tsur = “they sucked oil from the flinty rock”). The abundance is so extreme that rock itself pours oil — the most infertile substrate becomes a source of the most precious agricultural product.
29.7: Betseti sha’ar ’alei-qaret (when I went out to the gate over the city) — sha’ar = the city gate, the site of judicial proceedings, commercial transactions, and social meeting in the ancient Israelite/Near Eastern city (Deut 22:15; Ruth 4:1; Amos 5:10). Barehov ’akhinu moshavi = “when I prepared/set my seat in the public square” — rehhov = the broad open square near the gate; mokhehin = I set/prepared (from kun). Job took his judicial seat in the city’s public space — the position of the judge and leader.
29.8: Ra’uni ne’arim venikhba’u (the young men saw me and stepped aside/hid themselves) — nikba’u = hid themselves, stepped back (niphal of khaba’ = to hide). The young men’s retreat was a gesture of deference, not fear. Veqenim qamu ’amadu = “and the aged rose and stood to their feet” — the elders (qenim = aged men) rising in respect is a gesture of honor that the ancient world required for leaders and sages (Lev 19:32: “rise before the gray head”).
29.9: Sarim ’atseru bimillim (princes held back their words) — sarim = princes/officials; ’atsaru = restrained, held back (from ’atsar = to restrain). The highest social class — the princes — fell silent before Job’s wisdom. Vekhaf yasimmu lefiyhem = “and put their hand over their mouth” — the gesture of respectful silence in the presence of greater wisdom (Job 40:4 where Job himself will make this gesture before YHWH from the whirlwind).
29.10: Qol negidim nistatar (the voice of the nobles was hidden/fell silent) — nagid = a leader, ruler, commander (used of David as the designated king before his coronation, 1 Sam 25:30). Uleshon lehekhham davaqah = “and their tongue stuck to the roof of their mouths” — davaq = to cling, stick to (the same verb as Ruth 1:14, “Ruth clung to her mother-in-law”). Complete, involuntary silence before Job’s presence.
29.11: Ki ’ozen sheme’ah vateashereni (for the ear that heard me called me blessed) — ’ishar = to pronounce blessed/happy (the same root as the ’ashrei beatitudes of Ps 1:1; Matt 5:3–11). Ve’ayin ra’atah vate’ideli = “and the eye that saw me testified for me” — both the hearing and seeing of Job’s conduct brought forth spontaneous testimony to his righteousness. The two-witness principle of Deut 19:15 (al-pi shnayim ’edim) is invoked as Job’s universal community endorsement.
29.12: Ki ’amallet ’ani meshavea’ (for I rescued the poor when they cried out) — amallet = I rescued/delivered (piel of palat = to escape, deliver). ’ani meshavea’ = the poor one crying out (shavea’ from shavac = to cry for help; cf. meshavea’ in the Psalms as the cry of the oppressed). Veyatom velo-’ozer lo = “and the fatherless who had no helper” — yatom = orphan (one of the three protected groups in covenant law alongside the widow and stranger, Exod 22:21–22).
29.13: Birkat ’oved ’alai tavo’ (the blessing of the one perishing came upon me) — ’oved = the one perishing/dying (participle of ’abad = to perish). The blessing of the dying person — the final words of a dying man — is the most precious form of blessing in the ancient world (cf. Jacob’s deathbed blessing, Gen 49). Job received these from those he rescued. Velev ’almanah ’arnin = “and the widow’s heart I caused to sing/ring for joy” — ’arnin (hiphil of ranan = to ring out in joy). The widow is the second of the three protected covenant classes.
29.14: Tsedaqah lavashti vatilbasheni (I put on righteousness and it clothed me) — the Hebrew is reflexive: Job clothed himself in tsedaqah (righteousness), and it clothed him in return. The reflexive structure suggests that righteousness is not merely worn like a garment but becomes internalized — the garment takes possession of the wearer. Kemeil umitsaneph mishpati = “my justice was like a robe and turban” — me’il = the outer robe (worn by Samuel, 1 Sam 2:19; by the high priest, Lev 8:7; by princes); tsanif = turban/headdress (worn by the high priest, Exod 28:4). The two vestments are priestly and royal — Job’s justice clothed him in the robes of the double office.
29.15: ’Einayim hayiti la’iver (I was eyes for the blind) — ’einayim = eyes. Job’s justice function was literally to see on behalf of those who could not see (the blind) — to represent their interests in legal proceedings. Veraglaim la’isser ’ani = “and feet for the lame” — ’isser = lame (from ’isar = to be hobbled/lame). The double disability of blindness and lameness appears together in 2 Sam 5:6–8 (the Jebusites taunting David) and in Isa 35:5–6 (the messianic restoration). Job personifies the remedying of these disabilities.
29.16: ’Av ’anokhi la’evyonim (I was a father to the needy) — ’av = father; the patriarchal-protector role taken on behalf of those without a natural advocate. Veriv lo-yadati ’ehqerehu = “and I investigated the case of the one I did not know” — riv = a legal dispute/lawsuit (the same word as the covenant lawsuit form); haqar = to probe/investigate thoroughly. Job not only helped those he knew; he took on the cases of complete strangers, investigating their legal claims.
29.17: Va’ashabbera metsallot ’avval (I broke the fangs/jaws of the wicked) — metsallot = jaws, fangs (of a predatory animal); the wicked is a predator with fangs. Shabbera = I broke/shattered (piel). Umishinav ’ashlikh tered = “and from his teeth I forced the prey” — tered = prey (what is torn); Job forcibly extracted the victim from the wicked’s grip. The metaphor: Job as the anti-predator, rescuing prey from the jaws of the powerful wicked.
29.18: Va’omar ’im-qinni ’egva’ (then I said “in my nest I shall die”) — qen = nest; dying in one’s nest = dying at home surrounded by family and comfort, like a bird dying in its nest. The image is of a peaceful, natural, dignified death at the end of a long, productive life. Vekhahol ’arbeh yamim = “and I shall multiply my days like sand” — khahol = sand (the same comparison used for Abraham’s promised offspring, Gen 22:17). The expectation of long life, many descendants, peaceful death.
29.19: Shorshi patua’ ’el-mayim (my roots spread out to the water) — the tree-by-water imagery for righteous prosperity (Ps 1:3; Jer 17:8). Vetal yalin biqtsiri = “and the dew rested all night on my branches” — tal = dew (the nightly moisture that kept plants alive in the dry season; a symbol of divine blessing, Gen 27:28; Deut 33:28). Job describes his former life as a well-watered tree, rooted deep and refreshed nightly.
29.20: Kevodi hadash ’imadi (my glory was fresh with me) — kavod = glory/honor/weight (the same word used for YHWH’s glory in theophanies). Job’s honor was perpetually fresh, not diminishing. Veqashti beyadi tahalif = “and my bow was continually renewed in my hand” — the bow as a symbol of military and social power (Gen 49:24, Joseph’s bow remains firm; 1 Sam 18:4, Jonathan’s bow given to David). A bow “renewed” is a bow kept at full tension, ready for use.
29.21: Li shomeu veyehhelu (men listened to me and waited) — shomeu = they listened (with focused attention, the kind of hearing that leads to action). Vedammu lemodati = “and held their silence for my counsel” — damam = to be silent, still (cf. Ps 46:10: “be still and know that I am God”). The community’s posture before Job: attentive silence, waiting for his wisdom.
29.22: ’Ahar davari lo yishnnu (after my words they spoke no more) — Job’s words were so comprehensive and authoritative that follow-up discussion was unnecessary. Vetipah ’alay millatam = “and my speech fell upon them” — tipah = to drip/fall (as rain). Job’s word falling on people like rain echoes the anticipation of v23 (they waited for him as for rain).
29.23: Veyihhalu ki-malqosh (they waited for me as for the latter rain) — malqosh = the spring/latter rain (March-April in Israel, the second rainfall season; the critical late rain that filled the grain). The former rain (yoreh) opens the planting season; the malqosh completes the grain. Job’s words were as critically awaited as the spring rain — without which the entire season’s crop would fail. Ufehem patsou limlaqosh = “and they opened their mouths wide as for the spring rain” — the gesture of opening the mouth wide to receive falling rain from heaven.
29.24: ’Eshoq ’alehem lo ya’aminu (when I smiled they could hardly believe it) — shoq = to smile, laugh (used of YHWH laughing/mocking in Ps 2:4 and 37:13). Job’s smile of approval was so valued that receiving it was almost unbelievable to the recipient. Ve’or panay lo yappilu = “and the light of my face they did not let fall” — the “light of the face” is the approval and favor radiating from a leader’s expression (Num 6:25: “YHWH make his face shine upon you”). Job’s community treasured his approval as they treasured divine favor.
29.25: ’Evchar darkam ve’eshev ro’sh (I chose the course for them and sat as head/chief) — ro’sh = head, chief (the primary leader). Job’s leadership was not just reactive but directive: he chose (bahar) their path. Va’eshkon kemayim begedud = “I dwelt as a king among his troops” — gedud = a troop/raiding band; the military unit whose king lives among them. Job was not an absent leader but one who lived within his community. Ka’asher ’avelim yenahhem = “as one who comforts mourners” — naham = to comfort (the same verb used of the friends’ mission at 2:11, lenahhamo). The final verse of Job’s nostalgic portrait identifies the supreme mark of his former leadership: he comforted those who mourned.

5.1: qərāʾ nāʾ hayēš ʿōnekā wəʾel mî miqqəḏōšîm tippāneh: 'Call now; is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?' Eliphaz's rhetorical question deploys the divine council motif: the qəḏōšîm (holy ones) are the heavenly beings who surround YHWH's throne (Ps 89:5, 7; Zech 14:5). His point is that Job has no one in the divine court to intercede for him — a bitter irony since the reader knows Job's case has already been argued before YHWH's council in the prologue. The rhetorical device silences complaint by claiming the heavenly court is closed to the sufferer. Job's longing for an arbiter (9:33) is the direct counterpoint to this dismissal.
5.2: kî-ləʾəwîl yaharōg-kaʿaś ûpōṯeh tāmîṯ qinʾāh: 'For vexation kills the fool, and jealousy slays the simple.' Eliphaz here introduces his retribution framework: emotional excess (kaʿaś, vexation/anger, and qinʾāh, jealousy/zeal) is the mark of the ʾěwîl (the fool who lacks moral discernment). The grammatical subject of the killing is the emotions themselves — vexation is self-destructive. This functions as a veiled accusation: Job's bitter complaint in ch3 is evidence of the very fool-anger that brings ruin. The lexical pair ʾěwîl / pōṯeh (fool / simple) appears together in Proverbs (e.g., Prov 7:7; 22:3) as the two grades of moral deficiency.
5.3: ʾănî-rāʾîṯî ʾěwîl mašrîš wayyiqqōḇ nāwêhû pîṯʾōm: 'I have seen the fool taking root, but suddenly I cursed his dwelling.' The verb mašrîš (taking root, hiphil of šāraš) depicts the apparent stability of the wicked — a Psalm-like image of the wicked flourishing temporarily (cf. Ps 37:35). But pîṯʾōm (suddenly) marks the reversal: the dwelling (nāweh) is cursed. Eliphaz presents this as personal experience (ʾănî rāʾîṯî, I myself have seen), grounding retribution theology in empirical observation rather than divine revelation. Job's speeches will contest precisely this claim to empirical adequacy.
5.4: yirḥăqû bānāyw mîyešaʿ wəyiddakkəʾû ḇaššaʿar wəʾên maṣṣîl: 'His children are far from safety, crushed in the gate, with none to deliver.' The shaʿar (gate) was the site of legal proceedings in ancient Israel: the gate community could vindicate or condemn. To be dāḵaʾ (crushed, beaten down) in the gate is to be legally destroyed without advocate. This resonates brutally with Job's situation: his own children died (1:18-19), and no one interceded. Yet Eliphaz is applying it to the progeny of a hypothetical ʾěwîl — the audience (and reader) cannot miss the implication that Job's children died because of their father's sin. This is precisely the retributive calculus YHWH will condemn in 42:7.
5.5: ʾăšer qəṣîrô rāʿēḇ yōʾḵēl wəʾel-miṣṣinnîm yiqqāḥēhû wəšāʾap̄ ṣammîm ḥêlām: 'The hungry eat his harvest, even from behind thorns, and the thirsty pant after his wealth.' The verse depicts the complete unraveling of the wicked man's household economy — harvest, property, and water all stripped away by predators. The phrase miṣṣinnîm (from behind thorns/a thorn-hedge) suggests even his protective hedgerow is breached. The term ṣammîm is rare and disputed (some translate 'thirsty ones,' others 'snares'); the sense of comprehensive plunder is clear. This economic imagery for divine judgment is conventional in wisdom literature (Prov 13:22; 28:8).
5.6: kî lōʾ-yēṣēʾ meʿāp̄ār ʾāwen wûmēʾăḏāmāh lōʾ-yiṣmaḥ ʿāmāl: 'For affliction does not come from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground.' Eliphaz makes a causal claim: suffering (ʾāwen, trouble/wickedness, and ʿāmāl, toil/misery) is not a random natural phenomenon like weeds springing from the ground. The verse prepares for v.7's counter-assertion. The implicit syllogism: suffering has a cause → causes are moral → therefore suffering implies sin. The verse is theologically sophisticated but logically flawed in Job's case: it confuses 'suffering has a cause' with 'the cause is always the sufferer's sin.'
5.7: kî-ʾāḏām leyāʾmāl yûllāḏ ûḇənê-rešep̄ yagbîhû ʿûp̄: 'For man is born to trouble as the bənê-rešep̄ fly upward.' The phrase bənê-rešep̄ (sons of Resheph) is one of Job's most contested idioms. Resheph was the Canaanite deity of plague and fire (cf. Ps 78:48 where rešep̄ = bolts of fire/lightning). Bənê-rešep̄ thus means either 'sparks' (the visual image of sparks flying upward from a fire) or 'birds of prey' (some ancient versions). The majority view is sparks: just as fire inevitably produces sparks that fly upward, man inevitably produces trouble (ʿāmāl). The verse supports Eliphaz's retribution logic: trouble is as natural a product of human existence as sparks from fire — implying Job's situation is simply the universal lot made visible. Eliphaz intends this as comfort, but Job experiences it as cold.
5.8: ʾûlām ʾănî ʾeḏrōš ʾel-ʾēl wəʾel-ʾělōhîm ʾāśîm diḇrāṯî: 'As for me, I would seek God, and to God I would commit my cause.' Eliphaz now pivots to positive exhortation: the solution to Job's situation is not complaint but petition. The verb dāraš (seek, inquire) has cultic overtones in the OT (seeking YHWH at his sanctuary, consulting an oracle). ʾĒl and ʾělōhîm are used in parallelism here, as they often are in Job (the divine names throughout Job's poetic sections reflect its international/wisdom setting rather than the covenant YHWH-name). The irony: Job is doing exactly what Eliphaz prescribes (seeking God with his complaint), but Eliphaz cannot recognize Job's lament as legitimate petition.
5.9: ʿōśeh gəḏōlōṯ wəʾên ḥēqer nip̄lāʾōṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispar: 'who does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number.' The hymnic participles ʿōśeh... nip̄lāʾōṯ (doing great things... marvelous things) open a doxological section (vv.9-16) that celebrates God's sovereign control of creation and history. Nip̄lāʾōṯ (wonders, marvelous deeds) is the standard vocabulary for divine salvific actions in the Psalms (e.g., Ps 9:1; 26:7; 40:5). The participial construction identifies who God is by what he continuously does. The same vocabulary will appear in YHWH's whirlwind speech (38-41), where creation's incomprehensibility vindicates divine wisdom over human theodicy.
5.10: hannōṯēn māṭār ʿal-pənê-ʾāreṣ wəšōlēaḥ mayim ʿal-pənê ḥûṣôṯ: 'who gives rain on the earth and sends waters on the fields.' The participle chain continues: God's providential supply of rain is cited as evidence of his great deeds. In ancient Near Eastern thought, rain was the prerogative of the storm deity (Baal, in Canaanite religion); here it is attributed to ʾĒl/ʾělōhîm. This verse functions as a polemic: the God who controls rain is the God who controls all life — the same God who controls Job's circumstances. Eliphaz's intent is consolatory (this great God can restore you), but the verse also sets up YHWH's own interrogation of Job: 'Can you send forth lightnings ... do you know the ordinances of heaven and can you establish their rule on the earth?' (38:35, 33).
5.11: lāśûm šəp̄ālîm ləmārôm wəqōḏərîm śāgəbû yešaʿ: 'to set on high those who are lowly, and those who mourn are lifted to safety.' The reversal of fortunes motif (šəp̄ālîm = low ones raised; qōḏərîm = those in mourning/dressed in dark sackcloth, lifted) is a cornerstone of biblical theology of divine justice (cf. 1 Sam 2:7-8 in Hannah's song; Luke 1:52 in the Magnificat). YHWH characteristically reverses social stratifications — the proud brought low, the humble exalted. Eliphaz uses this to suggest that if Job humbles himself (submits to discipline), God will restore him. This theological point is not wrong in itself — the error is the application to Job's specific case as a formula.
5.12: mēp̄êr maḥšəḇôṯ ʿărûmîm wəlōʾ-taʿăśênāh yəḏêhem tûšiyyāh: 'He frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands achieve no success.' The vocabulary here is wisdom-register: ʿărûmîm (crafty/shrewd, the same word as the serpent in Gen 3:1) and tûšiyyāh (abiding success/sound wisdom, a wisdom-literature term, appearing also in Job 11:6; 12:16; Prov 2:7; 3:21; 8:14). God is the one who unravels the schemes of those who use cleverness without reverence. This is the wisdom teaching that human strategy ultimately cannot prevail against divine sovereignty — a point Paul cites in 1 Cor 3:19 from v.13.
5.13: lōkēḏ ḥăkāmîm bəʿormāṯām wăʿăṣaṯ nip̄tālîm niməhārāh: 'He catches the wise in their own craftiness, and the schemes of the wily are brought to a quick end.' ʿormāh (craftiness) is the instrument of their own entrapment: divine irony uses the wicked's own cleverness as the net. The hapax nip̄tālîm (twisted/wily ones, from pāṯal = to twist, wrestle) appears in Gen 30:8 of Jacob's wrestling and in Prov 8:8 of perverse speech. Paul quotes v.13 in 1 Cor 3:19 ('He catches the wise in their craftiness') as part of his argument that worldly wisdom is foolishness before God — a direct citation that establishes Paul's awareness of the Job context and its anti-sophistry thrust.
5.14: yômām yip̄gəšû-ḥōšeḵ ûḵallaylāh yəmašəšû ḇaṣṣohărāyim: 'They meet with darkness in the daytime and grope at noonday as in the night.' The inversion of light/dark (day becomes darkness, noon becomes night-groping) echoes the Deuteronomic covenant curse: 'You shall grope at noonday, as the blind grope in darkness' (Deut 28:29). This is covenant-curse language applied to the crafty enemies of God — a bold claim that the wicked are experiencing the darkness of divine judgment even in their apparent success. The image of groping in darkness will recur in Job 12:25 where Job applies it to YHWH's sovereign confusion of the mighty.
5.15: wayyōšaʿ mēḥereḇ mippîhem ûmiyadꞌ ḥāzāq ʾeḇyôn: 'But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty.' The transition from the wicked being frustrated (vv.12-14) to the needy being saved completes the reversal structure: God confounds the powerful and delivers the powerless. ʾeḇyôn (the needy/destitute) is the standard vocabulary in the Psalms and prophets for the socially vulnerable who depend entirely on divine justice (Ps 9:18; 12:5; 35:10; Amos 2:6; Isa 25:4). The ḥereḇ pîhem (sword of their mouth) anticipates the NT's metaphor of the word as a sword (Heb 4:12; Rev 1:16).
5.16: wattəhî laddal tiqwāh wəʿolātāh qāp̄əṣāh-pîhā: 'So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts her mouth.' The verse summarizes the theodicy argument of vv.11-16: divine action on behalf of the lowly generates hope (tiqwāh) for the dal (poor/weak) and silences ʿolātāh (injustice, wrongdoing — from ʿāwal, to be crooked/unjust). Injustice is personified as a speaker whose mouth is shut — a courtroom image. The hope of the poor is a crucial OT theological motif (cf. Ps 9:18: 'For the needy shall not always be forgotten, and the hope of the poor shall not perish forever'). Eliphaz uses this as comfort for Job, assuming Job is among the poor who will be vindicated — if he submits.
5.17: hinnēh ʾašrê ʾěnôš yôkiḥennû ʾělôah ûmûsar šadday ʾal-timʾās: 'Behold, blessed is the one whom God reproves; therefore despise not the discipline of the Almighty.' The ʾašrê formula (beatitude: blessed/happy is...) is the opening of Psalm 1 and a key wisdom genre marker. Applied here to the one who receives divine reproof (yāḵaḥ), it presents suffering-as-discipline as a mark of divine favor. Šadday (the Almighty, El Shaddai) is the primary divine name in Job's poetic sections (31 of the 48 OT occurrences are in Job) — likely reflecting the wisdom literature's use of a more universal divine name appropriate to a non-Israelite setting. Hebrews 12:5-6 quotes this verse directly: 'My son, do not regard lightly the discipline of the Lord, nor be weary when reproved by him. For the Lord disciplines the one he loves.' The NT citation transforms the verse from retribution comfort into positive theology of sonship.
5.18: kî hûʾ yakʾîḇ wəyeḥbāš yimḥaṣ wəyādāyw tirp̄ênāh: 'For he wounds, but he binds up; he shatters, but his hands heal.' The divine antithesis (wound/bind, shatter/heal) is a foundational OT theological pattern: YHWH kills and makes alive (Deut 32:39; 1 Sam 2:6); he brings down to Sheol and raises up. The verbs ḵāʾaḇ (to be in pain, cause pain) and ḥāḇaš (to bind up, as a wound) describe the same medical action — infliction followed by treatment. The theological claim is that divine judgment and divine restoration come from the same hands: suffering is not evidence of abandonment but of active divine engagement. This verse underpins the book's theodicy: the hands that wound are the hands that will bind — but the timing is God's, not ours.
5.19: ḇəšēš ṣārôṯ yaṣṣîleḵā ûḇəšeḇaʿ lōʾ-yiggaʿ bəḵā rāʿ: 'He will deliver you from six troubles; in seven no evil shall touch you.' The numerical saying (x, x+1) is a classic wisdom literary form (cf. Prov 6:16: six things YHWH hates, seven are an abomination; Amos 1:3: for three transgressions, for four; Eccl 11:2). The pattern implies completeness-beyond-completeness: no matter how many troubles come, YHWH delivers. The sevenfold structure frames vv.20-22 as illustrations. The verse is genuine pastoral comfort but employs a formula that, as the epilogue shows, Job's friends applied mechanically rather than discerningly.
5.20: ḇərāʿāḇ pāḏəḵā mimmāweṯ ûḇamilḥāmāh mîyəḏê-ḥāreḇ: 'In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.' The verb pāḏāh (redeem, ransom) is the commercial/legal term for buying someone out of slavery or death-obligation. It is used in the OT for YHWH's ransoming Israel from Egypt (Deut 7:8; 15:15) and will be used by Job himself in the hope that his gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer, 19:25) will vindicate him. The pairing of famine and sword echoes the classic triad of divine judgment (sword, famine, pestilence, cf. Jer 14:12; Ezek 5:12) — but here reversed into forms of divine deliverance.
5.21: bəšōṭ lāšôn tiṣṣāp̄ēn wəlōʾ-ṯîrāʾ mîššōḏ kî yāḇôʾ: 'You shall be hidden from the lash of the tongue, and shall not fear destruction when it comes.' Šôṭ lāšôn (scourge/lash of the tongue) is a vivid hapax for the destructive power of slander or false accusation. Šōḏ (destruction, devastation) is a standard prophetic term for sudden ruin (Isa 13:6; Joel 1:15; Zeph 1:15, where it occurs in the formula šōḏ miššadday, destruction from the Almighty). The promise of protection from tongue-violence is especially resonant in Job's situation: in the dialogues, the friends' words themselves become a kind of scourging.
5.22: ləšōḏ ûlərāʿāḇ tiśḥāq ûmijjayaṯ hāʾāreṣ ʾal-ṯîrāʾ: 'At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and shall not fear the beasts of the earth.' The image of laughing at destruction (śāḥaq) echoes the leviathan's response to Job's weapons in the whirlwind speech (41:29: 'He laughs at the rattle of the javelin'). The restoration of the righteous reverses the threat: what once brought terror becomes occasion for confident laughter. The promise of peace with nature's beasts (v.23) continues the Edenic-restoration imagery that appears throughout the OT in descriptions of eschatological restoration (Isa 11:6-9; 65:25).
5.23: kî ʿim-ʾaḇnê haśśāḏeh bəriṯeḵā ûḥayyaṯ haśśāḏeh hišlěmāh-lāḵ: 'For you shall be in league with the stones of the field, and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with you.' The covenant (bərît) with stones is unusual and debated — it may mean either a covenant of safety with the land itself (stones/rocks as obstacles removed) or an alliance metaphor (the land's hostility is dissolved). The šālôm-peace with wild animals evokes Hosea 2:18's eschatological covenant: 'I will make for them a covenant on that day with the beasts of the field, the birds of the heavens, and the creeping things of the ground.' The restoration language anticipates the new creation's peace but Eliphaz applies it pragmatically as a condition of Job's repentance-and-restoration.
5.24: wəyāḏaʿtā kî-šālôm ʾohŏleḵā ûp̄āqaḏtā nāweḵā wəlōʾ ṯeḥeṭāʾ: 'You shall know that your tent is at peace, and you shall inspect your fold and miss nothing.' Šālôm (wholeness, peace, well-being) of the tent is the culminating domestic image of restoration — the home as secure refuge. The verb pāqaḏ (to visit, inspect, take inventory) when applied to flocks means to count and find everything present. Nāweh (dwelling, fold, pasture) is the same word used in v.3 of the fool whose dwelling was cursed — the contrast is deliberate: repent, and your nāweh will be inspected with satisfaction rather than cursed.
5.25: wəyāḏaʿtā kî-raḇ zarʿeḵā ûṣeʾěṣāʾeḵā kəʿēśeḇ hāʾāreṣ: 'You shall know also that your offspring shall be many, and your descendants as the grass of the earth.' The restoration of children and descendants completes the picture: the specific losses of Job's prologue (children killed, 1:18-19) are directly addressed. The comparison to grass (ʿēśeḇ hāʾāreṣ) echoes the Abrahamic blessing language — offspring as innumerable as stars/sand/dust (Gen 13:16; 15:5; 22:17). Eliphaz presents the covenant blessing pattern as the natural result of submission to discipline. The epilogue does in fact restore Job's children (42:13-16), but not through Eliphaz's mechanism of formulaic repentance.
5.26: tāḇôʾ ḇeqeleḥ ʾeleyqāḇer kiʿălôṯ gāḏîš bəʿittô: 'You shall come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf gathered up in its season.' Qeleḥ (ripe age, full vigor) is a hapax legomenon describing the peak of physical maturity — death at the right time, not prematurely. The agricultural simile (gāḏîš, a harvest stack/shock of grain) gathered bəʿittô (in its season/time) is the ideal death: the full-term harvest, not green grain cut down untimely. This directly addresses Job's earlier lament about dying in misery (3:11: 'Why did I not die at birth?'). The ideal death in wisdom literature is the śeḇaʿ yāmîm (full of days, Gen 35:29; Job 42:17) — Job's restoration will fulfill exactly this promise.
5.27: hinnēh-zōʾṯ ḥăqarnûhā kēn-hîʾ šəmaʿāh wəʾattāh ḏaʿ-lāḵ: 'Behold, this we have searched out; it is true. Hear, and know it for your good.' Eliphaz closes his speech with an appeal to communal investigation: ḥāqar (to search out, investigate) is the wisdom vocabulary for careful inquiry (Job 8:8; Prov 25:2; Eccl 1:13). The plural 'we have searched' invokes the authority of the wisdom tradition — these are not Eliphaz's personal opinions but the collected findings of the sages. The final imperative ḏaʿ-lāḵ (know it for yourself, take it to heart) is pastoral but also condescending: Eliphaz presents himself as transmitting received wisdom to an ignorant sufferer. YHWH's verdict in 42:7 will demolish this claim to certainty.

6.1: wayyaʿan ʾiyyôḇ wayyōʾmar: Standard dialogue formula introducing Job's reply. The verb ʿānāh (to answer, respond) followed by the verb of speech is the LXX's standard rendering throughout the dialogues. Job's reply (chs. 6-7) has two distinct movements: an address to the three friends (6:1-30) defending the legitimacy of his complaint, and a direct address to God (7:1-21) that is essentially a lament psalm. The shift in addressee (human interlocutors → God) is a key structural feature of the dialogue sections.
6.2: lû-šāqôl yiššāqēl kaʿaśî wəhawwāṯî ḇammōʾznayim yiśśāʾû yaḥaḏ: 'Oh that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances.' The weighing image (šāqal, to weigh; mōʾznayim, scales/balances) invokes the Egyptian judgment scene but also the wisdom tradition of measuring evidence carefully. Kaʿaś (vexation, grief, provocation) is the same word Eliphaz used in 5:2 as the emotion that kills the fool — Job takes that word and proposes to weigh it objectively. Hawwāh (calamity, ruin) is a strong term (cf. Ps 5:9; 52:2; 55:11; Prov 17:4). Job's argument: if his distress were actually measured, the friends would understand why his words have been extreme.
6.3: kî-ʿattāh mîḥôl yammîm yiḵbād ʿal-kēn ḏəḇāray lāʿû: 'For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea; therefore my words have been rash.' The comparison to sand (ḥôl yammîm, sand of the sea) invokes the Abrahamic innumerable-descendants image but reverses it: Job's suffering outweighs even the immeasurable. Lāʿû (rash, impetuous — from lûaʿ, to swallow/gulp) is a self-critique: Job admits his words have been hasty. This is not a retraction of their content (he will maintain his innocence throughout) but an acknowledgment that extreme suffering produces extreme speech — and that extreme speech deserves the weight of the suffering that produced it, not the dismissal Eliphaz offered.
6.4: kî ḥiṣṣê šadday ʿimmāḏî ʾăšer ḥămaṯām šōṯāh rûḥî biʿûṯê ʾělôah yaʿărəḵûnnî: 'For the arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.' This is one of Job's most intense images. Ḥiṣṣê šadday (arrows of the Almighty) is warrior-deity imagery: God as divine archer whose arrows have found their mark in Job. The image of ḥamāh (poison/venom) drunk by the spirit extends the metaphor — these are envenomed arrows. Biʿûṯê ʾělôah (terrors of God) uses the rare biʿûṯāh (a terrifying event, hapax) for divine assault. Job is not complaining about natural illness but about experienced divine aggression — a theological claim of the highest seriousness. The friends interpret suffering as consequence of sin; Job experiences it as direct divine action, which he cannot reconcile with his innocence.
6.5: hăyinʿaq-pereʾ ʿălê-ḏešeʾ ʾim-yigʿeh-šôr ʿal-bəlîlô: 'Does the wild donkey bray when he has grass, or the ox low over his fodder?' The first of two animal analogies defending the naturalness of complaint under extreme conditions. The wild ass (pereʾ) only brays when it lacks grass; the ox only lows when its feed (bəlîl, mixed fodder) is absent. Job's argument: my cries are not complaints without cause — they are the natural sound of an animal in genuine distress. The analogy disarms the friend's criticism by appealing to observable animal behavior: complaint is evidence of need, not of character deficiency. This is a more honest account of lament than Eliphaz's formula allows.
6.6: hăyēʾāḵēl ṯāp̄ēl mibbəlî-mālaḥ ʾim-yēš-ṭaʿam bəriyr ḥallāmûṯ: 'Can that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any taste in the juice of the mallow?' The second food analogy shifts from animal hunger to human appetite. Ṯāp̄ēl (insipid, tasteless, a hapax) describes food that cannot be eaten without mālaḥ (salt), the standard preservative and flavor-agent of the ancient world. Ḥallāmûṯ (mallow/purslane) is another hapax; its juice was considered medicinal but bitter. Job's point: just as tasteless food requires salt to be acceptable, his extreme words are the natural seasoning of unbearable suffering. He is not apologizing for his speech but contextualizing it.
6.7: mēʾănāh lānəgôaʿ nap̄šî hēmmāh kidwê laḥmî: 'My appetite refuses to touch them; they are as food that is loathsome to me.' Nep̄eš (here rendered 'appetite,' but also soul/self/desire) refuses (māʾan) the friends' counsel because it is loathsome (dōweh, sick, faint, also used of menstrual illness, Lev 15:33, Lam 1:13 — a term of visceral revulsion). The verse connects back to the food imagery of vv.6-7: just as tasteless food is unpalatable, the friends' words are nauseating in their inadequacy. The self is not merely intellectually disagreeing with the friends but experiencing their counsel as physically repellent — a sign of the depth of his suffering and their failure to reach him.
6.8: mî-yiṯṯēn ṯāḇôʾ šeʾělāṯî wəṯiqwāṯî yiṯṭēn ʾělôah: 'Oh that I might have my request, and that God would fulfill my hope.' The mî-yiṯṯēn (who will grant? / oh that!) formula is Job's characteristic expression of desperate longing (cf. 11:5; 13:5; 14:4, 13; 19:23; 23:3; 29:2). It signals a wish for something that seems impossible given the current situation. Šeʾělāh (request, petition) and tiqwāh (hope, expectation) pair the specific petition with the underlying orientation of faith: Job still expects something from God, even in extremity. His hope (tiqwāh) is not yet extinguished — which is why the specific content of the wish (death, v.9) is so stark.
6.9: wəyōʾēl ʾělôah wîdakkəʾēnî yatṭēr yāḏô wîḇaṣṣəʿēnî: 'that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off.' The wish is for death by divine action: dāḵāʾ (to crush, oppress; the same root as the children 'crushed in the gate' in 5:4) and bāṣaʿ (to cut off/through, possibly from weaving: to cut the thread of life). Job does not wish for suicide but for God to complete what he has begun: if YHWH is against him, let the divine hand act decisively rather than prolonging the anguish. This is not despair without faith — it is a theodicy challenge: if God is going to crush me, do it; the prolonged suffering is the unbearable part.
6.10: wəṯəhî ʿôḏ niḥāmāṯî waʾăsallēḏāh bəḥîlāh lōʾ-yaḥmôl kî-lōʾ kiḥaḏtî ʾimrê qāḏôš: 'This would be my comfort; I would even exult in pain unsparing, for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.' Remarkable: death would be Job's comfort (niḥāmāh, consolation). But the grounds are theologically significant: he has not denied (kāḥaḏ, to hide, deny, suppress) the words of the Holy One (ʾimrê qāḏôš). Qāḏôš as a divine epithet is relatively rare in Job and occurs here as a term of absolute divine holiness. Job's innocence is grounded not in moral self-righteousness but in fidelity to divine speech: he has not suppressed or denied God's word. This is the book's central claim: suffering does not require either denial of God or denial of one's integrity.
6.11: māh-kōḥî kî-ʾăyaḥēl ûmah-qiṣṣî kî-ʾaʾărîḵ nap̄šî: 'What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?' The rhetorical questions confront the friends' demand for patience with physical and existential realism. Kōaḥ (strength, power) and qēṣ (end, limit) define the parameters of endurance: how much strength does a man have, and what end justifies the suffering? The verb yāḥal (to hope, wait) is the standard wisdom-psalm term for patient trust (Ps 27:14; 31:24; 33:18, 20, 22; 130:5, 7). Job is not rejecting hope in God but challenging the friends' easy prescription of patience by asking: what does patience look like when strength is gone?
6.12: ʾim-kōḥ ʾăḇānîm kōḥî ʾim-bəśārî nāḥûš: 'Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh bronze?' The rhetorical answer is obvious: no. ʾăḇānîm (stones) and nāḥûš (bronze/copper) represent indestructible materials — the human body is neither. The question exposes the unrealism of the friends' counsel: they prescribe endurance for a body that is flesh and blood, not stone and bronze. The image will recur in the Servant Songs: 'I have set my face like a flint' (Isa 50:7), where resolution in suffering requires divine strengthening precisely because human flesh is not stone.
6.13: hăʾim ʾên ʿezrāṯî ḇî wəṯûšiyyāh niḏḏəḥāh mimmennî: 'Have I any help in me, when resource is driven from me?' ʿEzrāh (help, resource within oneself) and tûšiyyāh (abiding success/sound wisdom, the same wisdom term as in 5:12) are both absent. Job is not feigning weakness as a rhetorical move — he is diagnosing his actual condition: all inner and outer resources have been expelled (nāḏaḥ, driven away). The verse bridges from Job's self-assessment (vv.11-12) to his indictment of the friends (v.14ff): when a man has no internal resource left, what he needs is ḥeseḏ from a friend, not reproof.
6.14: lammās mērēʿēhû ḥāseḏ wəyirʾaṯ šadday yaʿăzôḇ: 'He who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty.' One of Job's most theologically dense verses. Ḥeseḏ (covenant lovingkindness, steadfast loyalty) is the defining term of YHWH's covenant character (Exod 34:6-7) and is here claimed as an obligation owed between friends. To withhold ḥeseḏ from a mās (one who is dissolving/despairing — the participle of māsas, to dissolve, melt) is to ʿāzaḇ (forsake) the fear of the Almighty: cruelty to a suffering friend is tantamount to apostasy. The verse inverts the friends' logic: they claimed Job's suffering proves his sin; Job claims the friends' failure of ḥeseḏ proves their unfaithfulness. The NT fulfillment: Christ's ḥeseḏ toward the suffering is the embodiment of this verse (Matt 11:28-29).
