6.15: ʾaḥay ḇāgəḏû kəmô-nāḥal kiʾăp̄îqê nəḥālîm yaʿăḇōrû: 'My brothers are treacherous as a torrent-bed, as torrential streams that pass away.' The wadi metaphor begins: nāḥal (wadi, seasonal stream channel) and ʾāp̄îq (water channel, river-bed) are terms for the wadis of the Levant and Sinai — dry beds that flood dramatically in rainy season and run completely dry in summer. The simile indicts the friends for the most fundamental form of relational betrayal: they present themselves as reliable support but, like wadis, are present only when conditions are favorable. Bāgaḏ (treacherous, to deal deceitfully) is the term used for covenant betrayal (Jer 3:20; Hos 5:7; Mal 2:10, 11, 14, 15, 16).
6.16: haqqōḏərîm miqqāraḥ ʿălêhem yiṯʿallam-šāleḡ: 'which are dark with ice, and where the snow hides itself.' The wadi in winter/early spring: black with melted snow and hidden ice (qāraḥ, ice/frost). The streams appear full and reliable — but this is temporary, as vv.17-20 explain. The word yiṯʿallam (to hide itself, conceal) will recur in Job's speeches in the context of God's hiddenness (13:24: 'Why do you hide your face?'). The wadi that hides its snow/reliability anticipates God's hiddenness as another form of the friends' unreliability — or perhaps vice versa.
6.17: bəʿēṯ yəzōrəbû niṣmāṯû bəḥummô dāʿăkû mimməqômām: 'When they melt, they disappear; when it is hot, they vanish from their place.' The summer reversal: zāraḇ (to scorch, cause to vanish through heat) and dāʿaḵ (to be quenched, extinguished — used of lamplight going out, Prov 13:9; 20:20) describe the wadi's summer disappearance. The very heat that travelers hope will make a stream more accessible dries it completely. The theology of the friends as wadis: they appeared reliable in comfortable times; Job's extreme suffering is the summer heat that revealed their absence. Real friendship, like real ḥeseḏ, must survive the heat.
6.18: yiləpəṯû ʾorḥôṯ darkām yaʿălû ḇattōhû wayyōʾḇēḏû: 'The caravans of Tema look, the travelers of Sheba hope.' Wait — this verse reads: 'the caravans turn aside from their paths; they go up into the waste and perish.' The travelers — not yet mentioned — are arriving at a wadi that has gone dry. Ṯēmāʾ (Tema) is the Arabian trading station (Job 1:15; Isa 21:14; Jer 25:23) and Šəḇāʾ (Sheba) is the South Arabian trading kingdom (Job 1:15; 1 Kgs 10:1-13): both are trading routes dependent on water sources. When the expected wadi is dry, the caravan must abandon its planned route and perish in the desert (tōhû, the wasteland — the same word as Gen 1:2's formless void).
6.19: ḥăḇallôṯ ṯēmāʾ hibbiṭû ʾorḥôṯ šəḇāʾ qiwwû-lāmô: 'They are ashamed because they were confident; they come there and are disappointed.' The merchants' confidence (bāṭaḥ, to trust, rely on) turns to ḇōšāh (shame, disappointment) — the covenantal vocabulary of trust betrayed. The wadi that promised water delivered nothing. The traveler's expectation (qāwāh, to wait/hope/expect) was false. Job applies this directly to his own experience of the friends: he came to them in distress, expecting the ḥeseḏ that friends owe; he received reproof instead. His disappointment is not petulance but the deep disorientation of betrayed trust.
6.20: kî-yāḵēlû bāṭaḥ bāhū wîʾātā-ʿāḏêhā wayḇōšû: 'For you have now become nothing; you see my calamity and are afraid.' The application becomes explicit: ʾattāh (you — plural, addressing the friends directly) are nothing (lōʾ-hāyāh in v.21 makes this clearer). The verb yārēʾ (to be afraid) suggests that the friends are frightened by Job's calamity: his radical suffering threatens their retribution theology. If Job suffers, and Job is innocent, then the theology that suffering = sin is false — and their own security is threatened. The friends' unwillingness to offer ḥeseḏ may be partly defensive: to defend Job's innocence would require dismantling their own framework.
6.21: kî-ʿattāh hĕyîṯem lōʾ tirʾû ḥāṯaṯ wayyîrāʾû: 'Such you have become to me; you see my terror, and you are afraid.' The final diagnosis: the friends have become (hāyāh lĕ-) to Job what the dry wadi is to the caravan — nothing where there should have been water. Their fear (ḥāṯaṯ, dismay/shattered confidence; yārēʾ, afraid) is the response of men whose theological system is destabilized by Job's case. Job's 'terror' (ḥittat) is the very extremity that should have activated their ḥeseḏ — instead it activated their self-protective theology.
6.22: hăkî ʾāmartî hāḇû-lî wûmiḵōḥăkem šiḥăḏû ḇaʿăḏî: 'Have I said, "Make me a gift"? Or, "From your wealth offer a bribe for me"?' Job's forensic challenge: he is not asking for money (maṯṯān, a gift/bribe) or intercession through payment (šōḥad, a bribe/present). He wants something more costly: honesty. The introduction of bribery vocabulary (šōḥad) is significant — Job is not manipulating the friends for financial advantage; he is asking for the ḥeseḏ that costs nothing and yet is beyond their capacity to give: recognition of his innocence.
6.23: wûmiyyadꞌ-ṣar palṭûnî ûmiyyad ʿārîṣîm tipdûnî: 'Or, "Deliver me from the adversary's hand"? Or, "Redeem me from the hand of the ruthless"?' Job's forensic defense continues: he has asked for no material favor, no political intercession, no rescue from enemies. The vocabulary of rescue (pālaṭ, to deliver; pāḏāh, to ransom/redeem) would normally describe prayers for divine deliverance — Job applies it to what he could have asked of the friends but didn't. His actual request is simpler and harder: tell me where I went wrong.
6.24: hôrûnî waʾănî ʾaḥărîš ûmah-šāḡîṯî hāḇînû lî: 'Teach me, and I will be silent; make me understand how I have gone astray.' A genuine opening: Job commits to silence and learning if the friends can demonstrate his specific sin. Šāgāh (to wander astray, to err) is the verb for inadvertent moral error (Num 15:22; Ps 119:21, 118) rather than deliberate rebellion. Job is not claiming absolute sinlessness — he is claiming he is not aware of the specific sin that could account for his suffering. The offer of silence (ḥārîš) in exchange for evidence is a forensic proposition: show me the evidence, and I will concede. The friends never accept this challenge on its terms.
6.25: māh-niməṣû diḇrê-yōšer ûmāh-yôkîaḥ hôkēaḥ mikkkem: 'How forceful are upright words! But what does reproof from you reprove?' Niməṣû (are powerful, from māraṣ — to be sharp, strong, forceful) is used only a few times in Job (16:3; 1 Kgs 2:8). Upright (yōšer) words have genuine force; but the friends' reproof (hôkîaḥ, the same verb as disciplinary reproof in 5:17) has no power because it is not upright. The paradox: the friends claim to be offering true wisdom (as Eliphaz presented it in 5:27 as searched-out truth), but Job claims their reproof fails to reprove because it does not match his actual case.
6.26: halhôkîaḥ millîm taḥšōḇû wələrûaḥ ʾimrê nōʾāš: 'Do you think that you can reprove words, when the speech of a despairing man is wind?' The verbal reproof of a nōʾāš (despairing one, from yāʾaš — to despair, be without hope) is like criticizing wind: the wind cannot help its direction. Rûaḥ (wind/spirit/breath) here means the naturally uncontrolled outflow of despair. Job's defense of his extreme language: to reprove the speech of a despairing man is to miss the point — the speech is evidence, not sin; the despair requires comfort, not correction. The same point John Calvin makes in his commentary: 'It is unreasonable to require from the sick the same moderation as from those who are well.'
6.27: ʾap̄-ʿal-yāṯôm tappîlû wətiḵrû ʿal-rēʿăkem: 'You would even cast lots over the fatherless, and bargain over your friend.' The accusation escalates to its sharpest point: the friends are behaving like those who gamble over a fatherless child's inheritance (yāṯôm, orphan — the most vulnerable category in OT law) and make commercial transactions over their friend (kārāh, to dig/trade/bargain). The image of casting lots over the helpless will be fulfilled with horrific literalism at the crucifixion (Ps 22:18; John 19:24: 'they divided my garments among them and for my clothing they cast lots'). Job, stripped of everything, is being treated like the destitute — by his own friends.
6.28: wəʿattāh hôʾîlû p̄ənû-ḇî wəʿal-p̄ənêkem ʾim-ʾăḵazzēḇ: 'But now, be pleased to look at me, for I will not lie to your face.' The appeal to direct confrontation: p̄ānāh (to turn toward, face) and ʾim-ʾăḵazzēḇ (I will not lie, the conditional absolute oath). Job invites the friends to look at him directly — not through their theological framework — and commits his own speech to truthfulness. The contrast with the friend's wadi-like unreliability (v.15) is pointed: Job remains transparent even when they do not. The appeal for direct eye contact is a demand for person-to-person engagement over abstract theological dispute.
6.29: šûḇû-nāʾ ʾal-tehî ʿawlāh wəšûḇû ʿôḏ ṣiḏqî-ḇāh: 'Please turn; let no injustice be done. Turn now; my vindication is at stake.' The repeated šûḇ (turn back, return) is the standard repentance/return verb — here Job uses it against the friends: they need to repent of their false accusation. ʿawlāh (injustice, perversity) is what is being done to Job through false verdicts. Ṣiḏqî-ḇāh (my righteousness is still there/in it) is a claim that Job's integrity is at stake in this dispute — not just his feelings but his covenantal standing before God. The forensic register is fully deployed: Job is not merely complaining, he is making a legal claim.
6.30: hăyēš-bəlāšônî ʿawlāh ʾim-ḥikkî lōʾ-yāḇîn hawwôṯ: 'Is there any injustice on my tongue? Cannot my palate discern calamity?' Job closes his address to the friends with a self-appeal to sensory discernment: his tongue (lāšôn) is not producing ʿawlāh (injustice/perversity) and his palate (ḥēḵ) can distinguish hawwôṯ (calamity, a strong word for ruin — same root as v.2's 'calamity'). The taste metaphor connects back to the food imagery of vv.5-7: just as the palate can distinguish between food that is wholesome and food that is corrupt, Job's palate can distinguish between genuine calamity and imagined complaint. His speech is not corrupt; it is the accurate report of what a discerning palate has tasted.

7.1: hălōʾ-ṣāḇāʾ lěʾěnôš ʿal-ʾāreṣ waḵîmê śāḵîr yāmāyw: 'Has not man a hard service on earth, and are not his days like the days of a hired hand?' Ṣāḇāʾ (hard service, military conscription, compulsory service) is the term used for the Levites' temple service (Num 4:3, 23, 30, 35, 39, 43) and for military service (Num 1:3; 31:3). When applied to human existence generally, it frames life itself as involuntary conscription into a campaign with no discharge. Śāḵîr (hired laborer, day-worker) adds the dimension of economic servitude: both the soldier and the day-worker live entirely at the disposal of another, waiting for the day to end. Job's first verse of his divine soliloquy frames the entire human condition as servitude — a searing counter to the creation theology that calls humanity the crown of creation (Gen 1:26-28; Ps 8:4-8).
7.2: kəʿeḇeḏ yišʾap̄ ṣēl wəḵəśāḵîr yəqawweh pōʿolô: 'Like a slave who longs for the shadow, and like a hired hand who looks for his wages.' ʾĀḇeḏ (slave) and śāḵîr (hired laborer) continue the labor metaphor. The slave longs for ṣēl (shade — the midday rest that provides relief from the killing sun) and the day-worker longs for pōʿăl (wages — the moment of completion and reward). Both are endurance-under-duress: the slave endures the heat for the shade; the laborer endures the work for the wage. Job's comparison: his life is exactly this — endurance waiting for a relief that does not come (v.3: months of emptiness).
7.3: kēn huḥlaḥtî yarhê-šāwəʾ wəlêlôṯ ʿāmāl sippərû-lî: 'so I am allotted months of emptiness, and nights of misery are apportioned to me.' The passive verb huḥlaḥ (allotted, from nāḥal, to inherit/receive as a portion) makes YHWH implicit subject: Job has been given an inheritance of šāwəʾ (emptiness, vanity, futility — the same root as Ecclesiastes' šāwəʾ). The months and nights are precisely counted: sippərû-lî (are numbered for me, apportioned to me by divine accounting). The verse is a deliberate echo of the creation week's structure (numbered days) but applied to suffering rather than good work — Job's existence is a creation-week of misery.
7.4: ʾim-šāḵaḇtî wəʾāmartî māṯay ʾāqûm ûmiḏḏad-ʿereḇ wəśāḇaʿtî nəḏuḏîm ʿăḏê-nešep̄: 'When I lie down I say, "When shall I arise?" But the night is long, and I am full of tossing till the dawn.' The daily cycle of hope and disappointment: lying down triggers the wish for morning (māṯay ʾāqûm, when shall I rise?); but the evening (ʿereḇ) brings nəḏuḏîm (tossing, restlessness — from nāḏaḏ, to flee/wander) until dawn (nešep̄). Sleep, the natural human refuge from suffering, is denied. This insomnia-of-suffering becomes a liturgical image in the Psalms: 'in the night his song is with me' (Ps 42:8) and 'I call out by day but you do not answer, and by night, but I find no rest' (Ps 22:2).
7.5: lāḇaš bəśārî rimmāh wəḡûš ʿāp̄ār ʿôrî rāgaʿ wayyimʾās: 'My flesh is clothed with worms and dirt; my skin hardens, then breaks out afresh.' The physical description of Job's illness: rimmāh (worms, maggots — associated with bodily decomposition, Exod 16:24; Isa 14:11; 66:24) clothing the flesh while living; gûš ʿāp̄ār (crusted with dirt/dust) and the skin cycle of hardening (rāgaʿ, to harden/close) and breaking open (māʾas, here: to run or break out afresh — disputed). The language anticipates the resurrection: the body being clothed with worms will one day be clothed with incorruption (1 Cor 15:53-54). The same Hebrew verb lāḇaš (clothed with) connects the present horror to the eschatological hope: what is now clothed with worms will be clothed with immortality.
7.6: yāmay qallû miʾāreg wayyiḵlû bəʾep̄es tiqwāh: 'My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle and come to their end without hope.' ʾĀreg (weaver's shuttle — the wooden implement that passes the weft thread through the warp at high speed) is one of three rapid-motion images Job uses for the passing of life (cf. 9:25-26: swifter than a runner, swift as papyrus boats, as an eagle swooping). The shuttle moves so quickly the eye can barely follow it. But the modifier is devastating: bəʾep̄es tiqwāh (with the ending of hope, without hope) — the swift passage of days is not the harmless speed of a full life but the exhaustion of hope. ʾĚp̄es (nothing, ceasing) qualifies tiqwāh (hope) as entirely spent.
7.7: zəḵōr kî-rûaḥ ḥayyāy lōʾ-ṯāšûḇ ʿênî lirʾôṯ ṭôḇ: 'Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will never again see good.' The address shifts to God: zəḵōr (remember!) is the imperative plea of the lament psalms (Ps 25:6, 7; 74:2, 18, 22; 89:47, 50; 109:14; Lam 5:1) — calling YHWH to act by recalling the covenant. Rûaḥ ḥayyāy (breath of my life, spirit of my life) echoes the creation-breath of Gen 2:7 (nišmaṯ ḥayyîm). The plea grounds divine attention in the brevity of human life: 'Remember that I am a breath before you act.' The eye not seeing good (ṭôḇ) echoes the creation narrative's 'God saw that it was good' — Job is experiencing un-creation.
7.8: lōʾ-ṯəšûrənî ʿên rōʾî ʿênêḵā bî wəʾênennî: 'The eye of him who sees me will behold me no more; while your eyes are on me, I shall be gone.' The verse has a double edge: the human eye (ʿên rōʾî, the eye of the one who sees me) will see him no more at death; but also: God's eyes (ʿênêḵā, your eyes) are fixed on Job — and if God does not act before Job dies, the opportunity for vindication is gone. The theological claim: the window for divine response is brief. Death is not the problem solved but the deadline missed. The urgency of the divine address in v.7-21 is entirely shaped by this: Job is dying, and God must act now or not at all.
7.9: kālāh ʿānān wayyelāḵ kēn yôrēḏ šəʾôl lōʾ yaʿăleh: 'As the cloud fades and vanishes, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come up.' The cloud analogy (ʿānān kālāh wayyelāḵ, cloud that wastes away and goes) is an image of irreversible disappearance — the morning mist burned off by the sun. Šəʾôl (the realm of the dead) is the destination from which there is no return (lōʾ yaʿăleh, does not come up). Job's view here is entirely conventional pre-resurrection — death is final, the realm of the shades is one-directional. The NT's answer is Easter: 'But God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24). Jesus's descent to and ascent from the place of the dead (šəʾôl in the OT, Hades in the LXX) is the direct reversal of the 'does not come up' of v.9.
7.10: lōʾ-yāšûḇ ʿôḏ liḇêṯô wəlōʾ-yakkirennû ʿôḏ məqômô: 'he returns no more to his house, nor does his place know him anymore.' The permanence of death is extended to social erasure: the dead man's bêṯ (house/household) and māqôm (place) no longer recognize him. Nāḵar (to recognize, know by recognition) is used of social recognition — the dead man's household has moved on, his place is taken. This contrasts with 19:25-27 where Job's hope is for post-mortem vindication — here Job is focused on the urgency of divine action before that erasure occurs. The two passages together show Job's oscillation between despair (ch7) and faith (ch19).
7.11: gam-ʾănî lōʾ ʾeḥśōk-pî ʾăḏabbərāh bəṣar rûḥî ʾāśîḥāh bəmar nap̄šî: 'Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.' The defiant turn: the brevity of life (vv.6-10) justifies unrestrained complaint. Lōʾ ʾeḥśōk (I will not restrain, from ḥāśaḵ = to withhold, spare) is the inverse of Eliphaz's call to patience. Ṣar rûḥî (narrowness/anguish of my spirit) and mar nap̄šî (bitterness of my soul) combine the physical sensation of choking constriction with the taste-metaphor of bitter suffering that appeared in the mallow-juice verse (6:7). The complaint that follows (vv.12-21) is addressed directly to God — a lament of forensic intensity.
7.12: hăyām-ʾănî ʾim-tannîn kî-ṯāśîm ʿālay mišmār: 'Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you set a guard over me?' Yām (sea — the cosmic sea of chaos in Canaanite mythology, the watery deep of Gen 1:2) and tannîn (sea monster/dragon — used in Ps 74:13; 89:10; Isa 27:1; 51:9 for the chaos creature defeated by YHWH at creation) are the mythological entities that require divine restraint. The theological implication: YHWH treats Job as though he were a chaos-threat requiring constant surveillance (mišmār, guard, watch). Job is not a cosmic monster — he is a suffering man. The accusation is that God's response to Job is categorically disproportionate: one does not keep a guard over a sick man as though he were the primordial sea.
7.13: kî-ʾāmartî těnaḥăménî ʿarśî yiśśāʾ bəśîḥî miškaḇî: 'When I say, "My bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint."' Job reports the broken logic of the sufferer: the bed (ʿereś, couch/bed) was expected to provide comfort (nāḥam, the verb of consolation used throughout the psalms and prophets). The word śîḥ (complaint, meditation, speech — the same root as the Psalms' śîaḥ, 'pouring out my complaint to YHWH') shows that Job is not merely resting but lament-praying. But the bed does not bring comfort; instead (v.14) God terrifies him there. The expected sanctuary of sleep has been converted into a site of divine assault.
7.14: waḥittaṯṯānî ḇaḥălōmôṯ ûmēḥezyônôṯ təḇaʿăṯennî: 'then you scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions.' Bāʿaṯ (to terrify, overwhelm — used of the dread of God's presence, Exod 15:14, 15; Ps 18:4; 83:15) and ḥāṯaṯ (to shatter, terrify — the antonym of Job's earlier wish for security) describe God's action in the sleep-realm. Dreams and visions (ḥălômôṯ... ḥezyônôṯ) were the standard media of divine communication in the OT (Gen 28; 37; 40-41; Num 12:6). Here Job experiences divine communication not as revelation but as terror. The irony is pointed: where the prophets received divine visions with awe and hope, Job receives them with dread — because the God he encounters in sleep is the same God who has destroyed him while awake.
7.15: watiḇḥar maḥănāq nap̄šî māweṯ miʿaṣmôṯāy: 'so that I would choose strangling and death rather than my bones.' Maḥănāq (strangling) is a desperate image of death by asphyxiation — Job prefers this to continued existence in this body (ʿaṣmôṯ, bones, as the locus of life and vitality, cf. Ezek 37's valley of dry bones). The verse is not a suicidal threat in the modern clinical sense but a theological claim: the severity of divinely-inflicted suffering has crossed the threshold where the self chooses dissolution over continuation. This extreme language is what the friends will criticize as evidence of sin; YHWH's verdict in 42:7 will vindicate Job's honesty.
7.16: māʾastî lōʾ-lěʿōlām ʾeḥyeh ḥăḏal mimmennî kî-heḇel yāmāy: 'I loathe my life; I would not live forever. Leave me alone, for my days are a breath.' Māʾas (to loathe, despise, reject) applied to his own life (ḥayyê) is Job's most direct expression of self-rejection. The phrase lōʾ lěʿōlām ʾeḥyeh (I will not live forever) is a remarkable anti-immortality claim: Job does not want eternal life in this condition; he wants it to end. Heḇel yāmāy (my days are vanity/breath) echoes Ecclesiastes' opening (hevel havalim) but in Job's mouth it is not philosophical observation but experiential cry. The command ḥăḏal mimmennî (cease from me, leave me alone) is addressed to God: it is simultaneously a prayer (addressed to God) and a desire for divine absence (leave me).
7.17: māh-ʾěnôš kî-tǝḡaddəlennû wəkî-ṯāśîm ʾēlāyw libbəḵā: 'What is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart on him?' This is the deliberate inversion of Psalm 8:4: 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?' (Ps 8:4). The lexical parallels are exact: māh-ʾěnôš (what is man), tāśîm... libbəḵā (set your heart on him = God's attention). But Psalm 8's awe at divine condescension in blessing humanity becomes in Job 7 horror at divine condescension in tormenting humanity. The same act of divine attention — infinitely great God noticing infinitely small man — is experienced as honor in Psalm 8 and as persecution in Job 7. Both are honest responses; the NT resolves the tension in the incarnation: God became man (divine condescension as honor) and suffered (divine condescension as solidarity with Job's terror).
7.18: wattip̄qəḏennû liḇqārîm lirəgāʿîm tiḇḥānennu: 'visit him every morning and test him every moment?' The divine verbs continue: pāqaḏ (to visit, attend to — in both gracious and punitive senses) and bāḥan (to test, examine, assay — as metal is tested in fire, cf. Ps 17:3; 26:2; Prov 17:3; Zech 13:9). In the Psalms, both verbs can be positive: 'You have tested my heart, you have visited me by night' (Ps 17:3) in the context of divine vindication. Here, God's morning visitation (liḇqārîm) and moment-by-moment testing (lirəgāʿîm) are experienced not as divine care but as surveillance and torment. The complaint is that the divine attention appropriate to a covenant examination is being applied continuously without relief — every morning and every moment.
7.19: kammāh lōʾ-ṯišʿeh mimmennî lōʾ-ṯarp̄ennî ʿaḏ-bəlōʿî ṯūqqî: 'How long will you not look away from me, nor leave me alone till I swallow my spit?' The verb šāʿāh (to look away, to avert the gaze) inverts the standard prayer for divine attention: Job prays for divine inattention as relief. Rāp̄āh (to let go, leave alone — the same root as 'be still and know that I am God,' Ps 46:10) is the request to cease the divine grip. The hyperbole of 'till I swallow my spit' (literally: till my swallowing of my spittle, a proverbial expression for the briefest possible moment) expresses the desperation: Job cannot get even the most minimal pause in divine attention/assault.
7.20: ḥāṭāʾṯî māh ʾep̄ʿal lāḵ nōṣēr hāʾāḏām lāmmāh śamtanî ləmiḏʾākekā waʾehyeh ʿālay māśāʾ: 'If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind? Why have you made me your mark? Why have I become a burden to you?' Three devastating questions. First: ḥāṭāʾṯî māh ʾep̄ʿal lāḵ (if I have sinned, what have I done to you?) — a theodicy question about whether human sin actually damages God, implying that divine punishment may be disproportionate to any actual harm done. Second: nōṣēr hāʾāḏām (watcher/guardian of man — nōṣēr is used for watchers both benign, Ps 121:4, and hostile) — addressed directly to God as the one who surveils all human action. Third: śamtanî ləmiḏʾākekā (you have made me your mark/target — mip̄gāʿ or miḏʾāh, the target of an archer) — God is the archer (cf. 6:4: arrows of the Almighty) and Job is the target. The lament is not atheism but the most intense form of theism: Job is speaking precisely to the God he believes is doing this.
7.21: wəlāmmāh lōʾ-ṯiśśāʾ pišʿî wəṯaʿăḇîr ʾet-ʿăwōnî kî-ʿattāh ləʿāp̄ār ʾeškāḇ ûšiḥăraṯanî wəʾênennî: 'Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth; you will seek me, but I shall not be.' The final plea of the chapter pivots from complaint to petition: nāśāʾ p̄išaʿ (to lift, carry, forgive transgression — the standard forgiveness formula, Exod 34:7; Num 14:18; Ps 32:1, 5; 85:2) and ʿāḇar ʿāwōn (to pass over iniquity — used by Moses in Exod 34:7 and by Micah 7:18: 'Who is a God like you, pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression?'). Job is not confessing specific sin but using the full forgiveness vocabulary as a plea for divine release: if there is any sin, forgive it — before the opportunity is gone. The closing line is Job's ultimate forensic weapon: ûšiḥăraṯanî wəʾênennî (you will seek me, but I shall not be). When Job is dead, God will have lost his witness, his case, his defendant. The urgency of the divine response is framed as equally urgent for God's justice as for Job's — a remarkable theological claim that prefigures the NT's account of God's own stake in human redemption.

30.1: wĕʿattāh śāḥăqû ʿālay — 'but now those younger than me mock me' — śāḥaq (to laugh/mock, from the same root as Isaac's name) marks the exact inversion of ch29: there Job was honored by elders and princes; here he is mocked by the young of the lowest social tier. ʾăšer māʾastî ʾăḇôṯām lāšîṯ ʿim-kalbê ṣōʾnî — 'whose fathers I would have disdained to set with the dogs of my flock' — keleḇ (dog) in the ancient Near East was not a pet but a scavenger, a term of contempt (1 Sam 17:43; 2 Sam 9:8); guarding sheep with such men would have been below the dignity of Job's household. The social descent described is vertiginous: from revered elder (ch29) to the mockery of society's rejects.
30.2: gam-kōaḥ yĕḏêhem lāmmāh lî — 'what use to me is the strength of their hands?' — lāmmāh lî = 'what is it to me?'; kōaḥ yāḏ = the strength/capacity of the hand. ʿālêmô ʾāḇad kālaḥ — 'upon them old age has perished' — kālaḥ is a rare word (only here and 5:26) meaning full age or mature strength; the phrase says that vigor has already wasted away on these men. Job's contempt is not for poverty itself but for those who lack even the minimal productive capacity that might make them members of his community.
30.3: bĕḥeser ûḇĕrāʿāḇ galmûḏ — 'through want and famine they are barren/solitary' — galmûḏ means barren or desolate (used in Isa 49:21 of a barren woman); here applied to people who have been left without community or offspring in the wilderness of desolation. hāʿōrĕqîm ṣiyyāh ʾemeš — 'gnawing at waste and desolation in former times' — ṣiyyāh = drought/desolation (the wilderness noun); the image is of people driven to gnaw at desolation itself, the wilderness as the only food available.
30.4: haqqōṭĕpîm mallûaḥ ʿălê-śîaḥ — 'those who pluck saltbush leaves from among the shrubs' — mallûaḥ is the orache or saltbush (Atriplex), an edible but unpalatable shrub; śîaḥ = shrub/bush. wĕšōreš rĕṯāmîm laḥmām — 'the root of the broom plant is their food' — rōṯem (broom plant, Retama raetam) is used as fuel in Ps 120:4 and 1 Kgs 19:4-5; digging its roots for food marks extreme destitution. The two plants together — saltbush leaves and broom roots — mark a diet below any tolerable subsistence level.
30.5: min-gēw yĕgōrāšû — 'they are driven out from human society' — gēw (the midst of community, the middle); yĕgōrāšû from gāraš (to expel/drive away, the verb of divorcing and expelling — Gen 4:14 uses it for Cain's expulsion). yirʾû-bô yārîʿû kĕgannāḇ — 'people shout after them as after a thief' — the hue-and-cry against a thief (Exod 22:2-3 describes the thief caught in the act) applied to these wanderers; they are treated as criminals by their mere presence.
30.6: lišḵōn bĕnaḥălê nĕḥālîm — 'to dwell in the gullies of ravines' — naḥal (wadi/ravine) + the diminutive nĕḥālîm forms 'gullies within ravines' — the most remote and inhospitable terrain. ḥōrê ʿāpār wĕkêpîm — 'holes of the earth and among the rock-crevices' — ḥōr = hole/burrow; kêp̄ = craggy rock surface; the imagery of people forced underground into animal burrows. The same vocabulary governs Heb 11:38's description of OT heroes who 'wandered in deserts and mountains, and in caves and holes in the ground.'
30.7: bên-śîḥîm yinhāqû — 'among the bushes they bray' — nāhaq is the verb for a donkey braying (27:1); applied to people it is deeply dehumanizing. taḥat ḥărûl yĕsuppāḵû — 'beneath the nettles they are gathered together' — ḥārûl = thorn/nettle plant; sāpaḵ (to gather/pile); they huddle under thorn plants. The verse pairs donkey-braying with thorn-sheltering as the final images of sub-animal existence.
30.8: bĕnê-nāḇāl gam-bĕnê bĕlî-šēm — 'children of the base fool, children of namelessness' — nāḇāl is the biblical word for the moral-intellectual fool who denies God (Ps 14:1 'the nāḇāl says in his heart there is no God'; Prov 17:21; 1 Sam 25:25 where Nabal's name matches his character). bĕlî-šēm — 'without a name/without reputation' — to be without šēm (name) in the ancient world is to be without social identity, honor, or perpetuation; the worst state short of non-existence. nikkĕʾû min-hāʾāreṣ — 'they were flogged/driven out of the land' — nāḵāʾ (to smite/beat); the passive describes expulsion by force.
30.9: wĕʿattāh nĕgînāṯām hāyîṯî — 'and now I have become their song' — nĕgînāh is a musical-instrument piece or mocking song; in Lam 3:14 the same word describes the speaker becoming 'the song of all the people.' wāʾĕhî lāhem lĕmillāh — 'and I have become a byword to them' — millāh is word/byword/proverb; the once-honored patron becomes the content of their mockery. The full reversal of ch29's honor is now explicit: the one who commanded respect is now entertainment for social rejects.
30.10: tĕʿāḇûnî rāḥăqû mimmennî — 'they abhor me and keep their distance' — tāʿab (to abominate/make detestable, a strong disgust-word used in Ps 5:6; 14:1 for God's rejection of evil); the same root that governs Job's self-description in 9:31. ûmippānay lō-ḥāśĕkû rōq — 'and they do not withhold spitting in my face' — rōq = saliva/spitting; spitting was the ultimate act of contempt (Num 12:14; Deut 25:9; Isa 50:6 where the Servant has his face spat upon). The connection between Job's suffering and the Servant of Isa 50:6 (who 'did not hide his face from insult and spitting') is one of the chapter's most significant poetic echoes.
30.11: kî yitrô pittaḥ wayyĕʿannēnî — 'because he has loosed his cord and afflicted me' — yeter (cord/string, used for a bow-string or tent-cord); loosing it = releasing the tension that previously kept things in check. The subject is disputed: is it God who loosed Job's cord (his restraint), or has Job's affliction itself been loosed? The MKT renders this as God loosening the restraint over Job's mockers. wĕriṣṣāh šillĕḥû mippānāy — 'and they cast off all restraint in my presence' — šillēaḥ + riṣṣāh (the bridle or restraint); once God withdraws the protective force that commanded respect, every social inhibition against mistreating Job is removed.
30.12: ʿal-yāmîn pirhāḥ yāqûmû — 'on my right the rabble rises up' — pirhāḥ is a rare word (only here) often understood as 'brood/mob' or 'rabble'; it may derive from pāraḥ (to sprout/spread). riglay šillēḥû wayyāsōllû ʿālay — 'they push aside my feet and build their roads of ruin against me' — rŏgel (foot/leg); sālaʿ (to mound/build a siege-ramp); the military metaphor of a siege is applied to the mob: they are constructing an assault route against Job.
30.13: nāṯĕsû nĕṯîḇāṯî — 'they wreck my path' — nāṯaś (to pull down, used of demolishing walls in 2 Kgs 25:10); nĕṯîḇāh = path/course. lĕhawwāṯî yôʿîlû — 'they advance my downfall' — yāʿal (to profit/advance) used ironically: their 'profit' is Job's destruction; hawwāh = downfall/calamity (used in Ps 5:9; 38:12; 52:2). lō ʿōzēr lāmô — 'with no one to help them' — they accomplish Job's destruction without even needing outside assistance; the passivity of isolation is underscored.
30.14: kĕp̄ereṣ rāḥāḇ yĕḇōʾû — 'they come in through a wide breach' — pereṣ (breach in a wall, used in 2 Sam 5:20 and Amos 4:3 for military breaches); the wide-breach image continues the siege metaphor. taḥat šōʾāh hiṯgalgālû — 'they rush in amid the ruin/catastrophe' — šōʾāh (catastrophe/devastation, used in 38:15; Prov 1:27; Zeph 1:15 for divine judgment); hitgalgal (to roll/rush in). The entire passage (vv12-14) uses warfare imagery to describe social abandonment as a military assault.
30.15: hāhpak ʿālay ballāhôṯ — 'terrors have been turned loose against me' — ballāhôṯ (plural of ballāhāh, terrors/sudden frights) is a key Job term (9:34; 13:21; 18:11,14; 24:17); pl. indicates overwhelming multiplicity. tirrōdep̄ kārûaḥ nĕḏîḇāṯî — 'my dignity has been chased away like the wind' — nāḏîḇāh (dignity/princely quality, from nāḏîḇ = noble); the wind-chasing image for something ungraspably gone. kĕʿāḇ ʿāḇĕrāh yĕšûʿāṯî — 'my prosperity has passed away like a cloud' — yĕšûʿāh (deliverance/prosperity) normally used for divine salvation (cf. Ps 3:2; Isa 12:2 'God is my yĕšûʿāh'); its application to social/material prosperity shows how completely Job's world has been the arena of divine blessing.
30.16: wĕʿattāh ʿālay tiššāpēḵ nap̄šî — 'and now my soul is poured out upon me' — šāpak (to pour out, used of blood: Gen 9:6; and of water: 2 Sam 14:14); nepeš (soul/life) poured out like a liquid. This exact construction — nep̄eš + šāpak + ʿāl — appears in Ps 22:14 ('I am poured out like water... my heart has melted like wax'); the Psalm of dereliction uses the same self-dissolution language Job uses here. yōʾḥăzûnî yĕmê ʿānî — 'days of affliction have seized me' — ʿŏnî (affliction/poverty from ʿānāh, to be afflicted); the days of affliction as a grasping agent, seizing Job in their grip.
30.17: laylāh ʿaṣmōṯay niqar mēʿālay — 'at night my bones are racked/bored through' — nāqar (to bore/pierce, used in 2 Kgs 25:7 for boring out eyes); the night pain gnaws at Job's skeletal structure. wĕʿōrĕqay lō yiškāḇû — 'and the gnawing pain does not rest' — ʿōreq (from nāqar? or from ʿāraḵ = to gnaw?) — the pain as a living thing that does not sleep even when Job tries to; the sleeplessness of chronic pain given poetic form.
30.18: bĕrāḇ-kōaḥ yitḥappēś lĕḇûšî — 'with great force my garment is seized' — ḥāp̄aś (to disguise/search through; in the hitpa'el to grip/hold tightly). The garment-seizing by illness: disease literally grabs and distorts Job's clothing, twisting it around him. kĕp̄î kuttontî yaʾăzrēnî — 'it binds me like the collar of my tunic' — ketonnet (the long garment/tunic; the same word as Joseph's coat in Gen 37:3); the collar (pî = mouth/opening) becomes a strangling grip. Disease as a garment-seizing assailant is a striking pathological image.
30.19: hōrānî laḥōmer — 'he has thrown me into the mud' — yārāh in the hip'il = to throw; ḥōmer = clay/mud; to be thrown into mud is to be reduced to raw material. wāʾetmassēl kĕʿāpār wāʾēp̄er — 'and I have become like dust and ashes' — māšal (to be like, to resemble); ʿāpār wāʾēp̄er — the dust-and-ashes pair is the standard OT expression for the absolute lowness of human creaturely existence (Gen 18:27; Job 42:6); to become the metaphor itself is the deepest descent.
30.20: ʾeśāʿaq ʾēlêḵā wĕlō taʿănēnî — 'I cry out to you and you do not answer me' — šāʿaq (to cry out in distress, the verb of desperation); the divine non-answer as active theological abandonment. This is the direct parallel to Ps 22:2 ('I cry by day and you do not answer'). ʿāmadtî wattitbōnēn bî — 'I stand and you only stare at me' — bîn (to understand/perceive) in the hitpo'el = to stare with incomprehension; God is portrayed as observing without responding. The combination of the divine silence motif and the divine-gaze motif frames Job's abandonment experience in terms almost identical to the messianic Psalm of dereliction.
30.21: hēhāp̄aḵ lĕʿākzār lî — 'you have become cruel to me' — ʿakzār (cruel/savage, used in Prov 5:9; Isa 13:9 for God's judgment-day cruelty against enemies); Job is experiencing the treatment reserved for enemies applied to himself. bĕʿōṣem yāḏĕḵā tiśṭĕmēnî — 'with the power of your hand you persecute me' — śāṭam (to oppose/be hostile to; cf. Gen 27:41, Esau's hostility; and Satan's root in this stem in 1:6 interpretations); the divine hand as adversarial rather than protective.
30.22: tiśśāʾēnî ʾel-rûaḥ tarkîḇēnî — 'you lift me up to the wind; you cause me to ride it' — rāḵaḇ (to ride, used in Deut 33:26 for God who rides the heavens); Job being made to ride the wind = total disorientation and helplessness. wĕtĕmōgĕgēnî tûšiyyāh — 'and you dissolve my substance/effective power' — mûg (to melt/dissolve); tûšiyyāh (the distinctive Job word for effective wisdom/substance, also in 6:13; 11:6; 12:16; 26:3; Prov 8:14); Job's capacity for purposeful action is melted away by God's storm-riding.
30.23: kî-yāḏaʿtî māwet tĕšîḇēnî — 'for I know you will bring me to death' — the only certainty left is that God will direct Job toward death. wĕḇêṯ môʿēḏ lĕḵol-ḥāy — 'to the house appointed for all living' — bêṯ môʿēḏ is a remarkable compound: bêṯ (house) + môʿēḏ (the term for the divinely appointed Tabernacle/Temple meeting-place, as in ʾōhel môʿēḏ = tent of meeting). Death is called a 'house of appointment/meeting'; the irony that the Tabernacle-meeting language — the locus of divine-human encounter — should be applied to death is bitter and theologically charged.
30.24: ʾaḵ lō-bĕʿî yišlaḥ yāḏ — 'yet does not one in a ruin reach out his hand?' — the verse is difficult; the sense seems to be: even in a heap of ruins, people reach out and cry for help. Job is asserting the universal practice of crying for help in disaster as the ground for his own cries. The appeal to common human practice challenges any theology that would silence the sufferer's cry.
30.25: ʾim-lō bāḵîṯî lĕqĕšēh-yôm — 'did I not weep for those in trouble?' — qāšâ yôm = hard/difficult day, the day of hardship; Job's past compassion for others in distress. ʾāgĕlāh nap̄šî lāʾeḇyôn — 'was not my soul grieved for the poor?' — ʾāgal (to have grief/sorrow, related to Arabic root for deep interior distress); nepeš + ʾāgal for the poor echoes the socially engaged righteousness of ch29. Having wept for others' distress, Job expects (and receives instead) divine indifference to his own.
30.26: kî ṭôḇ qiwwîṯî wayyāḇōʾ rāʿ — 'when I looked for good, evil came' — the bitterest reversal formula: qiwwîṯî (I hoped/waited, the verb of tiqwāh, hope) for ṭôḇ (good) but rāʿ (evil) came. The language of hope-and-disappointment is applied not to a single event but to the whole shape of Job's life after his integrity. wăʾăyahel lāʾôr wayyāḇōʾ ʾōp̄el — 'and when I waited for light, thick darkness came' — ʾōp̄el (thick darkness, the same darkness of the inward parts of Job 3:6 and Prov 7:9); the antithesis of hoped-for light arriving as its opposite.
30.27: mēʿay ḥārû wĕlō-ḏāmû — 'my inner parts churn and do not rest' — ḥārāh (to burn/ferment; applied to the gut here as visceral turmoil); the meʿāyim (intestines/inner parts) as the seat of emotion mirrors Jer 4:19 ('my bowels, my bowels! I am pained at my very heart') and Lam 1:20. qiddĕmûnî yĕmê ʿānî — 'days of affliction have overtaken/confronted me' — qāḏam (to come before/confront, used in Ps 18:18 of enemies who came against); the days of affliction as active assailants meeting Job head-on.
30.28: qōḏēr hillaḵtî bĕlō ḥammāh — 'I go about in mourning without the sun' — qāḏar (to be dark/gloomy; used in 1 Kgs 18:45 of the sky blackening; applied to mourning dress in Ps 35:14; 42:9); ḥammāh = the sun (a poetic word, the warm/heat-giver). Mourning without the sun's comfort. ʾāqûm baqāhāl ʾăšawwēaʿ — 'I stand in the assembly and cry out' — qāhāl (the formal assembly of the community); šāwaʿ (to cry for help, the most urgent help-cry). Job's public, communal, unanswered cry for help.
30.29: ʾāḥ hāyîṯî lĕṯannîm — 'I have become a brother to jackals' — ʾāḥ (brother) with tannîm (jackals, or possibly sea-monsters; in Isa 13:21-22 and 34:13-15, jackals and ostriches inhabit desolate ruins, emblems of Edom's judgment). wĕrēʿa lĕḇnôṯ yaʿănāh — 'and a companion to ostriches' — rēaʿ (friend/companion); yaʿănāh (ostrich or possibly the screech-owl; its mournful cry makes it a wilderness-mourning symbol in Mic 1:8; Lam 4:3). The social companionship of the desolate creatures replaces human fellowship.
30.30: ʿôrî šāḥar mēʿālay — 'my skin has turned black on me' — šāḥar (to be dark; the same word as 'dawn' but here applied to skin darkening through disease or sun-exposure); the irony of using the dawn-word for skin-blackening. wĕʿaṣmî-ḥārāh miqqaḏḏāḥaṯ — 'and my bones burn with fever' — ḥārāh (to burn/be hot); qaddaḥat (fever/inflammation, also in Lev 26:16; Deut 28:22 as a covenant-curse); Job's body manifesting the covenant-curse vocabulary.
30.31: wayhî lĕʾēḇel kinnōrî — 'my lyre has become mourning' — kinnôr (the stringed instrument, David's instrument and the instrument of praise in Ps 33:2; 43:4; 150:3); turned to ʾēḇel (mourning, the term for the formal mourning period; Gen 27:41; Amos 5:16). wĕʿûgāḇî lĕqôl bōḵîm — 'and my flute to the sound of weeping' — ʿûgāḇ (a wind instrument, probably the flute; Gen 4:21 lists it as Jubal's instrument alongside the kinnor); bōḵîm (weeping ones). The chapter closes with the reversal of musical praise into mourning lamentation — the full range of cultural expression now in the service of grief.

31.1: bĕrît kāraṯî lĕʿênāy — 'I cut a covenant with my eyes' — kārat bĕrît is the standard idiom for formal covenant-making (Gen 15:18; 21:27; Exod 24:8); applied to the eyes it elevates visual discipline to the status of a binding legal agreement. The eyes as parties to a covenant is uniquely rigorous: not 'I tried not to look' but 'I am covenantally obligated not to look.' ûmāh ʾetbônān ʿal-bĕṯûlāh — 'how then could I gaze at a young woman?' — bĕṯûlāh (a young woman of marriageable age, often translated 'virgin'); bîn in the hitpo'el = to look at intently/contemplate. The eye-covenant becomes the foundation for Jesus's intensification of the prohibition in Matt 5:28 ('everyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her').
31.2: wĕmāh ḥēleq ʾĕlôah mimāʿal — 'what is God's portion from above?' — ḥēleq (allotment/portion, the term used for Levitical inheritance and land distribution); God's 'portion' here means what God assigns as consequence. wĕnaḥălat Šaddai mimmĕrōmîm — 'and the inheritance of Shaddai from the heights?' — naḥălāh (inheritance/heritage, the covenantal land-promise word); the verse grounds Job's ethical behavior not in personal virtue alone but in the framework of divine reward-structure (even as he maintains his integrity despite that structure apparently having failed him).
31.3: hălō-ʾêḏ lĕʿawwāl — 'is not ruin for the wrongdoer?' — ʾêḏ (calamity/disaster, the destruction that arrives); ʿawwāl (one who is crooked/unjust; from ʿāwal = to be bent, the root of ʿawlāh = injustice). wĕnēḵar lĕp̄ōʿălê ʾāwen — 'and disaster for those who work iniquity?' — nēḵar (foreign/strange, used as a noun for 'estrangement/alienness'); pōʿălê ʾāwen (workers of trouble/iniquity, a fixed phrase from the Psalms: 5:5; 6:8; 14:4). Job is affirming the retributive framework as a general principle — but his experience contradicts its application to his specific case.
31.4: hălō-hûʾ yirʾeh ḏĕrāḵay — 'does he not see my ways?' — the rhetorical certainty that God observes: this is simultaneously the ground of Job's confidence in his innocence (God sees that I am not guilty) and his perplexity (God sees but does not respond appropriately). wĕḵol-ṣĕʿādāy yispôr — 'and counts all my steps?' — sāpar (to count/number) of the divine accounting of human movement; the same forensic oversight Job complained about in 14:16 is here offered as a reason for confidence in his vindication — since God's scrutiny would reveal nothing amiss.
31.5: ʾim-hālaktî ʿim-šāwʾ — 'if I have walked with falsehood/emptiness' — šāwʾ (emptiness/vanity/falsehood; used in the third commandment 'do not take the name of the LORD in vain/šāwʾ'; and in Job 7:3; 15:31; 35:13); applying it to walking = living by deception. wattĕḥaš ʿal-mirmāh raglî — 'and my foot has rushed toward deceit' — mirmāh (deceit/treachery; the same word used of Jacob's deception in Gen 27:35 — 'your brother came with deceit/mirmāh'); the ethical autobiography continues with a negative conditional catalogue.
31.6: yišqĕlēnî bĕmōʾznê ṣeḏeq — 'let me be weighed in honest/just scales' — mōʾznê ṣeḏeq (scales of righteousness/justice) is the idiom of Lev 19:36 and Prov 16:11 ('honest scales and balances are the LORD's'); Job invites the divine weighing that Prov 21:2 warns about. wĕyēḏaʿ ʾĕlôah tummāṯî — 'and let God know my integrity' — tummāh (integrity/wholeness, from tāmam = to be complete; the same root as Job's description in 1:1 and 1:8); the invitation to divine knowledge of his integrity is both a legal demand and an act of radical transparency.
31.7: ʾim tāṭeh ʾaššurî min-haddāreḵ — 'if my step has turned from the path' — ʾaššûr (step/walk, poetic synonym for going; cf. Ps 17:5 'my steps have held to your paths'); min-haddāreḵ (from the right path). wĕʾaḥar ʿênay hālaḵ libbî — 'and my heart has followed my eyes' — the inversion of proper order: the heart should govern the eyes (not vice versa); when the eyes lead the heart, desire precedes judgment. ûḇĕḵappay dāḇaq mûm — 'and any stain has clung to my hands' — mûm (blemish/defect, the word for a physical blemish disqualifying a priest in Lev 21:17-23; applied here morally).
31.8: ʾezrāʿāh wĕʾaḥēr yōʾḵēl — 'let me sow and another eat' — the curse-formula for violated covenantal labor (Deut 28:30,33,51: the covenant curses include others consuming what you plant); Job invites these curses upon himself if guilty. wĕṣeʾĕṣāʾay yĕšōrāšû — 'and let my offspring be uprooted' — ṣeʾĕṣāʾ (offspring/descendants, from the root of 'going out'); šāraš in the po'al = to be uprooted by the roots; dynastic obliteration as the sworn consequence.
31.9: ʾim-nip̄tāh libbî ʿal-ʾiššāh — 'if my heart has been enticed by a woman' — pāṯāh (to entice/seduce; the same verb used in Exod 22:16 for seducing a virgin, and in Prov 1:10 for sinners enticing the young); the heart as the primary site of sexual temptation, not just the eye (v1). wĕʾarāḇtî ʿal-pétaḥ rēʿî — 'or if I have lurked at my neighbor's door' — ʾāraḇ (to lie in wait/ambush, the verb of setting a trap; used in Prov 1:11,18; 7:12 for the wayward woman); lurking at the neighbor's door = the precise act prohibited in the commandment against coveting the neighbor's wife.
31.10: tiṭḥan lĕʾaḥēr ʾišṯî — 'let my wife grind for another' — ṭāḥan (to grind grain, the work of a slave-concubine in Exod 11:5; Num 11:8; Isa 47:2); to grind for another = to be sexually enslaved to another. wĕʿālêhā yiḵrĕʿûn ʾăḥērîm — 'and let others bow over her' — kāraʿ (to bow down/kneel; used in sexual contexts in Gen 49:9; Judg 5:27); the sworn curse-consequence of adultery is his own wife's sexual degradation. The brutal reciprocity of the oath formula reflects how seriously the social fabric of family and neighbor honor was taken.
31.11: kî-hîʾ zimmāh — 'for that would be a deliberate outrage' — zimmāh is the strongest OT word for sexual wickedness: deliberate, calculated, outrageous depravity (Lev 18:17; 19:29; 20:14; Ezek 16:27; 22:9; Prov 10:23); distinguished from mere passion by its premeditated character. wĕhîʾ ʿāwōn pĕlîlîm — 'and an iniquity deserving judges' — pĕlîlîm (judges/referees; from pālal = to judge/mediate); the phrase 'iniquity of/for judges' = a crime serious enough to require judicial adjudication, not merely personal repentance.
31.12: kî ʾēš hîʾ ʾaḏ-ʾăḇaddôn tōʾḵēl — 'for it is a fire that burns all the way to Abaddon' — ʾăḇaddôn (destruction/the underworld realm, from ʾāḇaḏ = to perish; used in Job 26:6; 28:22; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11; Rev 9:11 where it names the angel of the bottomless pit as Abaddon). The fire of sexual betrayal burns to the depths of the realm of destruction — its consequences are eschatologically profound. wĕḇĕḵol-tĕḇûʾāṯî tĕšarrēš — 'and would consume all my harvest' — šāraš in the pi'el = to uproot entirely; tĕḇûʾāh = produce/income/harvest. Total economic destruction as the consequence of sexual sin.
31.13: ʾim-ʾemʾas mišpaṭ ʿaḇdî wĕʾāmāṯî — 'if I have dismissed the legal case of my servant or maidservant' — mišpāṭ (legal right/case/judgment); mĕʾēn (to refuse); ʿeḇed and ʾāmāh (male servant and female servant) — both gendered servants have legal standing in this oath. The willingness to acknowledge servant legal rights is remarkable; most ancient law systems gave the master almost total discretion over servants. bĕriḇām ʿimmāḏî — 'when they had a legal dispute with me' — rîḇ (dispute/lawsuit); ʿim (with/against); the servant as legal adversary of the master in a binding adjudication.
31.14: wĕmāh ʾeʿĕśeh kî-yāqûm ʾēl — 'what could I do when God rises up?' — qûm (to rise; used in legal contexts for the rising of a judge or accuser to pronounce judgment; cf. Isa 2:19,21 where God 'rises'); Job appeals to divine legal oversight as his ultimate accountability. wĕkî yip̄qōḏ māh ʾăšîḇennû — 'when he holds inquiry, what shall I answer him?' — pāqaḏ (to visit/examine/hold to account, the verb of divine visitation both in judgment and blessing; Exod 20:5; 32:34); the servant-equity clause flows from the recognition that God himself will audit Job's conduct toward those beneath him.
31.15: hălō-ḇabbeṭen ʿōśēnî ʿāśāhû — 'did not the one who made me in the womb make him also?' — the argument from common creatureliness: both master and servant were fashioned by the same divine Maker in the womb (bāṭen = womb/belly). wayyĕḵûnennû bāreḥem ʾeḥāḏ — 'and did not one God form us both in the womb?' — ʾeḥāḏ (one); rāḥem (womb); the theological anthropology here — one Creator, one womb, therefore one humanity that transcends status — anticipates Paul's 'there is neither slave nor free' (Gal 3:28) and James's appeal to a common creation as the ground for not discriminating between rich and poor (James 2:1-7).
31.16: ʾim-ʾemnaʿ mēḥēp̄eṣ dallîm — 'if I have withheld what the poor needed' — dallîm (the poor/weak, from ḏal = to hang thin/be weak; Exod 30:15; Lev 14:21); ḥēp̄eṣ (desire/what is wanted); withholding what the poor desired = failing to meet actual need. wĕʿênê ʾalmānāh ʾăkalleh — 'or caused the widow's eyes to fail from waiting' — kāllāh (to fail/be consumed; used in Ps 84:2 'my soul faints/kāllāh with longing'); the widow's eyes failing from sustained waiting — she has waited so long for help that even hope of it has been exhausted.
31.17: wĕʾōḵal pittî lĕḇaḏḏî — 'or if I ate my food alone' — pittāh (piece of bread/food; the most basic meal unit); lĕḇaḏḏ (alone/by oneself); the ethics of the shared table: in the ancient Near East, eating without sharing was a serious social offense. wĕlō-ʾāḵal yāṯôm mimmenāh — 'and the fatherless ate nothing from it' — yātôm (the fatherless child, whose special legal protection runs from Exod 22:22 through the Psalms and prophets); the shared meal as the concrete measure of social righteousness.
31.18: kî minnĕʿûrāy gĕḏēlānî kĕʾāḇ — 'for from my youth he grew up with me as with a father' — gāḏal (to grow up/be raised); the surprising identification of the fatherless as one who 'grew up with' Job as a father-figure. ûminneṭen ʾimmî ʾanḥennāh — 'and from birth I cared for the widow' — min-beṭen ʾem (from the womb of one's mother, a hyperbole for 'from earliest time'); nāḥāh (to guide/lead in the sense of pastoral care). Job's fathering of the fatherless and guiding of widows is presented as a lifelong character-formation, not occasional charity.
31.19: ʾim-ʾerʾeh ʾôḇēḏ mibbelî lāḇûš — 'if I have seen anyone perishing for lack of clothing' — ʾāḇaḏ (to perish/be lost; here with min-belî = 'without'); lāḇûš (clothing, the basic covering); the naked poor encountered (rāʾāh = seen) rather than helped. wĕʾên kĕsûṯ lāʾeḇyôn — 'or any poor person without covering' — ʾeḇyôn (the needy/destitute, a stronger economic term than dallîm); kĕsûṯ (covering/garment); the visual encounter with naked poverty as the trigger for the ethical obligation.
31.20: ʾim-lō bĕrăḵûnî ḥălāṣāyw — 'if he has not blessed me from his very loins' — ḥălāṣayim (the loins/hips, the body-part of warmth and strength; in Gen 35:11 'from your loins kings will come'); the blessing from the body = deep visceral gratitude, not polite thanks. ûmigēz kĕḇāśāy yiṯḥammām — 'and the wool of my sheep has not kept him warm' — gēz (shearing/fleece); the warm wool from Job's own flocks goes to warm the poor who bless him with embodied gratitude; practical material generosity confirmed by the recipient's bodily warmth.
31.21: ʾim-hănîp̄ôṯî ʿal-yāṯôm yāḏî — 'if I have raised my hand against the fatherless' — hānap̄ (to wave/brandish); the hand raised against the orphan as a gesture of threat or violence. kî ʾerʾeh bĕšaʿar ʿezrāṯî — 'when I see my support at the city gate' — šaʿar (city gate, the locus of judicial proceedings and public power in the ancient city; Gen 19:1; Ruth 4:1-2; Amos 5:12); ʿezrāh (support/help); to have allies at the gate = to have judicial power; Job swears he did not exploit that power against the most vulnerable.
31.22: kĕṯēp̄î missĕḵmāh tippôl — 'let my arm fall from its shoulder' — kāṯēp̄ (shoulder-blade/shoulder-joint); min-šĕḵem (from the shoulder); the imprecatory self-curse for using power against the fatherless: may the arm that could raise itself against the helpless be torn from its socket. wĕzĕrōʿî miqqāneh tiššāḇēr — 'and my arm be broken from the elbow' — zĕrôaʿ (arm/forearm, the seat of strength; used in Ezek 4:7; Deut 33:27 for the divine arm); tiššāḇēr = 'be broken/shattered'; the double anatomical curse (shoulder joint + forearm) ensures the arm of injustice is permanently disabled.
31.23: kî p̄aḥaḏ ʾēlî ʾêḏ ʾēl — 'for the destruction from God is a terror to me' — paḥaḏ (dread/terror; used in 3:25 for what Job feared coming upon him); ʾêḏ ʾēl = 'the calamity of God' (the divine disaster, also in Isa 10:3; Prov 1:26); Job's ethical behavior is motivated not only by compassion but by healthy fear of divine accountability. ûmiśśĕʾēṯô lō ʾûḵāl — 'before his majesty I cannot hold my ground' — śĕʾēṯô (his rising/majesty; the same word as 13:11); the towering divine majesty silences any thought of exploitation.
31.24: ʾim-śamtî zāhāḇ kislî — 'if I have made gold my trust/hope' — śwm + kislî (to set/place + my hope/security; kesel = the fat of the loins as seat of confidence, or simply confidence itself; cf. Ps 78:7 'to set their hope in God'); gold as alternative ground of security. wĕlakkettêm ʾāmartî mibṭaḥî — 'or called refined gold my security' — kéttem (fine gold, a poetic term appearing in 28:19; Ps 45:9; Prov 25:12; Song 5:11); miḇṭāḥ (trust/security; from bāṭaḥ = to trust; Ps 40:4; Jer 17:5-7 contrasts trust in humans with trust in God). The prohibition on financial security as primary trust is grounded here in a negative oath; Ps 52:7 and 1 Tim 6:17 make the same prohibition positive.
31.25: ʾim-ʾeśmaḥ kî-rāḇ ḥêlî — 'if I rejoiced that my wealth was great' — śāmaḥ (to rejoice/delight in; the delight-verb for Ps 97:12 'rejoice in the LORD'); rāḇ ḥayil = 'great wealth/army-force' (ḥayil = military force/wealth); joy in wealth-magnitude rather than in God. wĕkî-ḵaḇbîr māṣĕʾāh yāḏî — 'and that my hand had gained much' — kāḇîr (much/great; rare poetic word); the pride of self-made wealth as the spiritual danger. Job renounces both the accumulation-pride and the abundance-joy.
31.26: ʾim-ʾerʾeh ʾôr kî yāhēl — 'if I gazed at the light as it shines brilliantly' — ʾôr (light, the sun) yāhēl (shines brilliantly; from hālal in the sense of shining, the same root as hallelujah); wĕyārēaḥ yāqār hōlēḵ — 'and the moon as it moves in splendor' — yāqār (precious/glorious); astral worship through contemplative gazing. The prohibition on sun and moon worship runs from Deut 4:19; 17:3 through Jer 8:2; Ezek 8:16; the widespread nature of this temptation is reflected in how carefully Job includes it in his oath.
31.27: wayyippaṯ basseṯer libbî — 'and my heart was secretly seduced' — pāṯāh (to be enticed/seduced, the same verb as v9's heart-enticed-by-a-woman); the heart's private corruption — the inner liturgy of astral veneration that precedes any public act. wattišśaq-yāḏ lĕp̄î — 'and my hand was thrown a kiss toward them' — the kiss blown toward the celestial body (hand to mouth to the sky) was the classic gesture of astral worship known from Mesopotamian and Egyptian practice; 1 Kgs 19:18 uses the same idiom ('all who have not bowed to Baal and every mouth that has not kissed him').
31.28: gam-hûʾ ʿāwōn pĕlîlî — 'this too would be an iniquity deserving judgment' — the same legal formula as v11 (zimmāh + ʿāwōn pĕlîlî); sexual betrayal and idolatry are given identical legal weight. kî-ḵiḥaštî lāʾēl mimmāʿal — 'for I would have denied the God above' — kāḥaš (to deceive/deny by minimizing; used in Josh 7:11 for the denial of wrongdoing; Lev 19:11 'do not deny/deceive'); to worship the sun or moon is to deny ʾēl mimmāʿal (the God who is above/over) — the astral bodies are subordinate to the Creator, and worshiping them denies the hierarchy of being.
31.29: ʾim-ʾeśmaḥ bĕp̄îḏ śōnĕʾî — 'if I rejoiced at the ruin of one who hated me' — p̄îḏ (destruction/downfall; only here and 12:5; 21:20; 30:24; 31:3 — a rare Joban term for disaster); śōnēʾ (one who hates/is hostile); joy at an enemy's ruin. wĕhitʿôrartî kî-māṣāʾhû rāʿ — 'or exulted when disaster found him' — hitʿôrēr (to rouse/excite oneself in po'lel; to be stirred up with excitement); this oath clause anticipates Prov 24:17 ('do not rejoice when your enemy falls') and Christ's command to love enemies (Matt 5:44).
31.30: wĕlō-nāṯattî laḥăṭōʾ ḥiḵḵî — 'I did not let my mouth sin' — ḥēḵ (palate/mouth, the tasting organ of 12:11); nāṯan + laḥăṭōʾ = to give/allow to sin; the mouth as the instrument of verbal sin in asking God for the enemy's death. lišʾōl bĕʾālāh nap̄šô — 'by asking for his life with a curse' — ʾālāh (oath/curse; the solemn curse-word; Gen 24:41; Num 5:21); the prohibition on cursing the enemy to death extends the ethic of v29 from internal rejoicing to verbal action.
31.31: ʾim-lō ʾāmĕrû mĕṯê ʾāholî — 'if the men of my household have not said' — mĕṯê ʾōhel (men of the tent/household); the household testimony as evidence: what Job's own servants and family members say about his hospitality. mî-yittēn mibbĕśārô lō niśbāʿ — 'who has not been filled from his food?' — the rhetorical who-has-not?: everyone in the household has been fed to fullness (śāḇaʿ = to be satisfied/full) from Job's table.
31.32: ballayil lō-yālîn gēr — 'no stranger had to lodge in the street at night' — gēr (a stranger/sojourner, the protected alien resident whose care is commanded throughout the Mosaic law: Exod 22:21; 23:9; Lev 19:33-34; Deut 10:19); lûn (to spend the night, to lodge); the gate opened to the stranger at night is the hospitality ethic at its most demanding. ḏĕlatay lāʾōrēaḥ ʾep̄tāḥ — 'I opened my door to the traveller' — ʾōrēaḥ (the wayfarer/traveller; cf. Jer 14:8 where God is called 'like a stranger who turns aside to lodge'); the opened door to the traveler connects to the NT's 'do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels' (Heb 13:2).
31.33: ʾim-kissîṯî kĕʾāḏām pĕšāʿāy — 'if I have hidden my transgressions like Adam' — kāsāh (to hide/cover; the same verb as God's covering of nakedness in Gen 3:21); pešaʿ (transgression/rebellion); the explicit comparison to Adam's hiding his sin (Gen 3:8-12) is the only unambiguous reference in OT poetry to Adam's paradigmatic sin-concealment. This makes Job 31:33 the OT antecedent to Paul's Adam typology in Rom 5:12-14 and his treatment of covering and exposing transgression in 2 Cor 4:2. lĕṭamôn bĕḥûḇî ʿăwōnî — 'by concealing my iniquity in my heart' — ṭāman (to hide/bury; the verb of storing treasure or setting an ambush; Prov 2:1 stores wisdom; here iniquity is 'buried' in the chest/bosom).
31.34: kî ʾeʿĕrōṣ hāmôn rabbāh — 'because I feared the great crowd' — ʿāraṣ (to dread/be overwhelmed by; the root of marʿîṣ = terrifying; used for the numbing dread of the powerful); hāmôn rabbāh = the great crowd/multitude. ûbûz mišpāḥôṯ yĕḥiṯtannî — 'and the contempt of families/clans terrified me' — bûz (contempt/scorn; Prov 12:8; Ps 22:6 where the speaker is 'a worm and not a man... an object of scorn and contempt'); mišpāḥāh (clan/family group); the social pressure of communal shame is identified as the mechanism that keeps sin hidden 'like Adam': fear of public exposure rather than moral shame.
31.35: mî yittĕn-lî šōmēaʿ lî — 'O that someone would hear me!' — the repeated mî-yittēn optative ('O that!') of Job's greatest wish: a hearing. hēn-tāwî Šaddai yaʿănēnî — 'here is my signature — let Shaddai answer me!' — tāw is the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet (the equivalent of omega), written as an X-mark in ancient script; to place one's tāw = to sign one's legal document. Job formally signs and presents his petition to the divine court. wĕsēp̄er kāṯaḇ ʾîš rîḇî — 'O that my accuser had written out his charges!' — Job's longing for a written indictment so he could actually read and respond to the charges against him; the legal right to know one's accusation. This verse stands at the center of the book's legal metaphor: Job filing his signed petition against God's silence.
31.36: ʾim-lō ʿal-šikmî ʾeśśāʾennû — 'surely I would carry it on my shoulder' — šeḵem (shoulder, the body-part of burden-bearing; Isa 9:6 'the government shall be upon his shoulder'); Job would carry the divine indictment on his shoulder = bear it openly, proudly, as a sign of his willingness to be publicly examined. ʾeʿendehu kĕʿăṭārôṯ lî — 'and wear it as a crown on my head' — ʿăṭārāh (crown/garland; a mark of honor in Prov 12:4; 17:6; Isa 62:3); the indictment worn as a crown is the paradox of the wrongly-accused righteous person: what was meant to shame becomes the mark of honor.
31.37: mispār ṣĕʿāḏay ʾaggîḏennû — 'I would give him an account of all my steps' — mispār ṣĕʿāḏay (the number of my steps; the same forensic counting of 31:4 and 14:16); Job would render a complete account of every action. kĕnāgîḏ ʾeqrĕḇennû — 'I would approach him like a prince' — nāgîḏ (a leader/prince, one who goes in front; used of commanders: 1 Sam 9:16; 1 Chr 9:11); Job would not approach God as a cringing defendant but as a peer — standing upright, making a dignified legal presentation. The prince-analogy is the ethical climax of Job's self-defense.
31.38: ʾim-ʿālay ʾadmāṯî tizʿāq — 'if my land has cried out against me' — zāʿaq (to cry out; the same verb as the cry of Abel's blood in Gen 4:10 which 'cried out from the ground'); the land as a moral witness against its owner. wĕyaḥad tĕlāmêhā yiḇkāyûn — 'and its furrows have wept together' — telem (a furrow; the plough-line in Ps 65:10 that God's rain fills); weeping furrows personify agricultural land making a legal complaint. The oath extends from social ethics (servants, widows, orphans) to ecological ethics (land stewardship).
31.39: ʾim-ʾet-kōḥāh ʾāḵaltî ḇlî-ḵesep̄ — 'if I have eaten its produce without payment' — ḵōaḥ (power/produce; the productive force of the land); ḵesep̄ (silver/payment); consuming agricultural produce without paying its owners = land theft. wĕnep̄eš bĕʿālêhā hippāḥtî — 'or stripped the life/breath from its owners' — nāpah (to breathe/blow; pi'el hiṗpîaḥ = to blow out, to extinguish); nepeš bĕʿālāyw = the life of its owners; to consume the land's produce without payment is to extinguish the life-support of the legitimate owners. The ecological ethics connects directly to social ethics: land exploitation kills people.
31.40: taḥat ḥiṭṭāh yēṣēʾ ḥôaḥ wĕtaḥat-śĕʿōrāh bōʾšāh — 'let thistles grow instead of wheat and weeds instead of barley' — ḥôaḥ (thorn/thistle; the curse-vegetation of Gen 3:18 where 'thorns and thistles' mark the fall-curse on the ground); bōʾšāh (a noxious/stinking plant, perhaps the darnel); the reversal of cultivation-to-thorn-growth is the Gen 3 curse applied as a legal penalty. tammû diḇrê ʾîyôḇ — 'the words of Job are ended' — tāmam (to be complete/finished; the same root as Job's own tōm = integrity); the colophon formally closes Job's speeches. His last word before the divine answer is his signature of integrity.

32.1: wayyišbĕtû šĕlōšeṯ hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh mēʿănôṯ ʾet-ʾîyôḇ — 'so these three men stopped answering Job' — šāḇat (to cease/stop; the Sabbath-rest verb applied to the friends' exhaustion of arguments). kî hûʾ ṣaddîq bĕʿênāyw — 'because he was righteous in his own eyes' — bĕʿênāyw = 'in his own eyes'; the narrator's framing of the friends' stopping point: they ceased because Job maintained his ṣaddîq status in his own eyes — not because they believed him, but because they could not break his self-assessment. The phrase ṣaddîq bĕʿênāyw will be reprised in Elihu's anger in v2.
32.2: wayyiḥar ʾap̄ ʾĔlîhû ben-ḇaraḵĕʾēl habbûzî — 'the anger of Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite burned' — ḥārāh ʾap̄ (to be angry; the nostrils-burn idiom — ʾap̄ = both 'nose' and 'anger' because of the visible nostril-flaring of anger; used throughout from Gen 30:2 to Num 11:1). ʾēlîhû = 'He is my God' or 'My God is He'; Barachel = 'God blesses'; Bûzî = from Buz (Nahor's son in Gen 22:21, an Arabian location). wayyiḥar ʾappô bĕʾîyôḇ ʿal-ṣaddĕqô nap̄šô mēʾĔlōhîm — 'angry against Job for justifying himself rather than God' — nepeš (self/soul); ṣādaq in the pi'el = to declare righteous; Elihu is angry that Job justified himself at the expense of God's justice — but also (v3) angry at the friends for failing to answer.
32.3: wĕḇišĕlōšeṯ rēʿāyw ḥārāh ʾappô — 'he was also angry with his three friends' — the double anger is structurally remarkable: Elihu is enraged both at Job's self-justification AND at the friends' inadequate theodicy. ʿal ʾăšer lōʾ-māṣĕʾû maʿăneh wayyarĕšîʿû ʾeṯ-ʾîyôḇ — 'because they had found no answer and yet condemned Job' — māṣāʾ maʿăneh (to find/discover an answer); rāšaʿ in the hip'il = to declare wicked/condemn; the friends condemned Job without an adequate theological basis for doing so. This is Elihu's charge against the friends: conviction without adequate argument.
32.4: wĕʾĔlîhû ḥikkāh ʾeṯ-ʾîyôḇ biddĕḇārîm — 'Elihu had waited to speak with/about Job' — ḥikkāh (to wait for eagerly; the same root as tiqwāh's waiting; Ps 33:20 'our soul waits/ḥāḵāh for the LORD'); the technical meaning here is that Elihu waited in queue, holding back his words while the older men finished. kî zĕqēnîm hēmmāh mimmennû lĕyāmîm — 'because they were older than he was in days' — the cultural protocol of age-deference; Elihu has been waiting not from timidity but from respect for seniority, which makes his eventual anger more credible.
32.5: wayyarʾ ʾĔlîhû kî ʾên maʿăneh bĕp̄î šĕlōšeṯ hāʾănāšîm — 'when Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of the three men' — rāʾāh (to see, with understanding); ʾên maʿăneh bĕpî (there is no answer in the mouth); the three had not merely paused but had genuinely exhausted their argumentative resources. wayyiḥar ʾappô — 'his anger was aroused' — the second use of wayyiḥar ʾappô (vv2-5 use it three times for the three objects of his anger); the compressed triple use creates a sense of pent-up heat about to release.
32.6: ʾănî ṣāʿîr lĕyāmîm wĕʾatttem yĕšîšîm — 'I am young in days, and you are very old' — ṣāʿîr (young/junior); yĕšîšîm (the most advanced elders, from yāšēš = to grow very old; used only here and 15:10; a super-aged plural). ʿal-kēn zāḥaltî wāʾîrāʾ mēḥawwōṯ daʿtî ʾetḵem — 'therefore I was afraid and held back from telling you my opinion' — zāḥal (to shrink back/withdraw; used in Deut 32:24 for a serpent-like shrinking; rare); ḥawwāh + daʿat = declaring/showing knowledge. Elihu explicitly narrates his process: age-deference overcoming the urgency of what he had to say.
32.7: ʾāmartî yāmîm yĕdabbĕrû — 'I thought, let the aged speak' — the conventional wisdom protocol that age brings wisdom (cf. 12:12's same maxim, which Job both cites and subverts); Elihu has been operating by this protocol and finding it inadequate. wĕrōḇ šānîm yôḏîʿû ḥāḵmāh — 'and many years teach wisdom' — rōḇ šānîm (multitude of years); yôḏîʿû (make known/teach); the conventional claim Elihu is about to challenge in v8.
32.8: ʾāḵēn rûaḥ-hîʾ ḇĕʾĕnôš — 'but truly, it is the Spirit in a person' — ʾāḵēn (truly/surely, a strong adversative contra the preceding maxim); rûaḥ (spirit/breath, the animating principle); bĕʾĕnôš (in man/humankind). wĕnišmaṯ Šaddai tĕḇînēm — 'and the breath of Shaddai gives understanding' — nišmāh (breath; used in Gen 2:7 for God's breath that animated Adam: 'the LORD God breathed into his nostrils the nišmaṯ ḥayyîm'); Šaddai (El Shaddai, the most common divine name in Job's poetry); tĕḇînēm (makes them understand; from bîn). The verse grounds human understanding not in accumulated experience (age) but in the divine-breath given at creation — a direct link between anthropology (creation) and epistemology. John 20:22's inbreathing of the Spirit ('receive the Holy Spirit') re-enacts this creational breath for the new creation community.
32.9: lō-rabbîm yaḥkāmû — 'it is not the many/great ones who are wise' — rabbîm (many, or 'the great ones'); ḥāḵam (to be wise); the wisdom-egalitarianism of v8 has a direct corollary: greatness and seniority do not automatically confer wisdom. ûzĕqēnîm yāḇînû mišpāṭ — 'nor do the aged always understand what is right' — zĕqēnîm (elders/aged); yāḇîn (understand; the key Elihu verb from bîn); mišpāṭ (justice/right judgment). The verse decouples age and wisdom, seniority and judgment — a remarkable counter-cultural statement in an honor-culture that venerates elders.
32.10: lāḵēn ʾāmartî šimĕʿû-lî — 'therefore I say: listen to me' — lāḵēn (therefore) draws the logical conclusion from vv8-9: since wisdom comes from divine breath rather than age, Elihu has as legitimate a claim to be heard as the elders. ʾăḥawweh daʿtî ap̄-ʾānî — 'I too will state what I know' — ḥawwāh (to declare/show; same root as v6 and v17); ap̄-ʾānî ('even I' — the emphatic first person singular that refuses to accept automatic disqualification by youth). The claim is grounded in the creation-breath of v8, not in presumption.
32.11: hēn ḥikkîṯî lĕdiḇrêḵem — 'look, I waited for your words' — ḥikkîṯî (I waited; from ḥāḵāh, the verb of eager waiting used in v4); the formal acknowledgment that Elihu has fulfilled the protocol of waiting. ʾăḇîn ʿaḏ-taḵlîyûn millîm — 'I listened until you had finished your arguments' — ʾăḇîn (I was understanding/paying attention; from bîn in the imperfect used as a past narrative); taḵlîyûn (you completed; from kālāh = to finish/complete); the auditory attentiveness Elihu claims to have maintained throughout the dialogue.
32.12: wĕʿāḏêhem ʾeṯbônān — 'and I listened carefully to all of you' — hitbônen (to direct one's understanding toward, to give careful attention; the hitpa'el of bîn expressing reflexive and intensive understanding — the same verb as 'staring at' in 30:20). wĕhinnēh ʾên lĕʾîyôḇ mōḵîaḥ — 'and not one of you has refuted Job' — mōḵîaḥ (one who argues/refutes; the hiph'il participle of yāḵaḥ — the forensic-argument root used throughout Job; 13:3,15; 16:21; 23:7); the charge against the three friends: they spoke at length but never actually engaged Job's argument at the forensic level.
32.13: pen-tōʾmĕrû māṣāʾnû ḥāḵmāh — 'do not say: we have found wisdom' — ʾāmar + māṣāʾ ḥāḵmāh (to claim discovery of wisdom); Elihu warns the friends against the self-consoling claim that their failure to answer Job is actually a sign of superior wisdom. ʾēl yiddĕp̄ennû lōʾ ʾîš — 'let God deal with him, not us' — yiddĕp̄eh (from nādap̄ = to drive away/thrust aside; rare); the dismissive passing of Job's case back to God as an evasion of the friends' responsibility. Elihu refuses to allow this theological exit strategy.
32.14: wĕlō-ʿāraḵ ʾēlay millîm — 'he has not directed his argument against me' — ʿārak (to arrange/order, the legal-brief word; cf. 13:18 'I have ordered/ʿāraktî my case'); Job has not targeted his argument at Elihu specifically. wĕḇdibĕrêḵem lōʾ ʾăšîḇennû — 'and I will not reply to him with your kind of arguments' — the formal distancing from the three friends' approach: Elihu commits in advance to a different argumentative strategy. The verse establishes Elihu as a genuinely independent voice, not merely a fourth iteration of the retributive framework.
32.15: ḥattû lōʾ-ʿānû ʿôd — 'they are astonished, they say nothing more' — ḥāṯat (to be dismayed/shattered; the verb of complete psychological collapse under overwhelming evidence or divine presence; Josh 1:9; Isa 31:4); the three friends have been silenced not by persuasion but by the weight of Job's unanswered arguments. nāśĕʿû mēhem millîm — 'words have left them' — nāśāʾ (to carry away/lift up); millîm (words/arguments); the arguments have been carried away, leaving the friends empty-handed.
32.16: wĕhôḥaltî kî-lō yĕdabbĕrû — 'I waited because they did not speak' — ḥûl/ḥîl (to wait/writhe in labor-pains; the word for the birthing-labor used in Ps 22:9-10 and Isa 26:17-18); Elihu's waiting is not passive but like labor contractions. kî ʿāmĕḏû lō-ʿānû ʿôd — 'they stood there and answered no more' — ʿāmaḏ (to stand/remain standing); the three friends standing speechless is a visual tableau of intellectual defeat.
32.17: ʾaʿăneh ʾap̄-ʾānî ḥelqî — 'then I said: I too will give my answer, my portion' — ḥēleq (portion/allotment; the same word as the divine 'portion from above' in 31:2); Elihu's turn to speak is his appointed ḥēleq — his divinely allotted portion of the dialogue. ʾăḥawweh daʿtî ʾānî — 'I too will state what I know' — a slight variation of v10b; the repetition creates a rhetorical frame around the waiting-and-watching section (vv10-17) that preceded his actual speech.
32.18: kî māleʾtî millîm — 'for I am full of words' — māleʾ (to be full/filled; the fullness-of-speech word); millîm (words). hĕṣîqatnî rûaḥ ḇiṭnî — 'the spirit within me urges me on' — hēṣîq (to press/constrain; from ṣûq = to press/squeeze; used in Jer 19:9 for siege-pressure); rûaḥ (spirit, picking up the rûaḥ of v8's creation-breath; Elihu's inner compulsion to speak is the action of the same Spirit that gives understanding); ḇeṭen (belly/interior, the deep inner self). The compulsion to speak from an inner spiritual pressure is exactly what the NT prophets describe (1 Cor 9:16 'necessity is laid upon me'; Acts 4:20 'we cannot but speak').
32.19: hinnēh-ḇiṭnî kĕyayin lō-yippāṯēaḥ — 'my belly is like wine that has no outlet' — ḇeṭen (the inner vessel); yayyin (wine); pāṯaḥ (to open); the fermentation-pressure metaphor: wine sealed without a vent will burst the container. kĕʾōḇôṯ ḥăḏāšîm yibbāqēaʿ — 'like new wineskins about to burst' — ʾōḇ (wineskin/bellows; a skin bag); ḥăḏāšîm (new/fresh); bāqaʿ (to split/burst open). The new-wineskin image is used by Jesus in Matt 9:17 / Mark 2:22 / Luke 5:37-38 for the new wine of the kingdom that cannot be contained in old wineskins — the gospel's explosive new content demanding new forms. Elihu's metaphor prefigures the kind of pressure that the divine speech from the whirlwind will exert.
32.20: ʾăḏabbĕrāh wĕyirwaḥ lî — 'I will speak and find relief' — rāwaḥ (to breathe/find relief; from rûaḥ = breath/spirit; the physical relief of releasing pent-up breath as metaphor for finally speaking what has been held back). ʾep̄tāḥ śĕp̄āṯay wĕʾeʿĕneh — 'I will open my lips and answer' — the opening of lips as the formal beginning of speech; pātaḥ (to open); the verse signals Elihu's transition from preamble to the actual content of his argument that begins in ch33.
32.21: ʾal-nāʾ ʾeśśāʾ p̄ĕnê-ʾîš — 'I will not show partiality to any person' — nāśāʾ p̄ānîm (to lift the face/show favoritism; the judicial corruption idiom; Lev 19:15 prohibits it explicitly); Job has accused the friends of divine advocacy through partiality (13:8); Elihu renounces it in advance. wĕʾel-ʾādām lō ʾăḵanneh — 'nor will I give flattering titles to any man' — kinnāh (to give a title/honorific to; from kinnûy = nickname/epithet; rare). Elihu's commitment to titles-free, partiality-free speech sets up the authority of what follows — his argument stands on its merits, not on rhetorical deference.
32.22: kî lō yāḏaʿtî ʾăḵanneh — 'for I do not know how to flatter' — the confession of incapacity for flattery as a mark of integrity; yāḏaʿ (to know how); ʾăḵanneh (to give honorific titles, from v21). kimʿaṭ yiśśāʾēnî ʿōśenî — 'if I did, my Maker would soon take me away' — kimʿaṭ (quickly/soon; the word of almost-immediacy); nāśāʾ (to carry away/remove); ʿōśenî (my Maker; from ʿāśāh = to make/do). The ground of Elihu's integrity is theological accountability: to flatter would be to court immediate divine removal. The chapter ends with the same divine Maker who gave the understanding-breath (v8) serving as the guarantor of honest speech.

42.1: wayyaʿan ʾiyyôḇ ʾeṯ-YHWH wayyōʾmar: Standard dialogue formula — 'Then Job answered YHWH and said.' But the divine name here is the full covenant name YHWH (the tetragrammaton), not the generic ʾĒl or ʾělōhîm that dominates the poetic dialogues. This shift back to YHWH marks the return to the prose-frame register and signals that the dialogue has reached its covenant resolution. Job is answering — not arguing with — the God who has just spoken from the whirlwind for two chapters. The very form of the speech is response, not counter-petition: Job has received his theophany.
42.2: yāḏaʿtî kî-ḵōl tûḵāl wəlōʾ-yibbāṣēr mimməḵā məzimmāh: 'I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.' Job's first word is yāḏaʿtî (I know, I have come to know — perfect tense, indicating a new state of knowing established by the theophany). The content of his knowledge is divine omnipotence and purposefulness: kōl tûḵāl (you can do all things — yāḵōl with 'all') and lōʾ-yibbāṣēr məzimmāh (no purpose/plan can be cut off from you — bāṣar, to cut off/withhold, is used of withholding grape clusters from harvest). The response engages directly with what YHWH's whirlwind speeches were demonstrating: the Creator-God's comprehensive sovereignty over every created system. Job's theology has been corrected not by argument but by encounter.
42.3: mî zeh maʿlîm ʿēṣāh bəḇəlî-ḏaʿaṯ lāḵēn higgaḏtî wəlōʾ ʾāḇîn nip̄lāʾôṯ mimmennî wəlōʾ ʾēḏāʿ: 'Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.'
