The right hand that saves is YHWH's (Ps 98:1; Isa 63:5).
40.15: Rĕʾēh-nāʾ bĕhēmôt ʾăšer-ʿāśîtî ʿimmāk: 'Look at Behemoth, which I made just as I made you' — bĕhēmôt: plural of intensity from bĕhēmāh (beast, animal) — the super-beast. The identification is debated (hippopotamus most likely; others suggest a mythological creature). ʿĀśîtî ʿimmāk: 'I made alongside you' — Job and Behemoth are fellow-creatures of the same Creator.
40.16: Hinnēh nāʾ kōḥô bĕmotnāyw: 'Look at the strength in his haunches' — the anatomical survey of Behemoth's power. Wĕʾônô bišrîrê biṭnô: 'the power in the muscles of his belly' — ʾôn (strength, virility): the term for reproductive and physical potency. Behemoth's power is fundamentally bodily, concentrated in its core.
40.17: Yāpōẑ zĕnāḇô kĕʾārez: 'He swings his tail like a cedar' — the tail-comparison to a cedar tree suggests the large, powerful tail of a hippopotamus (not the stubby tail of an elephant, making 'elephant' identification less likely). Gîdê pāḥadāyw yĕśôrāgû: 'the sinews of his thighs are tightly woven' — the anatomical detail of the powerful thigh-muscles.
40.18: ʿAṣāmāyw ʾăpîqê nĕḥūšāh: 'His bones are like bronze pipes' — the mineral comparison for bone-density. Gĕrāmāyw kĕmôṭ barzel: 'his limbs are like bars of iron' — the dual mineral comparison (bronze/iron) establishes Behemoth's structural invulnerability to normal force.
40.19: Hûʾ rĕʾîšît darkê-ʾēl: 'He ranks first among God's works' — rĕʾîšît (first, chief): Behemoth as the premier example of divine creative power in the animal realm. Hāʿōśô yaggaš ḥarbô: 'only his Maker can approach him with a sword' — the exclusive divine prerogative over Behemoth. No human can subdue him; only God can.
40.20: Kî-yĕḇûl hārîm yiśśĕʾû-lô: 'The mountains produce food for him' — the scale of Behemoth's provisioning: mountains supply his diet. Kol-ḥayyat haśśāḏeh yĕśaḥăqû-šām: 'all the wild beasts play around him' — the paradox: the most powerful creature is surrounded by smaller animals that play near him without fear.
40.21: Taḥat ṣeʾĕlîm yiškāḇ: 'He rests beneath the lotus plants' — ṣeʾĕlîm (lotus plants, or perhaps a tree): the shade-vegetation of the Nile delta or Jordan valley, consistent with hippopotamus habitat. Bĕsēter qāneh ûḇiṣṣāh: 'sheltered in the reeds and swamps' — the marshy habitat of the hippo.
40.22: Yĕsukuhû ṣillālîm ṣalmāwet: 'The trees shade him with deep shadow' — ṣalmāwet (deep shadow, shadow of death): the word usually translated 'valley of the shadow of death' (Ps 23:4) here describes Behemoth's shade-canopy. ʿArĕḇê-naḥal yisabbĕḇûhû: 'willows of the brook encircle him' — the botanical precision of the riparian habitat.
40.23: Hēn yaʿăšōq nāhār lōʾ yaḥpôz: 'Even if the river floods, Behemoth is untroubled' — ʿāšaq (to oppress, overwhelm): flood-force does not move him. Yiḇṭaḥ kî-yāgîaḥ yardēn: 'he is at ease though the Jordan rushes into his mouth' — the Jordan at flood-force is merely a drink for Behemoth. The cosmic confidence of a creature designed for power in water.
40.24: Bĕʿênāyw yiqqāḥennû: 'Can anyone catch him while he watches?' — the rhetorical answer is no. Bĕmôqĕšîm yinnaq-ʾāp: 'can anyone trap him by piercing his nose?' — the standard animal-capture technique (nose-ring/rope): impossible with Behemoth. He cannot be mastered.

41.1: Timšōk liwyātān bĕḥakkāh: 'Can you pull up Leviathan with a hook?' — liwyātān (Leviathan): the great sea-serpent/dragon. The name likely derives from lāwāh (to coil/twine) + the augmented form. In Ugaritic mythology, Leviathan (ltn) is the seven-headed sea-dragon defeated by Baal. The OT uses the figure variably: as a literal creature (here and Ps 104:26) and as a mythological chaos-symbol (Isa 27:1; Ps 74:14). God's ownership of Leviathan is the theological point. Ûḇĕḥeḇel tišqîaʿ lĕšônô: 'or hold down his tongue with a rope?' — capture is impossible.
41.2: Hăḵĕrōm tāšîm bĕʾappô: 'Can you put a rush through his nose?' — ʾagmôn (rush, reed): the material for a nose-ring. The nose-ring/hook was the standard device for controlling large animals. Ûḇĕḥôaḥ tiṭqoḇ laḥyô: 'or run a hook through his jaw?' — the jaw-hook: equally impossible. Neither instrument of animal mastery works on Leviathan.
41.3: Hăyarbeh ʾēlêkā taḥănûnîm: 'Will he beg you for mercy?' — taḥănûnîm (pleas for favor/mercy): the petitioner's vocabulary. The absurdity: Leviathan as suppliant before a human. Ûyĕdabbēr ʾēlêkā rakkôt: 'will he address you with soft words?' — rāk (soft, gentle): the opposite of the harsh words he actually produces. Leviathan does not plead; he threatens.
41.4: Hăyikrōt bĕrît ʾittāk: 'Will he make a covenant with you?' — kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant): the covenant-making formula. Leviathan cannot be treaty-bound. Tiqāḥennû leʿeḇed ʿôlām: 'will you bind him as your permanent slave?' — leʿeḇed ʿôlām (permanent slave): the deepest category of servitude. Impossible with Leviathan.
41.5: Hătĕśaḥeq-bô kĕṣippôr: 'Will you play with him like a bird?' — the diminutive comparison: a pet bird vs. Leviathan. Ûtiqqĕšennû lĕnaʿărôtêkā: 'will you leash him for your young women?' — the domestication-play imagery. The absurdity drives home the point: no human treats Leviathan as a pet.
41.6: Yikrû ʿālāyw ḥabbārîm: 'Will trading partners negotiate for him?' — ḥabbār (partner, associate): the commercial-partner term. Leviathan cannot be commodified or traded. Yĕḥaṣṣûhû bên kĕnaʿănîm: 'will merchants distribute him among buyers?' — the Phoenician trading network (kĕnaʿĕnîm = Canaanites = traders) cannot handle Leviathan as merchandise.
41.7: Hătĕmallēʾ bĕśukkôt ʿôrô: 'Can you fill his skin with harpoons?' — sukkāh (here: harpoon/barbed spear — specialized fishing equipment): the instruments of large-game marine hunting. ûḇĕṣaltĕʾselê dāgāh rōʾšô: 'or his head with fishing lances?' — the futility of applying normal hunting tools to Leviathan.
41.8: Śîm-ʿālāyw kappekā: 'Lay your hand on him' — the imperative of attempted contact. Tizkōr milḥāmāh ʾal-tôsep: 'you will remember the encounter and never try again' — a single touch will be sufficient to teach permanent respect. The experiential knowledge of Leviathan cannot be acquired a second time.
41.9: Hēn tôḥaltô niḵzāḇāh: 'Any hope of mastering him is an illusion' — niḵzāḇāh (is proved false/deceptive): the hope of subduing Leviathan is inherently false hope. ʾAp-ʾel-marʾāyô yulāl: 'even the sight of him knocks a man down' — not the encounter but merely the sight produces collapse. Leviathan overwhelms by appearance alone.
41.10: Lōʾ-ʾakzār kî yĕʿûrennû: 'No one is fierce enough to rouse him' — ʾakzār (fierce, ruthless): the quality required to provoke Leviathan. ûmî hûʾ lĕpānay yitʿaṣṣāḇ: 'who then is able to stand before me?' — the pivot: if no one dares rouse Leviathan, who dares stand before the Creator of Leviathan? The rhetorical question moves from creature to Creator.
41.11: Mî hiqdîm-lî waʾăšallēm: 'Who has given to me first, that I should owe him in return?' — hiqḏîm (to anticipate, go before, give first): the claim that God owes anything to a creature because the creature acted first. Paul quotes this in Rom 11:35. Taḥat kol-haššāmayim lî-hûʾ: 'everything under all the heavens belongs to me' — the absolute sovereignty statement that makes obligation to a creature logically impossible. No creature can place God in its debt.
41.12: Lōʾ-ʾaḥărîš baḏāyw: 'I will not be silent about his structure/limbs' — baḏ (limb, part, branch): Leviathan's anatomical components will be narrated. ûḏbar gĕḇûrôt ûḥîn ʿerkô: 'his power and the grace of his proportion' — ḥîn ʿerkô (the grace/beauty of his arrangement): Leviathan is described as beautiful. The chaos-creature has aesthetic excellence.
41.13: Mî-gillāh pĕnê lĕḇûšô: 'Who can remove the outer layer of his skin?' — lĕḇûš (garment, outer layer): the skin as clothing. Bĕḵepal rissĕnô mî yāḇôʾ: 'who can break through his double layer of defense?' — riśsôn (bridle, bit — here: the double armor): the overlapping armor-like skin of the crocodilian.
41.14: Daltê pānāyw mî pātāḥ: 'Who can pry open the doors of his mouth?' — the jaws as locked doors. Sĕḇîḇôt šinnāyw ʾêmāh: 'his teeth are ringed with dread' — šinnāyw (teeth): surrounding each tooth is ʾêmāh (dread, terror): the teeth themselves inspire terror.
41.15: Gāʾăwāh ʾăpîqê māginnîm: 'His back is like rows of shields' — the scale-armor described as military shields in formation. Sāgûr ḥôtām ṣar: 'locked together like a tight seal' — ḥôtām (seal, signet): the scales fit as tightly as a seal impression, leaving no gap.
41.16: ʾEḥāḏ bĕʾeḥāḏ yiggāšû: 'Each one pressed so close to the next' — the interlocking precision of the scales. Wĕrûaḥ lōʾ-yāḇôʾ bênêhem: 'no breath of air passes between them' — the absolute seal of Leviathan's armor: not even air can penetrate. The most complete defensive structure in creation.
41.17: ʾîš bĕʾāḥîw yĕdubāqû: 'They cling together one to another' — the scales described as companions clinging. Yitlapĕtû wĕlōʾ yitpārāḏû: 'they lock onto each other and will not separate' — lāpaṯ (to grasp, seize): the scales' mutual grip is permanent.
41.18: ʿAṭîšōtāyw ʾôr tĕhallel: 'His sneezes flash light' — the dragon's fire-breathing described through the sneeze. Wĕʿênāyw kĕʿapʿappê šāḥar: 'his eyes shine like the gleam of dawn' — šāḥar (dawn): the eyelids of dawn as the comparison for Leviathan's eyes. Both fire and light: the creature embodies elemental power aesthetically.
41.19: Millĕpîw lappîḏîm yahalōkû: 'Burning torches go from his mouth' — the fire-breathing imagery. Kîḏôḏê ʾēš yimlāṭû: 'sparks of fire fly out' — kîḏôḏ (spark): the sparks of Leviathan's breath. The chaos-creature as a fire-elemental.
41.20: Minnĕḥîrāyw yēṣēʾ ʿāšān: 'Smoke billows from his nostrils' — the nostrils as smoke-vents. Kĕdûḏ nāpûaḥ wĕʾagmôn: 'like a boiling pot over burning rushes' — the domestic cooking-fire comparison: the world's most fearsome creature compared to a kitchen pot, but the quantity and heat are Leviathan's.
41.21: Napšô geḥālîm tĕlahaṭ: 'His breath sets coals afire' — nepeš (breath, soul, life): the breath as the source of fire. Wĕlahab mippiyw yēṣēʾ: 'a flame shoots out of his mouth' — the comprehensive fire-generation: both breath-heat and visible flame. The chaos-creature as lord of fire.
41.22: Bĕṣawwāʾrô yālîn ʿōz: 'Strength resides in his neck' — the neck as the seat of power. Wĕlĕpānāyw tādûṣ dĕʾābāh: 'dismay dances before him' — dĕʾābāh (dismay, sorrow, despair) as Leviathan's advance-companion. Terror precedes his movement.
41.23: Mapĕlê bĕśārô ḏāḇĕqû: 'The layers of his flesh are locked together' — dāḇēq (to cling, stick): the flesh as armored as the scales. Yāṣûq ʿālāyw bal-yimmôṭ: 'hard on him and immovable' — cast (yāṣaq) solid, not movable. The anatomical description emphasizes the invulnerability of every layer.
41.24: Libbô yāṣûq kĕmo-ʾāben: 'His heart is like cast stone' — the heart as the deepest/innermost layer: also cast solid. Wĕyāṣûq kĕpeleḥ taḥtît: 'as hard as the lower millstone' — the lower millstone (the fixed stone, not the rotating one): the hardest, most fixed stone in common experience. Leviathan's heart is the most interior expression of his invulnerability.
41.25: Mĕśāw yāgûrû ʾêlîm: 'When he rears up, the mighty are terrified' — ʾêl (strong man, mighty one): even the powerful fear Leviathan's rising. Miššĕḇārîm yiṯḥaṭṭāʾû: 'they fall back before his thrashing' — šāḇar (waves, or here: thrashings): the waves/chaos-motions of Leviathan cause retreat.
41.26: Massîgēhû ḥereḇ bal-tāqûm: 'The sword that reaches him cannot hold' — every weapon fails against Leviathan's armor. Ḥănît wĕdarda ûširyāh: 'spear, dart, and javelin' — the full inventory of ancient ranged weapons: all useless.
41.27: Yaḥšōḇ lĕṯeḇen barzel: 'He regards iron as straw' — the invulnerability scale: iron is straw, bronze is rotten wood. Wĕnĕḥūšāh kĕʿēṣ rāqāḇ: 'bronze is like rotten wood' — the materials of weapon-making are worthless against Leviathan. The standard military materials fail completely.
41.28: Lōʾ-yabriḥennû ben-qāšeṯ: 'The arrow cannot make him flee' — ben-qāšeṯ (son of the bow/arrow): poetic for the arrow. Lĕqaš nehpĕḵû-lô ʾabnê-qālaʿ: 'sling stones become chaff to him' — sling stones, which could kill Goliath (1 Sam 17:49), are meaningless against Leviathan.
41.29: Kĕqaš neḥšĕḇû tôṭāḥ: 'He regards war clubs as straw' — the close-combat weapons are as useless as the ranged ones. Wayiśḥaq lĕraaš kîḏôn: 'he laughs at the shaking of a lance' — śāḥaq (to laugh): the laughter motif appears again, now applied to Leviathan laughing at human weapons, as the horse laughed at fear (39:22).
41.30: Taḥtāyw ḥaddûḏê ḥereś: 'His underbelly is like sharp pottery shards' — the only potentially vulnerable surface (the belly) is itself sharpened. Yirpaḏ ḥārûṣ ʿălê-ṭîṭ: 'he plows jagged furrows through the mud' — Leviathan's passage itself creates destruction. His belly weapon-izes the ground.
41.31: Yaṯrîaḥ kaṣṣîr mĕṣûlāh: 'He makes the depths churn like a boiling pot' — mĕṣûlāh (depth, abyss): the deep sea becomes a boiling pot under Leviathan's movement. Wĕyāśem kayĕrôqāḥ: 'he churns the sea like a jar of perfume' — the sea reduced to a stirred cosmetic jar. Leviathan's power expressed through domestic compression.
41.32: ʾAḥărāyw yaʾîr nātîḇ: 'A glistening path shines behind him' — the phosphorescent wake of Leviathan's movement. Yaḥšōḇ tĕhôm lĕśêḇāh: 'one would think the sea had turned white' — the abyss (tĕhôm, the deep of Gen 1:2) appears white-haired behind Leviathan's wake. The deep itself appears to age in his presence.
41.33: ʾên ʿălāyw mōšēl: 'There is nothing on earth to compare with him' — māšal (to rule, to compare): no earthly creature masters Leviathan or serves as his comparison. ʿĀśûy lĕḇĕlî-ḥāt: 'he is fashioned without fear' — bĕlî-ḥāt (without fear): fearlessness built into his design. The creature who inspires fear in everything else was made without fear himself.
41.34: Kol-gāḇōah yirʾeh: 'He surveys everything that exalts itself' — Leviathan as the surveyor-king of the proud. Hûʾ melek ʿal-kol-bĕnê-šāḥaṣ: 'he is king over all the proud' — bĕnê šāḥaṣ (children of pride/arrogance): the proud and haughty are Leviathan's subjects. The chaos-monster who cannot be tamed by any human king is himself king of everything that exalts itself. The implication: Job's proud demand for a legal confrontation with God placed him in Leviathan's realm. The God who made and masters Leviathan is the one Job was challenging.

36.1: wayyōsep̄ ʾĕlîhûʾ wayyōʾmar: 'Elihu continued and said.' Yāsap̄ (to add, continue) — Elihu resumes without being challenged or invited. His four-speech sequence (chs. 32-37) is structurally distinct from the three-friend dialogues: Elihu is never condemned by YHWH (42:7 explicitly names only Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar), and his speeches transition into the divine speech from the whirlwind. Whether Elihu's theology is vindicated, silently ignored, or simply incomplete is one of the book's interpretive cruxes.
36.2: kattēr-lî zeʿēr waʾahawwêḵā kî ʿôḏ lᵉʾĕlôah millîm: 'Bear with me a little longer and I will show you, for I still have words to speak for God.' Kattēr (wait a little, endure a moment — possibly from a root meaning 'wait') is a hapax. Millîm lᵉʾĕlôah: 'words for God' — Elihu positions himself as God's defense counsel. The claim to speak on God's behalf is significant: it's the same role Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar claimed, but YHWH eventually rejects their speech as wrong (42:7). Elihu's claim is not refuted, but neither is it explicitly affirmed.
36.3: ʾeśśāʾ ḏaʿtî lᵉmērāḥôq ûlᵉpōʿălî ʾetēn-ṣeḏeq: 'I will draw my knowledge from a wide range and ascribe righteousness to my Maker.' Lᵉmērāḥôq (from a distance, from afar) — Elihu draws on broad knowledge, not just immediate experience. The phrase anticipates YHWH's own speech in ch. 38-41, where God surveys cosmic and natural phenomena from the widest possible vantage point. Ṣeḏeq (righteousness, justice) — Elihu's purpose is theodicy: to vindicate God's ways.
36.4: kî-ʾāmnām lōʾ-šeqer millāy ûtᵉmîm ḏēʿôt ʿimmāḵ: 'For truly my words are not false; one complete in knowledge stands before you.' Elihu's self-description as tᵉmîm ḏēʿôt (complete/perfect in knowledge) is striking — the same adjective used of Job in 1:1 (tām = blameless, complete). Whether this is overconfidence or genuine claim depends on how YHWH's subsequent silence about Elihu is read. The claim of complete knowledge is exactly what the divine speech will implicitly deny to all humans — including Elihu.
36.5: hēn-ʾēl kabbîr wᵉlōʾ yimʾās kabbîr kōaḥ lēḇ: 'Behold, God is mighty and despises no one; he is mighty in strength of understanding.' Kabbîr (mighty, powerful — used of God's arm in Isa 17:12 and his voice in Job 37:23) occurs twice, framing God's power as both cosmic and relational. The key claim: God's might does not produce contempt (mā ʾas = to reject, despise) — an implicit rebuttal of Job's charge that God treats him with sovereign disregard. Kōaḥ lēḇ: 'strength of heart/mind' — divine power joined with divine intelligence and compassion.
36.6: lōʾ yeḥayyeh rāšāʿ ûmišpāṭ ʿăniyyîm yittēn: 'He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives justice to the poor.' The retribution principle stated positively: God's power works against the wicked and for the vulnerable. ʿĀnî (poor, humble, afflicted) — the classic prophetic concern for the oppressed (Amos 2:7; 5:12; Isa 61:1). Elihu is presenting a theodicy that takes the poor's justice seriously while maintaining divine sovereignty. The claim here is essentially the same as Eliphaz's (22:6-9), but Elihu arrives at it differently.
36.7: lōʾ-yiḡraʿ miṣṣaddîq ʿênāyw wᵉʾet-mᵉlāḵîm lakkisseʾ wayyôšiḇēm lānetsaḥ wayyiḡbāhû: 'He does not take his eyes off the righteous; he seats them on the throne with kings and exalts them forever.' Gāraʿ (to diminish, take away) — God does not diminish his gaze from the righteous. The elevated-to-king imagery (kissēʾ = throne) has a messianic resonance: the vindicated righteous person is ultimately seated on a royal throne. The language anticipates Job's own eventual restoration (42:12-16) and the larger biblical pattern of the humiliated righteous exalted (Ps 113:7-8; 1 Sam 2:8; Luke 1:52).
36.8: wᵉʾim ʾăsûrîm bazziqîm yillāḵᵉdûn bᵉḥeḇlê ʿōnî: 'And if they are bound in chains, caught in ropes of affliction.' The condition: if the righteous suffer (ʾăsûrîm bazziqîm = bound in chains, a prisoner's vocabulary). Ziqqîm (chains, fetters — rare; cf. Ps 149:8; Nah 3:10) and ḥeḇlê ʿōnî (cords of affliction) describe physical and experiential suffering as a condition that may befall even the righteous. This is Elihu's key distinction from the three friends: for Elihu, suffering can be pedagogical rather than purely punitive — the question is how the sufferer responds to it.
36.9: wayyaggēḏ lāhem pāʿolām ûp̄išʿêhem kî yiṯgabbārû: 'Then he shows them what they have done, their transgressions — that they have acted arrogantly.' Gābar (to be strong, to act arrogantly, to prevail) — the arrogance (yiṯgabbārû, hithpael = to make oneself great, to act presumptuously) is what suffering reveals and addresses. God uses affliction as a diagnostic revelation: the suffering exposes the self-sufficiency and pride that the sufferer may not otherwise acknowledge. This is Elihu's theodicy: God speaks through suffering to bring the proud to clarity about their actual condition.
36.10: wayyiḡl ʾāznam lammûsār wayyōʾmar kî-yāšuḇû mēʾāwen: 'He opens their ears to correction and commands that they turn from evil.' Gālāh ʾōzen (to open/uncover the ear) is the technical idiom for intimate disclosure — used of prophetic revelation (1 Sam 9:15; 2 Sam 7:27; Isa 22:14) and here for the insight that suffering produces. Mûsār (correction, discipline, instruction — the central Proverbs term) is what the suffering conveys. The ear-opening through suffering is exactly what Job needs: not to hear more arguments from the friends, but to receive divine revelation that opens his understanding.
36.11: ʾim-yišmᵉʿû wayyaʿōḇōḏû yᵉḵallû yᵉmêhem baṭṭôḇ ûšᵉnôṯêhem bannᵉʿîmîm: 'If they listen and serve him, they will complete their days in prosperity and their years in pleasantness.' The conditional blessing: šāmaʿ (hear/obey — the covenantal Shema verb) + ʿāḇad (to serve) leads to ṭôḇ (goodness, prosperity) and nᵉʿîm (pleasantness, delight — cf. Ps 16:11; 133:1). This is the Deuteronomic covenant blessing structure (Deut 28:1-14) applied individually. The promise is not false; the problem the book has demonstrated is that it cannot be reversed: prosperity does not prove obedience, nor does suffering prove sin.
36.12: wᵉʾim-lōʾ yišmᵉʿû bašelaḥ yaʿāḇōrû ûyiḡwᵉʿû bilᵉʾî-ḏāʿat: 'But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword and die without ever understanding.' Bilᵉʾî ḏāʿat (without knowledge) — the worst outcome is not suffering but dying without ever understanding what the suffering was meant to teach. This death-without-insight is the opposite of the wisdom-goal that Proverbs sets: to die in full knowledge of God's ways. Elihu frames the stakes of Job's response: will he receive the instruction, or die incomprehendingly?
36.13: wᵉḥanpê lēḇ yāśîmû ʾāp̄ lōʾ-yᵉšawwᵉʿû kî ʾăsārām: 'The godless in heart store up anger; they do not cry to God when he binds them.' Ḥānēp̄ (godless, profane, polluted — used of one who has defiled the covenant, Isa 33:14; Jer 3:9) describes not atheism but practical denial of God's authority. Yāśîm ʾāp̄ (to store up/place anger) — resentment accumulating rather than being resolved in prayer. The contrast: the godless respond to divine binding with stored anger rather than with the prayer that would open them to instruction (lōʾ yᵉšawwᵉʿû = they do not cry out for help).
36.14: tāmōt bannōʿar nap̄šām wᵉḥayyāṯām baqqᵉḏēšîm: 'They die in youth, and their life ends among the cult prostitutes.' Qāḏēš (male cult prostitute, one devoted to a deity's sexual rites) — a reference to the Canaanite fertility cult practices condemned throughout the OT (Deut 23:17-18; 1 Kgs 14:24; 15:12; 22:46). Dying in youth and dying in the company of the cult prostitute are both signs of the dishonorable end: cut short, in a defiled context. The verse provides an ANE social illustration of how the path of refusing divine instruction terminates.
36.15: yaḥallēṣ ʿānî bᵉʿōnyô wᵉyiḡal balaḥaṣ ʾāznām: 'But he rescues the afflicted person through their suffering and opens their ears through oppression.' The paradox that is Elihu's central contribution: the rescue comes through the suffering (bᵉʿōnyô), not from outside it. Yiḡal (to open wide, uncover — from gālāh) through laḥaṣ (oppression, pressure) — the very pressure that the afflicted experience is the instrument of revelation. The ear-opening through pressure is the inverse of the hardening of the heart through prosperity; Elihu's theodicy makes affliction productive rather than punitive.
36.16: wᵉʾap̄ hᵉsîtᵉḵā mippî ṣar raḥāḇ lōʾ-mûṣāq taḥtêhā ûnaḥat šulḥānᵉḵā mālaʾ dāšen: 'Indeed, he was drawing you from the jaws of distress into a broad place, free from pressure, where your table would be spread with rich food.' Elihu applies the general principle to Job directly. Raḥāḇ (broad, spacious — the opposite of ṣar = narrow, confined) is the image of liberty and salvation (Ps 4:1; 18:19; 31:8). The promise of a full table (šulḥān mālaʾ dāšen = table full of fat/abundance) uses the hospitality image of Ps 23:5. Elihu sees Job's restoration as already initiated — God was in the process of deliverance before Job became impatient.
36.17: wᵉdîn rāšāʿ mālaʾtā dîn ûmišpāṭ yitmōḵū: 'But now you are full of the judgment due the wicked; judgment and justice have seized you.' Elihu's diagnosis of Job's current condition: he is experiencing the dîn rāšāʿ (judgment of the wicked) — not because he is wicked, but because his response to suffering has become the response of the godless (v.13). His accumulation of resentment and his legal challenge to God have moved him into the category of the wrongfully responding sufferer. This is a more nuanced charge than the friends' direct accusation of sin.
36.18: kî-ḥēmāh pᵉn-yᵉsîtᵉḵā ḇᵉśāpeq wᵉraḇ-kōp̄er ʾal-yaṭṭeḵā: 'Beware that anger does not lead you into scoffing; do not let the greatness of the required payment turn you away.' Ḥēmāh (wrath, rage) — the hot anger Job has expressed toward God. Śāpeq (scoffing, striking the hand, mockery) — the action of contemptuous rejection. Kōp̄er (ransom, expiation price, cover-price) — a technical legal term for the payment that resolves a legal dispute. Elihu warns that Job's rage may lead him to reject the divinely offered resolution of his case through scoffing pride.
36.19: hᵉyaʿᵃrōḵ šûʿāṯᵉḵā lōʾ bᵉṣar wᵉḵōl maʿămaddê-kōaḥ: 'Will your wealth hold you in distress, or all the forces of your strength?' The rhetorical question challenges Job's reliance on former resources. Šûʿāh (cry for help, distress) and maʿămaddê kōaḥ (resources of strength, what one can lean on) — even if Job marshaled all his former wealth and social power, these cannot resolve his case before God. The question strips away the human resources that might substitute for divine resolution.
36.20: ʾal-tišʾap̄ hallāylāh laʿălôt ʿammîm taḥtām: 'Do not long for the night, when peoples vanish from where they stand.' Šāʾap̄ hallāylāh (to pant/long for the night) — Job has expressed a longing for darkness and death (3:3-10; 7:4; 10:18-22). Elihu warns against this: the night in which peoples 'go up' (ʿālāh) from their place — i.e., the darkness of death — is not the resolution Job should seek. The warning is that seeking escape through death forecloses the possibility of resolution through response to divine instruction.
36.21: hišāmer ʾal-tēpen ʾel-ʾāwen kî-ʿal-zeh bāḥartā mēʿōnî: 'Be careful not to turn toward evil, for this is what you have been choosing over enduring affliction.' Bāḥar (to choose, elect — the covenantal election verb) — Elihu frames Job's response pattern as a choice: he has been choosing ʾāwen (evil, iniquity, nothingness) over ʿōnî (affliction, poverty). The word ʿōnî here means not just pain but the productive suffering through which God teaches (v.15). Job has chosen to fight his suffering rather than receive its instruction.
36.22: hēn ʾēl yāśgîḇ bᵉkōḥô mî kāmōhû môreh: 'Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher like him?' Śāgaḇ (to be exalted, inaccessibly high) — God's transcendence is not mere distance but the height that makes him the supreme teacher. Môreh (teacher, one who instructs) — a title used of human teachers (Isa 30:20) and here of God himself. The question mî kāmōhû (who is like him?) is the standard doxological rhetorical question (Exod 15:11; Mic 7:18; Ps 35:10). Elihu transitions from theodicy argument to praise: the God who uses suffering is the incomparable teacher.
36.23: mî-p̄āqaḏ ʿālāyw darkô ûmî-ʾāmar pāʿaltā ʿawlāh: 'Who has prescribed his path for him? Who can say to him, “You have done wrong”?' Pāqaḏ ʿāl darkô (to appoint/prescribe his path for him) — the verb of divine appointment used for human oversight of God. The question anticipates YHWH's own challenge to Job in 38:2-3: who dares to question God? Elihu frames the human inability to audit God's actions — the transition to the whirlwind speech is logical: no human can prescribe God's path, and YHWH's questions will demonstrate precisely this.
36.24: zᵉḵōr kî-taśgîʾā p̄āʿolô ʾăšer šōrᵉrû ʾănāšîm: 'Remember to extol his work, which people have celebrated in song.' Śāgāʾ (to magnify, make great) — extolling God's works is the antidote to the resentment that leads to rejecting divine instruction (v.13). The appeal to what humanity has sung (šārar = to sing, here in piel participle) about God's works is an appeal to the worshipping tradition: the psalms, hymns, and songs of Israel that celebrate divine action in creation and history. Job should situate himself within this tradition rather than positioning himself against it.
36.25: kāl-ʾāḏām ḥāzû-ḇô ʾĕnôš yabbîṭ mērāḥôq: 'All people have seen it; everyone looks at it from a distance.' The universality of natural revelation: all humanity can observe God's works in creation (ḥāzāh = to see, gaze upon — also used for prophetic vision). The looking from a distance (mērāḥôq) does not mean ignorance but appropriate creaturely distance before the inaccessible Creator. No human can stand close enough to fully comprehend; all humanity is in the position of observer-from-afar relative to God's cosmic operations.
36.26: hēn-ʾēl śāggîʾ wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ mispar šānāyw wᵉlōʾ ḥēqer: 'Behold, God is great, beyond our knowing; the number of his years cannot be counted.' Śāggîʾ (great, surpassingly great — an Aramaic-influenced form) — God's greatness is not merely quantitative but qualitatively beyond human comprehension. The un-numberable years (mispar šānāyw wᵉlōʾ ḥēqer = his years cannot be searched out) point to God's eternity: he antedates the world he created. This inaccessibility to counting and investigation is the foundation for the rhetorical questions of ch. 38-41.
36.27: kî yiḡraʿ niṭpê-māyim yāzōqû māṭār lᵉʾēḏô: 'For he draws up drops of water; they distill as rain from the mist.' Elihu begins the meteorological section (vv.27-33) that leads into the whirlwind speech. Gāraʿ niṭpê māyim (he draws up/diminishes drops of water) — the water cycle: evaporation is God's work. Zāqaq (to refine, distill, filter) — the rain is presented as a purification process, water drawn from the depths and refined through the atmosphere. The science is observational and accurate; the theological claim is that this natural process is God's continuous activity.
36.28: ʾăšer-yizzᵉlû šᵉḥāqîm ṭāp̄û ʿălê ʾāḏām rāḇ: 'Which the clouds pour down and drop on people in abundance.' Šᵉḥāqîm (clouds, thin clouds, sky-dust) — the upper atmosphere as the medium through which rain is delivered. Rāḇ (abundance, in great quantity) — the rain as extravagant divine provision, not merely adequate moisture but abundance. The movement from cosmic water-drawing (v.27) to abundant rainfall on humanity (v.28) describes God's care: what he draws from the depths he pours out for human benefit.
36.29: ʾap̄ ʾim-yāḇîn miprᵉśê ʿāḇ taršûʾôt sukkātô: 'Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds or the thunder from his canopy?' Miprᵉśê ʿāḇ (spreading of thick clouds) — the extension of storm clouds across the sky. Taršûʾôt sukkātô: 'the thunderings of his pavilion/canopy' — God's dwelling is a storm canopy from which thunder (taršûʾāh = crash, clatter — hapax) rolls. The question (ʾim-yāḇîn = can anyone understand?) introduces the epistemological challenge: human observation of weather cannot penetrate to the divine agency behind it.
36.30: hēn pāraś ʿālāyw ʾôrô wayyᵉšōreš rāʾšê hayyām: 'See, he scatters his lightning around him and covers the roots of the sea.' Pāraś ʾôrô (he spreads his light/lightning) — the lightning is God's self-illumination broadcast around him. Yᵉšōreš rāʾšê hayyām: 'covers the roots of the sea' — the deepest foundations of the ocean are covered by divine illumination. The lightning reaching to the sea's depths is a statement about the vertical extent of God's presence: from the storm above to the ocean floor below, he is present and active.
36.31: kî-bām yāḏîn ʿammîm yitten-ʾōḵel lᵉmakbîr: 'For by these he governs peoples and provides food in abundance.' Yāḏîn ʿammîm (he judges/governs peoples) — the same storm phenomena that demonstrate God's cosmic power are the instruments of his historical governance of nations. Yitten ʾōḵel lᵉmakbîr: 'he gives food in abundance' — rain (vv.27-28) becomes food provision (cf. Matt 5:45: he sends rain on the just and unjust). The storm is simultaneously judicial and nourishing — God's dual use of natural phenomena.
36.32: ʿal-kappāyim kissāh ʾôr wayyᵉṣaw ʿālêhā bᵉmaprîaʿ: 'He covers his hands with the lightning and commands it to strike its mark.' The anthropomorphic image: God holds lightning in his hands (kappāyim = palms) and then releases it with a command. Maprîaʿ (that which strikes, the one who intercedes/encounters — disputed; possibly from pāgaʿ = to strike or meet) — the lightning is not random but directed: it hits its appointed mark. The divine volition behind each lightning strike is the theological claim.
36.33: yaggîḏ ʿālāyw rēʿô miqqᵉneh ʾap̄ ʿal-ʿôleh: 'Its thunder announces his presence; the cattle also sense the approaching storm.' Rēʿô (his companion/friend — or 'his thunder' depending on the vowel pointing; most read this as 'thunder' from rāʿam) — the thunder is God's herald, announcing his approach. Miqqᵉneh ʾap̄ (the cattle also — from qānāh = livestock) — even animals perceive the approach of the storm before humans do. The animals' instinctive sensing of divine weather activity is a subtle claim: creation is more responsive to its Creator than the humans who argue about him.

37.1: ʾap̄-zōʾt yirʾaḏ libbî wayyiṭṭar mimmᵉqômô: 'At this my heart trembles and leaps from its place.' Elihu describes a physical-emotional response to the approaching storm he has been describing (36:27-33). Rāʿaḏ (to tremble, quake) — the same verb used for the trembling of the earth at divine presence (Ps 18:7; 77:18; Isa 5:25). Yiṭṭar mimmᵉqômô (leaps from its place) — the heart literally 'moves from its place' in fright. Elihu's physical response establishes the appropriate creaturely posture before divine power: awe and physical fear, not calm analysis. This sets the tone for the whirlwind speech to follow.
37.2: šimᵉʿû šāmôaʿ bᵉrōgez qōlô wᵉhegeh mippîw yēṣēʾ: 'Listen carefully to the roar of his voice and the rumble that goes out from his mouth.' Šāmôaʿ šāmaʿ (listen, truly listen — infinitive absolute for emphasis) — the same emphatic hearing-command as in Deut 6:4 (Shema). Rōgez (trembling, noise, commotion) — God's voice as the rumble of thunder. The identification of thunder with the divine voice is consistent in the OT: Ps 18:13-14; 29:3-9 (where the divine voice appears seven times in thunder). The invitation to listen to the storm is an invitation to hear God speak through the natural world.
37.3: taḥat kāl-haššāmayim yišrēhû wᵉʾôrô ʿal-kanpôṯ hāʾāreṣ: 'He sends it out under the whole sky, and his lightning to the ends of the earth.' The spatial comprehensiveness of the storm: taḥat kāl-haššāmayim (under the entire sky) and ʾal-kanpôṯ hāʾāreṣ (to the wings/edges of the earth). The four extremities (kānāp̄ = wing, edge) of the earth receive the divine lightning — there is no part of the created world outside the storm's reach. The cosmic scale of the storm both humbles the listener and communicates the universality of divine sovereignty.
37.4: ʾaḥărāyw yišʾag-qôl yārʿēm bᵉqôl gᵉʾônô wᵉlōʾ yᵉʿaqqᵉḇēm kî-yiššāmaʿ qôlô: 'After the lightning a voice roars; he thunders in his majestic voice and does not hold the lightnings back once his voice is heard.' Yārʿēm bᵉqôl gᵉʾônô: 'he thunders in the voice of his majesty' (gāʾôn = majesty, pride, exaltation — used of God's majesty, Exod 15:7; Isa 2:10). The lightning precedes the thunder as God's self-announcement: the visible act comes first, then the audible proclamation. The phrase wᵉlōʾ yᵉʿaqqᵉḇēm (he does not hold them/restrain them) — once God speaks, the forces he has released cannot be recalled.
37.5: yarʿēm ʾēl bᵉqôlô niplāʾôt ʿōśeh gᵉḏōlôṯ wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ: 'God thunders astoundingly with his voice; he does great things beyond our understanding.' Niplāʾôt (wonders, things too wonderful, things beyond normal capacity) — the same word used for God's deeds in the Exodus (Exod 3:20; 34:10) and creation (Ps 139:14). Wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ (and we do not know) — the epistemological limit: God's great acts exceed human comprehension even when observed. This is the refrain of the divine speech to follow: you cannot understand what you have not experienced.
37.6: kî lašeleg yōʾmar hᵉwē-ʾāreṣ wᵉgeśem māṭār ûgeśem miṭrôṯ ʿuzzô: 'For he says to the snow, “Fall upon the earth,” and to the gentle rain and to the great downpour of his might.' God speaks to the snow and rain — natural phenomena receive divine commands. The three precipitation types: šeleg (snow), geśem māṭār (rain-shower), and geśem miṭrôṯ ʿuzzô (downpour of his might — heavy rain). The divine word creates and commands the natural world, not just human history: the same dāḇar theology of Genesis 1 applies to the ongoing governance of weather.
37.7: bᵉyaḏ kāl-ʾāḏām yaḥtôm lāḏaʿat kāl-ʾanšê maʿăśēhû: 'He seals the hand of every person so that all humanity may know his work.' Ḥāṯam bᵉyad (to seal in the hand) — the storm forces human beings to stop their outdoor activity and remain inside (hands are sealed/stopped from working). The theological purpose: this forced cessation allows all humanity to perceive (lāḏaʿat = to know, recognize) God's work. Storm-compelled rest becomes an occasion for divine knowledge — a pattern that recurs in Israel's sabbath theology.
37.8: wayyābōʾ ḥayyāh beʾārāḇ ûḇimᵉʿônōṯêhā ṯiškôn: 'Then the wild animals go into their dens and stay in their lairs.' The animal response to storm: the wild animals (ḥayyāh = wild beast) return to their dens (ʾārāḇ = ambush, hiding place) and dwelling places. The animals instinctively respond to the storm as a divine signal to withdraw — they know their place relative to God's power. The implicit contrast: the animals find their proper shelter during divine activity; Job has been standing exposed in argument during his own storm of suffering.
37.9: min-haḥeder tāḇôʾ sûp̄āh ûmiqqimrôṯ qārāh: 'From the south comes the whirlwind and from the north comes the cold.' Sûp̄āh (whirlwind, storm-wind) — the word used for the whirlwind from which YHWH will speak (38:1). The storm approaches from the south; the cold from mezārîm (north winds, scattering winds). The specific directional geography of the storm reflects the actual weather patterns of the Levant. Elihu's description is building toward the actual whirlwind of ch. 38 — the same storm system he is describing rhetorically will arrive as divine voice.
37.10: minnišmat ʾēl yinnāṭeh-qāraḥ ûrōḥab māyim bᵉmûṣāq: 'By the breath of God ice is formed and the wide waters are frozen solid.' Nišmat ʾēl (the breath of God) — the same phrase used for God's life-giving breath in Gen 2:7 and Job 33:4. The divine breath that creates human life also freezes water: the same capacity to transform and constitute matter operates at all scales. Māyim bᵉmûṣāq (waters in a mold/cast) — the frozen water takes on a fixed form, like metal poured into a cast; the liquid becomes solid by divine breath alone.
37.11: ʾap̄-brî yatʿîh ʿāḇ yāp̄îṣ ʿănan ʾôrô: 'He loads the clouds with moisture and scatters his lightning-cloud.' Brî (moisture, fatness — from bārāh = to eat; possibly 'laden with moisture') — the clouds become heavy with water that God has loaded into them. Yāp̄îṣ ʿănan ʾôrô: 'he scatters his bright cloud/lightning cloud' — the dispersal of the charged clouds that produce lightning. The loading and scattering is God's direct action on the atmosphere, not natural process independent of divine agency.
37.12: wᵉhûʾ mᵉsibôṯ mithappêḵ bᵉṯaḥbûlôṯāyw lᵉp̄āʿolām kōl ʾăšer yᵉṣawwêm ʿal-pᵉnê ṯēḇel ʾarṣāh: 'It turns in every direction by his guidance, to accomplish whatever he commands across the face of the inhabited world.' Taḥbûlôt (guidance, steering, direction — from the root for a ship's rudder) — God steers the storm system with nautical precision. The storm goes wherever commanded (kōl ʾăšer yᵉṣawwêm = all that he commands it) across the face of the inhabited world (tēḇel = productive earth, the inhabited world). Divine sovereignty extends from cosmic weather patterns to specific commanded outcomes.
37.13: ʾim-lᵉšēḇeṭ ʾim-lᵉʾarṣô ʾim-lᵉḥeseḏ yamṣiʾēhû: 'He brings it whether as discipline, for his land's sake, or as steadfast love.' The threefold purpose of divinely directed weather: šēḇeṭ (punishment, discipline — rod/staff of correction), ʾarṣô (his land — perhaps 'for the sake of the earth/land'), and ḥeseḏ (steadfast love, covenant loyalty). The same storm can be judgment, provision, or grace depending on God's purpose. This directly challenges any single-axis interpretation of suffering (all judgment or all grace) — divine action through natural means is multi-purposeful and cannot be read by humans with certainty.
37.14: haʾăzîn zōʾt ʾîyôḇ ʿămōḏ wᵉhiṯḇônēn niplᵉʾôṯ ʾēl: 'Listen to this, Job; stop and reflect on the wondrous works of God.' Elihu addresses Job by name for the last time. ʿĂmōḏ wᵉhiṯbônēn (stand still and contemplate) — the imperative of stillness before divine wonder: cease arguing, stop, and reflect. Niplᵉʾôṯ ʾēl (the wonders of God) — the same vocabulary as v.5. The contemplative posture Elihu urges is exactly what the whirlwind speech will demand: stop your own speaking and receive the divine challenge. Elihu's final instruction to Job is the correct preparation for the encounter about to happen.
37.15: hᵉtēḏaʿ bᵉśûm ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem wᵉhôpîaʿ ʾôr ʿănānô: 'Do you know how God arranged these things and caused the lightning in his cloud to flash?' The rhetorical question form now adopted by Elihu anticipates YHWH's own series of unanswerable questions in ch. 38-41. Bᵉśûm ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem: 'when God places them' — the arrangement of storm phenomena is God's direct activity. Hôpîaʿ ʾôr ʿănānô: 'the lightning flashing from his cloud' — the cloud as the storage for divine light/energy. The progression from Elihu's questions to YHWH's questions is smooth: the same epistemological challenge, now at infinitely greater authority.
37.16: hᵉtēḏaʿ ʿal-miplaśê ʿāḇ mimplᵉʾôṯ dēʿôṯ tamîm: 'Do you understand how the clouds are balanced — the wonders of the one who is perfect in knowledge?' Miplaśê ʿāḇ (balancings of the clouds) — the suspension of water-laden clouds in the atmosphere was a scientific mystery of the ancient world; how do the clouds hang there? Dēʿôṯ tamîm: 'the one who is perfect/complete in knowledge' — the epithet for God as the epistemically complete being. Compare Elihu's earlier claim (36:4) to be tᵉmîm ḏēʿôt (complete in knowledge) — when he uses it of God, the claim is obviously correct; when he used it of himself, it was obviously overreach.
37.17: ʾăšer-bᵉḡāḏêḵā ḥammîm bihaśqîṭ ʾereṣ middārôm: 'Why your clothes are warm when the earth lies still under the south wind?' A more intimate observation: the warming effect of the south wind on human clothing. The question moves from cosmic (stars, seas, lightning) to bodily (your clothes feel warm). This range — from the astronomical to the personal — characterizes both Elihu's speech and YHWH's speech to follow: the Creator's knowledge encompasses the grandest and most immediate scales simultaneously.
37.18: tarqîaʿ ʿimmô lišᵉḥāqîm ḥăzāqîm kirʾî mûṣāq: 'Have you joined him in spreading out the sky, firm and hard as a poured metal mirror?' Rāqaʿ (to beat out, spread, hammer thin — used for hammering metal plates in Exod 39:3 and Num 17:4) — the sky is described as hammered out, spread like beaten metal. Kirʾî mûṣāq: 'like a poured metal mirror' — a rᵉʾî (mirror) made of mûṣāq (poured/cast metal). Ancient mirrors were polished bronze; the sky's firmness and reflective quality (reflecting light) suggested a metallic analogy to the ancient observer. The question asks whether Job participated in the making of this sky-dome — obviously not.
37.19: hôḏîʿēnû mah-nōʾmar lô lōʾ-naʿărōḵ mippᵉnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'Tell us what we should say to him; we cannot arrange our words because of our darkness.' Elihu concedes human limitation: lōʾ naʿărōḵ mippᵉnê ḥōšeḵ = 'we cannot arrange/set in order because of the darkness' — the ḥōšeḵ (darkness) is human ignorance. The use of the first person plural (nōʾmar, naʿărōḵ) includes Elihu himself in the category of the epistemically limited. This is the appropriate end of all human theodicy: we cannot arrange adequate speech to God.
37.20: hăyussappar lô kî ʾᵉḏabbēr ʾim-ʾāmar ʾîš kî yiḇalāʿ: 'Should he be told that I want to speak? Can anyone speak to him without being consumed?' Yiḇalāʿ (to be swallowed up, consumed) — the danger of human speech before God. The question is the inverse of Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:6: 'Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look at God'). Direct speech to God without invitation or appropriate mediation risks annihilation. The question prepares for YHWH's speech in ch. 38: God speaks to Job directly, which is itself an act of grace rather than destruction.
37.21: wᵉʿattāh lōʾ-rāʾû ʾôr bāhîr hûʾ bašᵉḥāqîm wᵉrûaḥ ʿāḇᵃrāh watᵉṭahᵃrēm: 'And now people cannot look at the bright light in the clouds; but the wind comes and sweeps them away.' The blinding brightness of the storm-lit sky exceeds human vision. ʾôr bāhîr (bright light) — the intensity of sunlight behind clouds, or lightning. Rûaḥ ʿāḇᵃrāh (the wind passes through) and sweeps the clouds away. The clearing of the sky after the storm is here the act of divine wind; the rûaḥ (spirit/wind) is the same word used for God's creative breath throughout Genesis. The storm clears to reveal what was hidden — a theological pattern that anticipates the clarity of the divine speech.
37.22: mizzāhāḇ yêṯê zāhāḇ ʿal-ʾĕlôah nôrāʾ hôḏ: 'From the north comes golden brightness; God comes in fearsome majesty.' Zāhāḇ (gold) — the northern sky's brightness compared to gold. Hôḏ nôrāʾ (fearsome majesty, awesome splendor) — hôḏ is the royal majesty-term (Ps 96:6; 104:1; 111:3), here combined with nôrāʾ (awesome, fear-inspiring). The approach of God in the storm is the approach of the divine King in his full royal splendor — the appropriate response is awe, not argument.
37.23: šaddāy lōʾ-mᵉṣāʾnūhû śāggîʾ-ḵōaḥ ûmišpāṭ wᵉrōḇ-ṣᵉḏāqāh lōʾ yᵉʿanneh: 'As for the Almighty, we cannot reach him; he is great in power, in justice, and in abundant righteousness; he does not oppress.' Lōʾ mᵉṣāʾnūhû (we cannot find/reach him) — the epistemological inaccessibility of God is the final word before YHWH speaks. Yet the inaccessible God is not arbitrary: mišpāṭ (justice) and ṣᵉḏāqāh (righteousness) characterize him. He does not oppress (lōʾ yᵉʿanneh — from ʿānāh = to answer/respond, here perhaps = to afflict, or 'he does not violate justice'). The God who is beyond reach is also the God of reliable justice — exactly the combination Job has struggled to reconcile.
37.24: lāḵēn yᵉrēʾûhû ʾănāšîm lōʾ-yirʾeh kol-ḥakᵉmê-lēḇ: 'Therefore people fear him; he does not attend to those who are wise in their own eyes.' The conclusion: the appropriate human response to the inaccessible majestic God is yirʾāh (fear/awe — the foundational Proverbs wisdom term, Prov 1:7). Lōʾ yirʾeh kol-ḥakᵉmê lēḇ: 'he does not see/attend to those wise in their own hearts' — self-generated wisdom does not earn divine attention; the self-wise are invisible to God's regard. This is Elihu's last word, and it is also the theme YHWH will embody in the whirlwind: true wisdom begins in creaturely awe, not in self-assured argument.
